whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: Rapid rate palpitations with or without - General fatigue or weakness or exhaustion; Dizziness, near syncope or syncope; Shortness of breath; Chest pain. More marked on exertion. SIGNS: Irregularly irregular pulse; Variable heart sound.
risk: LOOK FOR RISK FACTORS: Prior valvular heart disease or CHF or MI; Prior TIA or stroke or embolic episode; Hypertension, DM, COPD, CKD, Obesity. PRECIPITATING FACTORS: Post (cardiac) surgery; Alcoholism or binge drinking; Myo-pericarditis or ACS; Pneumonitis or pulmonary embolism; Sepsis, hyperthyroidism. CATEGORIZE AF: Paroxysmal AF (episodes <7 days); Persistent AF (7 days to 1 year); Long standing persistent AF (>1 year); Permanent AF (heart rate control as only option). MANAGEMENT PRINCIPLES: Categorize AF; Look for immediate intervention indicators; Assess stroke risk & need for anti-coagulation; Assess bleeding risk; Need for rate control; Consideration for rhythm control.
scores: STROKE RISK SCORE (CHA2DS2-VASc): Congestive heart failure/LV dysfunction=1; Hypertension=1; Aged >=75 years=2; Diabetes mellitus=1; Stroke/TIA/TE=2; Vascular disease (prior MI, PAD or aortic plaque)=1; Aged 65-74 years=1; Sex category (female gender)=1; Maximum Score=9. OAC if score >1 in men and >2 in women. BLEEDING RISK SCORE (HAS-BLED): Hypertension i.e. uncontrolled BP=1; Abnormal renal/liver function=1 or 2; Stroke=1; Bleeding tendency or predisposition=1; Labile INR=1; Age (e.g. >65)=1; Drugs (e.g. concomitant aspirin or NSAIDs or alcohol)=1; Maximum Score=9. Bleeding Risk High in score >3.
investigations: BASIC: Hemograms; Blood sugar, Creatinine; Electrolytes; 12 lead ECG. DESIRABLE: Plain X-ray chest; Thyroid evaluation; Liver function test; Troponins; Prothrombin time, INR (Coagulation profile); Echocardiography. OPTIONAL: Prolonged ECG monitoring; Trans-esophageal echocardiography; Exercise Stress Test; CT scan; MRI; EP study; Coronary angiography. WHAT TO LOOK FOR IN ECG: Ventricular rate; Chamber enlargement; Pre-excitation; Prior MI; Bundle branch block; QT interval.
drugs: HEART RATE CONTROL (in all except hemodynamic instability): Beta blocker or calcium blocker or combination; BB + digoxin in HF; rate aim to be less than 110/min. (PHC: start Metoprolol if HR >110/min & no evidence of CHF.) CHOICE OF ANTI-COAGULATION: Vitamin K antagonist - Aim for INR 2-3; Assess risk of bleeding; Take measures to reduce/modify risk of bleeding; Dietary modification & regular monitoring. MEASURES TO REDUCE HIGH BLEEDING RISK: Control SBP to less than 140 mmHg; Avoid dietary indiscretions; Avoid concomitant aspirin, anti platelets, NSAIDs; Avoid alcohol; Correct anemia. RHYTHM CONTROL - Pharmacological Cardioversion: CHF/CAD/Abnormal LVH -> Amiodarone; Normal Heart -> Flecainide/Ibutilide/Propafenone OR Pill in pocket (Flecainide OR Propafenone). Long Term Rhythm Control: CHF -> Amiodarone; Normal Heart -> Flecainide/Propafenone/Sotalol; CAD,LVH -> Amiodarone/Sotalol.
management: AT PHC/CHC: Detailed clinical evaluation; Basic investigations; Careful ECG evaluation; Start OAC if indicated (based on Stroke risk); Start Metoprolol if HR >110/min & no evidence of CHF; Refer if indicators for early intervention. AT DISTRICT HOSPITAL: Admit if indicators of early interventions; Immediate cardioversion after heparinization if hemodynamic instability; Manage precipitating factors if any; Assess stroke, bleeding risk & coagulation parameters; Detailed echocardiography; Start OAC, maintain INR around 2-3; Control HR by single drug or combination of BB & Ca Blocker; Refer if HR uncontrolled or CHF or angina. AT TERTIARY CENTRE: Re-assess clinical status, adequacy of AC; Consider need of NOAC; Optimise management of underlying cardiac disease; Stress life style and AF risk factor modification; Assess need for rhythm control and discuss pros & cons; Consider RFA in select patient.
referralRedFlags: LOOK FOR IMMEDIATE INTERVENTION INDICATORS: Systolic BP 90 mmHg, HR >150 or <50/min; Ongoing Angina; CHF or TIA or stroke; Major bleed on Oral Anti-coagulants. CONVERSION TO NSR indications: Hemodynamic instability; Uncontrolled symptoms despite HR control; Unacceptable rate control drug side effects; Patients preference.
algorithmNotes: MANAGEMENT ALGORITHM: Sign/symptoms suggestive of AF -> Confirm by 12 channel rhythm strip -> Hemodynamic Instability? If YES -> Drug version -> Successful? Yes=Continue drug, No=DC version. If NO -> Very rapid HR >130/min? If YES -> CHF? Yes=Careful BB/Dig, Amiodarone; No=BB or Ca Block or combine -> HR control aim <110/min -> if HR>110/min Consider DC version. If NO (not very rapid) -> Symptomatic? Yes=invasive options; No=Clinical Follow-up. Anti-coagulants in all EXCEPT: Reversible; Score <1 (men); <2 (women). KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: Patient with ANY of the following symptoms AND a pulse rate <50 bpm (persistent): Syncope/presyncope/dizziness; Lethargy/fatigue; Breathlessness/chest pain on exertion. BASIC EVALUATION - HISTORY: Syncope/presyncope (frequency, associated fall/injury/incontinence); Exertional angina or known coronary artery disease; Known hypothyroidism or kidney disease; On beta-blockers, Calcium Channel Blockers or digoxin; Patient with an implanted pacemaker or other device; Yellow oleander poisoning. EXAMINATION: Drowsiness/impaired consciousness; BP, heart rate.
risk: GENERAL APPROACH (rule out associated conditions): Renal dysfunction, hyperkalemia; Drug toxicity (BB, CCB, clonidine, Lithium); Sleep apnea (Epworth Sleepiness Scale for initial assessment). STABLE patients - Findings on 12-lead ECG: Atrioventricular block; Sinus node dysfunction; Other conduction disorders with 1:1 AV conduction; Non-diagnostic ECG.
scores: INDICATIONS FOR PERMANENT PACING: AV NODAL DISEASE - Complete heart block, advanced AV block, or Mobitz Type II block; Symptomatic patients with AV block other than above; Associated neuromuscular disease. SINUS NODE DYSFUNCTION - Symptomatic patients with sinus pauses >3 s long with symptom correlation; Asymptomatic patients with sinus pauses >6 s long. OTHER CONDUCTION DISORDERS WITH 1:1 AV CONDUCTION - Symptomatic patients with HV >=100 ms on EPS; Others (alternating BBB, infiltrative/neuromuscular disease).
investigations: TESTS TO BE DONE (PHC/CHC): 12-lead ECG; Blood urea, serum creatinine; Electrolytes; Blood sugar. ADDITIONAL TESTING: Advanced imaging (cMRI) if infiltrative disease suspected; Ambulatory ECG (for first/second degree AV block symptom correlation, suspected sinus node disease pauses, symptomatic LBBB or bifascicular block); Implantable Loop Recorder and EPS (consult published society guidelines); Transthoracic echocardiography. ECG examples: Sinus Bradycardia; Third Degree Heart Block.
drugs: TREATMENT OF UNSTABLE PATIENTS - 1. Treat associated conditions: Hyperkalemia; Suspected drug (BB or CCB) overdose: (i) Withhold the drug, (ii) IV insulin (1 U/kg bolus followed by 0.5 U/kg/h) with glucose monitoring (or IV glucagon if available). 2. Temporary pacemaker insertion (IV dopamine or adrenaline may be given till the time TPI can be placed).
management: RECOMMENDED PACING MODES: 1. SND with intact AV conduction - Atrial-based single or dual chamber pacing; VVI pacing reasonable if symptoms infrequent. 2. AV node disease - VVI/Dual chamber pacing if LVEF >50%; CRT (or HBP) if LVEF 36-50% and requiring ventricular pacing >40% of the time; CRT (or HBP) if LVD <35%.
referralRedFlags: INDICATIONS FOR URGENT TREATMENT/REFERRAL: Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg), impaired consciousness or ongoing chest pain; Recurrent or ongoing syncope/presyncope; Associated headache with or without neurologic deficit (suspect intracranial event); Patient with a pre-existing device; If ECG available, evidence of any of - Complete heart block, Sinus node disease with pauses >3 s long, Bradycardia (HR <50 bpm) (with or without hyperkalemia, serum K >5 mEq/L).
algorithmNotes: Two arms: Evaluation & treatment of UNSTABLE patients (treat associated conditions + temporary pacemaker) vs Evaluation & management of STABLE patients (12-lead ECG findings). Note: pulse rate threshold <50 bpm persistent.

whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: 1. Dyspnea/orthopnea/PND; 2. Pink frothy sputum; 3. Dependent pedal edema; 4. Recent weight gain; 5. Easy fatiguability; 6. H/o CHF/MI. SIGNS: 1. Tachypnoea; 2. Tachycardia or irregular pulse; 3. Basal crepitations; 4. Cardiomegaly; 5. Presence of murmurs; 6. Systemic desaturation. Clinical signs (body): Raised JVP, Pulmonary Oedema, Pleural Effusions, Hepatomegaly, Cardiomegaly, Ascites, Pitting Oedema.
risk: ADDITIONAL INFORMATION: Prior history of respiratory illness like asthma or COPD; Known patient of CHF/similar illness in past with response to therapy; Prior history of RHD, CAD, pregnancy, cancer chemotherapy; Risk factors: HT, DM, smoking, hyperlipidemia or premature CAD in first degree relatives. COMMON ETIOLOGY AND INDICATORS: 1. Ischemic cardiomyopathy (past MI); 2. Diabetic cardiomyopathy; 3. RHD (existing valvular disease); 4. Post-viral (acute onset breathlessness within last 3 months); 5. Peri-partum cardiomyopathy (onset in last trimester or after delivery); 6. Idiopathic cardiomyopathy; 7. Post-cancer chemotherapy. CONSIDER AT ALL LEVELS: Smoking cessation; Salt restriction; Physical activity; Weight reduction; Moderation of alcohol; Control of DM/HTN/Lipids; Secondary CVD prevention with aspirin and statins.
scores: Categorize (tertiary): Acute (<3 months) vs Chronic (>3 months); HFrEF (EF <35% / EF 35%) vs HFpEF (EF 35-50%). Confirm HFrEF on echo when LV ejection fraction <35%.
investigations: BASIC: Hemogram, ESR; Blood sugar; Urine examination; Urea/Creatinine; Sodium/Potassium; ECG; Chest X-ray PA view. WHAT TO LOOK FOR IN X-RAY: Cardiomegaly; Pulmonary venous congestion; Pneumonia or other lung pathology. WHAT TO LOOK FOR IN ECG: Pathological Q wave; Conduction abnormalities especially LBBB; Chamber enlargement; Atrial fibrillation (Note: if ST elevation present, manage as STEMI). DESIRABLE: 2D Echocardiography; BNP/NT pro-BNP; Troponin; Lipid profile; Thyroid function test; Iron profile. OPTIONAL: Prolonged ECG monitoring; Coronary angiography; Radionuclide imaging; CT scan; MRI; PET; Myocardial biopsy; Electrophysiological study.
drugs: FUROSEMIDE: 20-80 mg daily PO; IV 10-40 mg SOS in acute stage; change to oral when symptoms subside; monitor serum electrolytes, creatinine and uric acid on therapy. SPIRONOLACTONE: 25-50 mg once daily PO; keep watch on serum potassium and creatinine every 2-4 weekly. CARVEDILOL: 3.125 to 25 mg twice daily PO; start after decongestion with low dose with BP >100 mmHg and HR >60/min; uptitrate dose 1-2 weekly till maximum tolerable dose; keep watch on BP, heart rate and recipitation of CHF symptoms; increase diuretics and reduce carvedilol to manage reappearance of CHF. ENALAPRIL: 2.5 to 10 mg twice daily PO; start with low dose with BP >100 mmHg, normal electrolyte and creatinine less than 2.5 mg/dl; uptitrate dose 1-2 weekly till maximum tolerable dose; keep watch on BP and electrolytes before every increment and on follow-up. Tertiary: also consider ARNI and ivabradine; Pneumococcal and influenza vaccines.
management: AT PHC: Rule out respiratory cause (breathlessness with fever, cough and expectoration or known asthma/COPD); Likely CHF: decongest with furosemide. -> REFER TO CHC. AT CHC: Admit and stabilize; routine investigations; ECG (rule out acute ST-Elevation MI); X-ray chest (rule out respiratory etiology); decongest with IV furosemide; O2 therapy if systemic saturation <90%; start enalapril and spironolactone orally; consider carvedilol after decongestion. Keep watching: respiratory distress & O2 saturation, BP & heart rate, electrolytes & renal parameters. -> REFER TO DISTRICT HOSPITAL. AT DISTRICT HOSPITAL: Admit and re-assess; optimise therapy (furosemide/enalapril/spironolactone/O2) and stabilize; consider non-invasive ventilation if marked respiratory distress and O2 saturation <90%; Echocardiography (confirm HFrEF: LV EF <35%); search for etiological diagnosis; consider carvedilol after decongestion; refer back to CHC/PHC after stabilization. AT TERTIARY HOSPITAL: 1. Re-assess and confirm diagnosis of HF; 2. Categorize acute/chronic & HFrEF/HFpEF; 3. Optimize therapy (furosemide, enalapril, carvedilol, spironolactone, O2); 4. Consider ARNI and ivabradine; 5. Pneumococcal and influenza vaccines; 6. Investigate for etiology and manage; 7. Non-pharmacological invasive therapy: a. ICD in selected patients (Ref Arrhythmia STW), b. BiV (NYHA II/III, EF<35%, QRS>150msec sinus rhythm with LBBB, optimal medical therapy >3 months); 8. Etiology based interventions: a. PCI, b. Valve replacement, c. CABG.
referralRedFlags: PHC REFER IF: BP <90 mmHg or >200 mmHg; Heart rate <50/min or >120/min; Respiratory rate >30/min or cyanosis; Oliguria; Altered sensorium. REFER TO TERTIARY CARE IF: CHF uncontrolled; Unstable hemodynamics; Suspected ongoing ischemia; Abnormal electrolytes; Abnormal renal functions; Structural heart disease; Unclear etiology.
algorithmNotes: Flow: PHC -> Community Health Centre -> District Hospital -> Tertiary Hospital, with stabilize/decongest at each level and refer-back after stabilization. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. Abbrev: HFrEF=Heart Failure with reduced Ejection Fraction; HFpEF=preserved EF; PND=Paroxysmal Nocturnal Dyspnea; ICD=Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator; BiV=Bi-Ventricular Pacing.

whenToSuspect: PATIENT PRESENTING WITH CHEST PAIN. CONSIDER ANGINA IF: Diffuse retrosternal pain, heaviness or constriction, radiating to arms or neck or back; Associated with sweating; Easily reproduced with post-meal exertion; Consider atypical presentation (exertional fatigue or breathlessness or profuse sweating or epigastric discomfort). Likelihood more if known patient of CAD. STABLE ANGINA = any effort related pain fitting in previous category, relieved by rest or NTG in 1-2 min. ANGINA UNLIKELY IF: Variable location or characteristic; long lasting (hours to days) or short lasting (<a minute); restricted to areas above jaw or below epigastrium; localized to a point; pricking or piercing or stabbing type of pain; precipitated by movement of neck or arms or respiration.
risk: CATEGORIZE ANGINA: Acute Coronary Syndrome (angina at rest or >20 min; recent worsening of stable angina (crescendo) to CCS class III; new onset effort angina <1 month in CCS class II/III; post infarction angina) -> for management refer to STEMI/NSTEMI STW. GENERAL MANAGEMENT: 1. Manage factors potentiating angina (Anemia, Thyrotoxicosis, Pregnancy, febrile illness; Hypertension, Ventricular hypertrophy, CHF; Tachy or brady-arrhythmia; Drugs: bronchodilators, steroids); 2. Risk factor control; 3. Other atherosclerotic CV disease: PVD, stroke; 4. Secondary prevention: Statins, BB, ACE-I.
scores: RISK CATEGORIZATION (based on clinical features, GRACE score & TIMI score): A. Very high - Acute LVF, Hypotension, Uncontrolled Ventricular arrhythmia, Severe MR. B. High Risk - GRACE score >140 or TIMI score >4. C. Intermediate Risk - GRACE score 109-140 or TIMI score 2-3. D. Low Risk - GRACE score <108 or TIMI score 0-1.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: 1. Hemogram; 2. Urea, Creatinine, Electrolytes; 3. Sugar, HbA1C; 4. Lipids; 5. Liver function test; 6. ECG; 7. Plain X-ray chest. DESIRABLE: 1. Echocardiography; 2. Exercise Treadmill Test; 3. Thyroid Function Test; 4. Iron profile; 5. Uric acid. OPTIONAL: 1. Stress radionuclide/echocardiographic imaging; 2. CT scan including multi-slice coronary angiography; 3. Coronary Angiography; 4. Coronary Fractional Flow Reserve; 5. Intra-vascular Ultrasound/OCT.
drugs: ANTI-PLATELETS: 1. Aspirin 75 mg OD; 2. Clopidogrel 75 mg OD (if intolerant to aspirin). STATINS: Atorvastatin 40-80 mg OD; Rosuvastatin 20-40 mg OD. ACE-INHIBITOR: Ramipril 2.5-10 mg OD; Enalapril 2.5-10 mg BD. ANTI-ISCHEMIC: 1. Metoprolol - Short acting 25-100 mg BD, Long acting 25-100 mg OD; 2. Nitrates - Isosorbide mono-nitate 20 to 60 mg in 2 divided dose, Nitroglycerine sustained release 2.6 to 6.5 mg BD; 3. Calcium channel blockers - Verapamil 40-80 mg TDS, Diltiazem 30 to 90 mg TDS; 4. Nicorandil 5-10 mg BD; 5. Ranolazine 500-1000 mg BD; 6. Trimetazidine 20 mg TDS.
management: PHC/CHC LEVEL: 1. Control angina (Metoprolol; add nitrates if symptoms not controlled); 2. ECG for Q waves, ST-T changes, BBB or chamber enlargement; 3. Aspirin & high intensity statins; 4. Refer to higher centre electively. DISTRICT HOSPITAL: 1. Optimise anti-anginal treatment; 2. Echocardiography for LV function or structural heart disease; 3. Risk stratify by exercise treadmill test (DUKE risk score) if patient ambulatory and ECG interpretable; 4. Refer to tertiary if: angina uncontrolled on optimal medical therapy, echo reveals abnormality, non-ambulatory or un-interpretable ECG, high risk on exercise stress test for possible re-vascularization. TERTIARY LEVEL: 1. Reassess and optimise drug therapy (if uncontrolled choose from trimetazidine, nicorandil, ranolazine, ivabradine); 2. Risk stratify with exercise treadmill test if not already done; 3. Stress imaging if: non-ambulatory patient, abnormal/uninterpretable baseline ECG, exercise treadmill test equivocal, compromised LV function. REVASCULARIZATION: Revascularize if anatomy suitable; prefer CABG over PCI in DM with multivessel/left main disease; complete re-vascularization preferable; use invasive functional & imaging modalities (FFR, IVUS, OCT) when indicated; continue dual anti-platelets (aspirin and clopidogrel) after PCI.
referralRedFlags: RISK CATEGORY MANAGEMENT: Low/Intermediate Risk Group - optimal anti-anginal therapy; follow up 3-6 monthly at primary/secondary care centre; refer to tertiary when change in symptomatic status. High Risk Group - discuss pros & cons of possible revascularization and dual anti-platelet therapy; angiography if: angina not controlled on optimal medical therapy, high risk on non-invasive testing, cardiac arrest survivor or documented VT.
algorithmNotes: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES: STRENGTHEN SECONDARY PREVENTION WITH STATINS, BB & ACE-I.

whenToSuspect: CONSIDER ANGINA IF: Diffuse retrosternal pain, heaviness or constriction; radiation to arms or neck or back; associated with sweating; easily reproduced with post-meal exertion; atypical presentation (exertional fatigue/breathlessness/profuse sweating/epigastric discomfort/syncope). More likelihood if known patient of CAD/multiple risk factors. ACUTE CORONARY SYNDROME: 1. Angina at rest or >20 min; 2. Recent worsening of stable angina (crescendo) to CCS class III; 3. New onset effort angina <1 month in CCS class II/III; 4. Post infarction angina. ECG: If ST Elevation follow STEMI protocol (includes new onset LBBB); if no ST Elevation -> UA/NSTEMI. ANGINA UNLIKELY IF: variable location; long lasting or short lasting; restricted above jaw/below epigastrium; localized to a point; pricking/piercing/stabbing; precipitated by movement of neck/arms/respiration.
risk: Time windows: STEMI within 12 hours; STEMI 12-24 hours; STEMI after 24 hours (each with own pathway). LOOK FOR OTHER CAUSES OF CHEST PAIN (ongoing or within 12 hrs): Unequal or absent peripheral pulses / Dissection of Aorta; Respiratory evaluation - Pleuritis/Pneumonitis/embolism/pneumothorax; Pericardial rub; Neuralgia or herpes.
scores: STEMI after 24 hours - angiography with view to PCI only if any of: recurrent anginal pain not controlled by medical therapy; cardiogenic shock; acute LVF; mechanical complication; dynamic ST-T changes; life threatening ventricular arrhythmias.
investigations: GENERAL MEASURES: Routine bio-chemistry and serial cardiac enzymes (troponin); continuous ECG monitoring; Echocardiography (particularly for mechanical complication). STEMI diagnosis confirmed with patient history & ECG ideally within 10 mins from First Medical Contact (FMC); all delays related to FMC.
drugs: ANTI-PLATELETS: 1. Aspirin loading 325 mg then 75 mg OD; 2. Clopidogrel loading 300 mg then 75 mg OD; 3. Prasugrel loading 60 mg then 10 mg OD; 4. Ticagralor loading 180 mg then 90 mg BD. ANTI-ISCHEMIC: Metoprolol short acting 25-100 mg BD / long acting 25-100 mg OD; Nitrates - Isosorbide mono-nitare 20-60 mg in 2 divided dose, Nitroglycerine sustained release 2.6-6.5 mg BD, Nitroglycerine IV 5-25 mcg/min infusion. STATINS: High dose Atorvastatin 80 mg OD. ACE-INHIBITOR: Ramipril 2.5-10 mg OD; Enalapril 2.5-10 mg BD. OXYGEN: if oxygen saturation below 90%. MORPHINE: titrated in dose of 2-4 mg IV every 15 minutes. BETA-BLOCKER: oral beta-blocker if LVEF <40%. ANTI-THROMBOTICS: 1. Unfractionated heparin - Bolus of 60 U/Kg (maximum 5000 U) then 12 U/Kg hourly infusion to maintain APTT at 50-70 sec; 2. Enoxaparin 1 mg/Kg SC 12 hrly. THROMBOLYTIC THERAPY: Tenecteplase - 35 mg IV bolus if 60-70 Kg, 40 mg if 70-80 Kg, 45 mg if more than 80 Kg; Reteplase - 10 mg IV bolus, repeat after 30 min; Alteplase - 15 mg IV bolus then 0.75 mg/Kg over 30 min upto 50 Kg weight then 0.5 mg/Kg over 60 min up to 35 mg; Streptokinase - 1.5 million units IV over 60 min.
management: GENERAL MEASURES (STEMI within 12 hrs): 1. Admit in ICU with continuous ECG monitoring & defibrillation; 2. Routine bio-chemistry & serial cardiac enzymes; 3. Pain relief by opioid; 4. O2 if saturation <90%; 5. Aspirin 325 mg, Clopidogrel 300 mg and Atorvastatin 80 mg; 6. Echocardiography. PCI CAPABLE HOSPITAL: Proceed for PCI; radial route preferred; preferably within 90 minutes; during procedure - use unfractionated heparin, no routine thrombosuction, tackle culprit artery only unless shock, DES preferred; post procedure - continue dual antiplatelets for at least 1 year. PCI INCAPABLE CENTRE: A. Transfer to PCI capable hospital if PCI can be performed within 120 min. B. If transfer not feasible: THROMBOLYSE - within 12 hours of symptom onset if no contra-indication; preferably fibrin specific agent Tenecteplase/TPA/Reteplase or Streptokinase if fibrin-specific unavailable; therapy started within 10 min. POST THROMBOLYSIS: ECG at 60-90 min after starting thrombolysis to assess success (>50% ST settlement with pain relief); if successful transfer for PCI within 3-24 hours; if failed transfer immediately; Enoxaparin (preferred over unfractionated heparin) till PCI or discharge. STEMI 12-24 hrs: transfer to PCI capable hospital immediately; if ongoing pain thrombolysis and transfer immediately.
referralRedFlags: ECG REVEALS ST ELEVATION MI (includes new onset LBBB) -> refer to primary angioplasty/thrombolysis capable hospital. ABSOLUTE CONTRA-INDICATIONS TO THROMBOLYTIC THERAPY: Previous intra-cerebral hemorrhage or stroke of unknown etiology; Ischemic stroke in last 6 months; CNS neoplasm or AV malformation; Recent (within 1 month) major trauma/surgery/head injury; Recent (within 1 month) major GI bleed; Known bleeding tendency (except menstrual bleed); Aortic dissection; Severe uncontrolled hypertension.
algorithmNotes: STEMI DIAGNOSIS pathway: Primary-PCI capable centre -> Primary PCI (preferably <60 mins) or Rescue PCI; EMS or non primary-PCI capable centre -> PCI possible <120 min? Yes=immediate transfer to PCI centre (preferably <90 min, <60 min in early presenters); No=Immediate fibrinolysis -> successful? then transfer to PCI centre (3-24 hrs) -> Coronary angiography. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: CONSIDER ANGINA IF: Diffuse retrosternal pain, heaviness or constriction; radiation to arms or neck or back; associated with sweating; easily reproduced with post-meal exertion; atypical presentation (exertional fatigue/breathlessness/profuse sweating/epigastric discomfort). More likelihood if known patient of CAD/multiple risk factors. ACUTE CORONARY SYNDROME: 1. Angina at rest or >20 min; 2. Recent worsening (crescendo) to CCS class III; 3. New onset effort angina <1 month CCS class II/III; 4. Post infarction angina. ECG: If ST Elevation -> STEMI STW; if no ST Elevation -> UA/NSTEMI. ANGINA UNLIKELY IF: variable location; long/short lasting; restricted above jaw/below epigastrium; localized to a point; pricking/piercing/stabbing; precipitated by movement of neck/arms/respiration.
risk: LOOK FOR OTHER CAUSES OF PROLONGED CHEST PAIN: Dissection of aorta (unequal/absent peripheral pulses); Respiratory evaluation - Pleuritis/pneumonitis/embolism/pneumothorax; Pericardial rub; Neuralgia or herpes. UA/NSTEMI RISK CATEGORIZATION (clinical features, GRACE & TIMI): A. Very high risk - Acute LVF, Hypotension, Uncontrolled Ventricular arrhythmia, Severe MR; B. High Risk - GRACE >140 or TIMI >4; C. Intermediate Risk - GRACE 109-140 or TIMI 2-3; D. Low Risk - GRACE <108 or TIMI 0-1.
scores: GRACE SCORE points: Killip Class I=0, II=20, III=39, IV=59. SBP(mmHg): <80=58, 80-99=53, 100-119=43, 120-139=34, 140-159=24, 160-199=10, >=200=0. Heart rate(bpm): <=50=0, 50-69=3, 70-89=9, 90-109=15, 110-149=24, 150-199=38, >=200=46. Age(y): <=30=0, 30-39=8, 40-49=25, 50-59=41, 60-69=58, 70-79=75, 80-89=91, >=90=100. Creatinine(mg/dL): 0-0.39=1, 0.40-0.79=4, 0.80-1.19=7, 1.20-1.59=10, 1.60-1.99=13, 2.00-3.99=21, >4.0=28. Other risk factors: Cardiac arrest at admission=39, ST-Segment Deviation=28, Elevated Cardiac Enzyme Levels=14. TIMI SCORE (1 point each): 1. Age >65 yrs; 2. More than 3 risk factors; 3. Known CAD (>50% lesion); 4. Recurrence of angina in 24 hrs; 5. Aspirin use within 7 days; 6. ST deviation >0.5 mV; 7. Raised cardiac markers.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: 1. Hemogram; 2. Creatinine; 3. Sugar, HbA1C; 4. Fasting lipids; 5. ECG; 6. Troponin T/Troponin I; 7. Plain X-ray chest. DESIRABLE: 1. Echocardiography; 2. Exercise Treadmill Test; 3. C reactive protein; 4. B-Natriuretic Peptide; 5. D dimer; 6. Bleeding and coagulation profile; 7. Liver function test; 8. Coronary Angiography. OPTIONAL: 1. Stress radionuclide/echocardiographic imaging; 2. CT including coronary angiography; 3. MRI; 4. Coronary Fractional Flow Reserve; 5. Intra-vascular Ultrasound; 6. VQ scan.
drugs: ANTI-PLATELETS: 1. Aspirin loading 325 mg then 75 mg OD; 2. Clopidogrel loading 300 mg then 75 mg OD; 3. Prasugrel loading 60 mg then 10 mg OD; 4. Ticagralor loading 180 mg then 90 mg BD. ANTI-THROMBOTICS: 1. Enoxaparin 1 mg/Kg SC 12 hrly; 2. Unfractionated heparin - Bolus of 60 U/Kg (maximum 5000 U) then 12 U/Kg hourly infusion to maintain APTT 50-70 sec. ANTI-ISCHEMIC: Metoprolol short acting 25-100 mg BD / long acting 25-100 mg OD; Nitrates - Isosorbide mono-nitare 20-60 mg in 2 divided dose, Nitroglycerine sustained release 2.6-6.5 mg BD, Nitroglycerine IV 5-25 mcg/min infusion. STATINS: High dose Atorvastatin 80 mg OD. ACE-INHIBITOR: Ramipril 2.5-10 mg OD; Enalapril 2.5-10 mg BD.
management: PHC/CHC LEVEL: 1. ECG, Troponin; 2. Start - Aspirin, Clopidogrel, Heparin/LMWH, High dose atorvastatin, Metoprolol; 3. Risk stratify GRACE score or TIMI score - refer High/Intermediate risk to PCI capable centre, refer Low risk for further evaluation to DH; 4. Refer to PCI capable centre if: Acute LVF, Hypotension, Systolic murmur, Arrythmia. DISTRICT HOSPITAL: 1. Admit ICU with ECG monitoring & defibrillator; 2. Troponin & bio-chemistry if not done; 3. Serial ECG & echocardiography; 4. Continue Aspirin, Clopidogrel, Heparin & Metoprolol; 5. Add nitrates if needed; 6. Management for different risk categories (Very high/High/Intermediate or LVEF<40%: refer for revascularization; Low risk: conservative management, life style modification, risk factor control, secondary prevention). TERTIARY CENTRE: 1. Admit, reassess, monitor in ICCU; 2. Continue aspirin and heparin; 3. Load with clopidogrel/prasugrel/ticagralor if not already done; 4. Optimal medical therapy (BB, high dose atorvastatin, ACE-inhibitors, IV nitrates if ongoing pain/severe MR/LVF); 5. Detailed echocardiography; 6. Low risk: non-invasive risk stratification (exercise stress test, CT coronary angiography or stress imaging); 7. Very high/high/intermediate risk: coronary revascularization.
referralRedFlags: RED FLAG SIGNS: Pain lasting >20 minutes; Recurrent or ongoing pain or rest pain; Associated breathlessness, profuse sweating or syncope; Hemodynamic instability -> Refer as emergency to nearest Primary PCI/Thrombolysis capable centre (rest pain beyond 24 hrs or without above features may be referred early for further evaluation). REVASCULARIZATION STRATEGY: Very High risk - urgent re-vascularization (within few hours) after loading preferably with Ticagrelor or prasugrel if PCI planned; High risk - early revascularization (within 24 hours); Intermediate risk - revascularization (within 72 hours); Continue dual anti-platelets in PCI patients for at least 12 months in DES and 3 months in BMS.
algorithmNotes: UA/NSTEMI DIAGNOSIS pathway: Very high risk/clinical instability -> immediate invasive <2h; High risk (GRACE>140, TIMI>4) -> early invasive 2-24h; Intermediate risk (GRACE 109-140, TIMI 2-3) -> delayed invasive 25-72h; Low risk (GRACE<109, TIMI<=1) -> medical/non-invasive strategy. At non-PCI-capable hospital: Very high risk=immediate transfer; High=same-day transfer; Intermediate=transfer for PCI within 72h; Low=transfer if pursuing invasive treatment. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURE.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: Usually a sequela of viral infection which causes ciliary impairment and bacterial superinfection. DIAGNOSIS: persistence of nasal blockage/nasal discharge and facial pain/hyposmia beyond 7 days (maximum upto 3 months). Children may present with acute febrile illness/cough associated with these symptoms.
risk: RELATED SCENARIOS: Recurrent acute sinusitis = episodes of acute sinusitis interspersed with symptom free intervals of >3 months. Symptoms <7 days treated as viral upper respiratory infection. Invasive fungal sinusitis suspected if additionally: facial hypoesthesia, facial skin/palatal/turbinate discoloration, proptosis/diplopia/reduced or loss of vision. ALTERNATIVE SCENARIOS: consider alternate diagnosis if unilateral symptoms/bleeding/crusting/cacosmia (foul smell); rule out contributory factors - allergy/upper alveolar dental caries/DNS/LPR/smoking; rhinorrhoea & nasal congestion in 2nd trimester of pregnancy is hormonal (manage with saline irrigation/drops).
scores: INDICATIONS OF PARENTERAL ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY: Orbital/intracranial complications; Non-resolution of symptoms with at least 7 days of oral antibiotics; Worsening of symptoms while on oral antibiotics.
investigations: CLINICAL EXAMINATION (Preliminary): Anterior rhinoscopy (discharge, bleeding, crusting, polyposis); Oral examination (dental caries, post nasal drip, palatal discolouration); assess contributory factors. Desirable: Nasal endoscopy. LABORATORY (desirable in non-resolving/worsening cases despite antibiotic therapy): Endoscopy for guided nasal swabs/KOH smear; CT PNS (for suspected complications/non-resolving cases on antibiotics for 14 days); Screen for Diabetes/Immunodeficiency.
drugs: PHC/PRIMARY (duration of treatment 7-14 days): Oral antibiotics - Amoxycillin/Co-amoxyclav for 7-10 days (Levofloxacin and Azithromycin can be opted for patients intolerant/sensitive to penicillins); Topical budesonide/mometasone nasal spray once/twice a day for 2 weeks (earlier symptomatic relief); Normal saline nasal washes; Topical/oral decongestant (Oxymetazline/pseudoephedrine) for 3-5 days; Adequate hydration and steam inhalation; Antihistaminics (patients with co-existing allergy).
management: PHC/PRIMARY: oral antibiotics + nasal spray + saline washes + decongestant + hydration/steam + antihistamines as above. DISTRICT HOSPITAL: Surgical interventions for underlying anatomical conditions causing recurrent acute sinusitis (DNS/adenoid hypertrophy/anatomical variations on CT); Ophthalmology referral for suspected intraorbital complications; Dental deferral for suspected dental origin infection; Invasive fungal sinusitis - start antifungal medications, control underlying immunocompromising co-morbidity and consider debridement. TERTIARY: Acute invasive fungal sinusitis/complicated acute bacterial sinusitis and immunocompromised patients may be referred.
referralRedFlags: RED FLAGS FOR REFERRAL TO DISTRICT HOSPITAL: Known diabetic/immunocompromised; Suspicion of complications - (A) Orbital involvement (periorbital edema/erythema, displaced globe, ophthalmoplegia, visual disturbance), (B) Meningitis/altered sensorium, (C) Frontal fullness; Non-resolution with oral antibiotics for ten days; Pointers of invasive fungal sinusitis (facial hypoesthesia, facial skin/palatal/turbinate discoloration).
algorithmNotes: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. Abbrev: CT=Computerized Tomogram; PHC=Primary Health Center; DNS=Deviated Nasal Septum; LPR=Laryngo Pharyngeal Reflux.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: If symptoms persist for more than 12 weeks - nasal obstruction/nasal discharge and facial pain/reduced sense of smell. Examine the nose for nasal polypi. TWO PHENOTYPES: Chronic sinusitis with nasal polypi (CRSwNP); Chronic sinusitis without nasal polypi (CRSsNP).
risk: RULE OUT PRECIPITATING/EXACERBATING CONDITIONS: Occupational exposure to irritants/pollutants; Allergic rhinitis, aspirin sensitivity, asthma, laryngopharyngeal reflux, smoking; Adenoid hypertrophy, bottle feeding, passive smoking in children; Medications and hormones associated with nasal congestion (NSAIDS, antihypertensive, psychotropic drugs, prolonged use of topical nasal decongestants). In all patients especially with nasal polypi, RULE OUT allergy/allergic rhinitis: consider allergen avoidance; skin prick test; co-existing bronchial asthma needs treatment; consider AIT (Allergen Immuno Therapy) if indicated.
scores: Treatment depends on severity: Mild/moderate (no significant congestion/discharge/polypi/complications) vs presence of nasal purulent discharge vs presence of nasal polypi (each has own pathway, see Drugs/Management).
investigations: If no symptomatic relief to medical treatment, perform nasal endoscopy and consider NCCT of paranasal sinuses. In nasal purulent discharge: culture (then culture-directed antibiotics).
drugs: MILD/MODERATE: 1. Address etiology & exacerbating factors; 2. For allergic rhinitis - antihistamines and nasal steroid spray; 3. Saline nasal wash; 4. Steam inhalation; 5. Stretching exercises and yoga for nasal congestion; 6. Topical (oxymetazoline/xylometazoline) and oral decongestants - careful selection, short course (cardiovascular risks & rebound); 7. Intra nasal steroid sprays for 6-8 weeks (Fluticasone proprionate/Fluticasone furoate/Mometasone). NASAL PURULENT DISCHARGE: 1. Culture directed antibiotics; 2. If culture negative, empirical antibiotics (Amoxycillin/Co-amoxyclav/Fluoroquinolone/Roxithromycin) for at least 2 weeks; 3. Upper dental (particularly 1st molar) infection causing maxillary sinusitis - treat with metronidazole. NASAL POLYPI: initial nasal steroid spray then endoscopic surgery; Short course oral steroid (Prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg for 5-10 days) for temporary relief in extensive polypi; steroid therapy is NOT a replacement for surgery.
management: Stepwise medical therapy first (mild/moderate); if no relief -> nasal endoscopy + NCCT PNS. Surgical (endoscopic) for nasal polypi or where DNS/nasal polypi need surgery. Key points: 1. Identification of precipitating/exacerbating factors is key to successful outcome; 2. Always rule out DNS/nasal polypi in CRS as surgery may be necessary for complete resolution; 3. Ensure adherence to nasal saline washes/physical activity/medications; 4. Educate patients on correct technique of using steroid nasal sprays and nasal irrigation; 5. Prolonged use of topical nasal decongestant beyond 5-7 days may cause rebound congestion and rhinitis medicamentosa (strongly discouraged).
referralRedFlags: RED FLAG: Frontal swelling; Orbital symptoms (periorbital edema/erythema, displaced globe, double or reduced vision [loss of green-red color differentiation may be first sign], ophthalmoplegia); Signs of meningitis/intracranial complications (fever with headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, altered sensorium, vomiting); Known diabetic/AIDS/immunosuppressive medications (suspect invasive fungal sinusitis).
algorithmNotes: Abbrev: CT=Computerized Tomogram; AIT=Allergen Immuno Therapy; DNS=Deviated Nasal Septum. CRSwNP=with nasal polypi; CRSsNP=without nasal polypi.

whenToSuspect: CLINICAL SCENARIOS: Acute bleeding from the nose after physical trauma/barotrauma/nose picking/physical exertion; Acute bleeding from the nose in hypertensive/hematological disorders; Acute bleeding from the nose without any obvious cause.
risk: SYSTEMIC ASSESSMENT: Screen for coagulation disorders/anticoagulant medications/hematological malignancies. POINTS TO PONDER: Epistaxis in children is almost always anterior and from Little's area (mucosal drying by dry air); in adults often related to hypertension and arises posteriorly from posterior end of inferior turbinate; initial non-invasive methods may suffice in a large majority.
scores: Severity assessed by source of bleeding (scanty/moderate) on anterior rhinoscopy/endoscopy.
investigations: ESSENTIAL CLINICAL EXAMINATION: Local examination by anterior rhinoscopy/endoscopy to look for source (Little's area bleeder/clot/congestion, sharp septal spur, congested nasal mucosa as in URTI); General physical examination (Cardiovascular/Lower Respiratory/Neurological). INVESTIGATIONS Essential: 1. Hemoglobin level; 2. Coagulation profile; 3. Complete blood count. Desirable: CT scan with contrast in cases with no obvious cause/suspected benign or malignant lesion.
drugs: STEP-WISE MANAGEMENT: 1. Ensure patent airway/avoid aspiration by head down/lateral positioning; 2. Restore hemodynamic stability by IV fluid replacement/transfusion; 3. Control bleeding - bidigital compression of nose for 10 minutes in Trotter's position (cotton pledgets soaked in 4% xylocaine with adrenaline may be used); Short term tab labetalol for uncontrolled hypertension; Chemical/electrocauterization of bleeder in Little's area; 4. Tamponade by anterior nasal packing/epistaxis balloon; 5. Posterior nasal packing if not controlled; 6. Antibiotic prophylaxis and hospitalization after nasal packing; 7. H2 blockers/PPI in case of blood aspiration to avoid gastritis; 8. Persisting bleeding despite packing -> consider arterial ligation (sphenopalatine/anterior ethmoidal artery); 9. Selective embolization as alternative to surgery; 10. Address identified etiology. FOLLOW UP: continued nasal lubrication for 2 weeks with liquid paraffin; repeat anterior rhinoscopy/endoscopy; oral hematinics if needed.
management: Initial non-invasive (compression/cautery/packing) -> if persistent, arterial ligation or embolization -> tertiary level for recurrent profuse bleeding (JNA, aneurysmal bleeding by DSA). Quality assessment: recurrence of episodes; improvement in hemoglobin level over time.
referralRedFlags: RED FLAG SIGNS: Features suggestive of neoplasia (unilateral bleeding, nasal obstruction, visual/orbital symptoms, obvious mass lesion); Persistent bleeding despite nasal packing (altered blood counts/coagulation profile); Recurrent profuse bleeding - consider JNA in teenage boys, aneurysmal bleeding (especially following trauma) to be ruled out by DSA, to be managed by appropriate treatment at tertiary level.
algorithmNotes: Trotter's position = head down/lateral. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. Abbrev: JNA=Juvenile Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma; DSA=Digital Subtraction Angiography; CT=Computerized Tomograms; URTI=Upper Respiratory Tract Infection.

condition: Hearing Impairment in Pediatric Age Group (0-12 years)
whenToSuspect: Disabling hearing impairment (31 or more dB HL in better ear) may affect language development and learning outcomes and hence needs urgent intervention. WHEN TO SUSPECT IN CHILDREN: 1. Parental concern about delayed speech, language, developmental delay; 2. Family history of Hearing Loss (HL); 3. Exposure to ototoxic drugs/hyperbilirubinemia requiring exchange transfusion/Neonatal ICU stay >3 days; 4. In-utero infections (CMV/rubella/syphilis/herpes/toxoplasmosis); 5. Syndromes (NF) or neurodegenerative disorders (Hunter syndrome, FA) associated with HL; 6. Post-natal infection known to cause HL (Meningitis); 7. Head Trauma; 8. Recurrent/persistent (>=3 months) middle ear disease; 9. Chemo/Radiotherapy to head and neck.
risk: COMMON CAUSES OF HL: 1. Impacted wax; 2. Middle ear fluid associated with adenoid hypertrophy/cold climate; 3. Tympanic membrane perforation; 4. Sensorineural Hearing loss (SNHL) due to various causes. UNIVERSAL HEARING SCREENING for congenital deafness: community based hearing screening (co-ordinated with immunization schedule, by primary health care workers, using calibrated noisemakers/toys); all children who fail preliminary screen to undergo detailed evaluation at health care facility.
scores: Categorize as Conductive HL (CHL) vs Sensorineural HL (SNHL) - guides management.
investigations: EVALUATION (Essential): 1. Clinical examination to look for ear canal deformities, tympanic membrane and middle ear status by otoscopy/otoendoscopy; 2. Age appropriate audiological/behavioral observation tests in a soundproof room by audiologist/ENT specialist; 3. Tympanic membrane mobility test/tympanometry. DH level: Audiometric evaluation by Audiologist/Otolaryngologist.
drugs: CONDUCTIVE HL: Wax removal under direct vision by ENT specialist relieves hearing impairment; Appropriate surgery for tympanic membrane perforation; Middle ear fluid (OME) may be associated with adenotonsillar disease which needs treatment - initially medical treatment and surgery for OME persisting >3 months/earlier in presence of speech and language delay; for non-surgical candidates/delayed surgical management, amplification by hearing aid in bilateral CHL. SNHL: Appropriate amplification, preferential seating in classroom; periodic evaluation for hearing aid users for mould fitting and amplification settings; Screening for developmental delay by pediatrician/psychologist.
management: PHC LEVEL: Suspect HL; initial evaluation; referral if initial evaluation suggestive; follow up of rehabilitated/treated; prevention. DH LEVEL: 1. Audiometric evaluation; 2. Hearing aid dispensing (mould fitting & HA programming); 3. Rehabilitation by speech therapist; 4. Appropriate surgery for CHL; 5. Training programme for parents. TERTIARY: Surgical intervention options - Cochlear implant/BAHA (as per ADIP guidelines); interdisciplinary team based interventions in children with multiple disabilities. FOLLOW UP: home visits by Health Worker/ASHA; school visits to educate teachers; home/school visit by social worker.
referralRedFlags: RED FLAGS POINTING FOR URGENT HEARING EVALUATION: 6 months - no head turning to side of calling; 1yr - no babbling/speech like sound production; 1.5yrs - not saying mama/papa/dada or other names; 2yrs - not pointing to pictures/body parts when named or speaking less than 10 words; 3yrs - does not understand action words or not asking for things by names or not speaking small sentences; at any age - has regressed or lost previously acquired speech/language milestones.
algorithmNotes: Abbrev: ADIP=Assistance to disabled persons for purchase/fitting of aids and appliances; BAHA=Bone Anchored Hearing Aid; CBR=Community Based Rehabilitation; CMV=Cyto Megalo Virus; FA=Friedreich Ataxia; NF=NeuroFibromatosis; OME=Otitis Media with Effusion.

whenToSuspect: Rapidly progressive bacterial infections which spread along facial planes and spaces of head and neck region. May be fatal unless emergently treated. Most secondary to dental infection. Typical presentation: acute onset of pain and swelling in the neck with fever, malaise, trismus and dysphagia. They usually present as: Ludwig's angina (submandibular space infection); Peritonsillar abscess; Parapharyngeal abscess; Retropharyngeal abscess. Extension along spaces to multiple deep neck spaces is frequent (parotid, masticator, temporal, visceral vascular/carotid space). Cervical space infection may spread inferiorly to anterior mediastinum and posteriorly along prevertebral space. Tubercular cold abscess involves same spaces but no inflammatory signs or rapid progression.
risk: SYSTEMIC ASSESSMENT: Screen for diabetes mellitus, HIV infection, agranulocytosis and immunosuppressive therapy or chemotherapy. Signs of inflammation may be less marked and disease course more rapidly progressive in immunocompromised patients.
scores: INDICATIONS FOR I&D (Incision and Drainage): Necrotizing fasciitis; Abscess formation; No response to antibiotics over 48-72 hours; Deterioration despite antibiotics over 24 hours; Airway compromise or impending airway compromise; Mediastinal spread; Vascular complication like venous thrombosis.
investigations: CLINICAL EXAMINATION: Airway assessment to rule out stridor/respiratory compromise; signs of dehydration; monitor temperature, heart rate, respiratory rate, BP, signs of sepsis/septic shock; oral cavity exam (jaw opening, teeth, floor of mouth); oropharyngeal exam (inflamed medially displaced tonsil & uvula, bulge in lateral pharyngeal wall); palpation of neck (lymph nodes, cellulitis, abscess, subcutaneous crepitus); cranial nerve examination. ESSENTIAL INVESTIGATIONS: 1. Contrast enhanced CT scan of head and neck (standard). If CT not available: a. Lateral x-ray neck (prevertebral soft tissue thickening >7 mm at C2 or >2/3rd width of vertebral body at C6 suggestive of retropharyngeal abscess); b. Ultrasound neck (suggest abscess and guide aspiration). 2. Blood: Total and differential leukocyte count, blood sugar, urea. 3. Abscess Cultures with Gram stain (and anaerobic culture when available) to direct antimicrobial therapy.
drugs: DISTRICT HOSPITAL Antibiotics: Early and aggressive IV antibiotic therapy with a combination of Crystalline Penicillin, Aminoglycoside and Metronidazole OR Clindamycin. PHC: broad spectrum antibiotics and pain killers + IV hydration.
management: PHC/PRIMARY: 1. Cautiously assess airway - if compromised, do endotracheal intubation/consider tracheotomy; 2. Immediately gain IV access for hydration, broad spectrum antibiotics and pain killers; 3. Transfer to hospital with facility for surgical drainage. DISTRICT HOSPITAL: 1. Hospitalization (emergency, close watch & intensive management); 2. Airway management (secure airway early in impending compromise; tracheostomy if intubation difficult); 3. Correction of fluid and electrolyte imbalance; 4. Antibiotics (Crystalline Penicillin + Aminoglycoside + Metronidazole or Clindamycin); 5. Incision and drainage (Peritonsillar abscess drained intraorally; all other abscesses via external approach). FOLLOW UP: consider cold tonsillectomy for patients with history of multiple episodes of tonsillar abscess; treat initial cause (tooth or tonsil).
referralRedFlags: RED FLAGS FOR REFERRAL TO DISTRICT HOSPITAL: Breathing difficulty; Trismus; Torticollis/neck stiffness; Subcutaneous crepitus and skin discolouration or blisters (suggest necrotizing fasciitis); Toxaemia; Lower cranial nerve palsy; Facial puffiness suggestive of venous thrombosis; Mediastinal extension.
algorithmNotes: Abbrev: CT=Computerized Tomography; MRI=Magnetic Resonance Imaging.

whenToSuspect: DISEASES OF EXTERNAL EAR: Serous/purulent discharge with significant tenderness of external ear amidst edema (localized pus: furunculosis; generalized: Acute otitis externa - Staph/Pseudomonas); Thick discharge with itching in hot/humid climate: Otomycosis (Candida-white spores; Aspergillus-black spores); Scanty serous discharge & itching with desquamated debris in ear canal: Eczematous otitis externa (EAC). DISEASES OF MIDDLE EAR: URI with severe ear pain (children: inconsolable crying & ear tugging) relieved with mucopurulent blood stained otorrhoea: Resolving AOM; Mucopurulent discharge >12 weeks: CSOM (Active: otorrhoea in last 12 weeks; Inactive: no otorrhoea in last 12 weeks; Safe type: central/total perforation; Unsafe type: cholesteatoma/granulation); Recurrent painless profuse mucopurulent discharge with pale granulations/multiple perforations unresponsive to antibiotics: Tubercular otitis media (needs biopsy confirmation); Bloody otorrhoea following trauma: Traumatic perforation; Acute onset bloody discharge with neural deficits/neck nodes: Neoplasia; Watery otorrhoea (may be associated with trauma): CSF Otorrhoea.
risk: PATIENT EDUCATION: proper technique of ear mopping, contralateral lie (10 min) following instillation of drops & avoiding water entry (ear-plugs during bathing); adequate immunization (measles/H.Influenza/Pneumococcus) in recurrent AOM and correct posture during breastfeeding while avoiding bottle feeding; refrain from indigenous (oil/hot water/acid etc) ear treatments.
scores: CSOM types: Safe (central perforation Fig4A, total perforation Fig4B) vs Unsafe (cholesteatoma Fig5A, granulation Fig5B) - Unsafe needs surgical referral.
investigations: CLINICAL EXAMINATIONS: Otoscopy as part of Complete ENT examination by primary physician (Tele-otoscopy interpreted by physician); Hearing evaluation by conversation/whisper/Tuning forks tests; General and systemic clinical examination. INVESTIGATIONS: Pure tone audiometry; Routine hemogram including blood sugar (fasting and postprandial); CT/MRI in suspected complications; Soft tissue x-ray nasopharynx (examine adenoid enlargement in children); Culture & sensitivity of aural secretions.
drugs: Acute otitis externa: Oral Ciprofloxacin/Amoxicillin clavulanic acid combination for 7-10 days (2 weeks maximum) and analgesics; Ichthammol gycerine (1:9) packing of EAC in moderate to severe edema. Otomycosis: Cleaning and Clotrimazole ear drops. Eczematous otitis externa: Ciprofloxacin ear drops with steroid combination. AOM/Resolving AOM: Oral amoxicillin/Erythromycin/Clarithromycin for 10 days; with no response in 3 days start Amoxycillin clavulanic acid combination for 10 days. Active CSOM: Ciprofloxacin ear drops with dry mopping; a course of oral antibiotics may be prescribed in persistant otorrhoea after topical antibiotics. Traumatic perforation: Topical antibiotics for otorrhoea if any, maintain ear dry till healing. Suspicion of complications: start IV Amoxicillin clavulanic acid combination and refer to DH.
management: PHC/PRIMARY: treat external/middle ear conditions as above; refer pus pointing furuncle, inactive CSOM, active CSOM (for surgery) to DH. DISTRICT HOSPITAL: Surgical interventions except neurosurgical (eg I&D, tympanoplasty, mastoidectomy); Biopsy in suspected neoplasm; Medical management of co-morbidities (diabetes, tuberculosis, meningismus/meningitis). TERTIARY: Surgical management particularly of intracranial complications including neurosurgical interventions.
referralRedFlags: RED FLAGS FOR REFERRAL TO DISTRICT LEVEL: Periaural abscess or cellulitis; High grade fever, dizziness and toxic appearance; Severe headache with neck stiffness/vomiting/altered sensorium; Facial palsy/Neurological defects; Diabetic with severe deep seated ear pain/neural defects (Skull base osteomyelitis); Physical trauma with bloody/watery discharge (suspected CSF leak); Suspected tuberculosis/neoplasm.
algorithmNotes: Abbrev: AOM=Acute Otitis Media; CSOM=Chronic Suppurative Otitis Media; EAC=External Auditory Canal; URI=Upper Respiratory Infection; CT=Computerized Tomogram; MRI=Magnetic Resonance Imaging.

whenToSuspect: Acute pharyngitis may include a spectrum varying from simple viral pharyngitis to dreaded neck space infection. Acute onset of throat pain with or without fever, tonsillo-pharyngeal exudates, odynophagia and tender cervical lymphadenopathy is typically seen in BACTERIAL pharyngitis; Streptococcal (GABHS) pharyngitis is the commonest cause of bacterial pharyngitis. Rhinitis, conjunctivitis, skin or mucosal rashes point towards VIRAL etiology (initial symptoms in epidemics of flu). Sore throat followed by high grade fever, cough, drooling, lymphadenopathy, breathing difficulty suggest Epiglottitis or Laryngotracheobronchitis (especially pediatric). Adherent membrane extending beyond tonsillar confines with toxaemia suggests Diphtheria. Pharyngitis with generalized lymphadenopathy is consistent with Infectious Mononucleosis (IM). Smoking and GERD are common causes of non-infective pharyngitis.
risk: Rheumatic fever and acute glomerulonephritis are potential systemic complications of streptococcal pharyngitis. Hepatosplenomegaly can be found in IM. A sandpapery scarlatiniform rash may be seen in GABHS infection whereas maculopapular rashes with various viral infections and IM.
scores: CENTOR SCORE (clinical features, 1 point each): Fever=1; Anterior cervical lymphadenopathy=1; Tonsillar exudate=1; Absence of cough=1. Score 0-1: UNLIKELY to have GABHS; Score 4: LIKELY to have GABHS; Score 2-3: REQUIRE lab tests to confirm GABHS infection.
investigations: CLINICAL EXAMINATION (Preliminary): Temperature chart (fever usually absent/low-grade in viral); check vitals/dehydration; complete oral and oropharyngeal examination with tongue depressor; palpate for cervical and generalized lymphadenopathy. Desirable: Assess Centor criteria and ascertain its score. INVESTIGATIONS Essential: Throat swab for culture, routine hemogram including total and differential leukocyte counts and peripheral smear for atypical lymphocytes (seen in IM). Optional: GABHS rapid antigen detection test (RADT). Desirable: Lab tests to rule out EB Virus, Coxsackie virus, Herpes virus, fungal or Gonococcal pharyngitis.
drugs: ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY: a. Patients positive for all 4 Centor criteria treated with antibiotics without waiting for antigen testing or cultures; b. Centor score 2&3 treated with antibiotics only if antigen testing or throat swab culture positive; c. Centor score of only 1 NOT to be treated with antibiotics; d. Amoxicillin (50 mg/kg/d in 2-3 doses orally) for 10 days is first choice for GABHS infection - for penicillin-sensitive patients, Erythromycin or Azithromycin is antibiotic of choice. SYMPTOMATIC: Saltwater gargle, warm liquids, rest; Ibuprofen or Paracetamol for analgesia. Parenteral antibiotics (Ceftriaxone/cefotaxime) and steroids when airway compromised due to suspected epiglottis/Croup.
management: PHC/PRIMARY: 1. Assess for signs of toxicity, epiglottitis or oropharyngeal abscess; 2. Ensure vitals/hydration; 3. Saltwater gargle, warm liquids, rest; 4. Ibuprofen/Paracetamol; 5. Antibiotic therapy per Centor score (see Drugs); 6. Parenteral antibiotics + steroids if airway compromised. DISTRICT HOSPITAL: Management of complications e.g. Deep neck space infection, Diphtheria, Epiglottitis, Croup. FOLLOW UP: Recurrent (>7 episodes in previous year, or 5/year in last two years, or 3/year in last 3 years) tonsillitis episodes need to be evaluated for tonsillectomy.
referralRedFlags: RED FLAGS: Generalized lymphadenopathy; Cardiac murmurs; Purulent productive cough with tachypnea suggestive of LRTI; Hot potato voice; Unilateral tonsillar enlargement; Tonsillar membrane going beyond its confines; Agranulocytosis; Epidemic of flu.
algorithmNotes: Abbrev: GABHS=Group A Beta Hemolyticus Streptococcus; GERD=Gastro Esophageal Reflux Disease; LRTI=Lower Respiratory Tract Infection; EB=Epstein Barr; RADT=Rapid Antigen Detection Test.

whenToSuspect: WHAT IS AKI: Increase in Serum creatinine by >0.3 mg/dl in 48 hours AND/OR Urine output <0.5 ml/kg/h for 6-12 hours. SYMPTOMS: Reduced urine output; Dark, concentrated urine; Swelling over feet/face; Breathlessness. LOOK OUT FOR AKI IN PRESENCE OF: Hypotension; Volume loss (vomiting, diarrhea, bleeding); heat exposure or heat stroke; Pregnancy-related complications; Multiple organ failure; Nephrotoxic medication use; In neonates - oligohydramnios/birth asphyxia, respiratory distress.
risk: PRINCIPLES OF ASSESSMENT: Determine whether pre-renal, renal or post-renal; Identify and correct reversible factors; Look out for occult causes (envenomations, poisoning); Determine severity of AKI; Identify complications; Decide need for dialysis. AKI IN SPECIFIC SETTINGS: Neonatal - refer to paediatrician; Pregnancy - manage complications; Envenomations - antivenin, hemodynamic correction; Poisoning - specific antidote when available; Systemic disease - refer for investigations; Kidney transplant recipient - refer to nephrologist.
scores: Stage AKI using KDIGO criteria. INDICATIONS FOR DIALYSIS: Fluid overload; Pericarditis; Hyperkalemia; Severe metabolic acidosis; Encephalopathy; Severe uraemia; To create space for fluids or blood products.
investigations: PRELIMINARY ACTIONS: Monitor urine volume & body weight; Identify and treat life-threatening complications; Correct hydration status; Stop nephrotoxic drugs; Exclude urinary outlet obstruction; Stabilize blood pressure; Treat infection; Assess for dialysis need. DESIRABLE INVESTIGATIONS: Stage AKI (KDIGO criteria); Check electrolytes, acid-base status; Urinalysis; Rule out pre-existing kidney disease; Ultrasound of KUB region; Assessment for infection; Laboratory investigation for specific cause. FOLLOW-UP: Not resolving for >2 weeks: CECT to exclude cortical necrosis, kidney biopsy as indicated; look for systemic diseases (vasculitis, myeloma, TMA); Serum creatinine and urine protein q 6-12 months for life.
drugs: TREATMENT OF HYPERKALEMIA: Calcium gluconate 1000 mg slow IV under ECG monitoring (can be repeated upto 3 times); Salbutamol 10 to 20 mg in 4 mL of saline by nebulization; Insulin-dextrose: 10 to 20 units of regular insulin in 100 ml 25% or 50% dextrose. Pulmonary edema: IV furosemide and oxygen. Anti-snake venom if indicated.
management: PRIMARY CARE: Detailed history & physical exam; Identify and correct volume deficit; Stop nephrotoxic agents; Identify and correct bladder outlet obstruction; Give anti-snake venom if indicated; Identify hyperkalemia and start treatment; Identify pulmonary edema (start IV furosemide & oxygen); PD if indicated; Timely referral after stabilisation. SECONDARY CARE: + Identify and treat hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, pulmonary edema; Identify and correct urinary tract obstruction (USG, CT); Detailed investigation for infections; Manage pregnancy complications (deliver if indicated); Look for underlying CKD; Dialysis (PD or HD). TERTIARY CARE: + Investigations for specific cause (imaging, genetic tests); Kidney biopsy; Dialysis (PD or HD). FOLLOW-UP: UO >1L, stable/falling creatinine, no symptoms: stop dialysis.
referralRedFlags: RED FLAGS FOR URGENT REFERRAL: Indications for dialysis; Unexplained AKI; Involvement of other organs; Sepsis; Systemic disease; Complicated pregnancy.
algorithmNotes: Abbrev: AKI=Acute Kidney Injury; CECT=Contrast-enhanced CT scan; PD=Peritoneal dialysis; TMA=Thrombotic microangiopathy; CKD=Chronic Kidney Disease; HD=Hemodialysis; UO=Urine output; USG=Ultrasonography. Stage by KDIGO 2012 criteria.

whenToSuspect: WHAT IS CKD: Abnormalities of kidney structure or function, present for >3 months, with implications for health. WHEN TO LOOK FOR CKD: History of long-standing nocturia or constitutional symptoms; Edema, hematuria, proteinuria or renal stones; Long-term intake of painkillers or herbal medicines; Family history of kidney disease; Growth retardation, rickets, or proximal myopathy; Unexplained hypertension or anemia; Longstanding diabetes, hypertension, CVD, stroke, PVD; Systemic diseases (connective tissue disease).
risk: INITIAL ASSESSMENT: Confirmation of CKD diagnosis (repeat tests after 3 months); Staging and progression rate; Establishing cause of kidney disease; Identify and treat reversible factors (hypertension, volume loss, obstruction, infection); Look for complications (anemia, bone disease, dyselectrolytemias, CVD). LIFESTYLE MEASURES (all CKD): Weight control/weight gain monitoring in children; Regular physical activity; Reduce dietary salt <5 g/day; Stop tobacco; Stop/moderate alcohol; Stop unproven health supplements; Do not use NSAIDS; Avoid untested indigenous medicines.
scores: Estimate glomerular filtration rate using CKD-EPI equation; stage CKD by eGFR. PREPARATION FOR RRT: eGFR <30 - preserve veins in non-dominant arm for AV fistula, discuss RRT options; eGFR <15 - may need dialysis soon, counsel for AV fistula, list for transplant; Dialysis start depends on symptoms or eGFR <5 ml/min.
investigations: EVALUATION OF NEWLY DIAGNOSED: Serum creatinine, electrolytes, bicarbonate; Estimate GFR using CKD-EPI equation; Urinalysis (sediment, proteinuria quantitation); Ultrasound of kidneys and urinary tract; Calcium, phosphate, alkaline phosphatase, albumin; CBC including peripheral blood film; Iron profile - Serum iron, TIBC, TSAT; HBsAg, anti-HCV.
drugs: BP CONTROL (target <130/80, 120/80 if proteinuria): Restrict dietary salt <5 g/day; any anti-HT available; Diuretics - eGFR >45: thiazide, <45: furosemide, <30: do not use potassium sparing agents; ACEI/ARB preferred for proteinuric patients (>1 g/d) [caution/do not use if eGFR <30 or Potassium >5.5 mEq/L]. ANEMIA: establish iron replete state; if not, oral iron; consider IV iron for dialysis patients/those not tolerating orally; if Hb still <8 g/dl start erythropoietin, titrate to Hb 10-11 g/dl. HYPERPHOSPHATEMIA (PO4>5.5): start Ca-containing binders; non-Ca binders if serum Ca >9 mg/dl, vascular calcification or low iPTH. DIABETES (target HbA1c <7%): do not use metformin if eGFR <30. VITAMIN D: Supplement 60,000 units cholecalciferol q2W; correct acidosis with oral sodium bicarbonate; activated vitamin D if hyperparathyroidism. VACCINATION: HBV -ve: 20 ug IM in each deltoid at 0,1,2,6 months; children - complete primary schedule. NUTRITION: Salt <5g/d; Protein 0.6-0.8 g/kg/day (do not restrict unless high protein user); restrict green leafy vegetables if eGFR <30; avoid fruit juices, coconut water, carbonated beverages. Low potassium fruits/veg: apple, pineapple, papaya, pear, tangerine, watermelon, grape, plum, cabbage, carrot, cauliflower, onion, radish, peppers, chillies, brinjal, cucumber, green beans, peas, rice, bread.
management: PRIMARY CARE: Detailed history & exam; Identify and correct reversible factors; Stop nephrotoxic agents; Referral after stabilization. ADMISSION CRITERIA: acute worsening/new complication; creation of vascular access; PD catheter placement/initiation; HD initiation and kidney transplant. DISTRICT HOSPITAL: ascertain cause of CKD; tailor treatment; identify & manage complications; vaccination; correct acute factors; counseling (nutrition, lifestyle, pregnancy in child-bearing age); discuss RRT; vascular access creation/PD catheter insertion; send back to community with plan. TERTIARY CARE: + ascertain cause (imaging/biopsy/genetic); work-up for transplantation. CONSERVATIVE CARE: if life expectancy limited, multiple comorbidities/personal preference; shared decision-making with patient/family.
referralRedFlags: INDICATIONS FOR REFERRAL: Initial evaluation of all newly diagnosed cases; Rapid disease progression; New complication; Discussion for Renal Replacement Therapy (RRT).
algorithmNotes: Targets: BP <130/80 (120/80 if proteinuria); HbA1c <7%; Hb 10-11 g/dl. RRT=Renal Replacement Therapy; eGFR by CKD-EPI.

whenToSuspect: WHAT IS GN: Inflammation of the glomerulus; presentation depends on specific disease entity. May present as symptomatic urinary abnormalities/nephrotic syndrome/nephritic syndrome/AKI/CKD. IDENTIFY PRESENTING CLINICAL SYNDROME: Acute nephritic syndrome (sudden onset, hematuria, oliguria, edema, hypertension, reduced eGFR); Nephrotic syndrome (proteinuria >3.5 g/1.73m2/d; 50 mg/kg/d in children, edema, hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia); Asymptomatic urinary abnormalities (proteinuria, hematuria); RPGN (doubling of serum creatinine over days to weeks).
risk: CAUTION: Non-nephrotic proteinuria - rule out orthostatic cases; Isolated hematuria - rule out urological causes. LOOK FOR COMPLICATIONS: Malnutrition; Hypovolemia; AKI; Thromboembolism; Infections. IN CHILDREN <12y WITH NEPHROTIC SYNDROME DO NOT: give any vaccine while on steroids or within 3 months of stopping; prescribe bed rest unless indicated; restrict salt; restrict fluids; use ACE inhibition in renal dysfunction or steroid sensitive nephrotic syndrome.
scores: KIDNEY BIOPSY indicated if: Age <1y or >12y; Steroid resistant state; Family history of kidney disease; Features suggestive of systemic disease; Diagnosis unclear/therapeutic uncertainty; Doubling of serum creatinine over days-weeks. SEROLOGY: ASO; HIV (high risk); ANA; Anti-GBM antibody; Anti-PLA2R; HBsAg, anti-HCV; C3; ANCA; SPEP (>50y).
investigations: EVALUATION: Full history and clinical examination; Urinalysis (urine routine exam, microscopy); proteinuria quantitation (24h UP; ACR/PCR for follow up); Assess eGFR using age-appropriate formula; Renal imaging; Serologic testing; Kidney biopsy. PHC level: Urine dipstick test; Serum creatinine, electrolytes. District: 24-hr urinary protein estimation; serum creatinine, electrolytes, serum albumin, lipid profile; imaging of kidneys.
drugs: GENERAL TREATMENT: Lifestyle modifications; Sodium restriction (not in children); Diuretics (loop needed, reverse edema slowly); Avoid nephrotoxic agents; BP control (<130/80 mm Hg adults, <95th centile for age in children); ACE inhibition (with exceptions). RECOMMENDED PHARMACOLOGICAL TREATMENT - CHILDREN: Prednisolone 2 mg/kg x 6w then 1.5 mg/kg A/D x 6w; in relapse: Prednisolone 2 mg/kg x 2w then 1.5 mg/kg A/D x 4w. ADULTS: depends on diagnosis (biopsy, serology); choices: Corticosteroid (Prednisolone, IV methylprednisolone), CNIs (cyclosporine/tacrolimus), Cyclophosphamide, Azathioprine, Mycophenolate mofetil, Levamisole, Rituximab. BEFORE STARTING STEROIDS IN CHILDREN: look for latent TB (Mantoux, Chest X-ray); start 6 months INH (5mg/kg/day) if asymptomatic Mantoux +ve; watch for infections (peritonitis, pneumonia, skin). THROMBOSIS PROPHYLAXIS: evaluate bleeding risk (do not use if high); S alb <2 + non-ambulatory: start aspirin, OAC if high risk.
management: PHC/CHC: Detailed history; Urine dipstick test; Serum creatinine, electrolytes; Stabilize; Start antihypertensives and diuretics if needed. DISTRICT HOSPITALS: 24-hr urinary protein estimation; serum creatinine/electrolytes/serum albumin/lipid profile; imaging of kidneys; evaluate for secondary causes; treat complications; start general treatment; can treat uncomplicated NS in 1-12y old and infrequent relapses; prepare treatment plan and refer back. TERTIARY CARE: + Kidney biopsy. ADMISSION CRITERIA: Initial evaluation, kidney biopsy, or management of complications.
referralRedFlags: INDICATIONS FOR REFERRAL: All cases >12 years old and less than 1 year old; In children - Frequent relapses (>=3 per year), Steroid dependent or resistant state; Recent rise in serum creatinine; Appearance of complications; Pregnancy; Persistent asymptomatic urinary abnormalities (>6 months). RED FLAG SIGNS: Cold Peripheries; Accelerated hypertension; Increased capillary filling time; Seizures; Altered Sensorium (DO NOT USE DIURETICS - ALBUMIN can be given in severe Hypoalbuminemia).
algorithmNotes: GN presentations: nephritic, nephrotic, asymptomatic urinary abnormalities, RPGN, AKI, CKD. RPGN=Rapidly Progressive GN; NS=Nephrotic Syndrome.

whenToSuspect: WHAT IS UTI: At least 3 Symptoms (dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain) OR Dipstick +ve for leucocyte esterase and nitrite if <3 symptoms OR CUE >10 WBC/HPF in uncentrifuged urine + Urine culture >10^5 CFU of single species/ml in midstream urine (multiple species indicate contamination). DETERMINE UTI TYPE: Simple cystitis/lower UTI (dysuria, urgency, frequency); Pyelonephritis/upper UTI (fever, chills and rigors, loin pain, pelvic pain, renal angle tenderness, toxic/sick appearance); Complicated UTI (history of stones, congenital anomalies, obstruction, diabetes, immunosuppression).
risk: WHEN TO DO URINE CULTURE: Complicated UTI; Pyelonephritis; Special situations (all males except simple cystitis, children, pregnancy, recurrent UTI >2 episodes/6 months, catheter associated UTI). ASYMPTOMATIC BACTERIURIA: bacteria in urine culture >10^5 CFU/ml with no symptoms - no treatment required (EXCEPT pregnancy, before any urological intervention). LONG TERM CONSEQUENCES: renal scars, hypertension, CKD, poor quality of life.
scores: CHILDREN first UTI imaging: Age <1yr (Ultrasound, MCU, DMSA renal scan); Age 1-5yr (Ultrasound, DMSA scan, MCU if ultrasound/DMSA abnormal); Age >5yr (Ultrasound, if abnormal MCU and DMSA scan). All recurrent UTI need detailed evaluation with ultrasonography, DMSA scan and MCU.
investigations: Urinalysis/dipstick (leucocyte esterase, nitrite); CUE (WBC/HPF); Urine culture (>10^5 CFU single species). Tertiary: send for culture; imaging if no response to antibiotics in 48 hrs; urology services if obstruction.
drugs: FEMALE CYSTITIS/UTI (empirical, no tests): Nitrofurantoin 100 mg PO BD x 5d; TMP/SMX 1 DS tab PO BD x 3d (avoid nitrofurantoin if GFR <45). MALE UTI (acute cystitis/simple, Rx 7 days): Nitrofurantoin 100 mg PO BD x 7d; TMP/SMX 1 DS tab PO BD x 7d; Ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD x 7d; Levofloxacin 750 mg OD x 5d (all other male UTI - refer). PYELONEPHRITIS Empiric Outpatient: parenteral initial dose (Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV/IM x1, Gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV/IM x1) then Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BD x 7d / Levofloxacin 750 mg PO OD x 5d / Cefuroxime 500 mg PO BD x10-14d / Amoxy clav x10-14d / TMP-SMX 1 DS BD x7-10d. Inpatient: Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV OD +/-AMP; Gentamicin +/-AMP; others per c/s (Carbapenem, Piperacillin Tazo); IV until afebrile x48 hrs then switch to PO. PREGNANCY UTI: Nitrofurantoin 100 mg PO BD x5-7d (avoid near-term); Cephalexin 500 mg PO QID x5-7d; TMP/SMX 1 DS PO BD x5-7d (avoid 1st trimester & near-term, add folic acid). PROSTATITIS: Older >35 - Septran DS BD / Levofloxacin 500mg OD / ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD (avoid nitrofurantoin); Young males - Doxy 100mg bd / azithro 1 gm / oflox 300mg BD for chlamydia + Single dose Ceftriaxone 250mg IM for gonorrhoea; Rx 6 weeks. RECURRENT UTI prophylaxis: low dose nitrofurantoin 50 mg x6 months; single strength septran x6 months; or norflox 200mg/ciproflox 200mg/cephalexin 250mg; vaginal cream in post menopausal. SYMPTOMATIC: plenty of water; urine alkalinizer eg citrate (avoid if on nitrofurantoin); Phenazopyridine 200mg tid for 2 days; local Estrogen creams for recurrent UTI in post menopausal; Paracetamol for pain; cranberry can be used. CHILDREN: Upper UTI - IV gentamicin/amikacin/ceftriaxone; Lower UTI - oral cefixime/oflox/ciproflox/amoxyclav; Duration: Upper 10-14d, Lower 7-10d, Adolescents 3-5d.
management: PRIMARY CARE: History and examination; look for red flag signs and refer; refer special groups; treat acute cystitis (females, males) with Nitrofurantoin or Trimethoprim sulphamethoxazole; symptomatic relief. CATHETER UTI: Rx of asymptomatic CAUTI NOT recommended; remove catheter as soon as not required; if indwelling >2 weeks and still indicated, replace catheter; symptomatic CAUTI (fever, back pain, new onset delirium, rigors) - send culture, treat as complicated UTI; no role of routine antibiotic prophylaxis. TERTIARY: send for culture; imaging if no response in 48 hrs; urology if obstruction. CHILDREN: infants <3 months as upper UTI (PN) with IV antibiotics; refer Upper UTI(PN), infants UTI, recurrent UTI; prevention - avoid constipation, clean washrooms.
referralRedFlags: RED FLAG SIGNS - REFER: Pyelonephritis; Complicated UTI; Special situations (children, pregnancy, males except simple cystitis, catheter UTI); Non response within 3 days of AB; Recurrent UTI. All other UTI in males - refer (pyelonephritis or complicated UTI, prostatitis pelvic/perineal pain).
algorithmNotes: Abbrev: PN=Pyelonephritis; CAUTI=Catheter Associated UTI; MCU=Micturating Cystourethrogram; DMSA=renal scan; RUTI=Recurrent UTI; MSU=Mid Stream Urine.

whenToSuspect: Presentation with acute onset (within hours to days) paraplegia or quadriplegia. First question: Are there signs of Upper Motor Neuron (UMN) involvement? - Brisk reflexes; Extensor plantar; Increased tone; Sensory level; Bladder or bowel incontinence.
risk: IF UMN SIGNS (YES): Likely Acute myelopathy (tuberculosis), Trauma, Tumor, Transverse myelitis, Epidural abscess, Spinal cord stroke. IF NO UMN: ask weakness proximal or distal? PROXIMAL + reflexes absent: Likely Guillain Barre Syndrome (GBS) or Hypokalemic periodic paralysis. PROXIMAL + reflexes present + fatigable/extra-ocular movements affected: Likely Myasthenia gravis or Snake bite; if no sensory impairment & normal extra-ocular movements: Likely Myopathy (eg polymyositis). DISTAL + LMN signs (wasting, absent reflexes, sensory impairment): Likely Acute onset peripheral neuropathy (toxin/drug).
scores: ADMISSION CRITERIA: Any progressive weakness; Unable to walk; Unable to swallow or choking while drinking water; Respiratory distress (unable to complete sentences, falling serial single breath count, RR >20 or SaO2 <95%); Persistent tachycardia >100; Fever; Bowel and bladder involvement. DISCHARGE CRITERIA: Cause established; Progression of weakness stopped; Able to breathe on his own; tracheostomy care taught; Ryles tube feeding taught if not swallowing; urinary catheter care & turning taught; caregiver trained for physiotherapy at home.
investigations: UMN type: MRI (refer to district hospital/medical college with MRI). GBS/Hypokalemic: serum K+ levels. Myopathy: serum electrolytes, creatine kinase, NCS & EMG. Distal neuropathy: NCS and EMG, CSF for cells and biochemistry. Myasthenia/snake bite: NCS.
drugs: GBS/Hypokalemic periodic paralysis: check serum K+; intubate if unable to complete sentences/low single breath count/poor chest expansion; refer for plasma exchange or IVIg with ventilator. Myasthenia/snake bite: intubate if needed; refer for NCS, plasma exchange or IVIg with ventilator. UMN myelopathy: catheterize if urinary retention; treatment depends on MRI. Distal neuropathy: treatment as per diagnosis.
management: All types: Check pulse, BP and respiration first. UMN: catheterize if urine retention, transfer/refer to district hospital/medical college with MRI facility, treat per MRI, follow up at CHC. GBS/Hypokalemic: K+ check, intubate if needed, refer for plasma exchange/IVIg + ventilator, follow up at CHC. Myasthenia/snake bite: refer for NCS/plasma exchange/IVIg + ventilator. Myopathy: serum electrolytes & creatine kinase, refer for NCS & EMG. Distal peripheral neuropathy: refer for NCS & EMG, CSF analysis, treat per diagnosis, follow up at CHC.
referralRedFlags: Transfer/refer urgently if respiratory compromise, progressive weakness, bulbar involvement (choking), autonomic instability. Refer to nearest district hospital/medical college with required facility (MRI / NCS-EMG / plasma exchange / IVIg / ventilator).
algorithmNotes: Decision algorithm: UMN signs? Yes=myelopathy (MRI). No -> proximal vs distal -> reflexes present/absent -> GBS / hypokalemic / myasthenia / snake bite / myopathy / peripheral neuropathy. UMN=Upper Motor Neuron; LMN=Lower Motor Neuron; GBS=Guillain Barre Syndrome; NCS=Nerve Conduction Study; EMG=Electromyography; IVIg=IV Immunoglobulin.

whenToSuspect: CLINICAL FEATURES: Impaired memory; Anxiety; Hallucinations; Incontinence; Apathy; Aggression; Mood fluctuations; Forgetting learned activities; Depression; Sleep disturbances; Personality change. IMPORTANT: Dementia is complex and variable; no single test definitively diagnoses; clinical features should be a change from baseline normal functioning in a middle aged to old person; History should be taken from a close caregiver staying with the patient longer than the appearance of symptoms.
risk: EVALUATION: History & examination -> Cognitive decline in more than one domain? (No=No dementia). Yes -> Delirium or Depression? (Yes=Treat). No -> Functional decline -> Dementia. Suspected dementia -> Neuropsychiatry testing. Typical clinical profile (age, progression, no focal signs)? Yes=Probable AD; CT/MRI -> VD/mixed dementia. No=Optional tests -> Other causes. Screen treatable causes: thyroid disorders, B-12 deficiency, subdural hemorrhage, depression, vascular risk factors, normal pressure hydrocephalus, hypothyroidism, chronic meningitis.
scores: REASONS FOR REFERRAL: Not responding to adequate dose and duration of prescribed medications; Presence of red flags.
investigations: Lab investigation: TSH, Vit B12, vit D, LFT/RFT/FBS; CBC, biochemistry, liver function tests, hemogram, lipid profile, TFT, VDRL, vit B12 level, vit D level. Neuroimaging CT/MRI to rule out subdural hematoma/tumors/NPH (surgically remediable causes of rapid cognitive decline). Retrovirus after counselling (whenever feasible and high index of suspicion).
drugs: MEDICATIONS FOR ALZHEIMER'S DEMENTIA - FOR COGNITION: Donepezil 5 mg once after breakfast x 1 month, then 10 mg after breakfast to continue; if side effect/not tolerating - Rivastigmine start 1.5 mg BD/1 month, then 3 mg BD x 1 month, then 4.5 mg BD x 1 month, then 6 mg twice after meals x 1 month; Memantine: in moderate to severe dementia 5 mg BD x 1 month, then 10 mg BD to continue; Galantamine 8 mg BD if not tolerating. FOR DEPRESSION: Escitalopram 10 mg. FOR AGITATION: Identification of triggers; Non pharmacological interventions.
management: PHC (Medical Officer): Diagnose after detailed history; Screen treatable causes & depression & vascular risk factors; Lab investigations (CBC, biochemistry, LFT, hemogram, lipid profile, TFT, VDRL, B12, vit D); Referrals for MRI/CT; Initiate treatment & manage co-morbidities (depression, vision, hearing deficits, gait, thyroid, arthritis); therapy for vascular risk factors; healthy lifestyle; assess for palliative care; follow up & monitor side effects/red flags. DISTRICT HOSPITAL (Physician/Geriatric Specialist/Neurologist/Psychiatrist): Careful evaluation; Screen treatable causes (NPH, B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, chronic meningitis); Neuroimaging CT/MRI; Lab investigations; Upward/downward referral linkages; Encourage patient & caregiver support program; Avoid antipsychotics until necessary; Train MOs.
referralRedFlags: RED FLAGS: Fever; Rapid progression; Seizures; Recent head injury; Alcoholism and falls.
algorithmNotes: AD=Alzheimer's Disease; VD=Vascular Dementia; NPH=Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus. Avoid antipsychotics until necessary.

whenToSuspect: CLINICAL FEATURES: Episodic, few secs-mins; Abnormal jerky movements; Loss of consciousness/awareness; Blank staring; Sudden drops/falls, brief jerks; With or without urine/stool incontinence, tongue bite, drooling; Bizzare activity out of context; Any other episode lasting for few mins (usually <5 mins). Episodes could be single with high risk of recurrence. Prolonged motor convulsion of >5 mins with loss of consciousness = STATUS EPILEPTICUS (SE) - MEDICAL EMERGENCY.
risk: Differentiate provoked seizures (fever, acute CNS insult, antibiotics, metabolic causes) from epilepsy. Initiation of treatment: treat if 2 or more episodes of unprovoked seizures; treat single seizure if risk of recurrence high (focal seizures, mentally retarded, neurological deficits, family history of seizures, abnormal EEG, neuroimaging). Advice on prevention: regular medication, sleep 7-8 hrs, avoid excess TV/mobile/photic stimulation, regular diet, avoid alcohol. Folic Acid 5 mg/day with AEDs in all women of child bearing age; polytherapy and valproate to be avoided in women with epilepsy.
scores: STATUS EPILEPTICUS stages: IMPENDING SE (5 min) -> ESTABLISHED SE (30 min) -> REFRACTORY SE (60 min, 2 IV drugs fail = Benzo + IV AED) -> Super refractory (>24hr no control).
investigations: PHC: CBC, liver function tests, routine biochemistry, hemogram, lipid profile, vit D levels, TFT (whichever feasible). District: antiepileptic drug levels, CT brain when necessary.
