SE workup: Airway, blood pressure, temperature, IV access, electrocardiography, CBC, glucose, electrolytes, AED levels, ABG, oximetry, tox screen, central line; if alcoholic - thiamine & glucose; if diabetic - GLUCOTEST/blood sugar & glucose IV.
drugs: AED BROAD SPECTRUM (generalized): Sodium Valproate (avoid in women of child bearing age unless non responsive) start 200mg TDS, maintenance 600-2400 mg/d; Lamotrigine start 25mg HS (lower with VPA), maintenance 100-300; Levetiracetam start 250mg BD, maintenance 1000-3000; Topiramate start 25mg OD, maintenance 100-400. AED (focal): Carbamazepine start 100mg BD, maintenance 400-1200; Oxcarbazepine start 150mg BD, maintenance 600-1800; Phenobarbitone (also generalized) start 30mg HS, maintenance 60-180; Phenytoin start 200mg HS, maintenance 200-400. STATUS EPILEPTICUS: Out of hospital - Buccal/Intranasal midazolam (IMDZ) 0.3-0.5 mg/kg. ER - IV Lorazepam up to 0.1 mg/kg @ 2mg/min OR IV Midazolam 0.1-0.2 mg/kg bolus or 0.05-0.5 mg/kg/hr CIV OR IV Diazepam upto 0.25-0.4 mg/kg over 2-3 min. Then Phenytoin @50 mg/min, 20 mg/kg (repeat +10 mg/kg if not stopped in 15-20 min); if uncontrolled or CI - IV Valproate 25-40 mg/kg @3-6 mg/kg/min; if CI to both - Phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV @ <5-60 mg/min (prepare to intubate/ventilate). Levetiracetam 20-30 mg/kg IV at 5 mg/kg/min (max 3g) or 1500-3000 mg via NGT; or Lacosamide 200-400 mg IV at 40-80 mg/min; or Topiramate 150-800 mg bid via NGT. ICU (refractory): Midazolam loading 0.2 mg/kg or CIV 0.05-0.5 (up to 2) mg/kg/hr; Thiopental 5-7 mg/kg IV bolus then 3-5 mg/kg/hr for 48 hours; OR Propofol loading 2-5 mg/kg, CIV 1-15 mg/kg/hr; OR Pentobarbital up to 10 mg/kg, CIV 0.5-2 mg/kg/h; OR Ketamine bolus 1.5 mg/kg, CIV 0.01-0.05 mg/kg/h (max 10mg/kg/hr) - with EEG monitoring.
management: PHC (Medical Officer): clinical diagnosis from eyewitness history; differentiate provoked vs epilepsy; lab investigations; initiate treatment (AED broad spectrum, low dose, start low go slow except SE); emergency care of SE; treatment counselling (side effects, red flags, adherence, treatment diary); prevention advice; for excessive alcohol refer to ANM/MLP for psychosocial interventions; follow up under neurologist at STC centre; manage co-morbidities; alert to abuse/neglect. DISTRICT HOSPITAL: careful evaluation, specialized management, refer back to PHC for follow up; lab investigations + AED levels + CT brain; monitor AED side effects; Clinical Psychologist counselling.
referralRedFlags: REASONS FOR REFERRAL: Redflag Signs; Progressive problems/rapid new symptoms; Recent injury; Symptoms after alcohol binge; Status epilepticus after stabilization; Non response to adequate dose & duration; Serious side effects; Neuroimaging. RED FLAG SIGNS: Fever; Headache; Vomiting; Altered Sensorium; Severe Giddiness; Loss of function of body.
algorithmNotes: AED adverse effects: Valproate (wt gain, tremor, hair loss, PCOS, thrombocytopenia); Lamotrigine/Carbamazepine (skin rash, SJS); Carbamazepine/Oxcarbazepine (hyponatremia); Phenytoin (gum hyperplasia, hirsutism, osteomalacia); Topiramate (renal stones, weight loss). AED=Anti Epileptic Drug; SE=Status Epilepticus; VPA=Valproate; CIV=Continuous IV.

whenToSuspect: Types: CONTINUOUS HEADACHES (progressive acute/subacute onset persisting >1 week; possibilities - Cerebral Venous Thrombosis, Intra Cranial Space Occupying Lesion, Meningitis, Brain abscess). CHRONIC TENSION HEADACHE (continuous bilateral headache for many years with significant insomnia and psychosomatic complaints). MIGRAINE (side alternating, associated visual aura, photo-phono phobia, nausea, relieved by sleep or analgesics). ATYPICAL MIGRAINE (hemiplegia, visual impairment, vertigo). TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALGIAS (rapid onset peri-orbital and temporal pain with autonomic features). CLUSTER HEADACHE / PAROXYSMAL HEMICRANIAS / SUNCT (short lasting unilateral neuralgiform pain with conjunctival injection and tearing).
risk: CAUSES & FOLLOW UP TREATMENT: Intra cerebral hemorrhage -> good control of blood pressure; Seizures -> antiepileptic medications; Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis -> follow up of anticoagulation; Migraine -> give prophylaxis for adequate duration of time and taper after remission.
scores: INDICATIONS FOR ADMISSION: Patient with unrelenting headache; Immunosuppressed patients with continuous headache; First ever headache with worsening intensity; Progressive headache with other systemic disease; Severe symptomatic primary headache disorders.
investigations: Continuous/catastrophic headaches: CT with CT Angiogram/MRI with MR angiogram; Lumbar puncture. Migraine/atypical: brain imaging if atypical features/symptoms; CT/MRI with MR angiogram or CT angiogram if required. Tertiary workup by presentation: Sudden severe/first-worst -> CT+CT Angiogram/MRI+MR angiogram; Progressive -> MR venogram; Fever+personality change+altered sensorium+neck stiffness -> CT then CSF study; Progressive + seizure/focal deficit -> CT/MRI with contrast.
drugs: CHRONIC TENSION HEADACHE: Amitriptyline 10mg HS + lifestyle advice. MIGRAINE: Prophylaxis if frequency >2/month for 2 months - Propranolol 40 mg OD, Flunarizine 10 mg OD. Acute - Analgesics (Paracetamol 650 mg, or Indomethacin 50 mg) + Antiemetics (Prochlorperazine 10mg); lifestyle (good sleep at least 6 hrs, food habit modification, avoid triggers). ATYPICAL MIGRAINE: Analgesics for pain, refer to higher centre with imaging; migraine prophylaxis after ruling out stroke. TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALGIAS: Indomethacin 25mg TID for 7 days, refer to tertiary. TERTIARY treatments: Subarachnoid hemorrhage (angiogram, clipping/coiling of aneurysm); Intracerebral hemorrhage (stroke unit, neurosurgery); Cerebral venous thrombosis (IV UFH/subcutaneous heparin/LMWH); Meningioencephalitis (antibiotics/antivirals); Brain tumors (surgery, radiation, chemotherapy).
management: PHC: manage tension headache (Amitriptyline), migraine (prophylaxis + acute analgesics/antiemetics + lifestyle), trigeminal autonomic cephalgia (Indomethacin); refer continuous/atypical/cluster headaches. DISTRICT HOSPITAL: brain imaging, refer to higher/tertiary as needed. TERTIARY: treat catastrophic headaches per cause (see Drugs). DISCHARGE: primary headache disorders symptomatically improved can be discharged; secondary headache disorders with essential work up, diagnosis and treatment per case can be discharged.
referralRedFlags: RED FLAG SIGNS: First or worst headache of patient's life; Focal neurologic signs (not typical aura); Severe headache awakening from sleep; Headache with fever, change in personality/mental status/level of consciousness; Fever, neck stiffness or meningism; New onset of severe headache in pregnancy/postpartum or while on hormone treatment; Rapid onset with strenuous exercise; Sudden onset (thunderclap, maximal intensity within seconds to minutes); New headache type in a patient with malignancy or immunosuppression. REASONS FOR REFERRAL: Non responding headaches; Headaches with danger signs should be immediately referred and investigated.
algorithmNotes: CSF=Cerebrospinal Fluid; UFH=Unfractionated Heparin; LMWH=Low Molecular Weight Heparin; SUNCT=Short lasting Unilateral Neuralgiform pain with Conjunctival injection and Tearing.

whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: Acute onset - hours to days; Alteration in sensorium; With/without fever/headache/vomiting/seizures.
risk: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS: Vascular - ischemic/hemorrhagic stroke; Infections (viral/bacterial/fungal/protozoal meningoencephalitis/brain abscess); Metabolic encephalopathies; Seizures/postictal state; Intoxications - drugs/toxins. NOT RECOMMENDED at primary level: Stomach wash; Inj Mannitol; Inj Steroids. COMPLICATIONS: Raised ICP; SIADH; Vasculitis; Hydrocephalus.
scores: Intubate if GCS<8, impaired airway reflexes, abnormal respiratory pattern, signs of raised ICP, oxygen saturation <92% despite high flow oxygen, fluid refractory shock.
investigations: Primary: urgent blood for hemogram/sugar/electrolytes/malaria testing-peripheral smear/rapid antigen detection. Secondary (Desirable): Neuroimaging CT with contrast (rule out hemorrhage/infarcts/focal edema/lesions); Blood cultures aerobic/anaerobic; Fundus examination; CSF study to rule out meningoencephalitis (if imaging rules out mass lesions/herniations). Tertiary: MRI/CT with contrast (rule out abscess/herniations); CSF analysis - biochemistry/cytology/gram staining/culture-bacterial, AFB and fungal/viral PCR/TB-PCR/fungal antigen.
drugs: PRIMARY: Correct hypoglycemia (blood sugar 50 mg/dl) with IV 100ml of 25% dextrose; If seizing - IV/IM Lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg then load with Phenytoin 20 mg/kg at 50 mg/minute; if no IV access - intranasal/buccal Midazolam 0.2 mg/kg / intrarectal Diazepam 0.3-0.4 mg/kg. SECONDARY: Inj Thiamine 100 mg IV; cerebral malaria first dose IV Artesunate 2.4 mg/kg OR Quinine 20 mg/kg bolus; empirical pyogenic meningitis Inj Ceftriaxone 2 g + Inj Vancomycin 500 mg (add Inj Ampicillin 2 g if >50 years/immunocompromised + Inj Dexamethasone 8 mg). TERTIARY empirical antibiotic (within 30 min of arrival): Inj Ceftriaxone 2 g + Inj Vancomycin 500 mg + Inj Ampicillin 2 g (if >50y or immunocompromised) + Inj Dexamethasone 8 mg IV; continue till culture then tailor for 10-14 days; steroids stopped after 48 hours unless adrenal insufficiency/TBM. Viral-Herpes simplex/Zoster: Inj Acyclovir 500 mg IV 8 hourly for 10 days. Cerebral malaria: Inj Artesunate 2.4 mg/kg IM/IV 3 doses 12 hours apart then OD / Inj Quinine 20 mg/kg IV stat then 10mg/kg TDS till oral, then oral Artesunate+Pyrimethamine/Sulphadoxine for 3 days OR oral Quinine 10 mg/kg TDS for total 7 days + Doxycycline 3 mg/kg OD for 7 days.
management: PRIMARY CARE (Essential): Check Airway/Breathing/Circulation; rule out circulatory shock, ongoing convulsions and hyperthermia (core temp >40.5C) or hypothermia (<36.5C); establish IV access (urgent bloods); correct hypoglycemia; treat seizures; urgent referral to higher centres with intensive care. SECONDARY CARE (Taluk/District HQ): + establish & maintain airway (intubate per criteria); Inj Thiamine 100 mg IV; stomach wash/activated charcoal if drug overdose/non corrosive poison; start cerebral malaria treatment; emergency CT/referral to centre with 24 hour CT; empirical meningitis treatment. TERTIARY: neuroimaging MRI/CT with contrast (if abscess - emergency neurosurgical consultation for aspiration open/stereotactic); empirical antibiotics; antivirals/antimalarials per cause. If uncomplicated - back referral to secondary care for completing treatment/monitoring.
referralRedFlags: CRITERIA FOR REFERRAL: Altered sensorium/seizures/focal deficits/hemodynamic instability - where imaging and ICU management are required; If no definite diagnosis achieved after preliminary investigations. DISCHARGE: Afebrile, hemodynamically stable, seizure free >48 hours; diagnosis & treatment plan made and initiated; continuation of treatment with monitoring ensured.
algorithmNotes: TBM=Tubercular Meningitis; ICP=Intracranial Pressure; SIADH=Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH; GCS=Glasgow Coma Scale; AFB=Acid Fast Bacilli.

whenToSuspect: WHAT IS STROKE: An episode of neurological dysfunction caused by focal cerebral, spinal, or retinal infarction or haemorrhage. SYMPTOMS: Numbness or weakness especially on one side of body; Loss of consciousness or altered consciousness; Decreased vision in one or both eyes; Language difficulties (speaking/understanding); Difficulty walking, loss of balance/coordination; Confusion or loss of memory; Swallowing difficulties; Paralysis of any part of body including face; Sudden severe headache with no known cause; Neck pain; Nausea and vomiting. WARNING SIGNS (BEFAST): Balance (loss of balance/coordination); Eyes (sudden blurred/double vision); Face (deviation at angle of mouth); Arm (arm drift); Speech (slurred speech/inability to speak or understand); Time (act fast); Sudden new onset headache or loss of consciousness; Sudden giddiness, vomiting and imbalance.
risk: TYPES OF STROKE: Ischemic stroke (focal cerebral/spinal/retinal infarction); Intracerebral haemorrhage (focal collection of blood within brain parenchyma/ventricular system not caused by trauma); Subarachnoid haemorrhage (bleeding into subarachnoid space); Cerebral venous thrombosis (thrombosis of a cerebral venous structure); Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA - transient episode of neurologic dysfunction by focal cerebral/spinal/retinal ischemia, without acute infarction).
scores: Stroke onset time determines management: <4.5 HOURS (eligible for IV thrombolysis) vs >4.5 HOURS (mechanical thrombectomy 4.5-24 hrs per eligibility). Use NIHSS for severity. CODE Stroke for rapid assessment.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: CT Scan head; ECG; Blood Sugar; Lipids; Renal parameter. DESIRABLE: CTA; Echocardiogram. OPTIONAL: MRI/MRA; Holter monitoring. RECOMMENDED DIAGNOSTIC STUDIES (all patients): Noncontrast brain CT or brain MRI; Blood glucose; Oxygen saturation; Serum electrolytes/renal function tests; CBC including platelet count; Markers of cardiac ischemia; BT, CT, Prothrombin time/INR; APTT; ECG; FLP and carotid doppler (ischemic stroke). Selected patients: TT/ECT if on direct thrombin/factor Xa inhibitors; LFT; Toxicology screen; Blood alcohol level; Pregnancy test; ABG (if hypoxia); Chest radiography; Lumbar puncture (if SAH suspected and CT negative for blood); EEG (if seizures suspected).
drugs: ISCHEMIC (onset <4.5 hrs): IV tPA (0-4.5 hrs) or endovascular treatment according to eligibility and availability. >4.5 hrs: Endovascular treatment with Mechanical thrombectomy using stent retriever (4.5 hrs to 24hrs) per eligibility. HAEMORRHAGIC: Dysphagia assessment; Blood pressure/blood sugar monitoring and IV fluids; Prevention of Pneumonia; Prophylaxis for deep venous thrombosis, monitor and record ECG. SECONDARY PREVENTION: Aspirin (in ischemic stroke); Antihypertensives; Antidiabetics; Lipid lowering agents.
management: PRELIMINARY MANAGEMENT: Assess and manage ABCs; initiate cardiac monitoring; maintain O2 saturation >94%; establish IV access; determine blood glucose and treat; determine time of symptom onset or last known normal and obtain family contact; triage and RAPID TRANSFER to nearest district hospital with CT Scan facility or Stroke center with thrombolysis; notify referral hospital. STROKE UNIT MANAGEMENT: Medical/Nursing - control of BP, control of diabetes, swallow assessment, DVT prophylaxis, antiplatelet drugs. Rehabilitation - Acute phase: basic bed mobility, transfer techniques, communication training, prevention of complications; Subacute/chronic: mobility, gait & balance training, ADL training (grooming, eating, dressing), bowel/bladder training, perceptual & cognitive rehabilitation, assistive devices. REHABILITATION: Physiotherapy; Speech Therapy; Occupational Therapy; Vocational training.
referralRedFlags: BEFAST warning signs -> act fast, RAPID TRANSFER to CT/thrombolysis-capable centre (time is brain). DISCHARGE PLANNING: checklist (drugs, diet, compliance, exercises, health education); Follow up at 2nd week, 1st month, 3rd month and 6th month.
algorithmNotes: BEFAST=Balance, Eyes, Face, Arm, Speech, Time; NIHSS=National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale; tPA=tissue Plasminogen Activator; TIA=Transient Ischemic Attack; CTA=CT Angiogram; FLP=Fasting Lipid Profile.

whenToSuspect: FIRST VISIT (preferably first trimester) ASK: Age; LMP; Parity & obstetric history; complaints (excessive nausea & vomiting/bleeding PV); H/o medical illness (diabetes, hypertension, cardiac problem, epilepsy, chronic illness); Consanguinity, multiple pregnancy; H/o blood transfusion & prior surgery; Personal history (tobacco/alcohol); Family history (diabetes, hypertension, genetic/congenital problems, multiple pregnancy, infections including TB). EXAMINE: Height, weight, BMI; Pallor, Jaundice, Pedal edema; Pulse, BP, RR, temperature; Thyroid; Breast; Respiratory & CVS exam; P/A, P/S and P/V examination.
risk: HIGH RISK PREGNANCY: Any H/o medical illness, previous caesarean section, past obstetric mishap or congenital malformation; Past H/o PPH; Age >35 or <19 years or parity >4; Malnourished (BMI <18.5 or >30 kg/m2); Hemoglobin <7g%; BP >140/90 mmHg on 2 occasions 6 hours apart; APH; Discrepancy between fundal height and period of gestation >4 weeks; GDM/overt DM; Multiple pregnancy; Malpresentation at term; Previous uterine surgery. (High risk pregnancy to be delivered at district hospital/medical college; antenatal care preferably also at these centres.)
scores: FUNDAL HEIGHT vs GESTATIONAL AGE: 12wk just palpable above symphysis pubis; 16wk lower 1/3 between symphysis pubis & umbilicus; 20wk 2/3 of that distance; 24wk level of umbilicus; 28wk lower 1/3 between umbilicus & xiphisternum; 32wk 2/3; 36wk level of xiphisternum; 40wk sinks back to 32wk level (flanks full).
investigations: FIRST VISIT: Essential - Hemoglobin; Urine R & M; ABO & Rh grouping. Desirable - VDRL/RPR; HIV; HBsAg; WHO OGTT/DIPSI test for GDM; TSH in high risk (BOH, goiter, obesity, iodine deficiency areas). Optional - Aneuploidy screen by USG & double marker. SECOND VISIT: Essential - Hemoglobin; Urine albumin. Desirable - USG (Level II between 18-20 weeks for gross congenital malformations); WHO OGTT/DIPSI if >24 weeks & at least 4 weeks after 1st test. Optional - Quadruple test. THIRD/FOURTH VISIT: Hemoglobin; Urine albumin; Optional USG for fetal growth and liquor; auscultate FHS; measure abdominal girth & Symphysiofundal Height.
drugs: FIRST VISIT DO: UPT if in doubt; fill MCH/ANC card, RCH portal entry; give Tab Folic Acid daily; give first dose of tetanus toxoid. SECOND VISIT DO: IFA tablet one (if Hb >11g%) or twice (if Hb <11g%) daily with water or lemon juice; Calcium carbonate 500 mg with vitamin D 250 mcg tablet twice daily with meals (Calcium & IFA not given together); Single dose Albendazole 400mg; give second dose Tetanus Toxoid at least four weeks after first dose. THIRD/FOURTH: Continue IFA and calcium; if non compliant or Hb <9g% give parenteral iron sucrose (not >200mg at one time & not >3 times/week); refer Hb <7g% to higher centre.
management: Schedule: First visit (1st trimester), Second visit (2nd trimester), Third (28-34 weeks), Fourth (36-40 weeks). COUNSELLING AT ALL LEVELS: timing/place of next ANC visit per risk; rest, nutrition, balanced diet & exercise; HIV testing; danger signs; institutional delivery; birth preparedness; early & exclusive breastfeeding for six months; post partum contraception. BIRTH PREPAREDNESS: facility for delivery; support persons; birth companion; means of transport in emergency; blood donors. If bleeding per vaginum - do P/A & P/S, refer to CHC or higher centre.
referralRedFlags: DANGER SIGNALS to report to health facility: Fever; Persistent vomiting; Abnormal vaginal discharge; Palpitations, easy fatigability & breathlessness at rest/mild exertion; Generalized swelling/puffiness of face; Vaginal bleeding; Decreased or absent fetal movements at >28 weeks; Leaking of watery fluid per vaginum; Severe headache/blurring of vision/convulsion; Passing lesser urine and/or burning during micturition; Itching all over body. Refer to higher centre if discrepancy between fundal height and period of gestation.
algorithmNotes: Counselling is an important adjunct to management. Abbrev: LMP=Last Menstrual Period; GDM=Gestational Diabetes Mellitus; APH=Antepartum Haemorrhage; PPH=Postpartum Haemorrhage; IFA=Iron Folic Acid; MCH/RCH=Maternal & Child Health / Reproductive & Child Health.

whenToSuspect: Mostly done for gynaecological indications, but may also be considered in early pregnancy complications. Office endometrial biopsy (thin flexible/Karman cannula) or office hysteroscopy has obviated need for traditional D&C in gynaecological cases, but it still has a place when other modalities not available or do not yield adequate tissue.
risk: INDICATIONS - Gynaecological: Abnormal uterine bleeding in appropriate cases (refer to HMB STW); Post menopausal bleeding (ECC with D&C); Infertility (to rule out TB)/Amenorrhoea; As a haemostatic measure in women with excessive/prolonged vaginal bleeding; Inadequate tissue obtained after office sampling. Early pregnancy complications: Retained products of conception (treatment of inevitable, incomplete, missed, septic or induced abortion); Molar pregnancy. CONTRAINDICATION: Acute pelvic and vaginal infections.
scores: PROCEDURES - Gynaecological: Endometrial Aspiration Biopsy [EA] OR D&C OR Differential curettage (Endocervical curettage [ECC] with EA). Early pregnancy complications: Dilatation and Evacuation [D&E] OR D&C OR Evacuation (with or without suction).
investigations: ALL TISSUE REMOVED MUST BE SENT FOR HISTOPATHOLOGICAL EXAMINATION. Sample to histopathology and microbiology (where indicated). Bimanual pelvic examination prior to procedure.
drugs: ANESTHESIA (any of): General anesthesia; Regional anesthesia; Paracervical block with 1% xylocaine; IV sedation; IM/oral analgesia. Strict asepsis to be maintained. Antibiotics to be used judiciously and decided as per need of individual case. Abdominal cramps (most common side effect) managed by oral analgesics.
management: WHERE PERFORMED: In secondary or tertiary healthcare centres preferably where facilities for anaesthesia and operation theatre are available. Endometrial aspiration biopsy usually done as outpatient in non pregnant cases. PRE-OPERATIVE REQUISITES: valid indication; general medical fitness & no contraindication; written informed consent. DO'S: evacuation of urinary bladder before procedure; safety checklist; dorsal/lithotomy position; bimanual pelvic examination prior; sounding to measure uterocervical length ONLY in non pregnant women; REFER in case of complication. DONT'S: over abduction of legs; no sounding in pregnant uterus; no forceful insertion of any instrument; abandon procedure if suspected perforation and refer; insert dilator just beyond internal os and NOT till fundus. POST PROCEDURE: observe minimum two hours for haemorrhage/complications; discharge when comfortable; follow up after a week with histopathology report.
referralRedFlags: COMPLICATIONS: Excessive bleeding; Cervical laceration; Perforation of uterus; Injury to bowel and bladder; Pelvic infection; Post-operative intra uterine adhesions. WARNING SIGNALS to report back (explained at discharge): severe pain, bleeding, foul smelling discharge or fever. D&C is a blind procedure and may miss pathology; in suspected focal pathology, obtain tissue under hysteroscopic visualization.
algorithmNotes: Counselling is an important adjunct. EA=Endometrial Aspiration; ECC=Endocervical Curettage; D&E=Dilatation and Evacuation; HMB=Heavy Menstrual Bleeding.

icd10: H90.5 (as printed in STW; HMB is usually N92)
whenToSuspect: TO DO AT ALL LEVELS - HISTORY: Age; Parity; Detailed menstrual history including irregularities; Other medical illness (thyroid disorder, coagulopathy, jaundice); IUCD use; Lactation; Drug intake. EXAMINATION: General (evaluate pallor, calculate BMI); Systemic (CVS, RS and hepatosplenomegaly); Local examination where indicated and feasible (P/S and P/V).
risk: HIGH RISK FOR ENDOMETRIAL CANCER (patient having): Obesity; Diabetes; Hypertension; PCOD. Approach varies by group: Adolescents; Women of Reproductive Age; Age >40 years / Married >40 / Post Menopausal (need endometrial sampling/refer).
scores: Secondary level endometrial sampling results guide therapy: Secretory/Non Secretory endometrium without hyperplasia; Hyperplasia without atypia; Hyperplasia with atypia/malignancy (refer). ET (endometrial thickness) >12mm or high risk for endometrial cancer -> do endometrial sampling.
investigations: PRIMARY: Hemoglobin; CBC with peripheral smear; Bleeding time/Clotting time (BT/CT). SECONDARY (CHC): + ABO and Rh typing; Thyroid function test; USG of Abdomen & Pelvis. TERTIARY: Hemoglobin; CBC with peripheral smear; BT/CT; ABO and Rh typing; TSH; USG of Abdomen & Pelvis; endometrial sampling D&C/Hysteroscopic guided for thickened endometrium.
drugs: SUPPORTIVE: Reassurance; Hematinics; Tranexamic acid during episode of heavy bleeding. MEDICAL OPTIONS: NSAIDs; Tranexamic acid; OC Pills / Combined OC Pills; Progestogens (Norethisterone provides the best hemostasis); LNG IUS (Levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system); Ormeloxifene; Ablative techniques. ACUTE BLEEDING EPISODE: IV Tranexamic acid 1g stat slowly followed by oral Tranexamic acid 0.5-1g, 6-8 hourly for 5 days; Blood transfusion if indicated. HORMONE THERAPY: Norethisterone (max daily dose 40 mg) OR Medroxyprogesterone acetate (max daily dose 40 mg); given orally daily in divided doses from fifth day of cycle for three weeks, repeated cyclically for total of 4-6 cycles. Hyperplasia without atypia: Progestogens, LNG IUS, hormonal therapy to continue for 6 months.
management: PRIMARY: History & exam; if abnormality refer to higher centre. Adolescents - supportive (Tranexamic acid, OC Pills); Reproductive age - R/o pregnancy, speculum & pelvic exam, then OC Pills if contraception desired or NSAIDs/Tranexamic acid/Progestogens if not; Age >40 with any abnormality - refer. SECONDARY (CHC): Adolescents - supportive; Married <40 - do USG (ET <12mm vs >12mm/high risk -> sampling); Married >40 - endometrial sampling + histopath; Post menopausal - refer. Hyperplasia with atypia/malignancy - refer. TERTIARY: History, exam & USG -> No structural abnormality (medical options; if no relief Hysterectomy preferably after second opinion); Structural abnormality (thickened endometrium - sampling; Fibroids & polyps & adenomyosis -> refer to Fibroids & Polyps STW).
referralRedFlags: Refer to higher centre if: any abnormality at primary level; no relief on medical therapy; post menopausal bleeding; hyperplasia with atypia/malignancy; recurrence. Hysterectomy at tertiary preferably after second opinion.
algorithmNotes: Counselling is an important adjunct. LNG IUS=Levonorgestrel Intrauterine System; OC=Oral Contraceptive; ET=Endometrial Thickness; PCOD=Polycystic Ovarian Disease.

whenToSuspect: In women aged less than 40 and/or low parity it is MANDATORY to have a second opinion from a qualified gynaecologist. Hysterectomy to be considered ONLY when child bearing is completed & rarely in younger patients.
risk: INDICATIONS: Leiomyoma (Symptomatic fibroids especially if not responding to medical management; Asymptomatic fibroids >=14 weeks uterine size; Fibroid causing hydroureteronephrosis; Rapidly enlarging fibroids; Submucus myoma >4cm); Heavy menstrual bleeding (Failed medical treatment); Endometriosis (Failed medical treatment; Causing hydroureteronephrosis; Recurrence after failed medical/conservative surgical management); Prolapse (Third or fourth degree utero vaginal prolapse); Pre-invasive diseases (Endometrial hyperplasia without atypia with failed medical treatment; Endometrial hyperplasia with atypia; CIN II with poor compliance or CIN III); Others (Adnexal masses - individualised; Recurrent post-menopausal bleeding even in absence of malignancy). Simple ovarian cysts <5 cm without suspicious features kept on observation, reviewed after 6 months.
scores: HYSTERECTOMY SHOULD NOT BE DONE FOR: White discharge per vaginum; Cervicitis; Non specific abdominal or pelvic pain; Minor degree of utero vaginal prolapse; Fibroids small (<5 cm) or Asymptomatic (<12 weeks size uterus); Simple ovarian cyst <=5 cm.
investigations: Complete Blood Count; Blood grouping & cross matching; Fasting Blood Sugar & Post Prandial Blood Sugar; Renal Function Test; Liver Function Test; Urine Routine & Microscopy; Electrocardiogram; X ray chest; Others as indicated.
drugs: Post operative: adequate iron and calcium & Vitamin D3 supplements; evaluate need for hormones in very selected patients. (Possible need for post operative Hormone therapy in selected cases.)
management: ROUTES OF HYSTERECTOMY: Abdominal; Vaginal (Pelvic organ prolapse, Non descent hysterectomy); Laparoscopic (in appropriately selected patients). PRE-OPERATIVE COUNSELLING & INFORMED CONSENT: Need for hysterectomy; Alternative treatment options; Risks and benefits; Potential complications; Removal/conservation of ovaries & tubes; Route of hysterectomy; Possible need for post operative Hormone therapy. FOLLOW UP: Discharge summary with operative details; Review for histopathology report; Report if fever/bleeding/any other symptoms; Avoid lifting heavy weight for 8 weeks; Abstinence for eight weeks; Adequate iron, calcium & Vitamin D3 supplements; Evaluate need for hormones in selected patients. Ovaries should be preserved in most pre-menopausal women unless diseased; recommended to combine with bilateral salpingectomy to minimise risk of subsequent ovarian malignancy.
referralRedFlags: COMPLICATIONS TO BE EXPLAINED: Risk of Infection; Bleeding (primary/reactionary/secondary); Injury to bladder/bowel/ureter; Pain; Fever; Hernia (rare and late). Mandatory second opinion in women <40 and/or low parity.
algorithmNotes: Counselling is an important adjunct. CIN=Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia.

whenToSuspect: More than 500 ml of blood loss OR any amount of bleeding which causes derangement of vital parameters is PPH. RED FLAG SIGN: PR >120/min; Systolic BP <100 mm Hg; Tachypnea; SpO2 <95%; Deterioration of sensorium.
risk: Classify after delivery of placenta: TRAUMATIC PPH (uterus well contracted but bleeding continuing - cervical/vaginal/perineal tears, rupture uterus or scar dehiscence) vs ATONIC PPH (uterus flabby). If bleeding continues without any apparent cause, check for coagulopathy.
scores: Decision branches: Placenta delivered vs not delivered; Uterus contracted vs flabby. 3 ml of crystalloid solution should be used to replace every ml of blood lost during the initial part of the acute bleeding phase.
investigations: Draw blood for grouping and cross matching; check for coagulation defects (if bleeding continues without apparent cause). Monitoring of vitals; measurement of input and output. Check vitals and blood loss frequently at least every 15 minutes.
drugs: INITIAL: Start RL/NS, infuse 1 L in 15-20 minutes; Oxygen @ 6-8 L/minute by mask. OXYTOCIN: Inj Oxytocin 10 IU IM (if not given after delivery); Oxytocin infusion 20 IU in 500 ml RL/NS @ 40-60 drops/minute; IV bolus of oxytocin should NOT be given. ATONIC PPH PHARMACOTHERAPY (any alone or combination): Inj Methyl Ergometrine 0.2 mg IM or IV slowly (contraindicated in hypertension, severe anemia, heart disease; can be repeated after 15 minutes to a maximum of 5 doses = 1mg) OR Tab Misoprostol (PGE1) 800 ug per rectal or sublingual OR Inj Carboprost (PGF2 alpha) 250 ug IM (contraindicated in asthma; can be repeated every 20 minutes to a maximum of 8 doses = 2 mg). If bleeding not controlled: continue bimanual compression & Oxytocin infusion @10-20 units/hr. If controlled: continue Oxytocin infusion @5-10 units/hr (total Oxytocin not to exceed 100 IU in 24 hours). TRANEXAMIC ACID 1g slow IV - adjunctive, as early as possible irrespective of cause but definitely within three hours of delivery; can be repeated after 30 minutes if bleeding persists (standard treatment must continue meanwhile).
management: INITIAL: Call for help; rapid assessment (PR, BP, RR, Temperature); two IV lines wide bore cannula (16-18 gauge); blood for grouping/cross matching; RL/NS 1L in 15-20 min; O2 6-8 L/min; indwelling catheter to urobag; monitor input/output. PLACENTA NOT DELIVERED: continue Oxytocin drip, palpate uterus, attempt controlled cord traction if uterus contracted -> if still not delivered shift for manual removal of placenta (MRP). PLACENTA DELIVERED: fundal massage, inspect placenta for completeness, explore uterus for retained bits/membranes/clots and evacuate. TRAUMATIC PPH: explore for cervical/vaginal/perineal tears, repair; if persists shift to OT for exploration under GA and/or laparotomy. ATONIC PPH: bimanual compression + pharmacotherapy; if uncontrolled - explore for retained bits, intrauterine balloon tamponade using condom catheter; surgical (uterine compression sutures, stepwise devascularisation Uterine->Ovarian->Internal Iliac artery ligation, Hysterectomy). If controlled: repeat uterine massage every 15 min for 2 hours; monitor vitals every 10 min x30 min, every 15 min x30 min, then every 30 min for 3-6 hours until stable.
referralRedFlags: TIMELY REFERRAL to higher centre if facilities for blood transfusion or exploration/surgical intervention not available. Transport with IV fluids containing oxytocin on flow, preferably with uterine/vaginal tamponade in situ. Aortic compression as short time measure to reduce blood loss; Non-pneumatic anti-shock garment (NASG) during transport if available; Uterine artery embolization in selected patients if facilities available.
algorithmNotes: MRP=Manual Removal of Placenta; NASG=Non-pneumatic Anti-Shock Garment; PGE1=Misoprostol; PGF2alpha=Carboprost. Counselling is an important adjunct.

whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: Heavy menstrual/irregular bleeding; Urinary pressure symptoms; Heaviness and pain in abdomen, dysmenorrhoea; Mass in lower abdomen; Infertility; Asymptomatic. EXAMINATION: GPE (specially check for pallor); Abdominal examination (check for suprapubic mass or lump); P/S (inspect cervix for local abnormalities); P/V (assess for uterine size - enlarged and/or irregular uterus).
risk: ASYMPTOMATIC FIBROIDS <5CM DO NOT NEED TO BE TREATED. Categories: Endometrial polyps/sub mucus fibroids; Fibroids (Small <5cm vs Large uterine size >=12 weeks); Fibroid Polyps (Thin pedicle vs Thick pedicle). ALL patients of fibroid uterus do NOT necessarily need hysterectomy; all therapeutic options including observation to be explained.
scores: Small Fibroids (<5cm): Asymptomatic -> Observation; Symptomatic -> Medical Management. Large Fibroids (>=12 weeks size) -> discuss treatment by age & parity. Fibroid Polyps: Thin pedicle -> Polypectomy; Thick pedicle -> individualized management at tertiary centre.
investigations: Hemoglobin; Complete blood count; Thyroid function test; USG.
drugs: MEDICAL MANAGEMENT (symptomatic small fibroids): Tranexamic acid; Levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (if uterine cavity normal); Progestogenic agents - Norethisterone (max daily dose 40 mg) OR Medroxyprogesterone acetate (max daily dose 40 mg). Any hormone given orally daily in divided doses for duration of three weeks and repeated cyclically for total of 4-6 cycles of treatment.
management: Endometrial polyps/sub mucus fibroids: Hysteroscopic removal (endometrial polyps; select appropriate patient with submucus fibroids based on size and proportion of fibroid projecting into uterine cavity). Small fibroids: asymptomatic=observation; symptomatic=medical management, if no relief discuss by age & parity. Large fibroids: discuss treatment by age & parity -> Non surgical (Uterine artery embolization) OR Surgical (Myomectomy for younger age &/or low parity; Hysterectomy to be avoided in young patients aged <35 years as far as possible). Fibroid Polyps: thin pedicle=polypectomy; thick pedicle=individualized management at tertiary centre.
referralRedFlags: Thick pedicle fibroid polyps -> individualized management at tertiary centre. All therapeutic options need to be explained to the patient including just keeping the patient on observation.
algorithmNotes: Counselling is an important adjunct. Hysterectomy to be avoided in young patients (<35 years) as far as possible. LNG IUS=Levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system.

whenToSuspect: Acute onset of fever (<=5-7 days) with altered sensorium and/or new onset of seizures (excluding simple febrile seizures). SYMPTOMS: Fever, headache, vomiting, lethargy, unconsciousness; Seizures; Abnormal posturing; Paucity of limb movements. EXAMINATION: Vital signs; General (pallor, petechiae, rash, icterus); Neurological (GCS, abnormal posturing decerebrate/decorticate, active seizures, cranial nerves pupil size & reaction/doll's eye/squint/facial deviation, focal deficits, meningeal signs).
risk: HISTORY OF: Rash, vesicles, past chicken pox; residence (rural/urban, endemic for cerebral malaria, AES epidemic in neighborhood); animal contact, insect bite, dog bite; drug/toxin exposure; recent travel; trauma; seizures; recent immunizations; recurrent encephalopathy; past/concurrent systemic illness; pre-morbid developmental/neurological status. Refer to tertiary (PICU + ventilation) if: GCS<8, abnormal breathing pattern, shock not responding to fluid bolus, decerebrate/decorticate posturing, seizures persisting despite benzodiazepine and phenytoin.
scores: Lumbar puncture CONTRA-INDICATED or neuroimaging before LP if: papilledema; platelet count <50,000; focal neurological deficits; asymmetric/unreactive pupils; decerebrate/decorticate posturing.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: CBC, LFT, KFT, blood sugar, CECT Brain, CSF examination (cytology, biochemistry, culture, AFB staining, Gene Xpert), peripheral smear for malarial parasite, Rapid Malarial Antigen Test. DESIRABLE: MRI Brain, CSF PCR for Herpes simplex encephalitis, JE serology, EEG, Dengue serology and NS1 testing, HIV testing. OPTIONAL: CSF Neurovirology panel, anti-NMDA receptor antibody, PCR viral testing of other samples, Blood Tandem Mass Spectrometry & urine gas chromatography, antinuclear antibodies.
drugs: STEP III EMPIRICAL TREATMENT (start if CSF cannot be done/report delayed): Ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg/day in 2 divided doses X 10-14 days; Acyclovir (all suspected sporadic viral encephalitis): 3 mo to 12 y - 500mg/m2 8 hourly (min 21 days), >12 y - 10mg/kg 8 hourly (14-21 days confirmed); Artesunate combination therapy (stop if peripheral smear & RDT negative): 3mg/kg in child <20 kg and 2.4mg/kg in child >20kg IV/IM at 0,12 and 24 hours then once daily parenteral/oral X 3-7 days. RAISED ICP: Mannitol 0.25 g/kg bolus then 0.25 g/kg q6h up to 48 hours; hypertonic 3% saline 0.1-1 mL/kg/hr (target serum sodium 145-155 meq/L); hyperventilate to PaCO2 30-35 mmHg if herniation. SEIZURES: 1st line IV Lorazepam 0.1mg/kg or Midazolam 0.2 mg/kg or Diazepam 0.3 mg/kg (IM Midazolam 0.2 mg/kg if no IV); 2nd line Inj Phenytoin 20 mg/kg (in NS, 1mg/kg/min); refractory - midazolam infusion 1-18 mcg/kg/min; if no ICU - sodium valproate 20 mg/kg or levetiracetam 20-40 mg/kg or phenobarbitone 20mg/kg. Steroids: Pulse methylprednisolone in suspected ADEM/autoimmune encephalitis.
management: All patients need to be admitted. STEP I (rapid assessment & stabilization): airway (intubate if GCS<8, impaired airway reflexes, abnormal respiratory pattern, raised ICP, SpO2<92% despite high flow O2, fluid refractory shock); ventilation/oxygenation; circulation (IV access, fluid bolus 20 mL/kg NS if circulatory failure, inotropes if needed); identify herniation/raised ICP; treat fever/hypothermia; treat ongoing seizures (benzodiazepine then phenytoin). STEP II: history/exam/investigations. STEP III: empirical antimicrobials (see Drugs). STEP IV (supportive): euglycemia, hydration, control fever, treat raised ICP (head-end elevation 15-30deg), treat seizures, steroids if ADEM/autoimmune. STEP V (complications & rehab): physiotherapy, posture change, prevent bed sores & exposure keratitis; nutrition early feeding; psychological support.
referralRedFlags: Refer to tertiary care with PICU & mechanical ventilation if: GCS <8; abnormal breathing pattern; shock not responding to fluid bolus; decerebrate/decorticate posturing; seizures persisting despite benzodiazepine and phenytoin. DISCHARGE: Hemodynamically stable; improvement in consciousness; afebrile; started eating/drinking orally; seizures subsided; parents explained supportive care & physiotherapy.
algorithmNotes: GCS=Glasgow Coma Scale; ICP=Intracranial Pressure; ADEM=Acute Disseminated Encephalomyelitis; RDT=Rapid Diagnostic Test; JE=Japanese Encephalitis; PICU=Paediatric ICU.

whenToSuspect: DIARRHEA IS: >3 loose or watery stools/day. Acute Diarrhea <14 days; Persistent diarrhea >14 days; Dysentery = blood in stools. ASK: Duration; Blood in stool; Vomiting, fever, cough, recent measles, HIV status; Immunization status & pre illness feeding practices; Fluids/food/drugs taken during illness. EXAMINE: General condition; Nutritional status (weight/weight for height/MUAC); Classify malnutrition; Signs of dehydration. SKIN PINCH TEST goes back: Very slowly (>2 seconds), Slowly (brief instant), Immediately (normal).
risk: CLASSIFY DEHYDRATION: NO dehydration (not enough signs). SOME dehydration (2 of: restless/irritable, sunken eyes, drinks eagerly thirsty, skin pinch back very slowly). SEVERE dehydration (2 of: lethargy/unconscious, sunken eyes, not able to drink/drinking poorly, skin pinch back slowly).
scores: PLAN B ORS by age/weight over 4 hours (75 ml/kg): <4 months <5kg=200-400ml; 4-11m 5-7.9kg=400-600; 12-23m 8-10.9kg=600-800; 2-4y 11-15.9kg=800-1200; 5-14y 16-29.9kg=1200-2200; >=15y 30kg+=2200-4000. PLAN C IV fluid (Ringer lactate/NS; DNS if malnourished): Infant <12 months - first 30 mL/kg in 1 hour then 70 mL/kg in 5 hours; Older - first 30 mL/kg in 30 minutes then 70 mL/kg in 2.5 hours.
investigations: Some dehydration: electrolytes. Severe dehydration: CBC, electrolytes, RFT, VBG. Suspected cholera: stool hanging drop and stool culture. Dysentery (no response to antibiotic in 2 days): stool culture & stool routine for trophozoites of Amoeba. Persistent diarrhea: stool routine microscopy, urine routine microscopy, urine culture, sepsis screen.
drugs: PLAN A (no dehydration): extra fluids; WHO ORS after each loose stool (upto 2 yrs 50-100 ml, 2 yrs+ 100-200ml); Zinc supplement (2-6 months 10mg/day x 2 weeks, >6 months 20mg/day x 2 weeks). PLAN B (some dehydration): ORS 75 ml/kg over 4 hour period + zinc + feeding. PLAN C (severe): IV Ringer lactate/NS as per age table (above); ORS ~5 ml/kg/hour once child can drink. DYSENTERY: Ciprofloxacin 15mg/kg twice a day, reassess after 2 days (improvement=3 days; no improvement -> Cefixime 10 mg/kg/d 2 divided doses, reassess after 2 days, if better complete 3-5 days). If stool positive for Amoebiasis: Metronidazole 10mg/kg/dose 8 hourly x 7 days (10 days in severe cases). PERSISTENT DIARRHEA: zinc for 14 days; vitamins/minerals; if Giardia cyst/trophozoite Metronidazole 5mg/kg/dose x 8hrly x 5-7 days. Suspected cholera: Doxycycline single dose 300mg or Tetracycline 12.5mg/kg 4 times/day x 3 days (young children Erythromycin 12.5mg/kg 4 times/day x 3 days). Associated vomitings: Ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg/dose IV/oral.
management: PLAN A/B/C by dehydration severity (see Drugs/Scores). After Plan B 4 hours reassess & reclassify. PLAN C: urgent referral to hospital, mother continue ORS sips during transport or NG tube; start IV immediately; reassess every 15-30 min; after 6 hours (infants)/3 hours (older) evaluate & choose plan. PATIENT EDUCATION: danger signs, hygiene, hand washing, safe drinking water, feeding practices, vaccination per IAP. DISCHARGE (Plan C): sufficient rehydration (wt gain/clinical), IV fluids no longer needed, oral intake >= losses, medical f/u available.
referralRedFlags: REFER TO HOSPITAL: Severe malnutrition/HIV; Severe dehydration; Hypernatremic (Na >145mmol/L)/hyponatremic dehydration (Na <135 mmol/L); Dysentery with age <1 yr/measles in past 6 weeks/dehydration/sick; Dysentery with no improvement on antibiotics; Persistent diarrhea with dehydration; Persistent diarrhea with serious systemic infection (pneumonia, sepsis), infants <4 months, or no improvement over 5 days. DANGER SIGNS (return immediately): passing many watery stools, repeated vomiting/very thirsty, eating/drinking poorly, develops fever/blood in stools.
algorithmNotes: Follow up in 5 days if no improvement. MUAC=Mid Upper Arm Circumference; ORS=Oral Rehydration Solution; DNS=Dextrose Normal Saline; IAP=Indian Academy of Pediatrics.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: Fever AND two of - Nausea; Vomiting; Rash; Myalgia; Headache; Retro orbital pain; Arthralgia; Hemorrhagic manifestations. Plus live in/travel to dengue endemic area. WARNING SIGNS: Abdominal pain or tenderness; Persistent vomiting; Clinical fluid accumulation (pleural effusion, ascites); Mucosal bleed (malena, epistaxis, gum bleed); Liver enlargement >2 cms; Shock/DSS (weak rapid pulse, pulse pressure <20mm Hg, hypotension, cold clammy skin, restlessness).
risk: ASSESSMENT: Dengue without warning signs / Dengue with warning signs / Severe dengue (fluid accumulation with respiratory distress, severe bleeding, impaired consciousness).
scores: INDICATION FOR TRANSFUSION: Packed Red Cells (loss of blood 10% or more of total blood volume, refractory shock, fluid overload); Platelets (prolonged shock, prophylactic if PLT <10,000/cumm, systemic massive bleeding); Fresh Frozen Plasma/Cryoprecipitate (coagulopathy with bleeding).
investigations: ESSENTIAL: Hb, TLC, DLC, Platelets, PCV; Positive tourniquet test; NS1 antigen (ELISA method). DESIRABLE: Chest X-ray; LFT, RFT; CPK, albumin; USG abdomen; Dengue IgM. OPTIONAL: Echocardiography; PCR-dengue; CVP monitoring; USG guided measurement of collapsibility of IVC for monitoring hypovolemia.
drugs: WITHOUT WARNING SIGNS: Symptomatic ambulatory treatment; Paracetamol for fever (AVOID NSAIDs); daily monitoring (clinical, PCV, platelets). SHOCK - assess ABC & start oxygen. COMPENSATED SHOCK (tachypnea, tachycardia, normotensive): Ringer's Lactate/NS 10 ml/kg/hr; reassess hourly; no improvement -> RL 10-15ml/kg/hr -> RL 15ml/kg/hr -> Colloids 10ml/kg/hr; improvement -> RL 5-7ml/kg/1-2hr -> RL 3-5ml/kg/hr, continue till stable 24 hours. HYPOTENSIVE SHOCK (peripheral pulses not palpable): 20 ml/kg crystalloid or colloid in 15 minutes; no improvement -> PCV increased: Colloids 10-20ml/kg, PCV decreased: Blood Transfusion; improvement -> decrease infusion (10ml/kg/hr 1-2hr, 7ml/kg 2-3hrs, 5ml/kg 2-4hrs, 3ml/kg 2-4hrs, stop at 48 hours).
management: Without warning signs: ambulatory symptomatic + daily monitoring. With warning signs/severe dengue: hospital management with fluid resuscitation per shock type. In case of shock, start bolus and arrange urgent referral with continuous monitoring by a health professional to facilities with a PICU.
referralRedFlags: REASONS FOR REFERRAL: Cold extremities, restlessness; Acute abdominal pain; Decreased urine output; Bleeding and hemoconcentration; Rising PCV & thrombocytopenia without clinical symptoms. DISCHARGE (all must be present): Clinical - no fever for 48 hours, improvement in clinical status (general well-being, appetite, haemodynamic status, urine output, respiratory distress). Laboratory - increasing trend of platelet count, stable haematocrit without IV fluids.
algorithmNotes: AVOID NSAIDs (bleeding risk). DSS=Dengue Shock Syndrome; PCV=Packed Cell Volume; IVC=Inferior Vena Cava; CVP=Central Venous Pressure.

whenToSuspect: FEVER IS: Core (rectal) temperature >=38.0C (100.4F) or axillary temperature >37.5C. WHAT TO ASK: Fever duration; Localizing symptoms (RTI, UTI, GI tract infection, CNS infection); Rash, joint symptoms, skin/soft tissue swelling or redness; Vaccination within 24 hours, drug/toxin exposure; Family/neighbourhood history of similar illness. EXAMINE: Vital signs (Temp, HR, RR, BP, CFT); Appearance (sick, toxic, lethargic, irritable, inconsolable, dehydrated); General (ENT, rash, lymphadenopathy, skin, joints, genitalia, bones); Systemic (chest auscultation, abdominal palpation, CNS, CVS).
risk: CLUES TO SPECIFIC DIAGNOSIS: Fever + respiratory (cough/runny nose=URTI; membrane over tonsils/pharynx=Diphtheria; paroxysmal cough=Pertussis; barking cough=Laryngotracheobronchitis/croup). Fever + rash (red maculopapular=Measles/Rubella/Dengue; fine generalized maculopapular with systemic dysfunction/shock=Meningococcemia; itchy erythematous macules to clear vesicles=Varicella). Fever + other (parotid gland swelling=Mumps; arthritis=Chikungunya/acute rheumatic fever/JIA; strawberry red tongue, skin peeling, lymphadenopathy, conjunctival injection=Kawasaki disease).
scores: Investigate by duration/symptoms: <7 days fever alone (if <72h & not sick - no investigations; if >72h - TLC/DLC/PS/malaria/platelet); <7 days + localizing symptoms; <7 days + non specific; >7 days fever alone (+Widal, Mantoux, USG); >7 days + localizing symptoms (+Brucella/CMV/Herpes/JE serology, CT, bone marrow, ANA, HIV, PET).
investigations: Perform only where result impacts management. <7 DAYS FEVER ALONE: if fever >72h - TLC, DLC, P.S for leukocyte morphology, malarial parasite & platelet count; Desirable - rapid antigen for malaria, NS1 & dengue IgM, blood culture; Optional - CRP, procalcitonin. +Localizing: + (clean-catch) urine microscopy & culture, chest Xray, CSF analysis; USG, throat/pharyngeal swab, pus aspiration. >7 DAYS: + Widal test, Mantoux, ultrasonography; serology for Brucella/CMV/Herpes/Japanese encephalitis, CT scan, bone marrow, ANA profile, HIV serology, echocardiography, PET, Xpert MTB RIF assay, Mycobacterial culture.
drugs: Decrease body temperature with Paracetamol (15mg/kg by any route) and/or hydrotherapy. Consider first dose antibiotic in suspected meningitis, severe pneumonia, or severe malnutrition; consider anti-malarial in suspected malaria. Empiric treatment tailored to investigation reports & local antimicrobial sensitivity. Anti tuberculosis treatment (ATT) should NOT be started on empiric basis except in suspected TBM.
management: STEP 1 (initial assessment & stabilization): manage urgent issues, antipyretic, first dose antibiotic/anti-malarial if indicated. STEP 2 (consider hospitalization): all neonates; young infants with toxic appearance; severe malnutrition/toxic/inability to feed/lethargy/irritability/dehydration; >14 days illness without diagnosis; any reason deemed by physician. STEP 3 (consider referral to tertiary after stabilization): need for intensive care; complex multi-system disease; confirmed complications. STEP 4 (empiric management): based on likely diagnosis, sickness, availability. STEP 5 (discharge): afebrile >48 hours or defervescence; feeding well; symptoms resolved/resolving; ambulatory care feasible; IV antibiotic course completed.
referralRedFlags: Hospitalize toxic-appearing/neonates/severe malnutrition/prolonged undiagnosed fever. Refer to tertiary for intensive care, complex multi-system disease, confirmed complications.
algorithmNotes: Abbrev: RTI/URTI=Respiratory/Upper Respiratory Tract Infection; CFT=Capillary Filling Time; JIA=Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis; TBM=Tubercular Meningitis; ATT=Anti Tuberculosis Treatment.

condition: Sepsis and Septic Shock in Children
whenToSuspect: Sepsis to be suspected in children with any infections (fever with or without rashes/pneumonia/diarrhoea) at risk of life threatening organ dysfunction. WHEN TO SUSPECT (2-59 months): Poor Feeding; Lethargy; Decreased responsiveness; Unconsciousness; Cold/bluish peripheries; Rapid or shallow breathing; Chest indrawing; Stridor; Excessive vomiting; Decreased urine output; Convulsions; Stiff neck. VITAL SIGNS: pulse volume; HR & RR outside age range; CRT >3 seconds; pulse oximetry <95%; Systolic BP <70 in <1 year, >1 year if systolic BP < 70 + Age(yrs)x2 or lower than age range. Signs of severe dehydration: Diarrhoea plus any two of - lethargy/unconscious, not able to drink/drinks poorly, sunken eyes, skin pinch goes back very slowly.
risk: CHECK HISTORY OF: Prior treatment; Previous recurrent infections; Prior hospitalisation; Chronic systemic illness (congenital or acquired); Immunization (age appropriate). WHEN TO SUSPECT CARDIAC FAILURE: history of underlying heart disease; forehead sweating/suck rest suck cycle; murmur; hepatomegaly or basilar crept (be careful giving fluid bolus). For severe acute malnutrition consider SAM STW; for suspected Dengue follow Dengue Fever STW.
scores: Poor peripheral perfusion = fast pulse, cold peripheries, poor pulse volume, CRT >3 sec (Fast pulse: HR>180 in <12 month, HR>120 in >12 month). AVPU scale: A=Alert, V=responds to Voice, P=responds to Pain, U=Unresponsive; anything below A is a danger sign.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: CBC, peripheral blood film, urine routine, blood sugar, CRP, serum electrolytes, renal function test, liver function test. DESIRABLE: Blood culture, blood gas, relevant cultures, chest X-ray, Malaria rapid malarial antigen test, Dengue NS1/IgM, CSF study. OPTIONAL: PCT, USG to guide fluids.
drugs: ANTIBIOTICS: >3 months - Inj Ceftriaxone 100mg/kg/day (2 divided doses); <3 months - Inj Cefotaxime 200mg/kg (divided 6-8hrly) + Inj Gentamicin 5-7.5 mg/kg single dose/day; soft tissue infection - consider Inj Cloxacillin 200mg/kg divided 6 hourly or Inj Amoxicillin-Clavulanic acid 30 mg/kg/dose 8hrly. FLUIDS: Start O2 face mask @4-6 lit/min or hood @8-10 lit (nasal prongs 1-2 lit/min) to maintain SpO2 >95%; insert two IV cannulas; give first dose antibiotics within first hour; 20 ml/kg normal saline bolus over 20-30 minutes; if no improvement repeat 20 ml/kg over 30 minutes (monitor for hepatomegaly, O2 saturation, crepitations - stop fluids if appear). If improvement after 1st bolus + diarrhea: 70 ml/kg over 5 hours in infants and over 2.5 hours in hypovolemic shock; additional fluids if losses continue. If shock persists: Inj Adrenaline infusion @0.1 microgram/kg/min and refer (0.3 x body weight in mg in 50 ml NS or 5% dextrose at 1 ml/hr = 0.1 microgram/kg/min). CHF: Dobutamine/Milrinone infusion and Furosemide.
management: DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM: Child 2-59 months with febrile illness + warning signs -> Good peripheral perfusion (admit/initiate treatment per IMNCI) vs Poor peripheral perfusion (admit, initiate treatment, refer to centre with ICU/ventilation/24-hour monitoring; O2 + 2 IV cannulas + first dose antibiotics within 1 hour; 20 ml/kg NS bolus; reassess; repeat bolus if needed; adrenaline + refer if shock persists). COMPLICATIONS: Respiratory failure (RR increase & inability to maintain saturation >94% with O2 - CPAP/BIPAP or invasive ventilation); Congestive heart failure (Dobutamine/Milrinone + Furosemide); Infections on other sites (explore and treat).
referralRedFlags: WHEN TO REFER: Shock does not improve after 2nd fluid bolus; Signs of fluid overload; No facility for continuous monitoring (counsel parents & inform referring facility before referral). DISCHARGE: Completion of antibiotics per culture sensitivity; Afebrile for 48 hours; Vitals within normal limit for age; Good oral intake; Adequate urine output >1ml/kg/hr.
algorithmNotes: AVPU=Alert/Voice/Pain/Unresponsive; CRT=Capillary Refill Time; IMNCI=Integrated Management of Neonatal and Childhood Illness; CPAP/BIPAP=non-invasive ventilation.

whenToSuspect: COMMON PRESENTATION: Faulty feeding (not exclusively breastfed for 6 months, bottle feeding, delayed/inadequate complementary feeding); Poor appetite; Not gaining weight; Lethargic; Disinterested in surroundings; Delayed development. Additional symptoms of complications: Loose motions; Jaundice; Seizures. Inter-current infections: Pneumonia, Diarrhea, Sepsis, Skin infections, Severe dehydration, Untreated tuberculosis, HIV, Social challenges. EXAMINE FOR: vital signs (PR, RR, CRT); lethargy/irritability; loss of subcutaneous fat; muscle wasting; pallor; signs of Vitamin B, K and A deficiencies; respiratory distress; dehydration.
risk: TRIAGE: SAM + GOOD APPETITE + NO MEDICAL COMPLICATION -> Home based treatment + oral amoxicillin 50 mg/kg/dose twice a day for 7-10 days. SAM + COMPLICATIONS/POOR APPETITE/FAILED HOME TREATMENT -> Hospitalize. PRIMARY FAILURE OF TREATMENT: failure to regain appetite by day 4; failure to lose oedema by day 4; oedema still present Day 10; failure to gain at least 5g/kg/day for 3 consecutive days on catchup diet.
scores: DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA SAM & MAM: 0-6 months - SAM if MUAC <11.0 cm. 6-59 months - SAM if MUAC <11.5 cm or WHZ <-3 SD or bilateral pitting oedema; MAM if MUAC 11.5-12.4 cm or WHZ -2 to -3 SD. >5 years - SAM if BMI <=3SD (severe thinness); MAM if BMI <=2 SD (thinness). APPETITE TEST: passed if child (not fed for 2 hours), fed by mother in quiet place, consumes in 1 hour: 7-12 months >=25 ml/kg of F100; >12 months of locally prepared ready to eat food.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: Hemogram, RBS, LFT, KFT, Chest X-Ray, RDT-HIV, Gastric aspirate for CBNAAT/AFB. DESIRABLE: ECG, Stool pH, Stool microscopic, Urine culture, Serum electrolytes (Na, K, Cal), Serum B12, Serum Folate levels. OPTIONAL: Blood Culture, Blood gases, Ultrasound (inferior vena cava to ascending aorta ratio).
drugs: STABILISATION PHASE intake: IVF (DNS) 4 ml/kg/hr for 2-3 days with early/concomitant oral feeds (130 ml/kg/day). INFECTIONS (empirically): Inj Ampicillin 50 mg/kg IV/IM 6hrly + Inj Gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg IV/IM OD for 7-10 days; if no response in 48 hrs or critically ill - Inj Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg OD for 7-10 days; when oral - amoxicillin 40-45 mg/kg/dose twice a day for 7 days; prolonged diarrhea (>7 days) - Metronidazole 10-12 mg/kg 8 hrly for 7-10 days. HYPOGLYCEMIA (RBS <54mg/dL): conscious - 50 ml of 10% Dextrose or 1 tsf sugar in 3 tsf water orally; unconscious - 5 ml/kg of 10% Dextrose IV. HYPOTHERMIA (<35.5C/96F): skin to skin care, warming under warmer/lamp. ELECTROLYTE IMBALANCE: Potassium 3-4 mmol/kg/D orally for 2 wks; Magnesium 0.4-0.6 mmol/kg/D IM then oral for 2 wks. ANEMIA: Whole blood/PRBC transfusion (10 ml/kg over 3 hrs) if Hb <4 gm/dL or Hb 4-6.5 gm/dL with respiratory distress; Furosemide 1 mg/kg at start of transfusion. REHABILITATION - Feeding: 6 months+ F75 at least 5 times/day increasing to 150-200 kCal/kg/day (2-3 days) then F100 for next 5-7 days with home food; <6 months same with return to exclusive breastfeeding. Zinc 2 mg/kg/day x 2wks; Copper 0.3 mg/kg/day x 2 wks; Iron 3 mg/kg/day once weight gain started for 6 weeks. Vitamin A: >12 months 2 lac iu, 6-12 months 1 lac iu, <6 months 0.5 lac iu (if food not fortified); Vitamin D, A, B Complex RDA.
management: A. STABILISATION PHASE: Monitor vitals, urine frequency, stool/vomitus volumes; treat infections, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, severe dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, anemia (see Drugs). B. REHABILITATION PHASE (transfer to NRC when meets discharge criteria & accepts home foods): feeding (F75 then F100), electrolytes (zinc, copper, iron), vitamins. F75/F100 PREPARATION: F75 - Fresh whole cream milk 300 ml, Sugar 100 gm, Vegetable oil 20 ml, water to 1 Litre. F100 - milk 900 ml, Sugar 75 gm, oil 20 ml, water to 1 Litre.
referralRedFlags: Hospitalize SAM with complications/poor appetite/failed home treatment. CRITERIA FOR DISCHARGE from hospital to outpatient care: Clinically well and alert; no or resolving medical complications; no or resolving oedema; satisfactory oral intake (at least 75% target calorie intake of 150-200 kcal/kg/day & 0-6 months old have weight gain of 3-5 gm/kg/day for three days).
algorithmNotes: Abbrev: WHZ=Weight for Height Z-score; MUAC=Mid-upper Arm Circumference; SD=Standard Deviation; SAM=Severe Acute Malnutrition; MAM=Moderate Acute Malnutrition; BMI=Body Mass Index; NRC=Nutritional Rehabilitation Centre; CBNAAT=Cartridge Based Nucleic Acid Amplification Test.

whenToSuspect: PRESENTING SYMPTOMS: Cough, cold, with or without fever, difficulty in breathing (fast breathing and chest indrawing) of less than 2 weeks duration. GENERAL DANGER SIGNS: Not able to drink, persistent vomiting, convulsions, lethargic or unconscious, stridor in a calm child or severe malnutrition. Suspicion of foreign body aspiration: sudden onset choking and breathlessness. EXAMINATION: chest indrawing, alertness, cyanosis, clubbing, stridor, grunting; vital signs (RR, HR, temperature, CRT, pulse oximetry); nutritional status (for SAM); auscultation (breath sounds, crepitation, rhonchi).
risk: CHECK HISTORY: similar illness in past (improvement with nebulized medications - consider asthma); asthma/allergy in family; influenza like illness in family over past 2 weeks (consider influenza); immunization (Pneumococcus, Influenza). When to suspect H1N1: child with cold/cough/fever with similar illness in family - start Oseltamivir. When to suspect acute bronchiolitis: child below 2 years, first episode severe pneumonia with predominant wheezing. When to suspect asthma: child >3 years with recurrent cough/cold/wheezing, good response to bronchodilator, personal/family history of asthma. When to suspect chronic respiratory problem: severe malnutrition, clubbing, feeding difficulty, family history of sibling death due to pneumonia, multi site infections.
scores: DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM (age 2-59 months, cough/difficult breathing): NO PNEUMONIA (cough/cold, no breathing difficulty) -> home care. PNEUMONIA (fast breathing 2-12m >50, 1-5y >40, >5y >20 and/or chest indrawing, SpO2 >92%) -> ambulatory oral Amoxicillin + follow up. SEVERE PNEUMONIA (fast breathing and/or chest indrawing with any general danger sign) -> admit/refer. RED FLAG SIGNS: irregular or gasping respiration, cold extremities, altered sensorium, cyanosis.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: Hemogram, random blood sugar, CRP, chest X-ray. DESIRABLE: Blood culture, pleural tap, serum electrolytes, renal and liver function tests. OPTIONAL: ABG, lung ultrasound, PCT, tracheal aspirate (gram stain with culture), bronchoscopy/BAL, microbiology culture, investigations for atypical organisms, PCR for viral etiology.
drugs: OXYGEN INHALATION: by mask (1-2 L/min) or hood (4-6 L/Minute) to maintain oxygen saturation >95%. IV ANTIBIOTICS: children 2-59 months - Ampicillin 100-200mg/kg in four divided doses + Gentamicin 5-7.5 mg/kg single dose daily; children >5 years - Ampicillin/Amoxicillin, add macrolide (Azythromycin/Erythromycin) if atypical pneumonia suspected; suspected Staphylococcal pneumonia (pneumatocele on CXR, post measles, infected scabies or pyoderma) add Cloxacillin/Amoxiclavulanic acid. First line=Ampicillin; Alternate first line=First gen Cephalosporins; Second line=Amoxiclav/Cefuroxime/Cefotaxime/Ceftriaxone. SUPPORTIVE: Paracetamol for fever, IV fluid, bronchodilators (inhaled) as needed. SWITCH TO ORAL: when child afebrile, RR below age cutoffs, no chest indrawing, accepting orally - switch to oral Amoxicillin to complete total of 5-7 days (include IV duration); if on Cloxacillin/Amoxiclav continue oral for 2 weeks. H1N1: Oseltamivir per national guideline.
management: NO PNEUMONIA -> home care advice. PNEUMONIA -> ambulatory oral Amoxicillin & follow up. SEVERE PNEUMONIA no red flag -> admit/refer with oxygen by mask/hood, pulse oxymeter, IV fluids, oxygen, clinical supervision, X ray; Red flag positive -> admit/refer with ventilation facility, ICU, round the clock monitoring (if plan to refer give first dose of antibiotics, arrange transport, inform referral centre). COMPLICATIONS: Non responders (48-72 hours - change antimicrobials); Pleural effusion (diagnostic aspiration); Empyema (drainage with ICD); Lung abscess (antibiotics 4-6 weeks); Pneumothorax (intercostal drainage); Respiratory failure (consider ventilation); If associated SAM follow SAM guidelines.
referralRedFlags: RED FLAG SIGNS: Irregular or gasping respiration, cold extremities, altered sensorium, cyanosis. WHEN TO REFER TO HIGHER CENTERS: facilities not available for treatment/complications; suspecting chronic respiratory problems. DISCHARGE: when child switched to oral medications and accepting oral for 24 to 48 hours.
algorithmNotes: Fast breathing cutoffs: 2-12 months >50/min; 1-5 years >40/min; >5 years >20/min. ICD=Intercostal Drainage; BAL=Bronchoalveolar Lavage; CXR=Chest X-Ray; SAM=Severe Acute Malnutrition.

whenToSuspect: Universal screening for every patient attending any healthcare facility (AUDIT can be used for screening). Special attention to: H/o head injury; appearing under influence of alcohol; H/o impaired social/occupational functioning; daily alcohol consumption; drinking in large quantities (men: 5+ drinks/day; women: 4+ drinks/day). EXAMINATION: Vitals (BP, Pulse Rate, Temperature); Withdrawal signs (Tremor, Sweating, Tachycardia); Signs of hepatic dysfunction (Enlarged liver, Icterus, Abdominal swelling); Neurological signs (Cerebellar signs, Peripheral neuropathy, Confusion).
risk: ASSESSMENT (detailed history): age at initiation, quantity, frequency, progression (daily/morning drinking); time of last use; binge drinking (men 5 drinks/2 hours, women 4 drinks/2 hours); withdrawal state (insomnia, restlessness, anxiety, tremors); tolerance; craving; difficulty controlling; preoccupation; continued use despite harm; past abstinence/relapse; co-morbid illness; complications (gastritis, peripheral neuropathy, hepatic dysfunction, accidents; psychosocial - loss of work, fights, financial, legal).
scores: DIAGNOSIS: Hazardous/Harmful use (risky behaviours, harmful physical/psychosocial consequences). Alcohol dependence (THREE of six criteria for at least one month): 1) strong desire/compulsion; 2) difficulty controlling use; 3) withdrawal state or use to relieve withdrawal; 4) tolerance; 5) preoccupation; 6) persisting use despite harm. INDICATIONS FOR ADMISSION: Failure of outpatient treatment; H/o withdrawal seizures/delirium tremens; Co-morbid significant medical/psychiatric illness; Poly-substance use.
investigations: CBC; Liver function test; Blood sugar; Electrolytes; CT head (in case of seizure/delirium tremens).
drugs: WITHDRAWAL MANAGEMENT: Tab Diazepam 20-40mg/day in divided doses based on severity; monitor and titrate; if comfortable reduce dose by 10-20% per day, taper within 7 to 10 days; Thiamine 100 mg OD; significant liver dysfunction - Lorazepam (2 mg Lorazepam equal to 5 mg Diazepam). RELAPSE PREVENTION: Disulfiram 250 mg OD (pre-requisites: motivated patient, written consent, family supervision; inform about disulfiram-alcohol reaction - flushing, headache, vomiting, reduced BP, arrhythmias). Secondary care: withdrawal seizure - inpatient management with benzodiazepines (diazepam or lorazepam), frequent titration, higher dosage may be required. Tertiary: delirium tremens (R/o head injury, hepatic/Wernicke's encephalopathy, other causes; manage like withdrawal seizures; ICU when indicated).
management: PRIMARY CARE: Hazardous/Harmful users - Brief Intervention (FRAMES: Feedback, Responsibility, Advice, Menu of options, Empathetic attitude, Self efficacy) to reduce/stop; Dependent users - advice to stop & motivate using Brief intervention. SECONDARY CARE: treatment of withdrawal symptoms; management of withdrawal seizure (benzodiazepines, monitoring, nursing); treat additional psychiatric/substance use disorder. TERTIARY CARE: treatment of delirium tremens; consult other specialists (gastroenterology/medicine for hematemesis); management for suicidality or violence.
referralRedFlags: REFER TO SECONDARY CARE IF: H/o withdrawal seizures/hallucinations; additional psychiatric disorder; recurrent failed attempts at treatment. REFER TO TERTIARY CARE IF: H/o delirium tremens; major medical problems; additional substance use.
algorithmNotes: AUDIT=Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test; FRAMES=brief intervention framework; Brief intervention = open-ended, non-judgmental.

whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: Tension, anxiety, apprehension, fear, worrying; Sadness, lack of interest, uncontrolled negative thoughts; Unexplained physical symptoms (chest pain, abdominal pain, muscular tension, headache, nausea); Episodes of palpitations, difficulty breathing, feelings of choking, light headedness, dizziness, fainting, trembling; Attacks of fear, losing control or going crazy, fear of dying; Repetitive unwanted thoughts and behaviours.
risk: ASSESSMENT: duration of anxiety; degree of distress and impairment of day-to-day functioning; symptoms of depression; substance and alcohol misuse; physical disorders (thyrotoxicosis, pheochromocytoma, hypoglycaemia); psychosocial factors (ongoing stress, work, family).
scores: DIAGNOSIS: Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD - chronic tension/apprehension/worry about everyday routine); Panic Disorder (recurrent unexpected attacks of intense fear with physical symptoms); Social Phobia (fear/avoidance of social situations); Agoraphobia (fear of going out alone, enclosed/open spaces, public transport); Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD - recurrent unwanted thoughts and repetitive behaviours: washing/cleaning, checking, ordering).
investigations: Few investigations like ECG, ECHO may be necessary in some patients to exclude physical illness (do not investigate excessively). Rule out thyrotoxicosis, pheochromocytoma, hypoglycaemia.
drugs: PRIMARY: Mild - psychoeducation, may not need medications. No improvement (few weeks) - Escitalopram 5mg/day at night, increase to 10 mg/d in a week, if no improvement in 4-6 weeks increase to 20 mg/day, if no improvement in another 4-6 weeks refer. Severe/unbearable anxiety - Diazepam 5-10 mg at night (do not continue >1 month, taper and stop over 2 weeks). Escitalopram continued at least 1-2 years after remission; monitor side-effects (sexual dysfunction, sedation, weight gain). SECONDARY: Second SSRI for 2-3 months - Sertraline upto 200 mg/day, Fluoxetine upto 60 mg/day, Paroxetine upto 50 mg/day, Fluvoxamine upto 300 mg/day; if no response add cognitive behaviour therapy (CBT). TERTIARY: OCD - trial of third SSRI or clomipramine; other anxiety disorders - non-SSRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine, pregabalin) and tricyclic antidepressants; pharmacological augmenting strategies.
management: PRIMARY CARE: Psychoeducation (reassurance, explain symptoms, do not investigate excessively, discourage doctor shopping, do not avoid triggers, emphasize avoidance maintains fears, OCD education); pharmacological (Escitalopram). SECONDARY CARE (District Hospital): review diagnosis & treatment history; check adherence; second SSRI; CBT if available; refer to tertiary if unsatisfactory after second SSRI +/- CBT. TERTIARY CENTRE: evaluate treatment resistance (wrong diagnosis, inadequate drug/CBT, poor adherence, comorbidity); panic disorder - exclude mimicking medical conditions; treatment resistant OCD - inpatient intensive CBT; CBT preferred mode alone or with medications.
referralRedFlags: Refer to specialist if no significant improvement after adequate Escitalopram trial (4-6 weeks + 4-6 weeks). Refer to tertiary if unsatisfactory response after second SSRI and/or addition of CBT.
algorithmNotes: SSRI=Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitor; CBT=Cognitive Behaviour Therapy; GAD=Generalized Anxiety Disorder; OCD=Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder.

whenToSuspect: CLINICAL PRESENTATION: Oppositional Defiant Disorder (ODD - doesn't obey/listen, back-answers, rude; demanding, stubborn, throws tantrums when demands not met); Conduct Disorder (aggressive - angry/abusive/fights/hits/bullies/damages articles; stealing, lying, threatening, truant, runs away from home, bad company, cruelty to animals); ADHD (restless/always on move/can't sit/talkative; can't focus/poor concentration/easily distracted/disorganized/doesn't complete school work; mischievous/troubles people; impatient/can't wait for turn/acts without thinking). All 3: poor/erratic school performance and/or complaints about behaviour from school.
risk: DIAGNOSIS: Symptoms present and persistent over several months; classify into ADHD, ODD and CD (ADHD may be present with or without ODD and CD). CAUTION: Abrupt onset; recent onset (few weeks to few months); sudden increase in severity (consider another psychiatric disorder such as bipolar affective disorder, hypomanic or manic episode - follow relevant STW). REASONS FOR REFERRAL: severe complicated presentation; lack of response to treatment; severe aggression; highly dysfunctional family; alcohol and substance abuse.
scores: Assessment (history from multiple sources): Parent interview; Family situation; Schooling; Child interview. Secondary care uses Rutter's multi-axial system for reassessment.
investigations: Primarily clinical assessment (history from multiple sources: parent interview, family situation, schooling, child interview). No specific lab investigation listed.
drugs: MEDICATION (AVOID BEFORE 5 YEARS): Severe and persistent aggression - T. Risperidone under close supervision (starting dose 0.25 mg single daily morning dose after breakfast; increase by 0.25 mg weekly up to 1 mg single daily dose; not to exceed 1 mg/day; Response+ continue 3 months then slow taper; Response- 4 weeks trial then refer; monitor weight gain, extra-pyramidal symptoms EPS [if EPS add 1 mg Trihexyphenidyl OD morning]). Severe hyperactivity & impulsivity - T. Clonidine (starting dose 25 ug single daily dose before sleep, increase by 25 ug weekly up to 100 ug/day in 2-3 divided doses; monitor BP and drowsiness; advise against sudden discontinuation). SECONDARY: if failed Clonidine/moderate ADHD - T. Atomoxetine (start 10 mg single daily morning dose after breakfast, increase up to 1mg/kg/day under close supervision). TERTIARY: trial of Methylphenidate in moderate/severe ADHD under expert supervision; severe aggression/impulsivity - Risperidone and/or Lithium.
management: Work with FAMILY (psychoeducation - behaviours not intentional, not child's fault; parent management training - star-charting/contingency management, clear routines, time-out, praise; Dos: consistency, catch child being good, ignore negative behaviours; Donts: bribe, false promises/threats, harsh punishments, unfair comparison). Work with CHILD (avoid advice; anger management - count 10 to 1 backwards, move away, deep breaths, self-talk; ADHD - stop-think-act/halt-and-proceed). Work with SCHOOL (feedback, extra attention/support, extra coaching). SECONDARY CARE: review/reassess diagnosis (Rutter's multi-axial), Atomoxetine, systematic parent management/individual therapy. TERTIARY: multi-modal management, Methylphenidate, treat comorbid (bipolar, substance use), family therapy.
referralRedFlags: REASONS FOR REFERRAL: Severe complicated presentation; Lack of response to treatment; Severe aggression; Highly dysfunctional family; Alcohol and substance abuse.
algorithmNotes: ODD=Oppositional Defiant Disorder; CD=Conduct Disorder; ADHD=Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder; EPS=Extra-Pyramidal Symptoms.

whenToSuspect: CLINICAL PRESENTATION (recent onset behavioral changes). SOMATIC (physically unexplained) SYMPTOMS: weakness/tiredness; aches and pains; headache; non-epileptic attacks of fainting; chest pain and stomach pain; hyperventilation triggered by stress/distress. SYMPTOMS OF DEPRESSION: loss of interest in usual activities; recent deterioration in school performance; wanting to be alone/withdrawn; looks unhappy/off mood/crying; decreased sleep, loss of appetite, weight loss; talking about death and dying, self harm (self-cutting) or suicidal attempt. SYMPTOMS OF ANXIETY: always worrying/tense; exam/performance anxiety; excessive fear/avoidance; reluctance/refusal to go to school; very shy/avoids social situations; clinging to mother/scared of separation.
risk: DIAGNOSIS: Persistent emotional disturbance for several weeks significantly affecting child's life; unexplained by medical condition (hypothyroidism); depression and anxiety can co-occur; depression more common in adolescents. CAUTION: assessment of suicidal risk and plan of action important especially in depression; elicit h/o hypomania/mania in moderate-severe depression (consider bipolar disorder); physical conditions can cause similar symptoms (anemia, thyroid disturbance).
scores: Assessment: Parent interview & history; Child interview; Physical examination to rule out post-viral syndrome, recurrent malaria, chronic infections/physical illness, anaemia, PCOD or thyroid disturbance. Secondary care reassesses using Rutter's multi-axial system.
investigations: Physical examination to RULE OUT: post-viral syndrome; recurrent attacks of malaria; chronic infections/chronic physical illness; anaemia; PCOD or thyroid disturbance.
drugs: MEDICATION (moderate case of depression or anxiety in adolescents): Tab Fluoxetine - start at 10 mg OD morning, increase to 20 mg OD after 2 weeks depending on response; inform adverse effects (behavioral activation - marked restlessness/irritability, onset of hypomanic symptoms, worsening of suicidal ideas; stop drug if troublesome); avoid benzodiazepines (except as temporary measure for few weeks in severe anxiety/panic attacks - Clonazepam 0.25-1 mg/day).
management: Work with PARENTS (psychoeducation - child emotionally disturbed and not able to function, not child's fault; avoid criticism/over expectation/scolding; counsel about suicidal risk; manage mental health issues in parents). Work with CHILD (psycho-education; anxiety management & emotional regulation - muscle relaxation, deep breathing, pranayama/yoga, substituting distressing thoughts; counsel to confide distressing thoughts including death/dying; gradual return to usual life). Work with SCHOOL (feedback; graded return to school if school refusal). SECONDARY CARE: review/reassess (Rutter's multi-axial); individual therapy (negative thoughts, anxiety management, coping); parent counselling. TERTIARY: manage severe mental disorders (psychoses, recurrent mood disorders, severe depression, persistent suicidality, recurrent self-cutting); CBT for older children with severe OCD/depression/anxiety; ECT on case basis (severe depression, mania, psychosis, catatonia unresponsive to pharmacological management); psycho-social steps if abuse/maltreatment/neglect.
referralRedFlags: REASONS FOR REFERRAL: Frequent expression of suicidal ideation/attempted suicide/self-harm (self-cutting); Severe symptoms; Complicated picture or features of obsessive compulsive disorder (OCD); No response to interventions in 4-6 weeks.
algorithmNotes: PCOD=Polycystic Ovarian Disease; OCD=Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder; ECT=Electroconvulsive Therapy; CBT=Cognitive Behaviour Therapy. Suicidal risk assessment essential.

whenToSuspect: CLINICAL PRESENTATION: Slow in development / delayed milestones of development; Poor speech, self-care, learning and memory; Less intelligent compared to other children. DIAGNOSIS - distinguish: Intellectual disability (mental retardation) - global developmental delay + impairment compared to peers in intellectual functioning and adaptive skills; Specific Learning Disorder (SLD) - Average intelligence with poor academic skills (reading, writing, spelling and maths) in spite of adequate schooling; Specific speech delay - Only lagging in speech with normal intelligence, hearing and non-verbal communication; Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) - marked impairment in verbal and non-verbal social interactions (being solitary or "in his own world", poor response to name call and poor eye-to-eye contact) and stereotyped behaviors. *In children under 5 years, avoid diagnosis of ID, name it as global developmental delay and start intervention.
risk: BEHAVIOURAL PROBLEMS: Hyperactive; Impulsive behaviors; Sleeping and feeding problems; Aggression. EMOTIONAL PROBLEMS: Excessive crying; Irritability; Shyness and fears. OTHERS: Family situation; Parents' awareness of the child's problems; Quality of attention and care being given to the child; Past consultations and treatment educational history.
scores: DETAILED DEVELOPMENTAL ASSESSMENT: Assess if child is lagging behind in developmental attainments compared to same-age children; Ask mother to estimate the mental age of child; Ascertain if delay is global (all milestones) or restricted to one area (motor or speech).
investigations: PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: Height and weight; Head circumference; Vision and hearing; Any noticeable physical anomalies (club-foot) or unusual facial appearance; Motor abnormalities (stiffness/spasticity or weakness of limbs, unsteady gait); Any other problems (heart murmurs, organomegaly). TERTIARY: Investigate the cause - imaging, genetic tests, metabolic tests (as per requirement); arrange for genetic counselling.
