drugs: No medication can improve intelligence. Treatment of associated problems (vitamin or mineral deficiency or epilepsy, ADHD, vision/hearing issues) - refer to appropriate STW. Manage treatable disorders (like hypothyroidism and inherited metabolic disorders) and comorbid physical health problems (epilepsy, visual/hearing impairment, locomotor/orthopaedic problems).
management: PSYCHO-EDUCATION OF PARENTS: Normal - reassure parents; Mild delay ("at risk") - early intervention and follow-up; Explain causation; Teaching and training to improve skills and gaining independence; Systematic, persistent and repetitive training as per child's ability; Avoid overprotection, overindulgence and understimulation. EARLY INTERVENTION / SENSORY-MOTOR STIMULATION (UNDER 3 YEARS): Create opportunities to learn with interest and attention; Engage child to use eyes and ears (sounds, sights, touch, movements) and improve hand functions; Use play materials; Parallel vocalization to improve utterances; Improve conceptual skills (classifying, arranging, sorting, naming). HOME-BASED PARENT MEDIATED SKILLS TRAINING: Develop regular stimulating daily routines; Teach parent to teach child (imitation, pointing, pretend-play, self-help skills, household chores, social skills, vocational skills, self-protection); Find current level of adaptive abilities and choose a target skill; Modelling, breaking tasks into simple steps, praising improvements, hand-on-hand techniques. EDUCATION AND TRAINING: Liaise with schools; Assist enrolment to special school; Vocational skills for older adolescents. SOCIAL WELFARE: IQ testing and certification for social welfare benefits; Link parents with CBR programs or parent associations. SECONDARY CARE (DISTRICT HOSPITAL): Psychological testing for ID, SLD and diagnosis of ASD; Basic management of ASD; Appropriate management of behavior problems; Help parents access DEIC's, parent organizations, and benefits. TERTIARY CARE: Evaluate and manage severe IDD, ASD, multiple disabilities, severe comorbid disorders (ADHD, aggression, bipolar, psychotic) via multi-disciplinary approach.
referralRedFlags: REASONS FOR REFERRAL: Severe or multiple developmental problems; History of regression (loss of acquired skills); Definite family history of developmental problems (h/o similar problem in sibling); Co-occuring severe behavioral or emotional problems; Suspected case of ASD; Suspected SLD; Genetic counselling; Speech therapy or physiotherapy.
algorithmNotes: ID=Intellectual Disability; SLD=Specific Learning Disorder; ASD=Autism Spectrum Disorder; IDD=Intellectual Developmental Disorder; ADHD=Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder; DEIC=District Early Intervention Centre; CBR=Community Based Rehabilitation. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: CORE SYMPTOMS: 1. Depressed mood; 2. Loss of interest and enjoyment; 3. Easy fatigability/diminished activity. ADDITIONAL SYMPTOMS: Reduced concentration and attention; Reduced self-esteem and self-confidence; Ideas of guilt and unworthiness; Bleak and pessimistic views of the future; Ideas or acts of self-harm or suicide; Disturbed sleep; Diminished appetite. To make a diagnosis of depression, symptoms must be present for at least 2 weeks.
risk: CLINICAL ASSESSMENT: Cognition - Hopelessness (about future), Helplessness (about others), Worthlessness (about self). Assessment of Suicide Risk - Suicidal thoughts, Suicidal idea, Suicidal intent, Immediate risk for attempt; Assess friend and family support. Rule out Bipolar Disorder / Grief / Adjustment Disorder.
scores: SEVERITY OF DEPRESSION (by core + additional symptoms): Mild depression = 2 core symptoms + 2 or more additional; Moderate depression = 2 core + 3 or more additional; Severe depression = 3 core + 4 or more additional.
investigations: INVESTIGATION: Haemogram; Thyroid function tests; Electro Cardiogram; Electrolytes (Sodium); Rule out secondary medical cause of depression like Hypothyroidism; Rule out use of anticancer drugs (Cyclophoshamide)/anti retroviral drugs (Efavirenz, Zidovudine)/Antibiotics (Dapsone, Ethambutol)/Anabolic Steroids/Propanolol; Rule out associated comorbid medical condition - Diabetes, Stroke, Epilepsy, Cancer, Coronary Artery Disease and Auto Immune disorder.
drugs: MODERATE/SEVERE DEPRESSION: Tab Escitalopram 10 mg-20 mg/day or Cap. Fluoxetine 20 mg-40 mg/day; Tab. Clonazepam 0.25 mg-0.5 mg/day for sleep disturbance/anxiety symptoms (consider taper and stop after 2 weeks); If patient responds to SSRI in 2 to 4 weeks, then continue treatment for 6 to 9 months and taper and stop. BROAD MANAGEMENT: SSRI usually first choice (watch for GI bleed and drug interaction); Improvement starts in 2nd week, adequate response by 6 weeks; Treatment typically lasts 6-9 months, gradual tapering for first episode; Non Responder at secondary - Switch over to SNRI (Venlafaxine 75-150 mg, Mirtazapine 30 mg) or TCA (Amitriptyline 75-225 mg/Imipramine 75-225 mg); Watch for risk of overdose with TCA (Amitriptyline/Imipramine) and Mirtazapine. SPECIAL POPULATION: Pregnancy/Lactation - preferred drug Tab. Sertraline 50 mg (lowest possible dose); Elderly - Tab. Escitalopram 10-20 mg or Tab. Sertraline 100 mg (monitor for hyponatremia), avoid TCAs like Amitriptyline/Imipramine (anticholinergic side effects); Adolescents - Cap. Fluoxetine 20-40 mg/day (observe for switch/activation/suicidality). TERTIARY: Partial Responder - Optimise SNRI/TCA or Augment with Tab. Lithium 300 to 600 mg/per day or Tab. Thyroxine 25-50 ug per day; Non Responder - Add Tab. Sertraline 100 mg or Tab. Bupropion 300 mg to existing Venlafaxine 150 mg/Tab. Mirtazapine 30 mg/Amitriptyline 225 mg/Imipramine 225 mg; Add on low dose antipsychotic (Risperidone 2-4 mg/Tab. Olanzapine 5-10 mg) for psychotic symptoms.
management: AT PRIMARY CARE (MILD DEPRESSION): Advise Behavioral Activation; Practicing activity monitoring (write down activities/rate depression/schedule activities that make you feel good/to do list/clear goals); Focus on value categories (family, friends, goals at work, contribute to community); Recommend yoga & meditation; Handling daily task (monitor sleep/diet, good personal hygiene); Supportive psychotherapy/Brief Counselling; Validate problems and ensure frequent follow-up; If no improvement in 4 to 6 weeks, consider pharmacotherapy. AT SECONDARY CARE: Confirm Diagnosis and Suicide risk assessment; Assess for other Medical Comorbidities; Investigations - Haemoglobin, Thyroid Function Test, Electrocardiogram; Non Responder - Switch over to SNRI or TCA; Cognitive Behavioral Therapy/Problem Solving Therapy; Add on Yoga Therapy/Meditation. AT TERTIARY CARE: Reconfirm Diagnosis; Assess other psychiatric comorbidities; Add on Electro Convulsive Therapy for Catatonia/Suicidality; Add on Cognitive Behavioural Therapy/Inter Personal Therapy/Problem Solving Therapy.
referralRedFlags: REFERRAL TO SECONDARY CARE: Difficulty in making diagnosis; No improvement after 4 to 6 weeks of treatment with first line medications; Depression in special population (Elderly/Pregnancy/Lactation/Children/Adolescents); Comorbid medical illness/Substance use; Suicidal risk assessment. REFERRAL TO TERTIARY CARE: No improvement in 2nd line treatment; Immediate risk for suicidal attempt/thought; Needing intense counselling/psychotherapy; Co Morbid Substance - Cannabis/Poly substance.
algorithmNotes: SSRI=Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors; SNRI=Serotonin Norepinephrine Reuptake Inhibitor; TCA=Tricyclic Antidepressant. Note ICD10 printed as F45 on STW infographic. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: DIAGNOSIS: Presence of delusions (suspiciousness and fear without obvious explanation); Hallucinations (talking, smiling or laughing to self); Disorganized behaviour & poor self-care; Social and occupational dysfunction.
risk: REASONS FOR REFERRAL TO TALUK/DISTRICT LEVEL: Diagnostic confusion/suspicion of organic condition; Substantial risk of harm to self or others and catatonic symptoms; Comorbid substance use, depression/anxiety, intellectual disability; Poor symptom-control or functioning despite regular treatment or poor treatment adherence; Significant adverse effects (weight-gain, metabolic adverse effects, tardive dyskinesia); Questions regarding marriage, pregnancy, sexual dysfunction.
scores: INDICATION FOR REFERRAL FROM PHC to secondary care: Diagnostic confusion; Poor response to Risperidone; Intolerance to Risperidone; Poor adherence to treatment; Comorbid conditions; Challenging situations; Rehabilitation needs; Pregnancy. CONSIDER ELECTROCONVULSIVE THERAPY IF: 1. Serious suicidal risk; 2. Catatonia not responding to Lorazepam; 3. Refusal of food/fluids; 4. Severe aggression/violence; 5. Refractory/intolerance to clozapine.
investigations: TERTIARY - Diagnostic confusion: Inpatient observation for clarification of history, thorough neurological/mental status examination, diagnostic psychometry, brain CT Scan or MRI, neurology consultation and urine toxicology screen. Secondary - neuroimaging if organicity is suspected.
drugs: INITIATE TREATMENT (Primary care): T. Risperidone 2 mg HS x1 week f/b 3-4 mg HS + Trihexyphenidyl (THP) 2 mg (morning). FOLLOW UP: 2 weeks after initial contact - check changes in symptoms and adverse effects (excess sleep, extrapyramidal symptoms (EPS), tiredness), adjust dose of risperidone and THP; Once in 1-2 months - check symptoms, functioning and adverse effects (EPS, weight-gain, menstrual/sexual dysfunction), adjust dose of Risperidone (range: 2-8 mg/day) and THP (range 2-6 mg/day). ALGORITHM FOR CHOOSING ANTIPSYCHOTIC: Risperidone* -> Good response without intolerable side effects -> Stabilization phase (6 months: same dose of AP), Maintenance phase (reduce to minimum possible dose; 1st episode: about 2 years; 1+ episodes: indefinite period); Intolerable side effects or inadequate effect -> Choose different AP -> still inadequate for 2+ APs -> Investigate for medical/neurological causes and treat them -> No cause found or poor response despite treatment -> CLOZAPINE.
management: PRIMARY CARE - WELLNESS CENTERS: Identify, educate and refer to PHC; If immediate threat to self/others, refer to Taluk/District center; Follow-up and rehabilitation (monitor & manage challenges, liaise with higher centers, liaise with social welfare for disability certification). PHC: Initiate treatment, psychoeducation (medical model of psychosis, address misconceptions & build hope, inform about possible adverse effects). SECONDARY CARE (TALUK/DISTRICT HOSPITALS): Management by indication for referral - Diagnostic confusion (clarify diagnosis, neuroimaging); Poor response to Risperidone (positive symptoms follow algorithm; negative symptoms rule out/manage depression/anxiety/extrapyramidal, family counseling, consider less-sedating antipsychotics and adding SSRIs); Poor adherence (assess factors, consider depot antipsychotics); Comorbid conditions (depression/anxiety - brief psychological intervention, consider SSRIs; substance use - detoxification, see SUD module; developmental disabilities - behavioral); Challenging situations (suicidality - inpatient care, crisis management, ECT; violence - verbal de-escalation, IV sedation, brief inpatient care); Rehabilitation needs; Pregnancy (proactively address sexual/endocrine problems, educate about obstetric outcomes/relapse/psychosis risk). TERTIARY CARE CENTERS interventions: Psychoeducation (poor adherence; high family expressed emotions); Family therapy; Cognitive remediation; Cognitive behavior therapy; Social skills training; Vocational rehabilitation and supported education; Day care; Interventions for substance-use; Pregnancy-puerperium services.
referralRedFlags: REFERRAL TO TERTIARY CARE IF: 1. Diagnostic confusion (inpatient observation, brain CT/MRI, urine toxicology); 2. Poor outcome (psychosocial interventions in isolation or combination depending on context - inpatient, outpatient or day-boarding). At any point consider electroconvulsive therapy if: serious suicidal risk; catatonia not responding to Lorazepam; refusal of food/fluids; severe aggression/violence; refractory/intolerance to clozapine.
algorithmNotes: THP=Trihexyphenidyl; EPS=Extrapyramidal Symptoms; AP=Antipsychotic; ECT=Electroconvulsive Therapy; SSRI=Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitor; SUD=Substance Use Disorder. *Treat with adequate dose for 6-8 weeks to assess efficacy; #while choosing alternative AP consider adverse effect profile and cost; ^at any point if medication non-adherence, consider long-acting preparations. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: SOMATIZATION DISORDER / MEDICALLY UNEXPLAINED SYMPTOMS: Repeated subjective experience of physical symptoms which are not explained by any recognizable physical disease; Bodily complaints are often the most common reason for consultation with health professionals. PRESENTATION TO A DOCTOR: Pain at multiple sites; Fatigue and exhaustion; Significant distress and impairment; Functional disturbance of different organ systems; Focus of patient is on symptom relief rather than cause of illness. CHALLENGES FACED BY THE DOCTOR: Repeated request for investigations - despite negative findings and reassurance; Doctor shopping and not satisfied with repeated reassurance.
risk: PSYCHOSOCIAL ASSESSMENT: Encourage to talk about psychosocial stressors if any; Individual factors - poor copying skills, anxiety, life events, health anxiety, medical illnesses; Family related factors - Substance use in family, interpersonal relationship with family, financial status; Environmental factors - support system, peer relationship, work environment.
scores: DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA: A. One or more somatic symptoms that are distressing or result in significant disruption of daily life. B. Excessive thoughts, feelings, or behaviours related to the somatic symptoms or associated health concerns as manifested by at least one of: 1. Disproportionate and persistent thoughts about the seriousness of one's symptoms; 2. Persistently high level of anxiety about health or symptoms; 3. Excessive time and energy devoted to these symptoms or health concerns. C. Although only one somatic symptom may not be continuously present, the state of being symptomatic is persistent (typically more than 6 months). SEVERITY: Mild - only one of the symptoms specified in criterion B is fulfilled; Moderate - Two or more of the symptoms in criterion B fulfilled; Severe - Two or more of criterion B fulfilled, plus multiple somatic symptoms (or one very severe somatic symptom).
investigations: INITIAL ASSESSMENT: Detailed clinical examination - to rule out any medical illnesses which might explain the symptoms; Complete history of the onset of all symptoms, exacerbating and relieving factors; Assessment for any other psychiatric illness such as depression or anxiety disorders. COMMONEST SYMPTOMS: 1. Pain symptoms at multiple sites (abdominal, back, chest, dysmenorrhea, dysuria, extremity, head, joint, rectal); 2. Gastrointestinal sensations (pain, belching, regurgitation, vomiting, nausea); 3. Abnormal skin sensations (itching, burning, tingling, numbness, soreness) and blotchiness; 4. Sexual and menstrual complaints (ejaculatory or erectile dysfunction, hyperemesis of pregnancy, irregular menses, menorrhagia, sexual indifference). SECONDARY CARE: Investigations to rule out any medical illnesses that might explain the symptoms.
drugs: PRIMARY CARE: Low dose of antidepressant medications - Amitriptyline 12.5 mg to 50 mg (max) night dose; Explain that onset of medication effect will take 2-3 weeks. SECONDARY CARE: Use 2nd line medications - SSRIs (Escitalopram 10-20 mg, Sertraline 50-100 mg, Fluoxetine 20 mg) and SNRIs (Venlafaxine 75-150 mg, Duloxetine 30-60 mg); Combination of two psychotropic medications (might be required if poor response to single medication). TERTIARY CARE: Combination of two psychotropic medications (when required); Add on second and third line medications - Duloxetine, Mirtazapine, anticonvulsants (Lamotrigine, Pregabalin); Use of Gabapentin, Carbamazepine if chronic pain symptom predominates.
management: PRIMARY CARE: Detailed physical examination; Management of anemia and nutritional deficiencies; Avoid irrational use of pain medications; Low dose antidepressant (Amitriptyline 12.5-50 mg night dose); Explain onset of medication effect takes 2-3 weeks; Validate the somatic symptoms; Advise to engage in routine activities, physical exercise and relaxation techniques like deep breathing; Discuss with family members that the symptom, distress and disability are genuine; Strengthen supports; Regular follow up. SECONDARY CARE: Investigations to rule out medical illnesses; Complete history with behavioural observation; 2nd line medications (SSRIs/SNRIs); Combination of two psychotropic medications; Brief counselling; Psycho education (focusing on relationship between stress and physical symptoms); Relaxation training, regular exercise, yoga and meditation. TERTIARY CARE: Inpatient care if needed; Combination of two psychotropic medications; Add second and third line medications; Use of Gabapentin, Carbamazepine if chronic pain predominates; Structured Cognitive Behavioural Therapy, Cognitive restructuring, Mindfulness and acceptance based approach; Use of alternative medicine approach - Yoga; Collaborative approach (involve Physician, Neurology team and Pain Clinic referral); Vocational rehabilitation if needed; Physical therapies - guided exercise and physiotherapy.
referralRedFlags: REFER TO SECONDARY CARE IF: 1. Difficulty in making diagnosis; 2. No improvement after 4 weeks of treatment with first line medications; 3. Comorbid medical illness; 4. Suicidal risk; 5. Comorbid psychiatric illness. REFER TO TERTIARY CARE IF: 1. No improvement in 2nd line treatment; 2. High suicidal risk; 3. Needing intense counselling/psychotherapy; 4. Difficult patients.
algorithmNotes: SD=Somatoform Disorder; SSRI=Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitor; SNRI=Serotonin Norepinephrine Reuptake Inhibitor; MUS=Medically Unexplained Symptoms. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT - SYMPTOMS: Cough with yellow/rusty sputum; Breathlessness; Pleuritic chest pain; Malaise, myalgia, arthralgia; Extra pulmonary symptoms. PAST HISTORY: TB; Pneumonia; Airway disease; Diabetes; Chronic steroid use. CLINICAL EXAMINATION: 1. Vital parameters - Sensorium, Pulse, Blood pressure, Respiratory rate, Temperature, Oxygen saturation. 2. Systemic examination - a. Upper respiratory tract: nose & paranasal sinuses (frontal and maxillary sinus tenderness), throat examination (pharynx and tonsils); b. Lower respiratory tract: breath sounds (type, intensity), added sounds (crackles, wheeze, pleural rub). Signs of respiratory failure: RR >30/min, Abdominothoracic paradox, cyanosis, speaks in short sentences (Refer Respiratory Failure STW). Frank haemoptysis may suggest Pulmonary TB or malignancy.
risk: PATHWAYS BASED ON INITIAL ASSESSMENT FINDINGS: PATHWAY 1 (Acute URI/Respiratory Catarrh): Fever, tachycardia, pharyngitis, suffusion of eyes, rhinitis, hoarse voice; Respiratory system examination Normal. PATHWAY 2 (Acute Bronchitis): Fever, tachycardia; Respiratory system exam Wheeze; Consider acute exacerbation of asthma/COPD if history of these 2 illnesses. PATHWAY 3 (Community Acquired Pneumonia): Fever, tachycardia, tachypnea; Respiratory system exam Crackles/bronchial breath sounds; Consider acute exacerbation of asthma/COPD if history of these 2 illnesses.
scores: CAP SEVERITY ASSESSMENT - Use CRB-65 score for mortality risk in primary care: SCORE 0 = Low Risk = OP (out-patient); SCORE 1-2 = Intermediate Risk = IP (in-patient); SCORE 3-4 = High Risk = ICU. *65 in scoring mnemonic refers to age >65. Give 1 point for each: Confusion; Respiratory rate >=30/min; Low BP (DBP <=60 mmHg or SBP <=90 mmHg); Age >=65 years.
investigations: PATHWAY 1 (Acute URI): Total and differential count in suspected flu. PATHWAY 2 (Acute Bronchitis): Total and differential count if sputum is purulent; X-ray chest PA view. PATHWAY 3 (CAP): X-ray. OUTPATIENT CAP INVESTIGATIONS: Preliminary - Chest radiogram; Repeat if i. Patient not improving/worsening clinically, ii. Suspected underlying malignancy; Desirable - 1. Pulse oximetry in outpatients, 2. Sputum microbiology in suspected PTB & non-response after 48 hours of antibiotics.
drugs: PATHWAY 1 (Acute URI) TREATMENT: Symptomatic treatment for fever, myalgia (Paracetamol or other NSAID); Rest, Oral fluids (plenty); Oral antihistamines (Tab. CPM 4 mg BD) for severe runny nose or sneezing; Antibiotics in acute follicular tonsillitis: Amoxicillin/Ampicillin 500 mg tid X 5 days; In penicillin sensitive: Erythromycin estolate 250 mg q 6 hrly X 5 days with food. PATHWAY 2 (Acute Bronchitis) TREATMENT: Symptomatic treatment for fever (Paracetamol or other NSAID), Oral fluids; Inhaled bronchodilators: Salbutamol nebulization (5 mg/2.5 ml) 6-8 hourly; Antibiotics if purulent sputum and polymorphonuclear leukocytosis: Amoxicillin 500 mg tid X 5 days; In penicillin sensitive: Erythromycin estolate 250 mg q 6 hrly X 5 days with food; If asthma suspected refer to asthma STW. PATHWAY 3 (CAP OUTPATIENT, CRB-65 0-1): Targeted towards Streptococcus pneumoniae; Oral antibiotics after checking comorbidities (Diabetes, CVDs, CKD, CLD, Hepatic Pathology, Cancer, Alcohol Abuse, H/o antibiotics in last 3 months): a. Without comorbidities: Cap. Amoxicillin 500 mg TDS/Tab. Erythromycin 250 mg QID/Tab. Doxycycline 100 mg BD; b. With comorbidities: Cap. Amoxicillin 500 mg TDS + Tab. Azithromycin 500 mg OD; Duration 5 days in (A), extend to 7-10 days if no response within 3 days in (B). Do not give: a. Corticosteroids unless other indications; b. Fluoroquinolones (anti-tubercular activity). INPATIENT CAP (NON-ICU): a. Single agent IV beta-lactam; b. If suspected atypical pathogens/other end organ disease/diabetes/malignancy/severe CAP/antibiotics past 3 months: Combination of IV beta-lactam (Cefotaxime 2 g TID/IV Ceftriaxone 1 g BD/Amoxicillin-Clavulanic acid 1.2 g TID) + ORAL macrolide (Azithromycin 500 mg PO OD/Clarithromycin 500 mg PO BD). ICU SETTING: i. Without Pseudomonas risk - manage as above; ii. Suspected P. aeruginosa: IV Cefepime 1 G BD/IV Ceftazidime 2 G TID/Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 G QID/IV Cefoperazone-sulbactam 1.5 G IV TID/IV Meropenem 1 g TID; Combination therapy: Aminoglycosides (IV Amikacin)/Antipseudomonal fluoroquinolones (Levofloxacin/Moxifloxacin).
management: ADJUNCTIVE THERAPIES FOR CAP: a. Steroids not recommended in non-severe CAP; b. Non-invasive ventilation may be used in patients with CAP and acute respiratory failure. CONTRAINDICATIONS FOR NON-INVASIVE VENTILATION: a. Cardiorespiratory arrest; b. Presence of severe upper airway inflammation & edema; c. Severe haemodynamic instability - hypotension; d. Eu-capnic (normal PaCO2) coma; e. Multiple organ dysfunction or severe psychomotor agitation. DISCHARGE CRITERIA: Accepting orally, Afebrile and Hemodynamically stable for at least 48 h. POINTS TO NOTE WHILE SHIFTING: 1. If referring to higher center, give first dose of antibiotic (oral and if available parenteral), secure an IV line, start 0.9% Normal saline and oxygen supplementation through face mask at 4-6 litres/min during shift; 2. If patient drowsy/copious secretions, consider calling SUB-DISTRICT/DISTRICT hospital for endotracheal intubation and shifting on a transport ventilator.
referralRedFlags: REFERRAL TO A HIGHER CENTRE - CLINICAL CRITERIA: 1. Frank hemoptysis and/or Signs of respiratory failure; 2. CRB-65 score >1; 3. Oxygen saturation by pulse oximetry <=92% (patients <=50 yrs) OR <90% (patients >50 yrs); 4. Multi-lobar consolidation on chest X-ray; 5. Confusion/disorientation; 6. Hypothermia (core temperature <36 C).
algorithmNotes: CRB-65=Confusion, Respiratory rate, Blood pressure, age 65; URI=Upper Respiratory Infection; CAP=Community Acquired Pneumonia; PTB=Pulmonary Tuberculosis; CPM=Chlorpheniramine maleate. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: CLASSIC SYMPTOMS: Recurrent/episodic wheezing; Breathlessness; Cough and/or chest tightness. SUPPORTIVE FEATURES: History of atopy, family history of asthma, presence of triggers, presence of rhonchi on chest auscultation; No alternative explanation for these symptoms. TRY AND RULE OUT: Other obstructive airway disorders (see Table 1 for features that favour asthma over COPD); Other mimics - presence of fever, constitutional symptoms, purulent sputum, hemoptysis, focal chest signs on physical examination, foreign body aspiration, abnormal chest radiograph, etc. APPROACH TO DIAGNOSIS: Clinical assessment is the mainstay; Airway obstruction, and bronchodilator reversibility, on spirometry (if available) may support diagnosis; Refer patients for further work-up if diagnosis is in doubt.
risk: TABLE 1 - DIFFERENTIATING ASTHMA vs COPD: Age of Onset (Asthma: more often in childhood or early adulthood, variable; COPD: usually later in life, 4th-5th decade); Course (Asthma: Episodic; COPD: Progressive); Smoking/other exposures (Asthma: Uncommon; COPD: Common); Nasal Symptoms/Atopy (Asthma: Common; COPD: Rare); Family History (Asthma: Often; COPD: Uncommon); Triggers (Asthma: Often Identified; COPD: None); Wheeze (Asthma: Prominent and almost universal; COPD: May or may not be present).
scores: TABLE 2 - LEVEL OF CURRENT ASTHMA CONTROL (over preceding 4 weeks): Inadequately controlled (any one) vs Adequately controlled (all present). Daytime symptoms/use of rescue medication: More than twice a week (inadequate) vs Twice or less in a week (adequate). Night-time symptoms/awakening: Any (inadequate) vs None (adequate). Limitation of activities: Any (inadequate) vs None (adequate). Pulmonary function (if available): FEV1 <80% of predicted or PEF <80% of personal best (inadequate) vs FEV1 >80% of predicted or PEF >80% of personal best (adequate). FEV1=Forced Expiratory Volume in first second; PEF=Peak Expiratory Flow.
investigations: Airway obstruction and bronchodilator reversibility on spirometry (if available) may support diagnosis. Pulmonary function (FEV1/PEF) used to assess control level (Table 2). PEF monitoring for patient education. Refer for further evaluation if asthma remains poorly controlled.
drugs: INITIATION AND MODULATION OF ASTHMA PHARMACOTHERAPY (stepwise): STEP 1 - Infrequent & Intermittent symptoms: Levosalbutamol (50 ug) OR Salbutamol (100 ug), 2 puffs sos. STEP 2 - Regular Symptoms (<2 per week but >2 per month): Budesonide (100 ug) 2 puffs BD plus Levosalbutamol (50 ug) 2 puffs sos. STEP 3 - Regular Symptoms (>2 per week) or exacerbation in past one year: Formoterol/Budesonide (6/200 ug) as a single inhaler 2 puffs BD and sos. STEP 4 (poor control): Add tiotropium (9 ug) 2 puffs OD and low dose oral methylxanthine. STEP 5 (poor control): Add low dose oral corticosteroids (Refer). SEVERE ACUTE ASTHMA: Oxygen supplementation to maintain spO2 92-95%; Nebulized levosalbutamol/ipratropium (1.25 mg/0.5 mg) three doses at 20-minute interval, then 4-6 hourly or as needed; Injection hydrocortisone 200 mg intravenously, then oral prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg daily for five days. NON-SEVERE ACUTE ASTHMA: Continue additional inhaler doses as needed; Oral prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg daily for five days.
management: GUIDING PRINCIPLES: Mainstay of pharmacotherapy: Inhaled drugs; Frequency of symptoms determine treatment initiation; Reassess at 3-4 weeks (good response in favour of asthma diagnosis); Patient education for compliance, warning signs, triggers, inhaler technique, PEF monitoring; Inhaler technique to be monitored; Follow-up at 4-12 weeks, assess disease control by clinical parameters (Table 2); Step-up or step-down treatment as per level of control; Follow up three-monthly and modulate treatment as needed; Refer for further evaluation and management if asthma remains poorly controlled. SEVERE ACUTE ASTHMA (patient to be admitted); Discharge only when symptoms improve, wheezing absent or significantly reduced, heart rate <100 bpm, respiratory rate <30/min, room air spO2 >94%; Schedule follow-up outpatient visit at one week.
referralRedFlags: WHEN TO SUSPECT EXACERBATION: Acute symptomatic worsening, or reduction in PEF to below 80% of personal best while on continued treatment; Take two additional puffs of inhaler if symptoms persist, repeat if needed; If no response after 24 hours or symptomatic worsening or further reduction in PEF, contact physician. SEVERE ACUTE ASTHMA (admit): Inability to complete sentences, agitation, use of accessory muscles, respiratory rate >30/min, heart rate >110/min, pulsus paradoxus >25 mmHg, silent chest, and/or room air sPo2 <92%; Refer if no improvement. LIFE-THREATENING EXACERBATION: Altered sensorium, orthopnea, cyanosis, paradoxical breathing, hypotension, and/or bradycardia (heart rate <60 bpm) - immediately refer to higher centre with ICU facility.
algorithmNotes: FEV1=Forced Expiratory Volume in first second; PEF=Peak Expiratory Flow; COPD=Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease; spO2=oxygen saturation; BD=twice daily; OD=once daily; sos=as needed. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: Symptoms - chronic cough, progressively worsening breathlessness; Risk factors - tobacco smoking, use of solid fuels for cooking, occupational exposures, Age >35 years; Supportive features - Barrel shaped chest, hyperresonant note, diminished breath sounds and rhonchi on chest auscultation, forced expiratory time >6 seconds, features of cor pulmonale (loud P2, elevated JVP, edema) in advanced cases; No other alternative explanation for these symptoms. TRY AND RULE OUT: Other obstructive airway disorders (Refer Asthma STW Table 1 for features that favor COPD over asthma); Pulmonary tuberculosis - sputum AFB examination in case of cough (>2 weeks); Consider alternative diagnosis/complication - presence of fever, hemoptysis, orthopnea, chest pain, significant weight loss, focal chest signs, abnormal chest radiograph, etc.
risk: DISEASE EXACERBATION - Three cardinal symptoms: Increase in dyspnea; Increase in sputum volume and/or; Increase in sputum purulence. Classify As: Mild Exacerbation; Severe Exacerbation. Features Of Severe Exacerbation: Cyanosis; Respiratory rate >30/min; Heart rate >110/min; Systolic blood pressure <90 mmHg; SpO2 <90%; Paradoxical respiratory movements; Altered sensorium; Asterixis; Presence of severe co-morbid conditions (e.g. heart failure, arrhythmia).
scores: TABLE 1 - GRADING OF BREATHLESSNESS USING MODIFIED MEDICAL RESEARCH COUNCIL (MMRC) SCALE: Grade 0 = I only get breathless with strenuous exercise; Grade 1 = I get short of breath when hurrying on level ground or walking up a slight hill; Grade 2 = On level ground, I walk slower than people of the same age because of breathlessness or have to stop for breath when walking at my own pace; Grade 3 = I stop for breath after walking about 100 yards or after a few minutes on level ground; Grade 4 = I am too breathless to leave the house or I am breathless when dressing. TABLE 2 - SEVERITY CLASSIFICATION FOR COPD: MILD = Postbronchodilator FEV1 >=80% predicted, Dyspnea MMRC <2, Exacerbations in last one year <2, Complications NO; MODERATE = FEV1 50-79%, MMRC >=2, Exacerbations <2, Complications NO; SEVERE = FEV1 <50%, MMRC >=2, Exacerbations >=2, Complications YES. The category with the worst value should be used. Complications include respiratory failure, cor pulmonale, and secondary polycythemia.
investigations: Airway obstruction should be documented on spirometry on all patients provisionally diagnosed as having COPD (refer if necessary); Post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 defines airflow obstruction. Assess severity based on spirometry, severity of dyspnea (mMRC scale, Table 1), exacerbation frequency and presence of complications (Table 2). Pulmonary tuberculosis - sputum AFB examination in case of cough >2 weeks.
drugs: TREATMENT BY SEVERITY (stepwise): MILD COPD: Levosalbutamol (50 ug) 2 puffs prn -> Persistent Symptoms -> Add Tiotropium (9 ug) 2 puffs OD. MODERATE COPD: Tiotropium (9 ug) 2 puffs OD plus Levosalbutamol (50 ug) 2 puffs prn -> Persistent Symptoms -> Add low dose methylxanthines. SEVERE COPD: Formoterol/Budesonide (6/200 ug) as a single inhaler 2 puffs BD plus Levosalbutamol (50 ug) 2 puffs prn -> Persistent Symptoms -> Add Tiotropium (9 ug) 2 puffs OD and/or low dose methylxanthines. Refer if inadequate response, onset of new complications, or suspicion of alternative diagnosis. MILD EXACERBATION: Increase dose and/or frequency of levosalbutamol and/or ipratropium inhalation, or nebulized levosalbutamol/ipratropium (1.25 mg/0.5 mg) repeated as needed at 20-minute interval; Amoxycillin 500 mg TDS/Azithromycin 500 mg OD/Doxycycline 100 mg OD (BD on day 1) X 5 Days; Oral prednisolone 30 mg daily X 5 days. SEVERE EXACERBATION: Treatment as under Mild Exacerbation + Supplement oxygen with target spO2 of 92% (if spO2 monitoring available).
management: TREATMENT: Advice smoking cessation and counsel for other risk factors; Inhaled drugs are the mainstay; Treatment based on severity assessment; Follow up: Mild to moderate disease 3 to 6 Months, Severe disease 1-3 months; Ensure compliance and proper inhaler technique at each visit; If uncontrolled/complications develop, refer to higher center. ADMISSION CRITERIA: 1. Severe symptoms; sudden worsening of resting dyspnea; 2. Fall in oxygen saturation, cyanosis, confusion, drowsiness; 3. Failure of an exacerbation to respond to initial medical management; 4. Presence of serious comorbidities (heart failure, newly occurring arrhythmias, etc.). DISCHARGE CRITERIA: 1. Normalization of clinical and laboratory data to pre-admission levels; 2. Patient able to follow maintenance therapy; 3. Completion of acute medications; 4. Adequate control of comorbidities. Schedule follow up visit one week after discharge.
referralRedFlags: RED FLAG SIGNS FOR PEOPLE HAVING EXACERBATION: Altered sensorium; spO2 <88% despite therapy; Heart rate >110 bpm; Systolic blood pressure <90 mmHg; High risk comorbid conditions (arrhythmia, congestive cardiac failure, poorly controlled diabetes, renal or liver failure). Refer to higher centre for further management, and ensure continued supplemental oxygen and nebulization during transfer.
algorithmNotes: FEV1=Forced Expiratory Volume in first second; FVC=Forced Vital Capacity; mMRC=modified Medical Research Council; JVP=Jugular Venous Pressure; AFB=Acid Fast Bacilli; prn/sos=as needed; BD=twice daily; OD=once daily; TDS=thrice daily. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: Cough; Shortness of breath; Wheezing; Chest tightness. SIGNS: Tachycardia; Restlessness; Anxiety; Confusion; Seizure; Cyanosis; Asterexis; Arrhythmia. Look for underlying causes in hypercapnia, headache, bounding pulse, tremor/flap, papilloedema, coma.
risk: HYPOXIA (SPO2 <90%) - differential by cause: HEART FAILURE (Symptoms: Dyspnea on exertion or rest, Chest Pain, Wheezing, Fatigue; Signs: Tachycardia, Pulsus Alterans, Weak Rapid Thready Pulse, Pink Frothy Sputum, Cyanosis, Pallor, Distended Neck Veins). PNEUMONIA/LRTI (Symptoms: Cough with or without Sputum, Chest Pain, Fever with Chills/Fatigue/Malaise; Signs: Tachypnea, Tachycardia, Crackles and Rhonchi, Hypoxemia, Pleuritic Chest Pain). PULMONARY EMBOLISM (Symptoms: Sudden Shortness of Breath, Chest Pain, Calf Pain &/or Swelling, Hemoptysis; Signs: Syncope, Arrhythmia, Tachycardia). AIRWAY DISEASE: ACUTE ASTHMA (Symptoms: Wheeze, Shortness of Breath, Chest Tightness, Cough; Signs: Tachypnea, Tachycardia, Fall in SPO2, Use of Accessory Muscle). AE OF COPD (Symptoms: Worsening of Dyspnea, Increase in Sputum Production, Increased Cough; Signs: Tachypnea, Hypoxemia, Hypercarbia, Confusion, Drowsy, Peripheral Edema). BRONCHIOLITIS (Symptoms: Cough, Shortness of Breath, Wheezing; Signs: Cyanosis, Nasal Flares, Tachypnea, Paradoxical Breathing in children, Crackles and/or Rattling sounds in Lung).
scores: Diagnosis categories for treatment matrix: Heart failure; Acute Severe Asthma; AE COPD; ARI; Pneumonia LRTI; Pulmonary embolism. SpO2 <90% defines hypoxia threshold for oxygen therapy. For AECOPD keep lower target SpO2 = 88-92%.
investigations: INVESTIGATIONS: ABG, CRP, FBC, U&E; Chest Xray; Sputum culture, Blood culture (if febrile); Spirometry (COPD, Neuromuscular disease).
drugs: OXYGEN: Start oxygen therapy at SpO2 <90%; Monitor SpO2 during oxygen therapy to titrate flow rate: target SpO2 <96% (Oxygen delivery using Nasal cannulae/Simple face mask/Venturi mask/Non re-breathing mask); Note: for patients with AECOPD, keep lower target SpO2 = 88-92%. BRONCHODILATORS: Heart failure=SOS; Acute Severe Asthma=SABA +/- SAMA (Salbutamol +/- Ipratropium neb q20 min X 1 hr then prn); AE COPD=SABA + SAMA (Salbutamol neb hourly + Ipratropium neb 4 hourly); ARI=SABA + SAMA; Pneumonia LRTI=SOS; Pulmonary embolism=SOS. DIURETICS: Heart failure=Yes (IV Furosemide 40 mg or Torsemide 20 mg); others=SOS. ANTIBIOTICS: AE COPD - No risk factor Pseudomonas: Ceftriaxone or levofloxacin or moxifloxacin; Pseudomonas risk factor: levofloxacin or piperacillin tazobactam or ceftazidime or cefepime; Influenza suspect: Oseltamivir. Pneumonia LRTI - Mild/Mod cases: Amoxycillin PO/IV or Ceftriaxone IV; Severe Cases: Amoxycillin IV or Ceftriaxone IV; Atypical pneumonia: Azithromycin IV/PO or Doxycycline IV/PO. STEROIDS: Acute Severe Asthma=Yes (Methylprednisolone IV 40 to 60 mg or Prednisolone PO 60 mg); AE COPD=Yes (Methylprednisolone IV 60 to 125 mg IV q6-12 hourly); ARI=Yes; Pneumonia LRTI - Severe CAP (fiO2 >0.5 AND pH <7.3 OR lactate >4 mmolL-1 OR CRP >150 mgL-1): Methylprednisolone IV 0.5 mg/kg q12h. LMWH: Prophylactic if indicated (all); Pulmonary embolism - If high suspicion with low risk of bleeding: UFH (if thrombolysis anticipated), OR LMWH.
management: Treatment matrix applied per diagnosis (Heart failure, Acute Severe Asthma, AE COPD, ARI, Pneumonia LRTI, Pulmonary embolism) across Oxygen, Bronchodilators, Diuretics, Antibiotics, Steroids and LMWH columns. Stabilize CAB (Circulation, Airway, Breathing) and transfer to higher center where referral indicated.
referralRedFlags: REFERRAL: No relief OR Need for mechanical ventilation OR life threatening features: Stabilize CAB, transfer to higher center.
algorithmNotes: LRTI=Lower Respiratory Tract Infection; LMWH=Low Molecular Weight Heparin; SABA=Short Acting Beta Agonist; SAMA=Short Acting Muscarinic Antagonist; CAP=Community Acquired Pneumonia; UFH=Unfractionated Heparin; ABG=Arterial Blood Gas; FBC=Full Blood Count; U&E=Urea and Electrolytes; AE=Acute Exacerbation. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: DEFINITION: Emergency condition characterized by a sudden and painful inability to void voluntarily despite having a full bladder. HISTORY: Nature and duration of urinary symptoms prior to AUR; Associated symptoms like fever, weight loss, sensory loss or weakness of lower limbs; Past history of retentions; Rule out precipitating causes like diabetes mellitus, alcohol consumption, recent surgery, UTI, constipation, cold exposure, prolonged travel and neurological conditions; Medication history; Look for risk factors. EXAMINATION: Fever; Enlarged tender palpable bladder dull on percussion; Phimosis, meatal stenosis, urethral induration, stone, urethral discharge; DRE for estimating prostatic size, consistency, tenderness, exclude fecal impaction; Focused neurological examination - anal tone, perianal sensation and bulbocavernous reflex.
risk: RISK FACTORS OF SPONTANEOUS AUR DUE TO BPH: Old age; Severe lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS); Low peak flow rate; High postvoid residual urine (PVR); Enlarged prostate or large median lobe; High serum PSA; Symptom worsening; Increasing PVR during medical therapy. RISK FACTORS OF PRECIPITATED AUR: Surgical procedure with general or loco-regional anaesthesia; Bladder over-distension (eg prolonged journey); Exposure to cold; Medications with sympathomimetic or anticholinergic effects, diuretics, alcohol intake; Faecal impaction. CAUSES THAT BLOCK THE PASSAGE: BPH, Urethral Calculus, Urethral Stricture, Acute Prostatitis, Ca Prostate, Vesical Calculus, Faecal impaction. CAUSES THAT PARALYSE DETRUSOR: Neurological diseases e.g. spinal cord compression, transverse myelitis, stroke, head injury; Drug induced eg opioids, anticholinergics, anti-histaminics, anti-diarrhoeals, flavoxate.
scores: Categorize AUR as: Precipitated AUR (due to Drugs, Diabetes, Neurological disturbances, Urethral stricture, Pelvic and Perineal Surgery, Fecal impaction, Urinary/peri anal Infection) vs Spontaneous AUR due to BPH. IPSS=International Prostate Symptom Score used for assessment.
investigations: As AUR is an acute emergency, no investigation is required before catheterization to relieve symptoms. The volume of urine drained should be documented. DESIRABLE: CBC, S. Glucose, S. Creatinine and Electrolytes, USG KUB, Urine analysis & Urine culture of the drained urine. OPTIONAL (ONLY BY SPECIALISTS) - NOT TO BE DONE ROUTINELY: Cystoscopy, CT/MRI, RGU + MCU, Urodynamic studies.
drugs: Spontaneous AUR due to BPH with no prior history: alpha blockers for 2-4 days then T.W.O.C (Trial Without Catheter). FOR CATHETERIZATION: Use a 14 or 12 Fr Foley urethral catheter; Do not remove catheter earlier than a day.
management: MANAGEMENT ALGORITHM: Attempt gentle urethral catheterization. IF SUCCESSFUL: Keep catheter 1-3 days. Precipitated AUR due to (Drugs, Diabetes, Neurological disturbances, Urethral stricture, Pelvic and Perineal Surgery, Fecal impaction, Urinary/peri anal Infection) -> Treat the cause -> Trial without catheter -> If fails, refer to urologist. Spontaneous AUR due to BPH: No prior history of r/c acute retention +/- severe obstructive lower urinary tract symptoms -> Surgery; No prior history -> alpha blockers for 2-4 days -> T.W.O.C -> Succeeds = Continue medical management, Fails = Surgery. IF CATHETERIZATION FAILS: Suprapubic cystostomy if adequately trained OR Refer to urologist. INDICATIONS FOR HOSPITALIZATION: Patients of AUR with significant comorbidities; Patient of AUR with complications listed.
referralRedFlags: COMPLICATIONS DUE TO AUR: Urinary tract infection; Acute kidney injury. COMPLICATIONS DUE TO CATHETERIZATION: Post obstructive diuresis with dys-electrolytemia; Transient decompression hematuria; Urethral injury during catheterization. Refer to urologist if catheterization fails or trial without catheter fails. INDICATIONS FOR HOSPITALIZATION: AUR with significant comorbidities or with complications.
algorithmNotes: BPH=Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia; IPSS=International Prostate Symptom Score; TWOC=Trial Without Catheter; WW=Watchful waiting; LUTS=Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms; PVR=Post Void Residual; PSA=Prostate Specific Antigen; DRE=Digital Rectal Examination; RGU=Retrograde Urethrogram; MCU=Micturating Cystourethrogram. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: Blood in urine - red coloured or dark coloured; May be associated with pain: Pain during voiding (urethra), Pain in suprapubic region (bladder), Pain in flank (kidney); Acute retention of urine due to clots; Haematuria associated with clots (severe). EXAMINATION: Pulse, blood pressure; Check for pallor; Check for anasarca; Per abdomen examination: Palpable bladder, flank mass; Digital rectal examination: Enlarged prostate, hard nodular/smooth surfaced prostate; Rule out vaginal causes of bleeding. RED URINE BUT NOT HAEMATURIA: Foods - beetroot, blackberry, rhubarb; Medicines - rifampicin, pyridium. Even single episode of haematuria warrants complete evaluation.
risk: MAKE A CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (is haematuria INITIAL, TOTAL or TERMINAL): INITIAL - Urethra: stone, urethritis, stricture; Prostate: inflammation, benign hyperplasia, malignancy. TOTAL - Kidney: stone, malignancy (renal parenchyma, pelvis/ureter), genito-urinary tuberculosis; Ureter: stone, malignancy, genito-urinary tuberculosis; Bladder: infection, genitourinary tuberculosis, stone, malignancy. TERMINAL - Bladder: stone, tumor at bladder neck; Prostate: inflammation, benign hyperplasia, malignancy.
scores: Haematuria should be considered as a symptom of genitourinary malignancy in patients >40 years old until proven otherwise. Differential diagnosis for chronic conditions leading to haematuria: Stones, Renal cell cancer, Bladder tumor, Genito-urinary tuberculosis.
investigations: HOW TO INVESTIGATE - ESSENTIAL: Urine examination (routine, microscopy); Hemoglobin estimation; Kidney function tests (KFT); Ultrasonography of kidney urinary bladder and prostate region. DESIRABLE: Contrast enhanced computed tomography of kidney urinary bladder region/intravenous pyelography (if KFT normal); Magnetic resonance imaging of Kidney urinary bladder region (if KFT deranged); Urine cytology if >40yrs or smoker; Cystoscopy if >40 years or smoker. OPTIONAL: Urine culture; Urine for active sediments (if nephrotic/nephritic syndrome suspected); PT/INR (if bleeding disorder suspected); Serum prostate specific antigen (if required); Urine for acid fast bacilli - 3 samples (if tuberculosis suspected). DIFFERENTIAL DDx INVESTIGATIONS: Stones - Ultrasonography, Xray KUB, Intravenous pyelography or Computed tomography; Renal cell cancer - Ultrasonography, Computed tomography; Bladder tumor - Ultrasonography, Computed tomography, Urine cytology; Genito-urinary TB - Urine analysis, Urine acid fast bacilli, Urine tuberculosis culture, Gene xpert (optional), Intravenous pyelography or Computed tomography.
drugs: GENERAL: Start intravenous fluids if required (primary level); If Anaemia - may transfuse blood as required (primary level); Manage clot colic/flank pain with analgesics (primary level); If Acute urinary retention - catheterise with 20/22Fr 3 way Foley and may start continuous irrigation with normal saline (Primary level); Cystoscopic clot evacuation may be performed if feasible (tertiary level); If basic evaluation and management facilities are unavailable - refer (tertiary level). SPECIFIC: Suspect urinary tract infection - presents with dysuria, increased frequency of voiding and other irritative lower urinary tract symptoms with/without fever - treat with broad spectrum oral antibiotics (primary level). DDx TREATMENT: Stones >5mm or symptomatic - refer to urologist; Renal cell cancer & Bladder tumor - Mostly surgical treatment, refer to urologist; Genito-urinary TB - Oral Antitubercular treatment 6 months, refer to a urologist, close follow up.
management: PRIMARY LEVEL: IV fluids if required, blood transfusion if anaemia, manage clot colic/flank pain with analgesics, catheterise with 20/22Fr 3 way Foley + continuous irrigation if acute urinary retention, treat suspected UTI with broad spectrum oral antibiotics. TERTIARY LEVEL: Cystoscopic clot evacuation if feasible; Suspected nephrotic/nephritic syndrome (cola coloured urine, proteinuria, anasarca, hypertension) - Refer to nephrologist; If basic evaluation and management facilities unavailable - refer.
referralRedFlags: WHEN TO REFER (WARNING SIGNS): Deranged kidney functions; Suspecting malignancy; Haematuria with hypertension/albuminuria; Persistent severe haematuria. Suspected nephrotic/nephritic syndrome - refer to nephrologist (tertiary level). Genitourinary malignancy in patients >40 years until proven otherwise.
algorithmNotes: KFT=Kidney Function Tests; KUB=Kidney Ureter Bladder; PT/INR=Prothrombin Time/International Normalized Ratio. Even single episode of haematuria warrants complete evaluation. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: Inability to conceive even after one year of regular unprotected intercourse. Evaluation earlier than one year if female age is >35yrs, family history of infertility or very anxious couples. Infertility Incidence is 10-15%. Male factor contributory in 50% cases. HISTORY: Age of partners and duration of infertility; Use of contraception and lubricants; Knowledge of sexual cycle, technique, frequency; Sexual and ejaculatory dysfunction, volume of ejaculate; Medical illness (STD, diabetes, recent fever, chronic bronchitis, debilitating conditions); H/o Chemotherapy, Radiotherapy; Congenital anomalies, cryptorchidism, hypospadias, Chordee; Testicular torsion, drug history, trauma and swelling; H/o past surgeries (hernia repair, orchiopexy, retroperitoneal surgery); Family history (infertility, consanguinity, genetic disorders); Exposure to environmental toxins (pesticides, herbicides, chronic heat and radiation, sauna bath, tight non cotton undergarments, laptops & mobile); Partner history. PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: Body habitus (obesity, Klinefelter's), secondary sexual characters, gynecomastia; Penis: hypospadias, epispadiasis, chordee; Testes: volume, consistency, masses, contours; Epididymis: flat, turgid, nodularity; Vas deferens present/absent, thickened or beaded; Cords: presence of varicocele; Inguinal or scrotal scar; Rectal examination: cyst, dilated seminal vesicles.
risk: AIM: To ascertain contributory male factor; Identify potentially correctable conditions; Identify incorrectable conditions that may or may not be amenable to Assisted Reproductive Technique (ART); Identify underlying medical conditions responsible for infertility. HOW TO PROCEED: Both partners examined simultaneously; Ensure marriage is consummated, couple has frequent timed intercourse with knowledge of ovulatory cycle. DIAGNOSTIC CATEGORIES per semen analysis: Normal Semen Analysis (rule out sexual dysfunctions, anatomic abnormalities, female factor and unexplained); Low volume semen (Incomplete Collection, Retrograde ejaculation, Ejac. duct obstruction, Cong. Absence of VasDeferens, Hypogonadism); Azoospermia - Obstructive (Epididymal, vasal) and Nonobstructive (Genetic, Chromosomal, Hormonal, CT/RT, Post torsion testes, orchitis, Cryptorchidism, Idiopathic); Oligo-astheno-teratospermia / Isolated Asthenospermia (Antisperm antibodies, Sperm structural defect, Hypogonadism); Multiple defects (Varicocele, Cryptorchidism, Genital tract infection, Systemic illness, Prolonged abstinence, Drugs - Sulfasalazine, NFT, Colchicine, Chemotherapy, GnRh analogs, Spironolactone, Ketoconazole, Anabolic steroids, cocaine, alcohol; Chemicals - heavy metals, herbicides, organic solvents, fungicides, pesticides).
scores: WHO 2010 criteria for normal semen report: Volume >1.5 ml; Sperm conc. >15 million/ml; Sperm motility >40%; Progressive >32%; Sperm morphology >4% normal forms; Leukocyte density <1 million/mL. Note: If a patient is unable to produce semen consider retrograde ejaculation and anejaculation - need further evaluation.
investigations: SEMEN ANALYSIS (ESSENTIAL): At least two samples 1-2 months apart; Abstinence of 1-3 days; Collected in sterile, medical grade plastic wide mouth containers; Provided within the lab or transported within an hour at room temperature and examined immediately. OPTIONAL INVESTIGATIONS: Hormonal assay (Serum FSH, LH, Prolactin, Testosterone, Estradiol, T/E ratio); Culture (Urine, Semen, Prostatic fluid); Antisperm antibodies, Viability assay, Sperm function tests; Scrotal USG & doppler, TRUS, Genetic studies; Testicular biopsy (Multiple bilateral preferable).
drugs: OLIGO-ASTHENO-TERATOSPERMIA (low count, low motility, poor morphology) treatment by cause: High FSH/Severe Germ epith damage -> Refer for Assisted Reproductive Technique (IUI/IVF/ICSI); Raised ASA -> Steroids +; Varicocele -> Varicocelectomy; Infection -> Antibiotics; Idiopathic -> Empirical Medical Rx. Preventive measures: Avoid gonadotoxins, gonadotoxic drugs, smoking, tobacco, chronic heat, excess use of mobiles; Encouraging healthy life style (Nutritious diet, regular physical exercise, avoid stress, use of antioxidants and vitamins Vit. C, Vit E, Zinc).
management: PHC/CHC: History and Physical examination (PE); Proper Semen analysis; Normal Semen report (rule out unconsummation, sexual dysfunction, anatomic abnormalities); Abnormal Semen report - Refer to Urologist/infertility centre; Preventive measures; Female partner to be evaluated by gynecologist; Management of reversible nonsurgical causes (Infections etc.) and surgical cause i.e. varicocele if surgeon available; For further evaluation refer to district/tertiary hospital. DISTRICT HOSPITAL: Hormonal assay and Testicular biopsy; Management of sexual and ejaculatory dysfunction; Management of Varicocele and Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism; ART: AIH/AID and counselling for adoption. TERTIARY LEVEL: Additional testing (TRUS, Genetic, ASA, Sperm function tests); Advanced surgery (Microsurgical VVA, VEA, Varicocelectomy, TURED, Sperm retreival techniques, Cryopreservation and sperm banking); Advanced ART: IVF-ET/IVF ICSI.
referralRedFlags: Abnormal Semen report - Refer to Urologist/infertility centre. For further evaluation refer to district/tertiary hospital. AZOOSPERMIA treatment algorithm (Low volume/low pH/Fructose -ve vs Normal volume/Fructose +ve) leads to surgical/ART pathways. Female partner to be evaluated by gynecologist.
algorithmNotes: FSH=Follicle Stimulating Hormone; EDO=Ejaculatory Duct Obstruction; CABVD=Congenital Absence of Bilateral Vas deferens; VVA=Vaso Vasostomy; PTF=Primary Testicular Failure; VEA=Vasoepididymal Anastomosis; TRUS=Trans Rectal Ultrasonography; PESA=Percutaneous Epididymal Sperm Aspiration; ASA=Anti Sperm Antibodies; DI=Donor Insemination; TESE=Testicular Sperm Extraction; SV & ED=Seminal Vesicle & Ejaculatory Duct; TURED=Trans Urethral Resection of Ejaculatory Duct; ART=Assisted Reproductive Technique; AIH=Artificial Insemination Husband; AID=Artificial Insemination Donor; ICSI=Intra Cytoplasmic Sperm Injection; IVF-ET=Invitro Fertilization-Embryo Transfer; GUTB=Genito Urinary Tuberculosis. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: HOW WILL YOUR PATIENT PRESENT AND WHAT TO SUSPECT - CLINICAL SCENARIO -> SUSPECT: Flank pain -> Renal Stone; Colicky pain starting from back and radiating to front -> Upper ureteric stone; Colicky pain starting from back & radiating upto groin -> Mid ureteric stone; Colicky pain starting from back & radiating to upper thigh & scrotum/vulva -> Lower ureteric stone. Haematuria may be present with stone at any location; Lower ureteric stones may also present with difficulty in passing urine; Colics may be associated with nausea and vomiting; Can present as anuria in bilateral ureteric stones, ureteric stone in a solitary kidney. History of site & type of pain, associated symptoms hint towards possible site of stones. Remember to take past history of stone diseases, medications, family history etc.
risk: Recurrent stone formers, stone in children, bilateral stones, family history of stone, history of gut surgery, solitary kidney and cysteine stones require extended metabolic evaluation. Sites: Renal Pelvic Stone, Upper Ureteric Stone.
scores: Management stratified by stone size/location: Single Stone >5mm with baseline investigation normal; Renal Stone <1cm / Ureteric Stone >5mm <1cm (Medical expulsive therapy); Kidney/ureteric stone >1cm (Higher centre referral). MET (Medical Expulsive Therapy) offered: In Ureteric stones <10mm; In the absence of infection, obstruction or deranged renal function; MET can be tried for upto 4 weeks.
investigations: RADIOLOGY: X-KUB (Readily available, inexpensive, minimal radiation but needs preparation, may not be preferred in emergency settings); USG (Readily available, no radiation, safe test in pregnancy, detects radiolucent stones, high sensitivity for hydronephrosis, can miss a ureteric calculus); IVP (Anatomical and functional imaging, aids in planning surgery but high radiation and needs preparation, not useful in poor renal function); CT Scan (No contrast required, highly sensitive and specific, detect radiolucent stones, detect other causes of flank pain, but risks higher radiation and cost). TIPS FOR ORDERING: Order X-KUB and Ultrasound in all patients of suspected renal stones (90% of renal stones are radio-opaque); In acute colic NCCT should be preferred if available; Once stone detected, get Intravenous pyelography if stone is seen on X-ray; CT urography if stone is radiolucent. METABOLIC EVALUATION - Initial biochemical (all stone formers): Urine analysis, serum creatinine, electrolytes (calcium, phosphorous and uric acid), Intact parathyroid hormone and stone analysis preferable. Extended Evaluation (recurrent stone former, stone in children, bilateral stones, family history, gut surgery, solitary kidney, cysteine stones; at 3-4 weeks after stone clearance): initial metabolic evaluation plus 24-hour urinary levels of calcium, uric acid, and creatinine; preferable urinary oxalate and citrate levels too.
drugs: MEDICAL EXPULSIVE THERAPY (MET): Alpha blockers such as Tamsulosin (0.4mg/day); Alfuzocin (10mg/day); Doxazocin (4mg/day); Silodocin (8mg/day). MET should be offered in Ureteric stones <10mm; In the absence of infection, obstruction or deranged renal function; MET can be tried for upto 4 weeks. Medical expulsive therapy uses Alpha Blockers + Potassium Nitrate. Analgesics + Hydration for flank pain/hematuria. Empiric Antibiotic/hydration for fevers with chills & rigors/anuria. DIETARY/PREVENTIVE: Increase daily fluid intake to ensure urine output >2 lit/day; Restrict extra salt intake and increase dietary fibre; Do not restrict calcium intake; Increase citrate rich food (lemon, orange juice); Decrease oxalate rich foods (spinach, nuts, beet root, potato chips, French fries); Avoid purine rich foods (animal protein, alcoholic drinks like beer).
management: MANAGEMENT ALGORITHM: Flank Pain, Hematuria -> Analgesics + Hydration; Fevers with chills & rigors/Anuria -> Empiric Antibiotic/hydration -> Higher Centre Referral. Workup: X-Ray KUB, USG Abdomen, IVP/CT Scan + Urine Analysis, Initial Metabolic screen. Single Stone >5mm with baseline investigation normal -> Counsel the patient for future preventive strategies. Renal Stone <1cm / Uteric Stone >5mm <1cm -> Medical expulsive therapy (Alpha Blockers, Potassium Nitrate). Kidney/ureteric stone >1cm -> Higher Centre Referral.
referralRedFlags: WARNING SIGNS FOR IMMEDIATE REFERRAL: Anuria; Fever with chills and rigors; Suspected renal failure; Persistent haematuria. Kidney/ureteric stone >1cm -> Higher Centre Referral.
algorithmNotes: X-KUB=X-ray Kidney Ureter Bladder; USG=Ultrasonography; IVP=Intravenous Pyelography; NCCT=Non-Contrast Computed Tomography; MET=Medical Expulsive Therapy. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: A swelling, clinical or on USG, arising from any of the scrotal structures: scrotal wall &/or intrascrotal contents. HOW TO MAKE A CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS - AS PER SITE: Diagnose the site of swelling - Are you able to reach above the swelling? If YES = Scrotal swelling (if scrotal wall involved = PARIETAL SWELLING); If NO = INGUINOSCROTAL SWELLING (Scrotal wall normal & can be pinched off the swelling = INTRASCROTAL SWELLING). Testis palpated separately + swelling located well above testis = SPERMATIC CORD SWELLING; Testis not palpable normally + swelling located just above &/or behind testis = EPIDIDYMAL SWELLING / TESTICULAR SWELLING. AS PER NATURE: Diagnose the nature of swelling - PAINFUL (Fever Present = Acute infection; Fever Absent = Traumatic/Ischaemic e.g. torsion); PAINLESS (Cystic = Fluid accumulation e.g. Hydrocele, epididymal cyst; Solid = Ch. inflammatory e.g. Filariasis, TB, or Tumors).
risk: PARIETAL (SCROTAL WALL) SWELLINGS: Ac. Inflammation (Bilateral: Cellulitis, Fournier gangrene; Unilateral: Reactionary to epididymo-orchitis, Furuncle Abscess); Traumatic (Contusional, Blunt trauma); Ch. Inflammation (Filarial Elephantiasis); Fluid Accumulation (Edema in anasarca, IVC thrombosis, Urinary extravasation, Scrotal wall cysts); Neoplasm (Melanoma, Scrotal Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma). INTRASCROTAL SWELLINGS: Testicular (Cystic: Hydrocele; Solid Painless: Testicular tumor; Solid Painful: Torsion testis, Orchitis); Epididymal (Cystic: Epididymal cyst, Spermatocele; Solid Painless: Ch. Filarial epididymitis, Ch. Tuberculous Epididymitis, Adenomatoid tumor; Solid Painful: Ac. Epididymitis); Spermatic cord (Cystic: Varicocele; Solid Painless: Lipoma cord; Solid Painful: Funiculitis).
scores: RED FLAG SIGNS - PAINFUL SWELLING: Sudden onset, O/E tender enlarged testis (pain increases on elevating testis), Severe pain, Vomiting, No fever -> TORSION TESTIS (More common in adolescents) -> Confirm by Scrotal doppler, Attempt Manual detorsion if patient reports early -> REFER URGENTLY FOR EXPERT CONSULTATION. PAINLESS SWELLING: Solid testicular swelling is felt -> TORSION TESTIS (Testicular Tumor) -> Confirm by Scrotal USG + Serum tumor markers -> REFER ALL CASES FOR EXPERT CONSULTATION.
investigations: SUSPECTING AC. INFLAM DISEASE: Essential - TLC/DLC, Blood sugar; Desirable - Anti filarial antibody. SUSPECTING CH. INFLAMMATORY DIS.: Essential - TLC/DLC, ESR; Desirable - Anti filarial Ab, TB Gold test, Scrotal USG. SUSPECTING TESTICULAR TUMOR: Essential - Beta hCG, Alfa feto protein, Serum LDH; Desirable - Scrotal USG, Abdomino-Pelvic CECT Scan. SUSPECTING TORSION: Essential - TLC/DLC; Desirable - Scrotal doppler. SUSPECTING VARICOCELE: Essential - TLC/DLC; Desirable - Scrotal doppler.
drugs: PARIETAL SWELLINGS - FURUNCLE/ABSCESS: Broad Spectrum Antibiotic Amoxy + Clavulinic acid; Consider drainage if fluctuations+ or impending rupture. FILARIAL ELEPHANTIASIS: DEC 100 mg TDS x 20 days, Doxycycline 100 mg BD x 20 days, Scrotal Elevation/support. INTRASCROTAL - AC. EPIDIDYMO-ORCHITIS: If urinary tract instrumentation or dysuria - suspect bacterial type, treat by antibiotic and support; REFER if no response in 48 hrs; Treat all other cases as filarial by DEC 100 mg x TDS x20 days + Doxycycline 100 mg x BD x 20 days; Give anti inflammatory drugs to all. CHRONIC EPIDIDYMO-ORCHITIS: Mostly filarial in origin but if H/O UTI or urethral catheterization suspect bacterial, if H/O TB suspect tuberculosis; Treat by DEC 100 mg TDS + Doxycycline 100 mg BD for 20 days. HYDROCELE: Small size - no treatment; Moderate to large - Do hydrocelectomy; Aspiration can be performed under asceptic precautions in select cases; REFER if not trained to do the surgery. VARICOCELE: Counsel for semen analysis (2-3 times); Rest all cases given symptomatic treatment.
management: PARIETAL FURUNCLE/ABSCESS REFER if: abscess appears part of underlying disease, Nonresponders, Immunocompromised patient. FILARIAL ELEPHANTIASIS REFER if: Non responders, Huge size. AC. EPIDIDYMO-ORCHITIS REFER if no response in 48 hrs. CHRONIC EPIDIDYMO-ORCHITIS REFER if: No response to treatment, Epididymal abscess or local sinus discharging syrup like pus. VARICOCELE REFER if: discrepancy in size of testis and/or abnormal semen parameters present.
referralRedFlags: PAINFUL SWELLING with sudden onset, tender enlarged testis, severe pain, vomiting, no fever -> suspect TORSION TESTIS -> REFER URGENTLY FOR EXPERT CONSULTATION (confirm by scrotal doppler, attempt manual detorsion if early). PAINLESS solid testicular swelling -> suspect TESTICULAR TUMOR -> REFER ALL CASES FOR EXPERT CONSULTATION (confirm by scrotal USG + serum tumor markers).
algorithmNotes: TLC/DLC=Total/Differential Leucocyte Count; ESR=Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate; hCG=human Chorionic Gonadotropin; LDH=Lactate Dehydrogenase; CECT=Contrast Enhanced Computed Tomography; DEC=Diethylcarbamazine; USG=Ultrasonography; TB=Tuberculosis. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: Presence of Fever >2 weeks; Anorexia; Unexplained weight loss or no weight gain in last 3 months; History of contact with TB patient; With one or more of - Recurrent/chronic abdominal pain in presence of red flag signs, Abdominal distension/mass, Altered bowel habits. (Constitutional symptoms may or may not be present in suspected case of Abdominal TB.) CLINICAL FEATURES BY TYPE: PERITONEAL TB - Abdominal pain, distension, Fever, Weight loss. NODAL TB - Pain abdomen, Fever, Palpable abdominal lump. INTESTINAL TB - Recurrent intestinal colic, Altered bowel habits, Chronic diarrhoea, Partial/complete intestinal obstruction, Weight loss/anorexia, Palpable abdominal lump, Lower gastrointestinal bleeding. VISCERAL TB (LIVER/SPLEEN/PANCREAS) - Abdominal pain, Fever, Jaundice, Weight loss, Anorexia, Hepatomegaly, Splenomegaly, Hepatic abscess, Palpable abdominal lump, Abnormal LFTs. EXAMINATION FINDINGS: Anthropometry; General physical & systemic examination; Look for peripheral LAP, ascites, hepatosplenomegaly, doughy feel of abdomen, palpable abdominal lump, visible peristalsis.
risk: RED FLAGS: Pain abdomen waking child from sleep; Chronic, severe, or nocturnal diarrhea; Gastrointestinal blood loss; Localized distension or mass. CATEGORIZE BY SITE: Peritoneal TB; Nodal TB; Intestinal TB; Visceral TB (liver, spleen, pancreas). History of contact with a TB patient is important. Abdominal LN considered significant if SAD (short axis diameter) >15 mm.
scores: DO NOT start Empirical ATT with isolated: Recurrent/Chronic abdominal pain without red flag signs; Chronic diarrhoea without proper evaluation; Failure to gain weight. Pointers towards DR-TB investigation: Non response/Worsening/deterioration of constitutional symptoms after initial improvement (consider Inflammatory Bowel disease after ruling out Crohn's disease).
investigations: ESSENTIAL: Ultrasound abdomen. SPECIFIC USG FINDINGS: Abdominal LN measuring >15 mm in short axis, conglomerate and/or central necrosis; Omental/mesenteric thickening >15 mm with increased echogenicity; Ileocaecal wall thickening. NON SPECIFIC USG FINDINGS: Intraabdominal fluid (free or loculated) or Inter-loop ascites; Ascitic fluid with multiple septations; Abdominal LAP with SAD <15 mm in absence of red flag signs. ALSO: Chest X Ray; sputum/GA/IS (if CXR abnormal) for NAAT, TB culture; Ascitic fluid (if present) for cytology, protein & albumin; Peripheral LN-FNA (if size >2 cm) for cytology, NAAT, TB culture. ASCITES: Essential - if exudative ascites, ascitic fluid for NAAT, TB culture (No role of ADA). ENLARGED ABDOMINAL LN (SAD>15 mm): Desirable - USG guided Abdominal LN-FNA for cytology, NAAT, TB culture; Optional - USG/CT guided core biopsy of LN for histology, NAAT, TB culture. INTESTINAL INVOLVEMENT: Desirable - if Ileocaecal thickening with enlarged nearby LN, USG guided Abdominal LN-FNA for cytology/NAAT/culture; Isolated ileocaecal thickening / LN sampling not feasible - CECT Abdomen/CT enterography; Optional - Ileocolonoscopy + tissue biopsy (HPE, NAAT), Laparoscopy + tissue biopsy for HPE, NAAT.
drugs: Start treatment & follow-up as per NTEP. ATT for 6 months (2HRZE + 4HRE). Pyridoxine supplementation 10 mg/day. Steroids - not useful & not recommended. Supportive treatment - Management of SAM/Malnutrition as per national guidelines. Surgical treatment for: Acute intestinal obstruction, Bowel perforation; Persistence of obstructive symptoms despite conservative management & ATT.
management: TREATMENT: Start ATT (2HRZE + 4HRE) and follow up as per NTEP; Pyridoxine 10 mg/day; manage SAM/malnutrition; surgery for acute obstruction/perforation or persistent obstructive symptoms. MONITORING: Clinical follow up every month during treatment and every 3 months after; Radiologically by USG at end of treatment or if worsening/non response; Microbiological if worsening/non response. Obstructive symptoms may persist or worsen despite ATT. Monitor adherence to ATT and ATT-induced adverse drug reactions.
referralRedFlags: WHEN TO REFER: Diagnosis is uncertain & additional investigations are required; Acute intestinal obstruction or bowel perforation; DR-TB; No response to appropriate treatment; Oral drug (ATT) intolerance/cannot be given. RED FLAGS (urgent): Pain abdomen waking child from sleep; Chronic/severe/nocturnal diarrhea; GI blood loss; Localized distension or mass.
algorithmNotes: DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM: If abdominal TB suspected -> look for peripheral nodes, get CXR and Abdominal USG. USG suggestive of TB: CXR abnormal -> Sputum/GA/IS for NAAT/TB culture (Positive -> start treatment); Peripheral nodes + -> FNA for NAAT/TB culture/Cytopathology (Positive -> start treatment); No peripheral nodes & CXR normal -> Ascites (ascitic fluid aspiration: cytology, protein, sugar; if exudative with lymphocytic predominance -> NAAT/TB culture), Lymphadenopathy (FNA for NAAT/TB culture/Cytopathology), Ileocecal TB (CECT Abdomen/CT enterography). USG normal -> evaluate for other causes / refer to higher centre. ABBREV: ATT=Antitubercular treatment; CECT=Contrast Enhanced CT; CXR=Chest X-Ray; DR-TB=Drug Resistant TB; FNA=Fine Needle Aspiration; GA=Gastric Aspirate; IS=Induced Sputum; LAP=Lymphadenopathy; LN=Lymph Node; NAAT=Nucleic Acid Amplification Test; SAD=Sagittal/Short Axis Diameter; SAM=Severe Acute Malnutrition; H=Isoniazid, R=Rifampicin, Z=Pyrazinamide, E=Ethambutol.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: Documented, persistent unexplained fever for 2 weeks or more; Unremitting cough for 2 weeks or more; Unexplained documented weight loss of >=5% in last 3 months; No weight gain despite adequate nutrition; Unexplained loss of appetite; Chest pain (specific to pleural effusion); Contact with TB patients in past 2 years. EXAMINATION: Temperature, Weight, Mid Arm Circumference (MAC), Lymphadenopathy, cold abscess, discharging sinus; Chest examination findings depend on underlying pathology (consolidation, pleural effusion, etc.). Do HIV testing for all cases with TB.
risk: Types of intrathoracic TB: Pulmonary, Pleural, Mediastinal. CXR patterns: Fibrocavitary shadows; Miliary shadows; Hilar LN enlargement; Paratracheal LN. Risk: contact with Pulmonary TB in past 2 years; failure of nutritional rehabilitation in babies with SAM. Assess for drug resistance via U-DST (Universal Drug Susceptibility Testing).
scores: Weight-band based dispersible FDC dosing (4-7, 8-11, 12-15, 16-24, 25-29, 30-39 kg). MANAGING TREATMENT INTERRUPTIONS: Interruption up to 4 weeks -> resume therapy (restart if missed within 1st 4 weeks); Interruption over 4 weeks -> reinvestigate for DRTB (Rif resistance not detected -> retreat with 1st line drugs & check INH resistance; Rif resistance detected -> treat as MDRTB).
investigations: ESSENTIAL: Chest x-ray - TB suggestive: Hilar/paratracheal lymph nodes, fibrocavitary disease, Miliary pattern; Non specific: effusion, consolidation, bronchopneumonia, other shadows. Sputum/Induced Sputum/Gastric Lavage/Aspirate/pleural fluid for NAAT; Smear examination (if NAAT unavailable); if facilities exist send aliquot for culture if NAAT negative for MTB; Pleural tap: Gross, Cytology, Biochemistry, NAAT, MGIT/LJ, ZN if NAAT not available (if cannot be done at primary level then refer). DESIRABLE: Chest x-ray of family members. OPTIONAL (in institutions): CECT scan; Pleural Biopsy (caseating granulomas with Langerhans giant cells, epithelioid cells & lymphocytes); Image guided (USG/CT) mediastinal LN biopsy; Bronchoscopy & BAL. DO NOT DO: TST/Mantoux test (overemphasized, supportive only; do not diagnose TB or start ATT on basis of +ve TST ONLY); Serological tests - IGRA (Quantiferon/Quantiferon-Gold etc); Pleural fluid ADA.
drugs: TB TREATMENT REGIMEN: 2HRZE + 4HRE. Drug doses: Isoniazid (H) 7-15 mg/kg (max 300 mg/day); Rifampicin (R) 10-20 mg/kg (max 600 mg/day); Pyrazinamide (Z) 30-40 mg/kg (max 2000 mg/day); Ethambutol (E) 15-25 mg/kg (max 1500 mg/day). DISPERSIBLE FDC by weight band [Intensive phase HRZ 50/75/15 + E100 ; Continuation phase HR 50/75 + E100]: 4-7 kg = 1 + 1 ; 8-11 kg = 2 + 2 ; 12-15 kg = 3 + 3 ; 16-24 kg = 4 + 4 ; 25-29 kg = 3+1A* + 3+1A* ; 30-39 kg = 2+2A* + 2+2A* (*A = Adult FDC HRZE 75/150/400/275, HRE 75/150/275). Pyridoxine 10 mg/day for 6 months. Consider steroids in miliary TB with hypoxia and Endobronchial TB: Prednisone 2 mg/kg daily or Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg/day for 4 weeks, then reduce gradually over next 4 weeks before stopping. Nutritional support; treat co-morbid conditions HIV, SAM.
management: MONITORING: When to assess - 2 weeks (adherence, correct dose, combination & tolerance), then every month till completion. What to assess - appropriateness of therapy (correct combination, acceptance/tolerance, counsel to complete and not miss doses); response to therapy (clinical symptoms/adverse effects/weight/dose revision; X-ray at end of therapy and for worsening; NAAT not an appropriate follow-up tool; smear at end of treatment; repeat microbiological test (smear, MGIT, NAAT) at end of IP and end of therapy if still symptomatic or deteriorating/failing). After treatment completion, follow up clinically at 6, 12, 18 & 24 months. Treatment regimens: Microbiologically confirmed or clinically diagnosed RS pulmonary TB, or drug-sensitive previously treated TB = 2HRZE + 4HRE.
referralRedFlags: Refer if pleural tap / investigations cannot be done at primary level. CXR Normal needing detailed EPTB workup -> consider referral to higher center for further investigations. Rif resistance +ve -> follow DRTB pathway. Refer for DRTB / non-response in 4 weeks. May seek review from a higher centre.
algorithmNotes: DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM (pediatric intrathoracic TB, no risk factors for drug resistance): Persistent Fever >=2 weeks without known cause and/or Unremitting cough >=2 weeks and/or Weight loss >=5% or no weight gain in past 3 months despite adequate nutrition or failure of nutritional rehabilitation in babies with SAM; with or without contact with Pulmonary TB in past 2 years -> Chest X-ray. CXR suggestive -> Sputum/GA/IS for approved NAAT for MTB: MTB+ve = Microbiologically confirmed TB case (Rif resistance +ve -> follow DRTB pathway; Rif resistance -ve -> give 1st line ATT). U-DST further work-up: NAAT -> MTB+ve -> Rif resistance -ve/+ve -> Repeat NAAT, FL-LPA / SL-LPA to evaluate FQ and/or SLI resistance (H resistance via inhA and/or katG mutation; Eto resistance inhA). ABBREV: ADA=Adenosine Deaminase; BAL=Broncho-alveolar lavage; CBNAAT=Cartridge-based NAAT; EPTB=Extra-pulmonary TB; FDC=Fixed dose combination; FL-LPA=First line Line probe assay; SL-LPA=Second line Line probe assay; FQ=Fluoroquinolones; SLI=Second line injectables; IGRA=Interferon Gamma Release assay; MAC=Mid Arm Circumference; NAAT=Nucleic acid amplification test; TST=Tuberculin skin test; U-DST=Universal Drug Susceptibility Testing; SAM=Severe acute malnutrition.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: Persistent enlargement of lymph node for >2 weeks in one or more areas in cervical/axillary/inguinal regions; Size >2 cm or matted lymph nodes +/- chronic sinus; With/without associated systemic symptoms: fever, cough, poor appetite, weight loss; With no evidence of recent scalp/skin lesions of draining area. TB is unlikely if: the lymph nodes are few, small (<2 cm) and persistent for a long time (months to years) without any systemic symptoms.
risk: Look for Matted LN / Cold abscess / Sinus. BCG LYMPHADENITIS (differential): Age usually <2 years; Axillary and/or supraclavicular lymph node on the same side as BCG vaccination (usually given on the left); No systemic symptoms in immunocompetent children. NAAT or AFB smear positivity cannot differentiate between BCG and MTB.
scores: Treatment response interpretation: Disappearance of constitutional symptoms with decrement or no increment in lymph node size suggests response. Increment in lymph node size WITH disappearance of constitutional symptoms may suggest paradoxical reaction (provided drug resistance ruled out). Increment in lymph node size WITHOUT disappearance of constitutional symptoms suggests drug resistant TB/alternate cause.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: Lymphnode aspirate - Send for NAAT (also MGIT culture, particularly if the patient is at risk of DRTB); Smear for AFB. DESIRABLE: Lymphnode cytopathology (if NAAT and smear negative); Lymphnode Biopsy (Core/Excision); Chest X-ray; Hemogram with peripheral smear; Cytopathology.
drugs: Treat with 2 HRZE + 4 HRE (standard doses) if new case & Rifampicin resistance not detected or not known. If retreatment case or any other risk factor for DRTB, detailed & swift investigations for DRTB are advised before starting treatment. For non-matted node trial: Antibiotics Amoxicillin/Amoxicillin-Clavulanate are recommended (Avoid Linezolid & quinolones), review after 2 weeks. BCG LYMPHADENITIS treatment: Wait and watch if small; if large and suppurative, repeated aspiration or rarely incision and drainage is required.
management: TREATMENT AND RESPONSE: Treatment should be started and follow-up conducted as per NTEP guidelines. Do not treat for TB based on only positive mantoux test or FNAC suggestive of reactive lymph node with negative NAAT/AFB on smear. Children with disappearance of constitutional symptoms with no increase in lymph node size at the end of 6 months therapy can be kept on follow-up with no extension of therapy.
referralRedFlags: WHEN TO REFER TO AN EXPERT: Diagnosis is not established with FNAC/NAAT; Surgical facility is not available to do excision or core biopsy; DR is suspected due to any reason including non-response and the facility for DRTB testing is not available; If there is any pointer towards possible malignancy e.g. skin or mucosal bleed or significant pallor or generalised adenopathy irrespective of the size or associated hepato-splenomegaly.
algorithmNotes: DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM: Peripheral Lymph node >2cm in one or more sites. CHEST X-RAY: if abnormal get Sputum/Induced sputum/GA for NAAT and AFB; Mtb or AFB detected -> choose appropriate regimen based on NAAT result. LOOK IF Matted LN/Cold abscess/Sinus: YES -> Aspirate pus from sinus or from fluctuating lymph node (if no pus, do FNAC); send for NAAT, AFB smear, Cytopathology; send MGIT if indicated -> AFB detected on smear and/or Mtb detected on NAAT and/or suggestive cytopathology like caseation or epithelioid granuloma present -> choose regimen. NO -> Antibiotics Amoxicillin/Amoxicillin-Clavulanate (Avoid Linezolid & quinolones), review after 2 weeks; if no response -> Excision/core biopsy if facility available, send for NAAT, AFB smear, histopathology & TB culture -> AFB on smear/positive NAAT/caseous granuloma (if any positive) -> choose regimen; if none -> Refer or investigate appropriately, Excision Biopsy may be needed. ABBREV: AFB=Acid fast bacillus; BCG=Bacille Calmette Guerin vaccine; DR=Drug resistant; FNAC=Fine needle aspiration cytology; HRZE=Isoniazid/Rifampicin/Pyrazinamide/Ethambutol; MGIT=Mycobacteria Growth Indicator Tube; NAAT=Nucleic acid amplification test; NTEP=National TB Elimination Programme.

whenToSuspect: POTT'S SPINE (commonest, 50% of osteoarticular TB): Insidious onset back pain for >6 weeks (commonest thoracic > lumbar > cervical); Localized/Referred root pain; TB symptoms: Fever/anorexia/weight loss; CNS complications like Paraparesis (20-50%), cauda equina syndrome, paraspinal muscle wasting, severe pain; Examination: Local tenderness/deformity. DACTYLITIS (short bones): Swelling of short tubular bones of hands & feet (proximal phalanx or metacarpals of index/middle/ring fingers commonly affected); In children multiple or consecutive bones involved compared to a single bone in adults; May present without pyrexia or signs of inflammation. ARTHRITIS (large joints - hip/knee commonest): Insidious onset joint pain, swelling; Monoarticular arthritis; Commonly associated with pulmonary or lymph node TB.
risk: BOX A - Risk factors for TB: Contact history with TB case; Immunocompromised; HIV.
