BOX B - Clinical manifestation of Spinal TB (STB)/TB arthritis (TBA): Insidious onset back pain >6 weeks (STB); Spine deformity/Kyphoscoliosis/Gibbus/Paraplegia/Sensory loss/Autonomic dysfunction (STB); Insidious onset pain and swelling in joints for >6 weeks (TBA); TB Symptoms: Persistent Fever, Anorexia, Weight loss (>5% in last 3 months). Suspect if symptoms of STB/TBA present with/without constitutional symptoms of TB (Box B) and/or risk factors of TB.
scores: Radiological findings s/o osteoarticular TB and/or TB at additional site can be labeled as clinically diagnosed Osteoarticular TB. Confirm microbiologically in all cases, if possible, before ATT. Phemister's triad on plain X-ray (arthritis): Peri-articular osteoporosis, Peripherally located osseous erosion, Gradual joint space narrowing.
investigations: POTT'S SPINE ESSENTIAL: X-ray Spine (early stage may be normal; may show end plate erosions, joint space narrowing/collapse, decreased vertebral height, paravertebral soft tissue shadow); MRI Spine preferred, if not feasible do CT (Marrow edema; Destruction of intervertebral disc, adjacent vertebral bodies & opposing end plates; Pre/para vertebral or epidural abscess); Sputum/GA for NAAT, MGIT/LJ (if CXR abnormal); FNAC (if peripheral lymph nodes enlarged) for Cytology, NAAT & MGIT/LJ; Desirable: Image guided (USG/CT) aspiration of abscess (if feasible) for NAAT & MGIT/LJ. DACTYLITIS ESSENTIAL: Plain X-ray of involved parts (Diaphyseal expansile lesion; Periosteal reaction uncommon; Healing by sclerosis usually gradual); X-ray film of chest; Sputum/GA for NAAT & MGIT/LJ if CXR abnormal; FNAC (if peripheral lymph nodes enlarged); Desirable: Image guided (USG/CT) aspirate from involved bones. ARTHRITIS ESSENTIAL: Plain X-ray (Phemister's triad); Early stage synovitis & arthritis imaging may show wide joint space due to effusion; Bony ankylosis rare in TB arthritis (contrast to Pyogenic); USG/MRI of joint; X-ray film of chest; Sputum/GA for NAAT, MGIT (if CXR abnormal); FNAC (if peripheral lymph nodes enlarged); Desirable: Image guided (USG/CT) aspirate from joint fluid. MRI Spine is 100% sensitive, indicated in all cases.
drugs: TREATMENT & MONITORING: Start treatment for microbiologically/lab confirmed TB and probable TB. Regimen: 2HRZE + 10HRE (Standard doses) + Pyridoxine 10 mg/day. Follow up every month during treatment & subsequently every 3 months (Pott's spine with X-ray or MRI & Tubercular dactylitis or arthritis with plain X-ray).
management: Monitor on each visit: a. Symptomatic improvement, weight gain, side effects of medicines; b. Microbiology: sputum/GA if CXR abnormal at end of IP, site samples like aspiration of pus from lesions including psoas abscess (if worsening of symptoms/poor response); Imaging: MRI/CT/X-ray of affected parts at end of treatment or early if worsening. SURGICAL INDICATIONS IN POTT'S SPINE: Progressive neurological deficit; Paraplegia of recent onset or severe paraplegia; Persistent pain with spinal instability; Spinal deformity - severe kyphotic deformity at presentation, or in children (<10 years of age) at high risk of progression of kyphosis with growth after healing of disease. In case of synovial fluid or cold abscess aspiration (against gravity), send samples for confirmation of TB in 3 ways: Two dry slide for AFB (ZN staining); Two samples in formalin for histopathological examination; Two samples in saline for culture followed by DST and/or NAAT. Confirmed cases to undergo HIV/blood sugar testing/parent counselling.
referralRedFlags: WHEN TO REFER: Suspected osteoarticular disease if essential investigations are not available; Diagnosis (microbiological or probable) not established by investigations; Surgery needed (imaging suggest compressive myelopathy, motor deficits); No improvement with appropriate treatment; DR TB diagnosed or high suspicion. Refer to higher centre for biopsy (Percutaneous CT-guided biopsy preferred or open biopsy) to confirm diagnosis & DST and rule out other diseases.
algorithmNotes: DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM: X-ray of Spine (AP/Lateral) may show end plate erosions, narrow/collapsed joint space, reduced vertebral height, paravertebral soft tissue shadow (early X-ray may be normal); X-ray joints (AP/Lateral): Erosions, sclerosis, calcification or narrow joint space; USG abdomen for Iliopsoas Abscess; USG joints for joint effusion and diagnostic aspiration; Chest X-ray, ESR, blood sugar, HIV; MRI Spine (100% sensitive) indicated in all cases (marrow edema; destruction of intervertebral disc/adjacent vertebral bodies/opposing end plates; prevertebral, paravertebral and/or epidural abscesses); MRI Joints: synovial proliferation with periarticular picture s/o TBA. ABBREV: AFB=Acid fast bacillus; AP=Antero-Posterior; CT=Computed Tomography; CXR=Chest X-ray; DR=Drug Resistant TB; DST=Drug Sensitivity Test; ESR=Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate; FNAC=Fine Needle Aspiration Cytology; GA=Gastric Aspirate; IP=Intensive Phase; LJ=Lowenstein Jensen; MGIT=Mycobacteria Growth Indicator Tube; MRI=Magnetic Resonance Imaging; NAAT; s/o=Suggestive of; STB=Spinal TB; TBA=TB Arthritis; USG=Ultrasonography; ZN=Ziehl Neelson.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: Fever with one or more of - Headache; Vomiting; Seizures; Irritability/Lethargy/Drowsiness; Loss of function e.g. recent onset deviation of eyes/mouth and/or weakness of arm/leg and/or altered mentation; Malaise/Anorexia/Weight loss. Symptoms are usually of 5 to 7 days duration with insidious onset, particularly with history of exposure to infectious TB in past 2 years. EXAMINATION: Assessment of sensorium (use any standardized scale including Glasgow Coma scale/AVPU); Full/bulging anterior fontanelle; Meningeal irritation - Neck stiffness, Kernig's sign & Brudzinski's sign; Examine eye if feasible for papilloedema/choroid tubercles/optic atrophy; Cranial nerves; Motor system including power, reflexes & plantar responses; Peripheral lymph nodes; Chest examination for signs of pulmonary involvement.
risk: Risk factors (Criterion 2): HIV infection; Severe acute malnutrition; Recent contact with infectious TB. Evidence of TB elsewhere (Criterion 3). NEUROIMAGING (CECT) shows: Hydrocephalus (ventricular dilatation), Thick basal exudates, Tuberculoma.
scores: DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA: CRITERION 1 (>=3 of the following): >=5 days of symptoms; TLC <15,000/cumm; CSF WBC 10-500/cumm; CSF sugar <50% of blood sugar; CSF lymphocytes >50%; Neuroimaging finding of one or more (Basal exudates, Hydrocephalus, Infarct, Tuberculoma). CRITERION 2: 2 or more risk factors for TBM (HIV infection, Severe acute malnutrition, Recent contact with infectious TB). CRITERION 3: Evidence of TB elsewhere. If 2 or more criteria met -> Start treatment. AFB seen / CSF NAAT +ve = Microbiologically confirmed TBM -> Start treatment.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: CBC; CSF examination (Cell count and differential; Sugar with simultaneous blood sugar; Protein; NAAT*; MGIT culture; Bacterial culture); HIV; Contrast enhanced CT scan of head; CXR; Gastric lavage/Induced sputum in patients where CXR is abnormal and CSF NAAT is negative (*ICMR/NTEP approved NAAT test, use 3-5 ml CSF if possible). DESIRABLE: MRI brain with contrast when CECT head is not contributory. OPTIONAL: CSF cryptococcal antigen; Contrast CT chest/abdomen to look for extracranial sites of infection.
drugs: Treatment per NTEP guidelines. ANTI TB DRUG REGIMEN: 2 HRZE and 10 HRE (in appropriate doses); Pyridoxine 10 mg/day. CORTICOSTEROIDS: Prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day for 4 weeks & then taper over 4 weeks (slower taper needed in some patients; equivalent dose of another steroid formulation may be used either injectable/oral).
management: OTHER SUPPORTIVE THERAPY: Care of unconscious child; Nasogastric feeding if indicated; Anti edema measures (mannitol/hypertonic saline/glycerol/acetazolamide); Anticonvulsants if seizures. SURGICAL THERAPY if indicated: External ventricular drain; VP shunt. Cases should be managed at least at a district hospital.
referralRedFlags: Early referral to Medical College/higher centre to be considered if: Unresponsive child/rapid deterioration indicating need for intensive care; No diagnosis after initial evaluation; Surgical treatment needed; MDR TB meningitis; No improvement/deterioration after 2-4 weeks of treatment; Need for ICU care.
algorithmNotes: DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM: Suspected TBM? -> Immediate investigations: CBC, HIV, CECT head, CSF (cell count incl differential, CSF sugar with blood sugar, protein, NAAT, bacterial culture), CXR. Evaluate Criterion 1 (>=3 of: >=5 days symptoms, TLC<15000, CSF WBC 10-500, CSF sugar <50% blood sugar, CSF lymphocytes>50%, neuroimaging basal exudates/hydrocephalus/infarct/tuberculoma), Criterion 2 (2+ risk factors: HIV/SAM/recent contact), Criterion 3 (evidence of TB elsewhere). 2 or more criteria met -> Start treatment. If NO -> continue investigations & management for partially treated bacterial meningitis; IF NOT BETTER: Repeat LP after 48-72 hours, Expand search for TB elsewhere, Consider MRI contrast if not done earlier; Re-review criteria 1,2,3 and see if >=2 fulfilled -> Yes -> Start treatment; No -> Does patient have falling CSF glucose/dropping sensorium? Have new focal deficit? -> Yes -> Start treatment; No -> Continue evaluation. AFB seen/CSF NAAT +ve -> Microbiologically confirmed TBM -> Start treatment. ABBREV: AFB=Acid-fast Bacillus; CBC=Complete Blood Count; CECT=Contrast Enhanced CT; CSF=Cerebro-spinal Fluid; CXR=Chest X-ray; HRZE=Isoniazid/Rifampicin/Pyrazinamide/Ethambutol; ICU=Intensive Care Unit; LP=Lumbar Puncture; MDR=Multi-drug Resistant; MGIT=Mycobacteria Growth Indicator Tube; MRI; NAAT=Nucleic Acid Amplification Test; NTEP=National TB Elimination Programme; TBM=Tubercular Meningitis; TLC=Total Leucocyte Count; VP=Ventriculo-peritoneal; WBC=White Blood Cells.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: Any organ in abdominal cavity, including gut lumen & peritoneum may be affected. PERITONEAL: Abdominal distension; Pain abdomen; Fever. INTESTINAL: Recurrent intestinal colic; Partial/incomplete intestinal obstruction; Chronic diarrhoea; Weight loss; Palpable mass abdomen; Lower gastrointestinal bleeding. ESOPHAGEAL: Dysphagia; Odynophagia; Hematemesis; Constitutional symptoms. GASTRO-DUODENAL: Gastric outlet obstruction; Gastrointestinal bleed. PERIANAL: Simple/Complex peri-anal fistula; Persistent discharge; Fistulae which recur after multiple surgeries; Constitutional symptoms. PANCREATIC: Abdominal pain; Obstructive jaundice; Dilated pancreatic or bile duct with (peri)-pancreatic mass or cyst; Constitutional symptoms. HEPATO-BILIARY: FUO; Hepatomegaly; Jaundice; Elevated ALP; SOL; Hepatic abscess.
risk: Categorize by site: Peritoneal, Intestinal, Esophageal, Gastro-duodenal, Perianal, Pancreatic, Hepato-biliary. HIV & blood sugar test should be done in all suspected patients as per NTEP guidelines. Differentiating Crohn's disease is challenging. Malignancy is a more important concern in pancreatic/hepatobiliary disease.
scores: DEFINITIVE Abdominal TB = Microbiological positive or Caseating granulomas. CLINICALLY DIAGNOSED Abdominal TB = Consistent clinical/radiological picture, Elevated ascitic ADA, granuloma on histology, exclusion of differential diagnosis. Supportive peritoneal markers: SAAG <1.1 g/dL; Ascitic ADA >39 U/L; ascitic cytology lymphocyte predominance with absence of malignant cells.
investigations: PERITONEAL TB: Ascitic tap - Cytology (lymphocyte predominance, absence of malignant cells); SAAG <1.1 gram/dL; Culture, NAAT; Ascitic ADA >39 U/L; Imaging - Ultrasound (Ascites, lymph nodes, bowel thickening) for tissue acquisition for cytology & microbiology. GASTROINTESTINAL TB: Imaging - CT or MR enterography (preferred): bowel thickening, strictures, necrotic LN (CT chest may also be done); Endoscopic biopsies - UGIE for esophageal/gastroduodenal / ileo-colonoscopy for intestinal TB is mandatory to acquire tissue; Histology - Caseating granuloma specific, confluent granuloma & ulcers lined by histiocytes suggestive; Microbiology - Culture, NAAT; Barium studies if endoscopy/CT unavailable. PERIANAL TB: Uncommon; Imaging - MRI pelvis/Endoanal ultrasound/EUS; Tissue acquisition for histology & microbiology. PANCREATIC HEPATOBILIARY TB: Malignancy more important concern; Needs imaging/Endoscopic ultrasound guided tissue acquisition; Refer to higher centre. HIV & blood sugar in all suspected per NTEP.
drugs: TREATMENT: Start treatment & follow-up as per NTEP guidelines. 1st line treatment for adults & children with abdominal TB: 2HRZE/4HRE. Extend duration of treatment in cases of inadequate response. (START ATT 2HRZE, 4HRE.)
management: After START ATT (2HRZE, 4HRE): CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (4 & 8 weeks): No fever/pain, disappearance of abdominal distension, weight gain; Assess ATT induced hepatotoxicity with serial LFTs. Assessment for clinically diagnosed cases: Peritoneal TB - Ultrasound for ascites (4-8 weeks); Intestinal TB - Ileo-colonoscopy (8-12 weeks). Improvement + -> Complete ATT. No improvement at 8 weeks -> Refer (Exclude MDR). No improvement -> Refer (Exclude alternative diagnosis). End of therapy: Document mucosal healing in colonic & upper GI TB; Imaging in small bowel disease to document healing. Refer for surgical management for complications [intestinal obstruction (due to strictures), perforation]; Consider endoscopic dilatation for accessible strictures; Refer for biliary drainage in case of Jaundice due to biliary obstruction (hepatobiliary obstruction/pancreatic TB).
referralRedFlags: If diagnosis unclear (Peritoneal) -> Refer for Laparoscopy. If diagnosis unclear (Gastrointestinal) -> Refer for further evaluation (differentiating Crohn's disease is challenging); Trial of Anti tubercular therapy with careful follow-up. Pancreatic/Hepatobiliary -> Refer to higher centre. No improvement at 8 weeks -> Refer (Exclude MDR). Refer for surgical management of complications.
algorithmNotes: EVALUATION by site: Peritoneal TB (ascitic tap + USG), Gastrointestinal TB (CT/MR enterography + endoscopic biopsy), Perianal TB (MRI pelvis/EUS + tissue), Pancreatic/Hepatobiliary TB (imaging/EUS guided tissue, refer higher centre). FOLLOW UP: Definitive Abdominal TB or Clinically Diagnosed Abdominal TB -> START ATT (2HRZE, 4HRE) -> Clinical assessment 4 & 8 weeks -> Improvement + -> Complete ATT; No improvement at 8 weeks -> Refer (Exclude MDR). ABBREV: ADA=Adenosine Deaminase; ALP=Alkaline phosphatase; ATT=Anti-Tubercular treatment; CT=Computed Tomography; EUS=Endoscopic ultrasound; FUO=Fever of Unknown Origin; GI=Gastro-intestinal; HRZE=Isoniazid/Rifampicin/Pyrazinamide/Ethambutol; LFT=Liver function tests; MDR=Multi-drug resistance; MR=Magnetic Resonance; Mtb=Mycobacterium Tuberculosis; NAAT=Nucleic Acid Amplification Test; NTEP=National TB Elimination Programme; SOL=Space occupying Lesion; SAAG=Serum Ascites Albumin Gradient; UGIE=Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: Swelling (>1 cm) in neck, armpit or groin (>2 cm) +/- redness, fluctuation, sinus discharge; May or may not be associated with fever, weight loss, night sweats or cough; History of similar swelling in the past / past history of tuberculosis; History of contact with a patient with a diagnosis of TB.
risk: COMPLICATIONS: Abscess formation; Rupture may lead to sinus formation. BCG LYMPHADENITIS (differential): Age usually <2 years; Axillary and/or supraclavicular LN on same side as BCG vaccination (usually given on left); No systemic symptoms in immunocompetent children. NAAT/AFB smear positivity cannot differentiate between BCG & MTB.
scores: RESPONSE TO THERAPY (at 3 months): Resolution - Decrease in size of LN with settling of systemic symptoms. Delayed response (Paradoxical reaction) - Increase in size of LN or new signs of inflammation (up to 3 months of starting treatment) OR appearance of new LN at same/other site. May require tissue cultures (if not done) to rule out treatment failure/resistance; Therapeutic drug monitoring to ensure adequate drug levels. If cultures reveal susceptible TB it is likely due to paradoxical worsening (may require anti-inflammatory agents if inaccessible / surgical removal if accessible).
investigations: DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM: Lymph node enlargement >1cm +/- systemic symptoms (tender LN, fluctuation, pain, fever, weight loss, night sweats). ACCESSIBLE: FNAC/LN aspirate -> Send for NAAT# (Xpert/TrueNat/ICMR or CTD approved test); +ve -> Treat as LN TB; -ve -> LN biopsy (refer if indicated) -> LN tissue for NAAT, Cultures$/LPA (MGIT/LJ). ABDOMINAL/MEDIASTINAL (INACCESSIBLE): Sputum/induced sputum -> Send for NAAT#; +ve -> Treat as LN TB; -ve -> Refer for FNAB -> Tissue for NAAT, Cultures/LPA.
drugs: Treatment: As per NTEP Guidelines (1st line ATT). BCG lymphadenitis: Wait & watch if small; if large & suppurative, repeated aspiration or rarely incision & drainage is required. Drug resistant TB -> Refer to DOTS plus centre. Paradoxical worsening may require anti-inflammatory agents (inaccessible) or surgical removal (accessible).
management: ASSESS RESPONSE TO THERAPY AT 3-4 MONTHS: Response at 3 months - Yes -> Symptom resolution -> Continue ATT. No -> Increase in size of LN ± signs of inflammation -> Check old culture report, Send new cultures if not available -> Susceptible TB (Paradoxical worsening / Therapeutic drug monitoring -> Refer to higher centre) OR Drug resistant TB (Refer to DOTS plus centre); Constitutional symptoms persist -> R/o alternate diagnosis.
referralRedFlags: REFER TO HIGHER CENTRE IF: Non responders; Needs treatment for Drug Resistance; Large Nodal Mass/Abscess requiring surgical intervention. Drug resistant TB -> Refer to DOTS plus centre. Inaccessible disease with -ve NAAT -> Refer for FNAB.
algorithmNotes: Lymph node enlargement >1cm +/- systemic symptoms -> ACCESSIBLE: FNAC/LN aspirate -> NAAT (+ve treat as LN TB; -ve LN biopsy -> tissue for NAAT, Cultures/LPA). INACCESSIBLE (abdominal/mediastinal): Sputum/induced sputum -> NAAT (+ve treat as LN TB; -ve refer for FNAB -> tissue for NAAT, Cultures/LPA). Treatment as per NTEP. ABBREV: ATT=Anti Tubercular Treatment; BCG=Bacille Calmette Guerin; CTD=Central TB Division; DOT=Directly Observed Treatment Short-course; FNAB=Fine Needle Aspiration Biopsy; FNAC=Fine Needle Aspiration Cytology; LJ=Lowenstein Jensen; LN=Lymph Node; LPA=Line Probe Assay; MGIT=Mycobacterial Growth Indicator Tube; MTB=Mycobacterium Tuberculosis; NAAT=Nucleic Acid Amplification Test; NTEP=National TB Elimination Programme; PCR=Polymerase Chain Reaction.

whenToSuspect: SPINE TUBERCULOSIS: Persistent localized pain in spine region >6 weeks, night pains; Local tenderness/cold abscess; Recent onset deformity in the back; Recent neurological deficit (better to refer); Persistent heaviness around the waist/Girdle pain; Fever, cough, weight loss & night pains; History of close contact with TB. OTHER JOINTS/BONES: Persistent localized pain & swelling >6 weeks; Mono-articular joint involvement; Discharging sinus (+/-); Fluctuant swelling with or without inflammation; Painful restriction of involved joint movements; Wasting around the area; Fever, cough, weight loss & night pains; History of close contact with TB.
risk: FINDINGS S/o TB - X-ray (spine): Regional Osteopenia, Decreased/obliterated disc space, Vertebral erosions +/- reduced vertebral height, Paravertebral shadow. MRI (Spine): Contiguous VB involvement with relatively preserved disc, Pre & paravertebral septate collection (Abscess), Epidural encroachment +/- intraosseous abscess. X-ray & MRI (extraspinal): Regional osteoporosis with bone destruction on X-rays, Inflammation of bone (T1WI & T2WI) +/- abscess on MRI. On-treatment worsening: Early (<3 months) = Paradoxical; Late (>4 months) = ?drug resistance.
scores: TB +ve on any test vs TB -ve on all tests pathway. Stop ATT after 12 months if all three parameters - clinical, Lab (ESR, CRP) & radiological - return to normal. In case of spine, decision to stop ATT taken by evaluating healed status on contrast MRI. Mildly elevated ESR, CRP (non specific tests) can be ignored.
investigations: PRESUMPTIVE TB: ESR, CRP, LFT, KFT, HIV; Imaging - X-rays spine & chest, (MRI, if feasible) -> Features s/o TB. In case of psoas abscess or soft tissue abscess: USG guided biopsy, bone/joint biopsy (open or percutaneous). SEND SAMPLES FOR: Gram staining & Culture sensitivity, AFB staining, HPE, NAAT, Solid & liquid mycobacterium culture, LPA if feasible. MTB +ve -> Start ATT; MTB -ve -> Refer to higher centre. Biopsy facilities not available: Refer. Lesion not accessible for biopsy: Refer.
drugs: Treatment & follow-up as per NTEP. TB +ve on any test: NAAT sensitive to Rifampicin - Start 4 drug first line ATT; GeneXpert resistant to Rifampicin - refer/culture for TB and sensitivity to other drugs. Continue for standard 12 months regime if clinical symptoms improving & CRP decreasing; Intensive 4 drug regime (not more than 4 months); Stop ATT after 12 months if clinical, Lab (ESR, CRP) & radiological return to normal; in spine, evaluate healed status on contrast MRI before stopping.
management: FOLLOW UP: Follow up every month with CRP, LFT during intensive phase; Follow up every 3 months during continuation phase with CRP/LFT; On treatment worsening of symptoms - Early (<3 months) Paradoxical, Late (>4 months) ?drug resistance; Any aberrance in course such as appearance of neural deficit: Refer. TB -ve on all tests: Index of suspicion high & ESR/CRP raised -> Refer; Index of suspicion low & CRP normal -> Reassurance; Clinical symptoms not improving & ESR/CRP increasing -> Refer (?Suspected drug resistance).
referralRedFlags: Refer to higher centre where advanced diagnostic & therapeutic facilities including surgical procedures are available. Recent neurological deficit - better to refer. Biopsy facilities not available / lesion not accessible for biopsy: Refer. MTB -ve on all tests: Refer to higher centre. Any appearance of neural deficit: Refer.
algorithmNotes: DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM: Presumptive TB -> ESR, CRP, LFT, KFT, HIV + Imaging (X-rays spine & chest, MRI if feasible) -> Features s/o TB -> biopsy (USG guided for psoas/soft tissue abscess; bone/joint biopsy open or percutaneous) -> Send samples (Gram stain & culture sensitivity, AFB staining, HPE, NAAT, solid & liquid mycobacterium culture, LPA) -> MTB +ve Start ATT; MTB -ve Refer to higher centre. ABBREV: AFB=Acid-fast Bacillus; ATT=Anti-Tubercular Treatment; CRP=C-Reactive Protein; ESR=Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate; HIV; HPE=Histopathological examination; KFT=Kidney Function Tests; LFT=Liver Function Tests; LPA=Line Probe Assay; MRI=Magnetic Resonance Imaging; NTEP=National TB Elimination Programme; USG=Ultrasonography; VB=Vertebral body; WNL=Within Normal Limits.

whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: Cough, fever, breathlessness or pleuritic chest pain; May be associated with weight loss, night sweats or difficulty lying down; Past history or history of contact with a patient with a diagnosis of tuberculosis. EXAMINATION reveals tachycardia, increased jugular venous pressure, hepatomegaly, ascites & peripheral edema; A pericardial friction rub and distant heart sounds present on cardiovascular examination. If clinical picture +/- heart US suggest pericarditis or pericardial effusion, refer for echo-cardiogram.
risk: COMPLICATIONS: Constrictive pericarditis (Kussmaul's sign = lack of inspiratory decline in JVP; elevated & distended jugular veins with prominent Y descent; Pericardial knock rare). Cardiac tamponade (Sinus tachycardia; Hypotension with narrow pulse pressure; Elevated JVP; Muffled heart sounds; Pulsus paradoxus = decrease in systolic BP by >10 mmHg on inspiration; Ascites). Myopericarditis (Abnormal ejection fraction with myocarditis & pericarditis - elevated cardiac enzymes & ST elevation on ECG). Effusive constrictive pericarditis (Mixed picture; main clue elevated JVP clinically & right atrial pressure on ECHO despite removal of pericardial fluid).
scores: Pericardial fluid interpretation: Exudate with pleocytosis but TB NAAT -ve OR ADA <40 U/L (work up HIV/viral) vs Exudate + TB NAAT +ve OR Exudate ± ADA >=40 U/L = Likely Pericardial TB. Constrictive physiology + pericardial thickness >4 mm -> ATT + steroids; assess Response at 11 weeks.
investigations: INVESTIGATION - Essential tests: Chest X-ray, ECG, Echocardiogram. Desirable: Cardiac enzymes, CT/MRI of Thorax, Pericardiocentesis, Pericardial biopsy. PERICARDIAL FLUID: TC, DC, LDH, sugar, protein; ADA, TB NAAT; AFB C/s. PERICARDIAL TISSUE for TB NAAT, AFB C/s, Histopathology (Granulomas ± caseation, TB NAAT +/-).
drugs: TREATMENT: Antitubercular therapy advised as per NTEP. Steroids recommended in large pericardial effusions, prominent pleocytosis & pericardial fluid with high inflammatory markers or early constriction. Give Prednisolone 60 mg/day for 4 weeks, 30 mg/day for 4 weeks, 15 mg/day for 2 weeks & 5 mg/day for 1 week. Total duration of systemic steroids is 11 weeks.
management: Constrictive physiology + pericardial thickness >4 mm -> ATT + steroids -> Response at 11 weeks? Yes -> Stop steroids but continue ATT; No -> Refer for pericardiectomy -> Pericardial tissue for TB NAAT, AFB C/s, Histopathology -> Continue ATT and check response. NON RESPONSE TO STEROIDS & ATT: Should prompt referral to a specialist center for confirmation of diagnosis; Should prompt evaluation for alternative causes of effusio-constrictive pericarditis (viral infections, systemic lupus erythematosus, primary effusion lymphomas or pericardial malignancies); Non response of cardiac symptoms to anti-tuberculous therapy - cardiac surgical evaluation may be required.
referralRedFlags: If clinical picture +/- heart US suggest pericarditis or pericardial effusion -> refer for echo-cardiogram. Non response to steroids & ATT -> refer to specialist center. Constrictive pericarditis with no response at 11 weeks -> Refer for pericardiectomy.
algorithmNotes: DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM: SUSPICION OF PERICARDIAL TB -> CXR & ECG. Cardiomegaly or Pericardial calcification (suggestive). CXR normal, ECG ST elevation -> ECHO ± CT thorax -> Pericardial effusion -> Pericardiocentesis -> Pericardial fluid (TC, DC, LDH, sugar, protein; ADA, TB NAAT; AFB C/s). Exudate with pleocytosis but TB NAAT -ve OR ADA <40 U/L -> HIV (+ve look for features of lymphoma; -ve R/o viral pericarditis: +ve symptomatic treatment, -ve therapeutic trial of ATT). Exudate + TB NAAT +ve OR Exudate ± ADA >=40 U/L -> Likely Pericardial TB -> Start ATT. Constrictive physiology + pericardial thickness >4 mm -> ATT + steroids -> Response at 11 weeks (Yes: stop steroids continue ATT; No: refer for pericardiectomy). CXR & ECG Normal -> Look for alternate diagnosis. ABBREV: ADA=Adenosine Deaminase; ATT=Antituberculous Therapy; CXR=Chest X-ray; ECG=Electrocardiogram; ECHO=Echocardiogram; JVP=Jugular Venous Pressure; NTEP=National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme.

whenToSuspect: HISTORY: Fever; Pleuritic chest pain; Cough; Breathlessness; Anorexia; Weight loss; History of TB contact. EXAMINATION: Dullness to percussion; Decreased/absent breath sound.
risk: Pleural effusion - consider Alternate etiology: transudative, exudative (bacterial, malignancy). Increase in pleural effusion can suggest Paradoxical reaction OR Drug-resistant TB OR Alternative etiology.
scores: PLEURAL FLUID ADA stratification (if sputum/pleural fluid AFB/NAAT negative): <40 U/L -> Pleural biopsy (treat based on biopsy & microbiology); 40-69 U/L -> if Age <40 years, fever, non-smoker, alternate etiology unlikely -> Initiate empiric anti-TB therapy (else Pleural biopsy); >=70 U/L -> Initiate empiric anti-TB therapy. Re-evaluate if no response in 4 weeks.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: CXR (to confirm pleural effusion); Sputum for AFB/NAAT; Refer immediately for pleural tap; Thoracentesis (ultrasound-assisted); Pleural fluid analysis - Cell count (total and differential), Protein, Glucose, Gram stain, Bacterial cultures, Stain for acid-fast bacilli, Adenosine deaminase (ADA), NAAT, Cytology evaluation. DESIRABLE: CT chest (before pleural biopsy); Pleural biopsy (image-guided/thoracoscopic) (if diagnosis is uncertain); Histopathology; MGIT.
drugs: TREATMENT AND RESPONSE: As per NTEP. Therapeutic pleural tap can be done under ultrasound assistance if the effusion is large and the patient is breathless. Empiric anti-TB therapy initiated per ADA stratification pathway.
management: FOLLOW UP: Most patients who respond to treatment will have improvement in their general condition by 2 weeks, and significant improvement in pleural effusion by 4-8 weeks; Disappearance of constitutional symptoms with decrease in pleural effusion suggests responsiveness to treatment; Increase in pleural effusion can suggest Paradoxical reaction or Drug-resistant TB or Alternative etiology; A follow up CXR at 4-8 weeks after starting ATT is useful to assess progress.
referralRedFlags: WHEN TO REFER: Facility for ultrasound assistance is not available; Diagnosis is not established after thoracentesis and facilities for pleural biopsy is not available; Drug-resistant TB is detected (according to NTEP); Worsening pleural effusion on follow up.
algorithmNotes: DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM: Pleural effusion -> Sputum examination and thoracentesis -> Sputum or pleural fluid positive for AFB or NAAT? Yes -> Start anti-TB treatment. No -> Pleural fluid ADA: <40 U/L -> Pleural biopsy -> treat based on results of biopsy and microbiological examination; 40-69 U/L -> Age <40 years, fever, non-smoker, alternate etiology unlikely? Yes -> Initiate empiric anti-TB therapy, No -> Pleural biopsy; >=70 U/L -> Initiate empiric anti-TB therapy. If no response in 4 weeks, re-evaluate. Alternate etiology (transudative/exudative bacterial/malignancy) -> Treat accordingly. ABBREV: ADA=Adenosine Deaminase; AFB=Acid-fast Bacilli; ATT=Anti Tubercular Treatment; CT=Computed Tomography; CXR=Chest Radiograph; MGIT=Mycobacterial Growth Indicator Tube; NAAT=Nucleic Acid Amplification Test; NTEP=National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme.

whenToSuspect: SUSPECT TBM WITH FOLLOWING CLINICAL FEATURES: Fever (Duration of 5 days or more); Headache & Vomiting; Altered sensorium; Cranial nerve palsy; Hemiparesis/any limb weakness; Seizures; Neck pain and stiffness. ALWAYS ENQUIRE FOR ASSOCIATED FEATURES: Constitutional symptoms; Active TB elsewhere; Past history of TB & ATT; Contact with TB patient; HIV seropositivity; Low socio-economic status; High endemic area. (Fever duration variable from days to weeks to months; clinical judgement & evaluation of other conditions also required; delaying work up for meningitis is not recommended.) IF TBM SUSPECTED: Refer to a centre where facility of evaluation (at least Lumbar puncture & CT scan) is available.
risk: SUSPECT COMMON COMPLICATIONS: Hydrocephalus and raised ICP (worsening headache with vomitings and/or altered sensorium); Optico-chiasmatic arachnoiditis (vision loss in one or both eyes +/- headache); Myelitis and/or arachnoiditis (paraparesis or quadriparesis +/- sensory disturbances, bladder involvement); Epidural abscess/Pott's spine (back pain and/or weakness in one/both lower limbs/bladder/bowel disturbances); Tuberculoma (seizures, new onset focal deficits, worsening headache); Seizures (consider tuberculoma/electrolyte or metabolic imbalance/cerebral infarction); Cerebral infarction and stroke (sudden onset weakness of one half of body, new onset confusion, altered mental status, seizures); Hyponatremia, SIADH (persistent or worsening mental status).
scores: CSF FINDINGS IN TBM vs OTHER MENINGITIS: Tubercular - Cell count usually <500, Lymphocytic (neutrophilic in some acute cases), Protein High, Sugar Low, confirm by AFB smear & culture, NAAT. Pyogenic - Cell count in thousands, Neutrophilic, Protein Moderately High, Sugar Very low, Gram stain/culture. Fungal - Cell count Variable, Lymphocytic, Protein High, Sugar Low, India Ink/Fungal Culture/Cryptococcal antigen. Viral - Cell count 50-500, Lymphocytic, Protein Normal to marginally high, Sugar Normal, PCR for specific virus. A negative NAAT result does NOT rule out TBM; decision to give ATT should be based on clinical features and CSF profile (NAAT: Xpert/TrueNat).
investigations: CLINICAL HISTORY & EXAMINATION: Symptoms type & duration, onset & progression; Headache, altered sensorium, focal deficits; Neck rigidity, Kernig's sign; Cranial nerve palsy; Fundus examination (papilledema). LABORATORY: CBC, ESR, CRP; LFT, RFT, Electrolytes; Blood sugar, HIV; Chest X Ray PA view; USG whole abdomen; Mantoux (optional). IMAGING: NCCT/CECT head - preferred as initial investigation; MRI brain (and spine if indicated) in selective cases. CSF mandatory (perform CT head prior to CSF if papilledema &/or focal deficits). CSF EVALUATION - ESSENTIAL: Cell count & type; Protein; Sugar (& corresponding blood sugar); NAAT; Grams stain; Bacterial culture; AFB stain; AFB culture/sensitivity; India Ink; Cryptococcal antigen. DESIRABLE: Fungal smear & culture; Cytopathology. OPTIONAL: Wet mount; VDRL; Toxoplasma PCR; Viral PCR. Common neuroimaging findings: Basal exudates and Hydrocephalus, Tuberculomas, Infarction, Arachnoiditis, Pott's spine.
drugs: ANTI-TUBERCULAR TREATMENT: Intensive Phase - 2 months of RHZE or RHZS; Continuation phase - 3 drugs RHZ for at least 10 months (treatment duration may be increased per clinician decision; RHZ used in view of high toxicity of Ethambutol on TBM as per specialty experts). STEROIDS: Preferably Dexamethasone 0.4 mg/kg/day intravenously in 3-4 divided doses during hospital stay; if not feasible, oral Dexamethasone 0.4 mg/kg/day in divided doses or oral Prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day in single morning dose; Discharge on oral steroids on tapering doses for a total duration of 8-12 weeks.
management: FOLLOW UP: Regular follow up essential every month for at least first 3 months & can be increased thereafter till treatment is stopped; Monitor liver function tests & any other features of drug toxicity; Observe for clinical improvement or any deterioration; Closely observe for development of any complications (hydrocephalus/raised ICP, arachnoiditis, myelitis, tuberculoma, seizures, infarction, hyponatremia/SIADH).
referralRedFlags: IF TBM SUSPECTED: Refer to a centre where facility of evaluation (at least Lumbar puncture & CT scan) is available. Complications (hydrocephalus/raised ICP, vision loss, paraparesis, stroke) may need referral for specialist/surgical care.
algorithmNotes: DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM: Suspect TBM with clinical features -> refer to centre with LP & CT scan -> Evaluation at centre of care (clinical history & exam, laboratory, imaging NCCT/CECT head preferred, CSF mandatory essential analysis). CSF findings differentiate TBM from pyogenic/fungal/viral meningitis. A negative NAAT does not rule out TBM; decision for ATT based on clinical features and CSF profile. If some CSF tests unavailable, store in sterile container, refrigerate & transport in icebox. ABBREV: ATT; CBC=Complete Blood Count; CECT=Contrast Enhanced CT; CRP=C Reactive Protein; CSF=Cerebrospinal Fluid; E=Ethambutol; ESR; H=Isoniazid; ICP=Intracranial pressure; LFT=Liver function tests; MRI; NAAT; NCCT=Non-contrast CT; NTEP; PCR; R=Rifampicin; RFT=Renal function tests; S=Streptomycin; SIADH=Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone; TBM=Tubercular meningitis; Z=Pyrazinamide.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: Presence of ulcer or discharging sinus over lymph node, bone & joints; Persistent asymptomatic reddish/reddish brown lesion of >6 months duration which may show scarring; Persistent warty or verrucous lesion of >6 months duration. OTHER FEATURES: Extracutaneous involvement; Lymph node & lungs involvement; Other organ systems involvement (bones, GIT & CNS). ETIOLOGY: M.tuberculosis, M.bovis, NTM, BCG (rarely). TYPES OF CLINICAL DISEASE: Primary Inoculation tuberculosis; Tuberculosis verrucosa cutis; Lupus vulgaris; Scrofuloderma; Acute miliary tuberculosis; Orificial tuberculosis; Metastatic tuberculous Abscess (tuberculous gumma); Normal primary complex-like reaction; Postvaccination; Perforating regional adenitis; Lichen scrofulosorum; Papulonecrotic tuberculid; Facultative tuberculids; Nodular vasculitis & Erythema nodosum.
risk: CASE DEFINITION: A) Confirmed case - M.tuberculosis complex identified by either culture or NAAT, or histology shows typical morphology; Full course of ATT which led to complete clearance of lesions. B) Probable case - Typical skin lesion with no positive features/investigation as mentioned above (A).
scores: Decision: Histology diagnostic and/or culture +ve -> Treat for cutaneous TB. Histology equivocal and culture -ve -> Mantoux Test (Positive often strong -> treat). Histology negative & culture -ve -> Do not Treat (Mantoux Negative). Strong clinical suspicion -> Start ATT.
investigations: INVESTIGATIONS: Histopathology - Granulomas with epithelioid histiocytes & Langerhans-type giant cells; FNAC if indicated; IGRA/PCR - Not recommended for diagnosis. SCREENING FOR SYSTEMIC INVOLVEMENT: Examination - Lymph node to be examined (FNAC), Other organ system if indicated; Essential - Chest X-ray, FNAC from the indurated part of lesion; Desirable - Histopathology, Culture from biopsy sample (Not swab). FNAC can be done if facilities for skin biopsy are not available.
drugs: TREATMENT: Similar to Pulmonary TB as per NTEP. DR-TB to be kept in mind. No role of steroids, oral or topical, in management of cutaneous TB.
management: FOLLOW UP: 1st follow-up after 4-6 weeks; majority improves. If no response after 8 weeks -> consider Alternate diagnosis/DR-TB; refer to higher centre.
referralRedFlags: If no response after 8 weeks -> Alternate diagnosis/DR-TB; refer to higher centre. Refer to dermatologist with expertise in cutaneous TB for biopsy/diagnosis.
algorithmNotes: DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM: Suspected TB case based on clinical signs (ulcers/discharging sinuses over LN/bones/joints; persistent asymptomatic raised reddish/reddish brown lesion >6 months which may show scarring; persistent warty skin lesion >6 months). Skin biopsy* for histopathology ± culture + CXR -> refer to dermatologist with expertise in cutaneous TB. Histology diagnostic and/or culture +ve -> Treat for cutaneous TB; Histology equivocal and culture -ve -> Mantoux Test (Positive often strong -> treat); Histology negative & culture -ve -> Do not Treat (Negative). Strong clinical suspicion -> Start ATT. (*FNAC can be done if facilities for skin biopsy are not available.) ABBREV: ATT=Anti-Tubercular treatment; BCG=Bacille Calmette Guerin vaccine; CNS=Central Nervous system; CT=Cutaneous Tuberculosis; CXR=Chest X-ray; DR-TB=Drug resistant Tuberculosis; FNAC=Fine needle aspiration cytology; GIT=Gastro-intestinal tract; IGRA=Interferon Gamma Release assay; LN=Lymph node; NAAT=Nucleic acid amplification test; NTEP; NTM=Non-Tuberculous Mycobacterium; PCR=Polymerase chain reaction test.

whenToSuspect: SUSPECT - consider following symptoms in history: H/O infertility (primary or secondary); Chronic lower abdominal or pelvic pain; Amenorrhoea or other menstrual disturbances; Abnormal vaginal discharge; Constitutional symptoms of TB (low grade fever, weight loss etc.); Other symptoms related to extra-genital TB (abdominal, CNS, bone and lymph nodes etc.). CLINICAL EXAMINATION: General Physical Examination; Pelvic Examination (cervical growth, uterine size and mobility, adnexal tenderness & mass).
risk: SUGGESTIVE FINDINGS IN FGTB (Imaging/Radiological): HSG (avoid in acute phase) - blocked fallopian tubes usually cornual, tobacco pouch appearance of tubes, beaded tubes, filling defect in uterine cavity (Asherman syndrome); USG - cogwheel appearance of tubes, uterine cavity may show thin diffuse endometrium with irregular borders; CT/MRI - tubo-ovarian mass. ENDOSCOPY: Hysteroscopy (tubercles, pale endometrium & endometrial adhesions); Laparoscopy (direct visualization of tubercle like lesions on uterus, tubes and other pelvic organs including peritoneum & caseous nodules). Menstrual blood should not be used for NAAT.
scores: FEMALE GENITAL TB (STEPWISE DECISION): Clinical history; General physical and pelvic examination; Pelvic ultrasound; HSG as indicated in infertility. DEFINITE FGTB needing ATT if any positive: AFB microscopy positive; AFB culture positive; Gene Xpert or other NAAT +ve; Histopathological demonstration of epithelioid granuloma. PROBABLE FGTB needing ATT if any positive: clinical findings/suspicion of TB with tubo-ovarian masses on imaging; with laparoscopic findings of beaded tubes/caseous nodules/tubercles/adhesions/hydrosalpinx & pyosalpinx; with hysteroscopic findings of tubercles/caseous nodules/pale endometrium/intrauterine adhesions. NEGATIVE FGTB (No ATT): No microbiological/histological/radiological/laparoscopic & hysteroscopic evidence of FGTB.
investigations: Abdominal and Pelvic USG (TVS): Uterus, adnexa & pelvis evaluated preferably by transvaginal scan; Endometrial cavity & vascularity looked carefully with colour Doppler. SPECIFIC INVESTIGATIONS: Endometrial sampling or biopsy with Pipelle device or Karman cannula (4 mm) for microbiological & histopathological examination; Endoscopy - Hysteroscopy & laparoscopy to evaluate uterus, adnexa & other pelvic organs along with lower abdomen, Laparoscopic biopsy from peritoneum or abdominal/pelvic lesions; MTB diagnosis from biopsy specimen (endometrium & other tissues) by Smear microscopy (AFB smear) & culture, Gene Xpert or other NAAT, HPE of biopsy specimen. In addition, standard investigations for TB to be carried out. Menstrual blood should not be used for NAAT.
drugs: TREATMENT: Treatment of FGTB should be as per NTEP. Patients requiring specific treatment such as infertility, Asherman syndrome & tubo-ovarian mass etc. should be referred to higher centres.
management: FOLLOW UP (flexible depending on clinical presentation and response to ATT): 1 month - Clinical Evaluation (General & Gynaecological); 3 months - Clinical Evaluation (General & Gynaecological); 6 months - Clinical Evaluation & Investigations (endometrial biopsy, hystero-laparoscopy & USG as needed).
referralRedFlags: Patients requiring specific treatment such as infertility, Asherman syndrome & tubo-ovarian mass etc. should be referred to higher centres.
algorithmNotes: STEPWISE DECISION: Clinical history -> general physical & pelvic exam -> pelvic ultrasound -> HSG as indicated in infertility. DEFINITE FGTB (start ATT) if AFB microscopy/culture positive, Gene Xpert/NAAT +ve, or epithelioid granuloma on histopathology. PROBABLE FGTB (start ATT) if clinical suspicion + tubo-ovarian masses on imaging, or laparoscopic (beaded tubes/caseous nodules/tubercles/adhesions/hydrosalpinx/pyosalpinx) or hysteroscopic (tubercles/caseous nodules/pale endometrium/intrauterine adhesions) findings. NEGATIVE FGTB -> No ATT. ABBREV: AFB=Acid-Fast Bacilli; ATT=Anti-Tuberculosis Therapy; CNS; CT=Computed Tomography; FGTB=Female Genital TB; FNAC=Fine-needle Aspiration Cytology; HSE=Histopathology Examination; HSG=Hysterosalpingography; MRI=Magnetic Resonance Imaging; MTB=Mycobacterium Tuberculosis; NAAT=Nucleic Acid Amplification Test; NTEP=National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme; PCR=Polymerase Chain Reaction; TVS=Transvaginal Scan; USG=Ultrasonography.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT (+/- SYSTEMIC SYMPTOMS: Fever, Weight loss, Night sweats). PRESENTING SYMPTOMS - Urinary Tuberculosis: LUTS (frequency, urgency and nocturia) with dysuria and/or haematuria for at least 2 weeks; Not responding to a 3-7 day course of antibiotics. Male Genital Tuberculosis (MGTB): Scrotal pain or swelling for 2 weeks or more; Not responding to a 7-14 day course of antibiotics; Discharging sinuses in the scrotum; Rarely infertility.
risk: Deranged renal function or HDN/HDUN (hydronephrosis/hydroureteronephrosis) -> Refer to higher centre/Urologist. Watch for complications at each follow-up: Severe LUTS suggestive of small capacity bladder; Deteriorating renal function.
scores: EXTENSION CRITERIA (extend treatment duration up to 9-12 months): Extension packets of infection, concurrent smear positive cavitary pulmonary disease, CNS involvement, Delay in positive cultures converting to negative. MDR/RR or XDR-TB: Treatment as per NTEP guidelines.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: CBC; KFT; LFT; ESR; Chest X ray; Urine R/M and Culture; Urine for AFB x 3-5 days (early morning, first void); Urine Culture for MTB x 3-5 days (early morning, first void); NAAT assay (GeneXpert MTB/RIF); USG KUB; USG Scrotum (for MGTB only). SPECIFIC INVESTIGATIONS: If normal renal function - IVP/CT urography; If deranged Renal Function - MR Urogram, Retrograde Pyelography, Nephrostogram. OPTIONAL (if other tests inconclusive with high suspicion of GUTB): FNAC/Biopsy from accessible mass lesions or fluid collections; Cystoscopic biopsy of Genitourinary tract.
drugs: TREATMENT - DRUG SUSCEPTIBLE TB (DS-TB REGIMEN): 2 MONTHS H,R,E,Z + 4 MONTHS H,R,E. EXTENSION CRITERIA (duration can be increased up to 9 to 12 months): Extension packets of infection, concurrent smear positive cavitary pulmonary disease, CNS involvement, delay in positive cultures converting to negative. MDR/RR OR XDR-TB: Treatment as per NTEP guidelines.
management: FOLLOW UP: At 8 weeks - Resolution of systemic symptoms, improved urinary symptoms, repeat culture if baseline culture positive; After completion of ATT - Repeat culture if baseline culture positive; Repeat imaging - if partial or impending ureteric stricture; Watch at each follow-up visit for: Severe LUTS suggestive of small capacity bladder, Deteriorating renal function.
referralRedFlags: Deranged renal function / HDN/HDUN -> Refer to higher centre/Urologist. MDR/RR or XDR-TB -> Treatment as per NTEP guidelines. Impending ureteric stricture or small capacity bladder may need urological intervention.
algorithmNotes: DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM: Essential investigations -> if Deranged renal function or HDN/HDUN -> Refer to higher centre/Urologist. SPECIFIC INVESTIGATIONS: If normal renal function -> IVP/CT urography; If deranged renal function -> MR Urogram, Retrograde Pyelography, Nephrostogram. OPTIONAL (if inconclusive with high suspicion of GUTB): FNAC/Biopsy, Cystoscopic biopsy. ABBREV: ATT=Anti-tubercular treatment; CT=Computed Tomography; CBC=Complete Blood Count; CXR=Chest X-Ray; DJS=Double J Stent; DS-TB=Drug Susceptible Tuberculosis; E=Ethambutol; ESR=Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate; H=Isoniazid; HDN=Hydronephrosis; HDUN=Hydroureteronephrosis; IVP=Intravenous Pyelogram; LFT=Liver Function Test; LUTS=Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms; MDR=Multi Drug Resistant; MTB=Mycobacterium Tuberculosis; MR=Magnetic Resonance; NAAT=Nucleic Acid Amplification Test; NTEP; RFT=Renal Function Test; R=Rifampicin; RR=Rifampicin Resistant; USG KUB=Ultrasonography Kidney Ureter Bladder; XDR=Extensively Drug Resistant; Z=Pyrazinamide.

whenToSuspect: OCULAR SYMPTOMS: Blurred vision; Redness; Photophobia; Pain in the eye; Floaters; Flashes of lights. Refer to Ophthalmologist for detailed examination. EYE CARE FACILITY should have: Mandatory - Slit lamp, ophthalmoscope (direct or indirect), intraocular pressure assessment device; Preferred - Fundus camera, Fundus fluorescein angiogram (FFA), Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT). EXAMINATION OF EYES - Clinical signs: Assess visual acuity; Anterior chamber cells, Keratic precipitates, Synechiae, Irregular pupil, RAPD; Complicated cataract, high or very low intraocular pressure; Vitritis, Pars plana exudates, Retinal vasculitis, Retinitis, Choroiditis, Optic nerve head swelling.
risk: Presentations: Granulomatous anterior uveitis; Intermediate uveitis; Panuveitis/Posterior uveitis; Retinal vasculitis; Choroiditis. Referral triggers: Not confident to treat; Vision threatening; Non-response to treatment; Side effects due to treatment; Atypical reaction.
scores: Mantoux Test (standardised tuberculin units): 10 mm induration considered positive. Investigations to rule out other causes of clinical presentation.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: CXR for healed/active pulmonary TB. DESIRABLE: Mantoux Test (standardised tuberculin units) - 10 mm induration considered positive. OPTIONAL: CT Chest (if available) for healed/active pulmonary TB. IMAGING OF EYE (ascertaining diagnosis, extent of disease & follow up, teleconsultation): Retinal photographs using fundus camera; Optical coherence tomography scans (if available); Fluorescein angiograms (if available). Investigations to rule out other causes of clinical presentation.
drugs: TREATMENT: ATT - 2 months of RHEZ + 7 months of RH depending on clinical response & side effects to treatment; Add pyridoxine 10 mg/day. CORTICOSTEROIDS: Topical steroids eye drops for severe/anterior chamber inflammation; Systemic corticosteroids for severe inflammation in consultation with Uveitis expert. For treatment in children refer to paediatrician.
management: MONITORING AND FOLLOW UP: Frequency of follow up - 1-2 weeks in 1st month followed by monthly for 3 months & then 3 monthly; Eye - Clinical grading of inflammation using fundus photographs & OCT scans (if available); Steroids - Topical: Monitor IOP, cataract and any signs of bacterial/fungal infection; Systemic steroids: Monitor body weight, blood sugar & blood pressure.
referralRedFlags: REFERRAL TO HIGHER CENTRE: Not confident to treat; Vision threatening; Non-response to treatment; Side effects due to treatment; Atypical reaction.
algorithmNotes: When to suspect (ocular symptoms) -> Refer to Ophthalmologist for detailed examination -> Examination of eyes (clinical signs) -> Investigations (CXR essential; Mantoux desirable, 10 mm positive; CT chest optional; eye imaging - fundus photos, OCT, FFA) + rule out other causes -> ATT (2 months RHEZ + 7 months RH) + pyridoxine 10 mg/day + corticosteroids (topical +/- systemic) -> Monitoring & follow up. ABBREV: ATT=Antitubercular treatment; E=Ethambutol; H=Isoniazid; IOP=Intraocular pressure; OCT=Optical coherence tomography; R=Rifampicin; RAPD=Relative Afferent Pupilary Defect; Z=Pyrazinamide.

condition: Microbiological Work-up for Adult Extrapulmonary TB
whenToSuspect: Reference workflow for collecting and processing samples for microbiological work-up in all suspected adult extrapulmonary TB, before starting treatment. Diagnostic algorithm of NTEP to be followed in the Microbiology labs.
risk: LOGISTICS (sample collection & transportation): Collect samples in sterile containers before treatment is started (mention date & time of collection); Specimens to be sent in sterile saline (NOT in formalin); Establish linkages between peripheral centres, District centres and Tertiary centre/medical colleges/IRL (specify contact person, department, contact number during referrals); Transportation at 2-8 C; Maximum time for transportation in cold chain should be 5 days from time of collection; Quantity of sample mentioned is only for microbiological work-up (histopathology, cytology, ADA, glucose, protein need additional sample); PHC and CHC should perform smear microscopy and molecular diagnostic tests; If sample less than 500 ul, refer directly to Tertiary centre/medical colleges/IRL for culture (residual sample in needle and syringe can be used for smear); MGIT to be used for culture, if not available LJ medium should be used.
scores: REJECTION OF SAMPLES: Unlabelled samples (all specimens MUST be labelled & have a unique patient identifier); No collection date indicated; Insufficient quantity / no specimen in container; Damaged - specimen leaked or broken in transit; Samples greater than 3 days old at room temperature and more than 5 days in refrigeration are unreliable specimens for testing. Precious samples should be transported to IRL.
investigations: SAMPLE BY TYPE (sample / amount): Osteoarticular/Musculoskeletal - Tissue, pus, synovial fluid; Biopsy specimen 1cm x 1cm (sample any caseous area, add 0.5-2 ml sterile saline to avoid drying); Optimum fluid/pus 2-3 ml (swabs are sub-optimal). Pleural - Pleural fluid 10-15 ml. Meningitis - CSF 3-5 ml. Lymphadenitis - FNA/Biopsy; specimen 1cm x 1cm (add 0.5-2 ml saline); Optimum FNA sample 2 ml. Urogenital - Urine; entire early morning urine sample (3-5 days). Female Genital - Endometrial curettage/biopsy; specimen 1cm x 1cm (add 0.5-2 ml saline). Gastrointestinal - Tissue, pus, peritoneal fluid; Biopsy 1cm x 1cm (at least 6 biopsies including any caseous area, add 0.5-2 ml saline); Optimum fluid/pus 5-10 ml. MICROBIOLOGICAL PROCEDURES: AFB Smear Microscopy (except in GI TB); NAAT; Culture (MGIT, if not available LJ medium); Drug susceptibility testing if culture is positive.
drugs: Not applicable (microbiology reference). Microbiological procedures: AFB Smear Microscopy (except GI TB), NAAT (Xpert MTB/RIF / TrueNat), Culture (MGIT or LJ medium), Drug susceptibility testing if culture positive.
management: PROCESSING: Preferably immediately; if not possible, store/transport at 2-8 C; If sample is adequate, attempt molecular testing at that site; If biopsy is not possible or at an inaccessible site, refer patient to the next higher centre immediately where appropriate test can be done; If sample obtained at a centre is inadequate, send directly to nearest Tertiary centre/medical colleges/IRL.
referralRedFlags: If sample less than 500 ul -> refer directly to Tertiary centre/medical colleges/IRL for culture. If biopsy not possible or inaccessible site -> refer patient to next higher centre immediately. Inadequate sample -> send directly to nearest Tertiary centre/medical colleges/IRL. Precious samples should be transported to IRL.
algorithmNotes: WORKFLOW: Sample collection (sterile container, sterile saline not formalin, label + date/time) -> Processing (immediate or store/transport 2-8 C, max 5 days cold chain) -> Microbiological procedures (AFB smear except GI TB, NAAT, Culture MGIT/LJ, DST if culture +ve). ABBREV: ADA=Adenosine Deaminase; AFB=Acid fast bacilli; CHC=Community Health Centre; FNA=Fine needle aspirate; LJ medium=Lowenstein Jensen medium; MGIT=Mycobacteria Growth Indicator tube (liquid culture medium for mycobacteria); NAAT=Nucleic Acid Amplification Tests (Xpert MTB/RIF/TrueNat); PHC=Primary health Centre; TB=Tuberculosis; IRL=Intermediate Reference laboratory.

condition: ATT Drug Dosages (Drug Sensitive TB - NTEP)
whenToSuspect: Reference for Drug Sensitive-TB treatment as per NTEP. For all TB patients (public or private), follow Standards for TB care in India guidelines. Principle (except confirmed DR-TB): administer daily FDC of 1st line ATT in appropriate weight bands, under direct observation. Regimen for Drug-Sensitive TB cases: 2HRZE/4HRE (for H & R sensitive TB cases and cases where the sensitivity pattern cannot be established).
risk: During treatment if weight of the patient increases by >5 kg and crosses the next weight band, the patient should be given the next higher weight band FDC drugs. Children & adolescents up to 18 years weighing <39 kg are treated using paediatric weight bands; those weighing >39 kg are treated with adult weight bands.
scores: REGIMEN FOR DS-TB: Intensive Phase (IP) = 2 HRZE, 56 doses (8 weeks of H, R, Z, E under direct observation); Continuation Phase (CP) = 4 HRE, 112 doses (16 weeks of H, R, E; only Pyrazinamide stopped in CP). CP needs to be extended up to 24 weeks in certain forms of TB like CNS TB, Skeletal TB; in disseminated TB or slow response the treating physician may extend on a case-to-case basis.
investigations: Not applicable (drug dosing reference). FDC = Fixed Dose Combinations (two or more active ingredients in fixed doses). In NTEP for Adults: 4-FDC (given in IP) consists of HRZE and 3-FDC (given in CP) consists of HRE.
drugs: ADULT first-line drug dosages: Isoniazid (H) 5 mg/kg daily (4-6 mg/kg); Rifampicin (R) 10 mg/kg daily (8-12 mg/kg); Pyrazinamide (Z) 25 mg/kg daily (20-30 mg/kg); Ethambutol (E) 15 mg/kg daily (12-18 mg/kg); Streptomycin (S) 15 mg/kg daily (15-20 mg/kg) [only in certain situations like TB meningitis or if a first line drug needs replacing due to ADR]. Pyridoxine 10 mg/day. ADULT FDC by WEIGHT CATEGORY [Intensive Phase H75/R150/Z400/E275 ; Continuation Phase H75/R150/E275] number of tablets: 25-34 kg = 2 / 2; 35-49 kg = 3 / 3; 50-64 kg = 4 / 4; 65-75 kg = 5 / 5; >75 kg = 6 / 6. PAEDIATRIC first-line drug dosages: Isoniazid (H) 7-15 mg/kg (max 300 mg/day); Rifampicin (R) 10-20 mg/kg (max 600 mg/day); Pyrazinamide (Z) 30-40 mg/kg (max 2000 mg/day); Ethambutol (E) 15-25 mg/kg (max 1500 mg/day). PAEDIATRIC dispersible FDC by WEIGHT BAND [Intensive HRZ 50/75/150 + E100 ; Continuation HR 50/75 + E100] tablets: 4-7 kg = 1 + 1 ; 8-11 kg = 2 + 2 ; 12-15 kg = 3 + 3 ; 16-24 kg = 4 + 4 ; 25-29 kg = 3+1A* + 3+1A* ; 30-39 kg = 2+2A* + 2+2A* (*A = Adult FDC HRZE 75/150/400/275, HRE 75/150/275; added in higher weight bands >25 kg). Pyridoxine 10 mg/day. Paediatric dispersible FDCs (flavoured): Rifampicin 75 mg + Isoniazid 50 mg + Pyrazinamide 150 mg; Rifampicin 75 mg + Isoniazid 50 mg. Dispersible loose drugs: Ethambutol 100 mg; Isoniazid 100 mg.
management: SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS: Adult TB Meningitis - IP 2 months RHZE or RHZS, CP 3 drugs RHE for at least 10 months, steroids (Dexamethasone 0.4 mg/kg/day), follow up monthly first 3 months. Adult abdominal TB - extend duration if inadequate response; surgical management for complications; biliary drainage if obstruction. Intra-ocular TB - 2 months RHEZ + 7 months RH, pyridoxine 10 mg/day, topical/systemic steroids. Paediatric TB meningitis - 2 HRZE and 10 HRE, Prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day for 4 weeks then taper. Paediatric osteoarticular - 2HRZE + 10HRE. Paediatric abdominal TB - steroids not recommended; manage SAM/malnutrition; surgery for obstruction/perforation; do not start empirical ATT with isolated recurrent/chronic abdominal pain without danger signs or chronic diarrhoea without proper evaluation.
referralRedFlags: During treatment if weight increases by >5 kg crossing the next weight band, upgrade to the next higher weight band FDC. Streptomycin only in certain situations (e.g. TB meningitis) or to replace a first line drug due to ADR. Extend CP for CNS/Skeletal/disseminated TB.
algorithmNotes: DS-TB REGIMEN 2HRZE/4HRE: IP = 8 weeks (56 doses) HRZE under direct observation; CP = 16 weeks (112 doses) HRE (Pyrazinamide stopped). Extend CP up to 24 weeks for CNS TB / Skeletal TB. Weight-band based daily FDC dosing for adults and paediatrics. ABBREV: ADR=Adverse drug reaction; ATT=Anti-Tubercular treatment; CNS=Central Nervous system; CP=Continuation phase; DR-TB=Drug resistant Tuberculosis; DS-TB=Drug sensitive Tuberculosis; E=Ethambutol; FDC=Fixed dose combination; H=Isoniazid; IP=Intensive phase; MRI; NTEP=National TB Elimination Programme; R=Rifampicin; S=Streptomycin; SAM=Severe acute malnutrition; TB=Tuberculosis; Z=Pyrazinamide.

whenToSuspect: DIAGNOSIS OF ATT HEPATITIS - Clinical symptoms present (abdominal pain, vomiting, unexplained fatigue, yellowing of sclera, altered sensorium): AST/ALT increased to 3 times of baseline/ULN; Jaundice (Bilirubin 2 ULN). No clinical symptoms: AST/ALT increased to 5 times of baseline/ULN.
risk: RISK FACTORS FOR ATT HEPATITIS: History of underlying liver disease (jaundice, ascites, GI bleeding); Physical findings suggestive of liver disease (Splenomegaly, ascites, icterus, edema); Alcoholism; Hypoalbuminemia and Malnutrition; Elevated aminotransferases at baseline; HIV; IV drug abuse; Elderly age.
scores: CHILD PUGH (CTP) SCORE: Bilirubin <2 mg/dl=1, 2-3=2, >3=3; Albumin >3.5 gm/dl=1, 2.8-3.5=2, <2.8=3; INR <1.7=1, 1.7-2.2=2, >2.2=3; Ascites Absent=1, Slight=2, Moderate=3; Encephalopathy Absent=1, Grade 1-2=2, Grade 3-4=3. ATT SELECTION FOR UNDERLYING LIVER DISEASE: Child A Cirrhosis (Score 1-6)/Stable Liver disease - 9 months of therapy with HRE OR 2 months HRE followed by 7 months of HR; Child B Cirrhosis (Score 7-10)/Advanced Liver Disease - One hepatotoxic drug regimen: Two months of therapy with INH (or) RIF with ETH & aminoglycoside, followed by 10 months of INH/RIF & ETH; Child C Cirrhosis (Score 11-15)/Very advanced liver disease - No hepatotoxic drug, 18 to 24 months treatment using combination of ETH, FQL, cycloserine & aminoglycoside/capreomycin; In Acute hepatitis - Avoid hepatotoxic drugs, ATT with non-hepatotoxic drugs if urgent ATT required, Wait till improvement in liver function if no urgent need.
investigations: Exclude viral hepatitis (HBsAg, Anti-HCV, IgM-antiHAV, IgM-AntiHEV); Get PT/INR, Ultrasound liver. For risk factors: evaluate for underlying liver disease (HBsAg, Anti-HCV, Ultrasound). LFT monitoring throughout. Non-resolution of LFT abnormalities -> exclude alternative causes of liver disease.
drugs: REINTRODUCTION OF ATT HEPATOTOXIC DRUGS (only if ALT and AST <2 ULN & normal bilirubin): Start one drug at a time (helps identify the culprit). SEQUENTIAL: Initiate Rifampicin 10 mg/kg -> 1 week repeat LFT -> Initiate Isoniazid 5 mg/kg -> 1 week repeat LFT -> Initiate Pyrazinamide 25 mg/kg. INCREMENTAL: Initiate Rifampicin 150 mg/day (gradually increase dose by day 4) -> Initiate Isoniazid 100 mg/day at day 8 (gradually increase by day 11) -> Initiate Pyrazinamide 500 mg/day on day 15 (gradually increase by day 18). If ATT hepatitis severe (liver failure, coagulopathy or altered sensorium): Pyrazinamide reintroduction may be avoided. Alternative: low dose of one drug followed by full dose after three days. Duration of ATT: count only when full ATT is started. When hepatitis diagnosed - Stop all hepatotoxic drugs; if urgent ATT needed change to non-hepatotoxic drugs (Fluoroquinolones, ethambutol & aminoglycosides).
management: PATIENT TO BE STARTED ON ATT: Risk factors present? Yes -> Evaluate for underlying liver disease (HBsAg, Anti-HCV, Ultrasound); Chronic Liver disease + -> Intensive education & counselling, Modified ATT may be needed based on Child Pugh Status, LFT monitoring; No CLD or Cirrhosis -> Start ATT, counsel about symptoms of ATT Hepatitis. No risk factors -> Start ATT, counsel about symptoms. WHEN HEPATITIS DIAGNOSED: Exclude viral hepatitis, get PT/INR, Ultrasound liver -> Stop all hepatotoxic drugs (Need urgent ATT: change to non-hepatotoxic drugs Fluoroquinolones/ethambutol/aminoglycosides; No need for urgent ATT: repeat LFT after a week & reintroduce; Non-resolution: exclude alternative causes). URGENT ATT (life/organ threatening): Sputum + Pulmonary TB, TB meningitis or CNS TB, Pericardial TB, life-threatening forms (e.g. Intestinal TB with obstruction), Ocular TB, Joint or Spinal TB. NO need for urgent ATT: Sputum-ve Pulmonary TB, TB lymphadenitis, Tubercular pleural effusion, Tubercular ascites, Intestinal TB, Genitourinary TB, Bone TB.
referralRedFlags: Jaundice and coagulopathy/encephalopathy -> Refer to higher center immediately. Child C cirrhosis / very advanced liver disease -> specialist hepatology input (no hepatotoxic drugs). Non-resolution of LFT abnormalities -> exclude alternative causes of liver disease.
algorithmNotes: WORKFLOW: Assess risk factors before starting ATT -> if present evaluate liver disease & use Child Pugh to choose modified ATT. Diagnose ATT hepatitis by AST/ALT >=3x ULN with symptoms or >=5x ULN without symptoms, or jaundice -> stop hepatotoxic drugs, exclude viral hepatitis, get PT/INR & USG liver -> reintroduce (sequential or incremental) once AST/ALT <2 ULN & bilirubin normal. HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY GRADES 0-4 (0 normal; 1 restless/sleep disturbance/tremors; 2 lethargy/disorientation to time/asterixis; 3 somnolent/disoriented to place/rigidity; 4 unrousable coma/decerebrate). ABBREV: ALT=Alanine transaminase; AST=Aspartate transaminase; ATT=Anti-tubercular treatment; ETH=Ethambutol; FQL=Fluoroquinolone; GI=gastro-intestinal; HAV=Hepatitis A virus; HBsAg=Hepatitis B surface Antigen; HCV=Hepatitis C virus; HEV=Hepatitis E virus; HRE=Isoniazid/Rifampicin/Ethambutol; IgM=Immunoglobulin M; INH=Isoniazid; INR=International normalized ratio; IV=Intravenous; LFT=Liver function tests; PT=Prothrombin time; RIF=Rifampicin; ULN=Upper limit of normal.

whenToSuspect: ACNE: Comedones (open-blackheads, closed-whiteheads) +/- any one or more of - Papules, pustules; Painful nodules containing pus; Cysts; Scarring; Sites: Face and/or trunk; Symptoms: None/pain/pricking. ROSACEA: Photosensitivity; Persistent erythema, telangiectasia +/- papules and pustules in absence of comedones; Sites: Convexities of the face (cheeks, forehead, nose, chin); Bulbous enlargement of nose (rhinophyma); Symptoms: Sensitivity to hot and spicy food, and emotional triggers. USEFUL: Acne and rosacea can co-exist; treat acne early so scarring is minimal; in Indian scenario consider topical corticosteroid induced acne and rosacea.
risk: CLINICAL EVALUATION CLUES: History of cosmetics/topical steroid use (alone or with fairness creams); Age of onset usually around puberty (onset before 8 years requires hormonal evaluation); Recent drug intake (>fortnight/month) - drug induced acne; Contact with cutting oils/halogens (iodides/bromides); Menstrual irregularities/weight gain/hirsutism - look for PCOS; Premenstrual flare; Persistence or onset/recurrence after 25 years; Dry and gritty eyes - ocular rosacea (needs ophthalmology). ACNE VARIANTS: Acne conglobata, Drug induced acne, Topical corticosteroid induced acne, Hormonal acne, Occupational acne, Acne excoriee, Acne fulminans, Hidradenitis suppurativa. ROSACEA DIFFERENTIALS: Connective tissue disease (lupus/dermatomyositis), Steroid induced rosacea, Seborrheic dermatitis, Contact/atopic dermatitis.
scores: Acne severity: Mild-moderate vs Moderate not controlled with topicals vs Severe nodulocystic. Rosacea severity: Mild papulopustular vs Moderate (not controlled with topicals) vs Severe/phymatous/ocular.
investigations: Hormonal evaluation if onset before 8 years or PCOS suspected (menstrual irregularities, hirsutism, weight gain). Before Isotretinoin for severe nodulocystic acne: document normal lipid profile and liver functions. Ocular rosacea: ophthalmologic evaluation.
drugs: ACNE: Stop unsupervised topical corticosteroid and cosmetic use on face; clean face with soap/mild cleanser; Mild-moderate: 2.5% Benzoyl peroxide gel OR 0.025% Tretinoin cream OR 1% Adapalene gel +/- Clindamycin gel for local application at night; Moderate not controlled with topicals: Cap Doxycycline 100mg OD for minimum 4-6 weeks; Severe nodulocystic: Isotretinoin at tertiary level after normal lipid profile & liver functions; Acne fulminans: Prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day and refer to higher center; Hormonal acne: anti-androgens at tertiary level; Drug induced acne: stop offending drugs if feasible. ROSACEA: Avoid triggers (alcohol, caffeine, spicy food, cosmetics, topical steroids); Photoprotection; Mild papulopustular: topical Azelaic acid 15% OR Metronidazole 1% OR Ivermectin 1%; Moderate not controlled with topicals: Cap Doxycycline 100mg OD for minimum 4-6 weeks; Severe/phymatous/ocular: refer to specialist for low dose Isotretinoin/interventional treatment.
management: Treat acne early to prevent scarring. Mild/moderate managed at primary level with topicals +/- oral Doxycycline. Severe nodulocystic acne, hormonal acne, severe/phymatous/ocular rosacea -> tertiary/specialist. Stop unsupervised topical corticosteroid use.
referralRedFlags: Severe nodulocystic acne -> Isotretinoin at tertiary level. Acne fulminans (fever and bone pains with severe necrotic acne) -> start Prednisolone and refer to higher center. Hormonal acne -> tertiary level anti-androgens. Severe/phymatous/ocular rosacea -> refer to specialist.
algorithmNotes: Distinguish ACNE (comedones present) from ROSACEA (no comedones; persistent erythema/telangiectasia on facial convexities). Treat by severity; consider topical-corticosteroid-induced acne/rosacea in Indian setting. ABBREV: PCOS=Polycystic ovarian syndrome.

whenToSuspect: DEFINITION: Excessive hair shedding and/or sparsening leading to visible scalp that may be either patchy or diffuse. HISTORY: Duration and age of onset of hair loss; whether patchy or diffuse scalp involvement and if other hair-bearing areas affected; relevant medical history; hair care practices including cosmetic procedures. EXAMINE scalp for scarring vs non-scarring by looking for: Loss of skin markings; Loss of hair follicle ostia; Pigmentary changes. GENERAL HAIR CARE: Hair fall up to 100 per day may be normal; regular cleaning with plain shampoo; avoid hair oil application and damaging mechanical/chemical hair care procedures.
risk: NON-SCARRING ALOPECIA (smooth bald areas with small black intact follicles): CONGENITAL (Congenital hypotrichosis, Monilethrix, Trichorrhexis nodosa, Loose anagen hair syndrome, Woolly hair syndrome - refer to tertiary centre) vs ACQUIRED [Patchy: Telogen effluvium, Alopecia areata, Tinea capitis, Trichotillomania; Diffuse (Pattern hair loss): Androgenetic alopecia in males, Female pattern hair loss]. SCARRING ALOPECIA (fibrosis & damage to follicles - all must be referred to dermatologist): PRIMARY [Pustules/boggy lesions: Folliculitis decalvans/Dissecting cellulitis of scalp; Pigmentary changes: Lichen plano pilaris, Discoid lupus erythematosus] vs SECONDARY (trauma/burns/dermatoses like morphea, pemphigus, neoplasms affecting scalp).
scores: Androgenetic/female pattern hair loss graded by extent of thinning/recession; <50% vs >50% scalp involvement guides alopecia areata management. High regrowth potential with non-scarring alopecia; guarded regrowth potential with scarring alopecia.
investigations: Telogen effluvium: Hemogram, and if indicated serum ferritin, TSH. Tinea capitis: KOH mount for confirmation if available. Female pattern hair loss with hyperandrogenism: hormonal workup to rule out PCOS or virilising tumors of ovary/adrenal. Scarring alopecia: Trichoscopy, scalp biopsy for histopathology; Lichen plano pilaris/DLE - direct immunofluorescence, workup to rule out SLE.
drugs: TELOGEN EFFLUVIUM: reassurance & treat underlying disorder; if persistent consider 1 ml of 5% topical Minoxidil once a day. ANDROGENETIC ALOPECIA (males): 1 mL of topical 5% Minoxidil solution BD; if effective continue; addition of oral Finasteride 1 mg/day may be considered; refer for hair transplant if required. FEMALE PATTERN HAIR LOSS: 1 ml of 5% Minoxidil solution OD; treat underlying condition; oral anti-androgens (Finasteride, Spironolactone, cyproterone acetate with oral contraceptives) may be added; severe non-responsive -> tertiary care. ALOPECIA AREATA: <50% scalp - topical 0.05% Betamethasone lotion OD, intralesional Triamcinolone once in 2-4 weeks (5 mg/ml for scalp, 2.5 mg/ml for beard/eyebrows) for limited involvement, topical Minoxidil 5% OD; >50% scalp or facial/body hair or occipital margin - refer to tertiary centre for immunosuppressants (oral steroids mini pulse, Methotrexate or Cyclosporine). TINEA CAPITIS: Oral antifungals - Griseofulvin 10-20 mg/kg, Terbinafine 5 mg/kg for 6-8 weeks; topical antifungal shampoos; avoid comb sharing. TRICHOTILLOMANIA: counselling and referral to psychiatrist if needed. SCARRING ALOPECIA: Folliculitis decalvans - long term oral antibiotics Doxycycline/Clindamycin for 10-12 weeks, consider low dose oral steroids, Isotretinoin; Lichen plano pilaris - oral steroid mini pulse +/- Methotrexate/Azathioprine/Cyclosporine; DLE - photoprotection, topical steroids, Hydroxychloroquine 5mg/kg/day after baseline ocular examination, usually required 6-12 months.
management: Non-scarring acquired alopecia managed at primary level (Minoxidil, treat underlying cause); refer congenital and extensive/refractory cases to tertiary care. All scarring alopecia must be referred to a dermatologist for histological confirmation & management. Trichotillomania needs psychiatric referral.
referralRedFlags: Congenital alopecia -> tertiary centre. Alopecia areata >50% scalp / facial-body hair / occipital margin -> tertiary centre for immunosuppressants. Pattern hair loss severe non-responsive -> tertiary care. ALL scarring alopecia -> dermatologist. Trichotillomania -> psychiatrist.
algorithmNotes: Examine scarring vs non-scarring (loss of follicular ostia/skin markings/pigment change = scarring). Non-scarring: congenital vs acquired (patchy: telogen effluvium/alopecia areata/tinea capitis/trichotillomania; diffuse: pattern hair loss). Scarring: primary (folliculitis decalvans, lichen plano pilaris, DLE) vs secondary -> refer. ABBREV: DLE=Discoid lupus erythematosus; KOH=Potassium hydroxide; PCOS=Polycystic ovarian syndrome; SLE=Systemic lupus erythematosus; TSH=Thyroid stimulating hormone.

whenToSuspect: 1. IMPETIGO: Wet yellow brown crusts overlying red inflamed skin; types Non bullous (commoner) & bullous; usually children; common sites face (perinasal, perioral) > extremities; extensive with scabies/atopic eczema. 2. ECTHYMA: Black thick crust (eschar) with underlying ulcer & surrounding redness & edema. 3. FOLLICULITIS: Hair follicle centred pustule/papule (rule out non-bacterial causes: oils, chemicals, waxing, epilation, occlusive dressing; recurrent may indicate nasal Staph aureus carriage or human-pet transmission). 4. FURUNCLE: Painful follicle centric nodule/pus point/impending bulla/ulcer with marked surrounding erythema, edema and induration. 5. CARBUNCLE: Confluence of multiple closely spaced furuncles + pus draining from multiple follicular orifices; commonly nape of neck > breasts, buttocks in uncontrolled diabetes. 6. CUTANEOUS ABSCESS: Painful, warm, red, fluctuant skin swelling. 7. CELLULITIS: Acute spreading infection involving subcutaneous tissue; painful, red, tender, diffuse swelling mostly involving limbs. 8. ERYSIPELAS: More superficial, bright red, edematous, painful area with clear demarcated edge; sites lower extremities>face; often with lymphangitis/lymphadenopathy; broken skin/portal of entry. 9. STAPHYLOCOCCAL SCALDED SKIN SYNDROME (SSSS): Superficial peeling of skin due to toxin producing strains of staphylococcus.
risk: GENERAL PRINCIPLES: Skin hygiene, handwashing/local hygiene, avoid oil application, adequate nutrition; for recurrent/severe lesions evaluate for nasal carriage, diabetes, underlying skin conditions (scabies, atopic dermatitis); in immunocompromised/diabetics consider need for gram negative coverage. SUSPECT NEED FOR MRSA COVERAGE IF: Poor immune status; Severe systemic signs; MRSA infection elsewhere; No improvement in 48-72 hours; Penetrating trauma. RED FLAGS: Temperature >100.4 F, WBC >12,000 or <4000/uL, heart rate >90 bpm, or respiratory rate >24/min may indicate sepsis; Severe pain followed by deceptive absence may indicate necrotising fasciitis; Dark discoloration of overlying skin.
scores: CELLULITIS/ERYSIPELAS severity: MILD (typical, no focus of purulence) -> outpatient oral antibiotics; MODERATE (with systemic signs of infection) -> hospitalization & parenteral antibiotics; SEVERE (poor response to oral, immunocompromised, deeper infection like bullae/skin sloughing, or systemic signs like hypotension/organ dysfunction) -> empiric broad spectrum IV.
investigations: Swabs for gram staining and pus culture are desirable. Blood cultures and biopsies not routinely recommended but useful with co-morbid conditions (malignancy on chemotherapy, immunocompromised states, animal bites etc.). COMPLICATIONS: Subcutaneous abscesses, blistering (often haemorrhagic), ulceration, tissue necrosis, myositis, septicemia.
drugs: IMPETIGO: Topical antibiotics for 5 days; oral antibiotics for extensive/numerous lesions, lymphadenopathy or outbreaks. ECTHYMA: Oral antibiotics for 7 days; gentle crust removal after soakage with sterile saline; topical antibiotics over exposed ulcer. FOLLICULITIS: Topical antibiotics for 5 days; oral for multiple lesions; Paracetamol 500mg/Ibuprofen 400mg SOS. FURUNCLE (small): oral + topical antibiotics. CARBUNCLE / large furuncle / abscess: Incision and drainage/debridement; ancillary antibiotics if systemic inflammatory signs; severely ill - Inj Ceftriaxone 2g BD OR Inj Amoxicillin-clavulanate 1.2gm TDS, alternatively Inj Clindamycin 600-900mg TDS. CELLULITIS/ERYSIPELAS: MILD - oral antibiotics, elevation, anti-inflammatory (Ibuprofen 400mg BD, Indomethacin 75mg BD); MODERATE - Inj Ceftriaxone 2g BD OR Inj Amoxicillin-clavulanate 1.2gm TDS, alternatively (penicillin allergy) Inj Clindamycin 600-900mg IV TDS; SEVERE - empiric broad spectrum IV (Vancomycin + Piperacillin/tazobactam), surgical debridement, sensitivity-based modification. SSSS: preferably in-patient; mild - oral anti-staphylococcal antibiotics; severe - IV antibiotic; consider MRSA coverage. PHARMACOTHERAPY (prefer beta-lactams): Amoxycillin 500mg TDS (25-50 mg/kg/day), Cloxacillin 500mg QID (50mg/kg/day), Cephalexin 250-500mg QID (25-50 mg/kg/day), Amoxicillin-clavulanate 625mg TDS; if penicillin allergic - Erythromycin 500mg QID (40 mg/kg/day), Clindamycin 300-600mg BD/TID (20mg/kg/day). Topical: Mupirocin cream 2%, Fusidic acid cream 2%, Framycetin cream 1%. Nasal carriers: 2% Mupirocin ointment for 5 days a month. Oral for suspected/confirmed MRSA: Cotrimoxazole 2 DS tablets BD, Doxycycline 100mg BD, Minocycline 200mg BD, Linezolid 600mg BD. IV for MRSA: Vancomycin 15 mg/kg BD, Linezolid 600mg BD, Clindamycin 600-900mg TDS.
management: Categorize disease severity (mild/moderate/severe) for cellulitis/erysipelas to decide outpatient oral vs inpatient parenteral vs broad-spectrum IV. Incision & drainage for carbuncle/abscess. SSSS usually remits within a week in children but high mortality in adults - preferably manage in-patient.
referralRedFlags: Severely ill -> hospitalization & IV antibiotics. Necrotising fasciitis suspicion (severe pain then deceptive absence, dark skin discoloration) -> urgent surgical referral. Sepsis red flags (fever, abnormal WBC, tachycardia, tachypnea). SSSS in adults -> high mortality, in-patient care. Suspect/confirm MRSA -> escalate per coverage criteria.
algorithmNotes: Nine entities (impetigo, ecthyma, folliculitis, furuncle, carbuncle, cutaneous abscess, cellulitis, erysipelas, SSSS) each with specific management; categorize cellulitis/erysipelas severity. Antibiotic susceptibility patterns may vary with region and time. ABBREV: MRSA=Methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus; WBC=White Blood Cells; SSSS=Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome; SOS=as needed.

condition: Cutaneous Adverse Drug Reactions - Part A (FDE, Maculopapular)
whenToSuspect: Cutaneous adverse drug reactions (cADR) are undesirable clinical manifestations to a drug (predictable or unanticipated, with/without systemic involvement). COMMON TYPES: Non-severe - Fixed drug eruption (FDE), Maculopapular/Exanthematous reactions; Severe - DRESS, Acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis, Angioedema/Anaphylaxis, Erythema multiforme/SJS/TEN. PART A covers FDE & Maculopapular/Exanthematous. COMMON PRESENTATION: Sudden onset itchy rash, symmetrically distributed, spreads rapidly; may have had previous similar allergic reaction. FDE: distinctive eruption recurs at same site on drug re-exposure; acute FDE dusky red-violaceous plaques +/- vesiculation/bullae; common sites lip, genitalia, proximal extremities, low back, sacrum; resolves with persistent hyperpigmentation. MACULOPAPULAR/EXANTHEMATOUS: abrupt erythematous maculopapular eruption starting on trunk, spreads symmetrically to extremities; mild systemic symptoms (pruritus, low grade fever, mild eosinophilia).
risk: RECOGNIZE DANGER SIGNS: Mucosal lesions, purpuric lesions, skin tenderness, bullous lesions (peeling/sloughing of skin); Systemic: high grade fever, jaundice, decreased urine output. RED FLAG SIGNS (maculopapular): mucosal involvement, purpuric lesions, bullous lesions, skin tenderness, facial/acral edema, erythroderma, systemic symptoms (high grade fever, hepatitis, renal involvement, significant eosinophilia).
