Common FDE drugs: Sulfonamides, tetracyclines, quinolones, NSAIDS, dapsone, antimalarials, barbiturates, nitroimidazoles. Maculopapular drugs: co-trimoxazole, cephalosporins, anti-tubercular drugs, aminopenicillins, quinolones, dapsone, NSAIDS, anticonvulsants, nevirapine, abacavir, allopurinol, leflunomide.
scores: TIMELINES FOR DRUG REACTIONS: 5-15 minutes - Anaphylaxis, urticaria, angioedema; Few hours - Reactivation of fixed drug eruption; Few hours to 2 weeks - Maculopapular exanthem, erythema multiforme, SJS, TEN, first episode of FDE; 4-12 weeks - DRESS syndrome, Dapsone syndrome, anticonvulsant induced hypersensitivity syndrome.
investigations: HISTORY ELICITATION: prior adverse drug reaction; in polypharmacy list all recently introduced drugs/dose increments (keep all in suspect list); concomitant viral infection or illnesses affecting drug metabolism/excretion (e.g. CKD). Maculopapular labs: CBC (eosinophilia supports diagnosis), LFT, serum creatinine, urine M/E; tertiary additional ANA, HIV, skin biopsy. DRUG PROVOCATION TEST: in absence of any reliable in vitro test, oral drug challenge is the only way to detect the responsible drug; take written consent; contraindicated in active illness or pregnancy; assess risk-benefit; a negative test only indicates not allergic at the time of challenge; patch test has low sensitivity (do not rely on in severe cADR).
drugs: FDE - PHC: Withdraw the drug; bullous/moist/oozy lesions - normal saline compresses; Topical Betamethasone valerate cream BD for cutaneous lesions; Antihistamine Tab Pheniramine maleate 25 mg BD/TID for itching; review in 1 week. Secondary: continue primary care; if severe add short course oral steroids Prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg for 3-5 days. Tertiary: admit if generalized & severe; histopathology in doubtful cases; severe oral mucositis - parenteral steroids; provocation tests after resolution (1-6 months). MACULOPAPULAR - Primary: withdraw suspect drug(s); Pheniramine maleate 25 mg TID; Calamine lotion; refer if symptoms persist or red flags. Secondary: confirm by history & clinical findings; admit if red flags; severe cases Prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day x 5-7 days (after ruling out infection). Tertiary: admit if red flags; consider DRESS if rash progressing or organ involvement.
management: Action required: prompt urgent care at a specialised centre - withdrawal of all drugs, initiation of oral or IV corticosteroids, care of the eye, evaluation of secondary infection/sepsis. Managed across PHC (withdraw drug, topical steroid, antihistamine), Secondary (short oral steroids if severe), Tertiary (admit, parenteral steroids, provocation testing).
referralRedFlags: REFER TO HIGHER CENTER IF: Atypical symptoms; Uncertain diagnosis; Severe reaction (multiple lesions, bullae, severe mucosal lesions, systemic symptoms). Danger signs (mucosal/purpuric/bullous lesions, systemic involvement) warrant urgent specialised care.
algorithmNotes: Withdraw the offending drug(s) immediately (except life saving drugs if not suspected); take measures to prevent similar events (record, educate, allergy card); recognize danger signs. Drug provocation when avoidance impractical (e.g. polypharmacy/antitubercular therapy), under observation, daytime. ABBREV: cADR=Cutaneous adverse drug reactions; FDE=Fixed drug eruption; DRESS=Drug Rash with Eosinophilia & Systemic Symptoms; SJS=Stevens Johnson Syndrome; TEN=Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis; ANA=Antinuclear antibody.

condition: Cutaneous Adverse Drug Reactions - Part B (DRESS, SJS/TEN)
whenToSuspect: PART B covers DRESS / Stevens Johnson syndrome / Toxic epidermal necrolysis. DRESS SYNDROME: potentially life threatening systemic adverse reaction; onset 2-6 weeks after start of drug intake (up to 12 weeks); rash may continue to progress weeks to months after discontinuation; commonly with anticonvulsants, dapsone, allopurinol, abacavir, leflunomide, minocycline. WHEN TO SUSPECT DRESS: exposure to high risk drug; fever (>38-40 C), rash, leukocytosis with eosinophilia, lymphadenopathy, hepato-renal dysfunction; rash involves >50% body surface area, facial edema, desquamation or dusky erythema; occasionally pustules and targetoid lesions. SJS/TEN: acute severe mucocutaneous reactions with epidermal detachment and/or tenderness, widespread erythematous lesions with central dusky erythema or vesiculation, often with high grade fever; usually with aromatic anticonvulsants, allopurinol, nevirapine, abacavir, NSAIDs, co-trimoxazole.
risk: SJS/TEN CLASSIFICATION by extent of detachment (% BSA) with widespread atypical targets/erythematous macules: SJS <10% (atypical targets Present); SJS-TEN 10-30% (Present); TEN >=30% (Present); TEN without SPOTS >=10% (atypical targets Absent). Atypical targets = red macules with purpuric vesiculations/crusted centers. D/d Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, pemphigus.
scores: SCORTEN PROGNOSTIC FACTORS (1 point each): Age >40 years; Tachycardia >120 bpm; Neoplasia; Initial detachment >10%; Serum urea >60 mg/dL; Serum bicarbonate <20 mmol/L; Blood glucose >252 mg/dL. SCORTEN SCORE -> Estimated mortality: 0-1 = 3%; 2 = 12%; 3 = 35%; 4 = 58%; >=5 = >90%. Assess prognosis with SCORTEN within 24 hours of presentation and repeat 3 days later.
investigations: Chest X-ray; ECG; Laboratory CBC, LFT, KFT, electrolytes, magnesium, phosphate, lactate; Blood gas analysis; Microbiology - pus culture from infected areas and blood culture; Skin biopsy - not usually required unless diagnosis in doubt; Optional - in TEN biopsy and direct immunofluorescence useful to rule out SLE and pemphigus. DRESS monitoring: CBC, absolute eosinophil count (optional), LFT, renal function monitored at least weekly; CXR, ECG and ECHO to rule out myocarditis.
drugs: DRESS - Primary: withdraw drugs; assess vitals, stabilise & refer; symptomatic relief antihistamines, emollients; do not add unnecessary medications. Secondary: First line systemic steroids Prednisolone 0.5-2 mg/kg slow taper after symptoms/signs resolve (over months if needed); Antihistamines Pheniramine 25 mg TID, bland emollients (liquid paraffin); refer if severe organ involvement. Tertiary: Second line Cyclosporine (if renal function normal); multidisciplinary; intensive care if severe liver failure/hemophagocytic syndrome/GI bleeding/multiorgan failure. SJS/TEN - Secondary: stabilise, nutrition & fluid replacement; skin care dilute potassium permanganate baths/saline compresses/Chlorhexidine baths; detached epidermis left in situ covered with non-adherent dressing; topical antibiotics (Mupirocin/Fucidin) on sloughed areas; oral care Chlorhexidine rinse, soft paraffin on lips; eye care refer to ophthalmologist; broad spectrum antibiotics if sepsis/secondary infection. Adjuvant systemic therapy (within first 24-72 hours of onset): systemic steroids limited to early phase, Prednisolone 1-2 mg/kg or equivalent IV Dexamethasone for 3-7 days; Cyclosporine 3-5 mg/kg for 10-14 days (with monitoring).
management: Primary: see DRESS primary care (withdraw drugs, stabilise, refer). Secondary: weekly bloods, organ monitoring (CXR/ECG/ECHO), systemic steroids, skin/oral/eye care. Tertiary: admit in specialized units within dermatology wards if vitals stable; barrier nursing; if SIRS/sepsis or shock admit to ICU; long term follow up for ophthalmic, skin and respiratory tract complications. If skin detachment >10% refer to a center with an ICU familiar with management of skin failure; if <10% follow treatment as described.
referralRedFlags: DRESS with severe organ involvement (liver/renal/cardiac) -> refer to tertiary for multidisciplinary intensive care. SJS/TEN with skin detachment >10% -> refer to center with ICU familiar with skin failure. SIRS/sepsis/shock -> ICU. Any drug belonging to any medicinal system can cause cADR.
algorithmNotes: DRESS suspected by high risk drug + fever + eosinophilia + lymphadenopathy + hepato-renal dysfunction. SJS/TEN classified by % epidermal detachment; prognosticate with SCORTEN within 24h, repeat at day 3. ABBREV: DRESS=Drug Rash with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms; SJS=Stevens Johnson Syndrome; TEN=Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis; BSA=Body Surface Area; SCORTEN=Severity-of-illness score for TEN; SIRS=Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome; SLE=Systemic Lupus Erythematosus; ICU=Intensive Care Unit.

whenToSuspect: DEFINITION: Superficial fungal infection caused by dermatophytes; affects keratin bearing structures i.e. skin, nails and hair. TINEA CORPORIS/CRURIS: itchy scaly lesion on skin; typically annular (ring-like) lesions with variable scaling and erythema; always examine groins, buttocks, nails, palms and soles; ask for lesions in other family members. TINEA PEDIS/MANUUM: dermatophytic infection of palms (Tinea manuum) and soles (Tinea pedis); generally unilateral; toe webs commonly involved; scaling along creases of palms/soles or diffuse; occasionally dried vesicles; scaly margin at wrist/insteps; coexistent nail involvement common. ONYCHOMYCOSIS: discoloration of nail with build up of keratinous debris under nail plate; generally affects isolated nails asymmetrically; whole nail may crumble in advanced cases; look for involvement of palms/soles; ask for diabetes and signs of peripheral vascular disease.
risk: ADVISE ALL PATIENTS: take treatment regularly and never stop without consultation after obtaining some relief (to prevent relapse); do not self medicate; do not ever use any steroid containing OTC creams. Lesions are often modified by self application of topical steroids/combination products - the ring may be incomplete, scaling minimal, pigmentation prominent. Do not use any steroid containing cream.
scores: Extent guides therapy: limited involvement -> topical antifungal; extensive disease -> systemic (oral) antifungal. Onychomycosis: limited disease <50% nail surface (not reaching lunula) -> topical lacquer; otherwise systemic. Tertiary: treat till complete clinical and mycological cure (KOH negativity).
investigations: For doubtful cases: KOH microscopy of scales shows typical septate hyphae; culture and other advanced methods not required in routine practice. Tertiary care: send for culture, speciation and antifungal susceptibility testing if available; treat till KOH negativity.
drugs: TINEA CORPORIS/CRURIS - Topical (limited): Clotrimazole 1%/2% cream BD, Miconazole 2% cream BD, Terbinafine 1% cream BD, Ketoconazole 2% cream BD; extensive disease - oral antifungals (or creams over most bothersome lesions in addition to systemic). Children (weight based oral): Terbinafine 3-6 mg/kg/day or <20kg 62.5mg, 20-40kg 125mg, >40kg 250mg; Fluconazole 6 mg/kg/day; Griseofulvin 10-20 mg/kg/day. TINEA PEDIS/MANUUM - Systemic (always treat till all lesions resolved, 3-8 weeks or more; follow up 2-weekly): Tab Terbinafine 250mg BD, Tab Griseofulvin 500mg BD, Tab Fluconazole 50-150 mg OD; pruritus relief Tab Cetirizine 10mg HS or Tab CPM 4mg TDS; topical over limited areas Luliconazole cream OD, Sertaconazole cream BD; systemic Cap Itraconazole 100-200 mg/day, Tab Terbinafine 250mg BD. Pregnancy: preferably topical (Miconazole/Clotrimazole cream); if required fluconazole may be preferred. ONYCHOMYCOSIS - Topicals (limited <50% nail, not reaching lunula): Amorolfine 5% nail lacquer once a week or Ciclopirox 8% nail lacquer thrice a week; Systemic: Tab Terbinafine 250mg BD (6 weeks for fingernails, 12 weeks for toenails) OR Cap Itraconazole 100 mg BD for 12 weeks OR 200 mg BD/day for seven days a month (2 pulses for fingernails, 3 for toe nails).
management: GENERAL MEASURES - DOS: daily bath with regular soap and normal temperature tap water, dry skin well after bath, wash clothes separately in hot water and dry inside out in the sun. DON'TS: do not share towels and clothes, do not re-wear clothes before washing. Tinea pedis: avoid walking barefoot in public/community bathing areas, wash feet, dry toe clefts, wear cotton socks. Onychomycosis: keep nails trimmed, use separate clippers, avoid cosmetic nail procedures (it may take several months after treatment for a normal-looking nail). Ensure treatment for adequate duration to prevent relapse.
referralRedFlags: REFER TO SPECIALIST/TERTIARY CENTRE IF: Very extensive disease; No/minimal improvement with regular treatment after 4 weeks; Cure not achieved despite prolonged treatment and good compliance; Recurrent infection; Co-morbid conditions (pregnancy/lactation/hepatic/renal/cardiac disease); History of prolonged topical/oral/parenteral/steroid use.
algorithmNotes: Tinea corporis/cruris vs pedis/manuum vs onychomycosis - topical for limited involvement, systemic (oral) antifungal for extensive disease; treat for adequate duration to prevent relapse. Treating nail infection is important as it is a potential focus for spread of fungus to other body sites. ABBREV: KOH=Potassium hydroxide; OTC=Over the counter; CPM=Chlorpheniramine maleate; HS=at bedtime; BD=twice daily; OD=once daily; TDS=thrice daily.

whenToSuspect: ACUTE: Red, edematous plaques with small, grouped vesicles. SUBACUTE: Erythematous plaques with scaling or crusting. CHRONIC: Lesions may have scaling or lichenification. MAJOR FORMS - EXOGENOUS ECZEMAS (known trigger): Allergic contact eczema, Dermatophytide, Eczematous polymorphic light eruption, Infective eczema, Irritant contact eczema, Photoallergic contact eczema, Post-traumatic eczema. ENDOGENOUS ECZEMAS (no known trigger): Asteatotic eczema, Atopic eczema, Chronic superficial scaly eczema, Eyelid eczema, Hand eczema, Juvenile plantar dermatosis, Nummular eczema, Pityriasis alba, Eczema associated with systemic disease, Seborrhoeic eczema, Venous eczema.
risk: HISTORY: Associated atopy, allergic rhinitis or asthma in patient and family; age of onset usually early (<5 years) in atopic dermatitis; predominant flexural involvement in atopic dermatitis; possible allergens implicated; high risk occupations with allergen/irritant exposure (agricultural work, masons, hair-dressers); associated photosensitivity (esp parthenium dermatitis); change in severity with season (summer exacerbation in parthenium, winter exacerbation in atopic dermatitis). DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS: Tinea corporis, Psoriasis, Cutaneous t-cell lymphoma (CTCL).
scores: Atopic dermatitis distribution: Infantile - most commonly face then extensors of knees and elbows; Childhood/Adult phase - flexural involvement (cubital and popliteal fossa). Contact dermatitis can be irritant or allergic; eczema pattern corresponds to allergen/irritant exposure pattern; can be localized or widespread.
investigations: Most cases of eczema can be diagnosed clinically. Secondary infection is common (may cause flare) - confirmed by swabs for culture and sensitivity. Patch tests detect allergens in suspected allergic contact dermatitis. KOH preparation or biopsy when dermatophyte infection or other diagnoses are suspected.
drugs: GENERAL PRINCIPLES: Avoid allergens and irritant materials; daily bath with mild soap, keep nails short, avoid scratching; Moisturizers are cornerstone - apply immediately after bathing while skin still damp and multiple times during day; Antihistamines (e.g. levocetirizine) for pruritus; Topical corticosteroids (TCS) mild over face/flexures/genitals, mid potent over palms/soles and lichenified lesions; Topical calcineurin inhibitors (TCIs) for face/flexures/genitals and/or as maintenance; if secondary infection (pain, pus, yellow crust) treat with topical/oral antibiotic. PRIMARY/SECONDARY: active eczema - daily use of TCS of appropriate strength until completely clear +/- antihistamine +/- oral antibiotic if superinfection; Maintenance for resistant/relapse-prone areas (flexural skin) - intermittent mid-potency TCS (2-3 days/week) and/or TCI (3-5 days/week). TERTIARY: severe disease may require phototherapy or systemic treatment (short course oral corticosteroids, cyclosporine, azathioprine).
management: Primary/Secondary level: TCS + emollients + antihistamine, treat superinfection, maintenance intermittent TCS/TCI. Tertiary level: severe disease - phototherapy or systemic agents (oral corticosteroids/cyclosporine/azathioprine). Avoidance of provoking agents, moisturizers and early treatment are the aim of eczema management.
referralRedFlags: Severe or refractory disease -> tertiary level for phototherapy/systemic treatment. Suspected allergic contact dermatitis -> patch testing. Diagnostic doubt (rule out tinea, psoriasis, CTCL) -> KOH/biopsy or specialist.
algorithmNotes: Classify by acuity (acute/subacute/chronic) and form (exogenous with trigger vs endogenous). Remove cause for exogenous eczemas; pharmacological intervention (TCS/emollients/TCI) for endogenous. ABBREV: TCS=Topical corticosteroids; TCI=Topical calcineurin inhibitors; KOH=Potassium hydroxide; CTCL=Cutaneous t-cell lymphoma.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: Appearance of fluid-filled, itchy or painful blisters (either flaccid or tense) on skin, over a normal or erythematous base; Appearance of raw, erythematous erosions +/- crusting on skin; Appearance of erosions/blisters inside oral cavity, eyes, nose and genitals. AUTOIMMUNE BLISTERING DISEASES: Pemphigus vulgaris/variants; Pemphigus foliaceus/variants; Bullous pemphigoid; Pemphigoid gestationis; Mucous membrane pemphigoid; Linear IgA bullous disease/chronic bullous disease of childhood; Dermatitis herpetiformis; Epidermolysis bullosa acquisita; Bullous systemic lupus erythematosus.
risk: ADDITIONAL INFO: Age at onset and duration of blistering; history of recent drug intake; history of prior varicella/chicken pox; history of similar illness in family; history of itching, pain, burning; predominant sites affected; associated photosensitivity. RED FLAG SIGNS: Fever +/- chills and rigors; Hypotension (indicating hypovolemia due to fluid loss or sepsis); Altered sensorium (indicating dyselectrolytemia or sepsis). DIFFERENTIALS: Bullous Impetigo, Varicella, Stevens Johnson Syndrome/TEN; Epidermolysis bullosa (hereditary, neonatal/infancy onset, pressure sites, scarring, teeth/nail abnormality); Congenital syphilis in a neonate (get VDRL for mother and child).
scores: Likely PEMPHIGUS group: flaccid blisters/erosions +/- crusting on skin +/- mucosae; usually adults, rarely children. Likely SUB-EPIDERMAL group: tense small to large blisters with clear or hemorrhagic fluid on itchy erythematous base, commonly healing with hypopigmentation +/- scarring; seen in children/adults/elderly (most common bullous pemphigoid). Severity by % body surface area: pemphigus mucosal/mucocutaneous BSA <5% vs mucocutaneous BSA >5%; bullous pemphigoid limited <10% BSA vs extensive >10% BSA.
investigations: Get a Tzanck smear; Get a biopsy for histopathology from margin of a lesion; Get a peri-lesional biopsy for direct immunofluorescence, if facility is available. General: hemogram, basic biochemistry including renal and hepatic function tests, blood sugar; pus culture and if sepsis suspected also blood culture. EXAMINATION questions: flaccid or tense blisters? crusted erosions? clear or hemorrhagic fluid? umbilicated blisters? erythematous/urticarial base? healing with/without scarring? hyper/hypopigmentation? mucosae involved?
drugs: PEMPHIGUS (start treatment only if facility for monitoring & management of complications available): Mucosal/mucocutaneous with BSA <5% - Oral Prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day with one or more of: Azathioprine 2-3 mg/kg/day, Mycophenolate mofetil 35 mg/kg/day (start at lower dose), Cyclophosphamide 1-2 mg/kg/day, Methotrexate 0.3 mg/kg/week, Dapsone 100-150 mg/day. Mucocutaneous with BSA >5% - at primary level stabilize, initiate general measures and refer; tertiary level - Dexamethasone-Cyclophosphamide pulse therapy, Rituximab. BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID: Limited (<10% BSA) - start treatment and refer to tertiary; topical Clobetasol propionate (up to 30 gm/day); Oral Prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day +/- Dapsone 100-150 mg/day, Doxycycline 100-200 mg/day, Niacinamide 500 mg thrice/day, Azathioprine 2-3 mg/kg/day, Mycophenolate mofetil 35 mg/kg/day, Methotrexate 0.3 mg/kg/week. Extensive (>10% BSA) - manage at tertiary; Oral Prednisolone 0.75-1 mg/kg/day +/- Dapsone/Doxycycline/Niacinamide/Azathioprine/Mycophenolate mofetil/Methotrexate.
management: GENERAL MEASURES: monitor temperature, respiratory rate, pulse rate; administer antibiotics if lesions infected/foul smelling; fluid-electrolyte balance; supportive (clean non-adherent dressings, normal soap bath, topical antibiotics, aspiration of large blisters with 18G needle, avoid deroofing blisters as roof acts as natural dressing). Oral hygiene (Chlorhexidine mouth wash, soft pediatric brush, avoid eroding gingival margin); skin hygiene (diluted potassium permanganate bath/compresses, emollients/coconut oil, 2% savlon scalp wash); encourage oral intake (fluids and calories); liquid/semisolid diet for oral erosions. Start systemic immunosuppression only where monitoring & complication management available.
referralRedFlags: RED FLAGS: Fever +/- chills/rigors; Hypotension (hypovolemia/sepsis); Altered sensorium (dyselectrolytemia/sepsis). Pemphigus mucocutaneous BSA >5% and bullous pemphigoid extensive (>10% BSA) -> manage at tertiary level. Start treatment only if facility for monitoring and management of complications is available, else stabilize and refer.
algorithmNotes: Determine pemphigus (flaccid, intra-epidermal) vs sub-epidermal (tense, e.g. bullous pemphigoid) group via Tzanck smear, histopathology from lesion margin, and peri-lesional direct immunofluorescence. Correct diagnosis; prevention/treatment of sepsis; and regularity of treatment brings best results. ABBREV: IgA=Immunoglobulin A; TEN=Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis; VDRL=Venereal Disease Research Laboratory; BSA=Body Surface Area.

whenToSuspect: VARIANTS OF PSORIASIS: Plaque psoriasis (erythematous plaques with silvery white scales); Guttate psoriasis (shower of numerous erythematous papules <1 cm on trunk and extremities, younger patients); Palmoplantar psoriasis (chronic erythematous well defined plaques symmetrically on palms and soles, occasional nail involvement; differentiate from palmoplantar eczema); Erythrodermic psoriasis (generalised erythema and scaling involving >90% of BSA; triggered by withdrawal of systemic corticosteroids/potent topical steroids or HIV); Pustular psoriasis (crops of localized or generalised sterile pustules and lakes of pus with surrounding erythema, often with fever; in pregnancy presents as impetigo herpetiformis).
risk: GENERAL PRINCIPLES: Establish diagnosis (usually clinical & bedside tests - Auspitz sign, Grattage test; if in doubt refer for evaluation & skin biopsy); Assess for psoriatic arthritis and metabolic syndrome (obesity, dyslipidemia, diabetes, hypertension); Counsel about variable natural course and lifestyle modifications (weight reduction, avoidance of smoking and alcohol); Avoid Methotrexate and Cyclosporine A in children scheduled for live vaccines; Rule out tuberculosis, HIV, Hepatitis B and C before systemic immunosuppressive treatment; Pregnancy test prior to systemic therapy (Acitretin avoided in child bearing age group); Systemic steroids should NOT be given for psoriasis except for generalized pustular psoriasis of pregnancy.
scores: Treatment chosen by body surface area (BSA): Topical therapy if <5% BSA; Phototherapy if >5% BSA or palmoplantar psoriasis; Systemic therapy if >5% BSA, severe recalcitrant disease, palmoplantar psoriasis or arthritis; Biologics for resistant cases. Erythrodermic = >90% BSA; admit if febrile & unstable vitals.
investigations: Diagnosis usually clinical & bedside (Auspitz sign, Grattage test); skin biopsy if in doubt. Before systemic therapy: rule out tuberculosis, HIV, Hepatitis B and C; pregnancy test. Erythrodermic/pustular: Complete Hemogram, Liver & Kidney Function test; serum calcium in generalized pustular psoriasis (patients may have hypocalcemia).
drugs: TOPICAL THERAPY (<5% BSA): Moisturizers (white soft paraffin); Topical corticosteroids, Tacrolimus ointment, Tazarotene, Calcipotriol, Coal tar, Dithranol, Salicylic acid combinations. LIMITED PLAQUE PSORIASIS (<5%): Face and flexures - 1% Hydrocortisone/low potency steroid cream OD for 2 weeks; Trunk and extremities - Betamethasone cream (or other potent steroid, preferably with Salicylic acid 3-6%) OD for 2-4 weeks. PHOTOTHERAPY (>5% BSA/palmoplantar): Narrow band UVB, Targeted phototherapy, Topical/systemic PUVA or Psoralens with sunlight (PUVAsol). SYSTEMIC THERAPY (>5% BSA/severe recalcitrant/palmoplantar/arthritis): Methotrexate/Cyclosporine A/Retinoids-isotretinoin (preferred in adolescent girls), Acitretin/oral antibiotics (guttate psoriasis)/novel small molecules; Resistant cases - Biologics. GUTTATE: antibiotics for streptococcal infection; PUVAsol; refractory - systemic. ERYTHRODERMIC: Methotrexate or Cyclosporine A; maintenance Acitretin/NbUVB/PUVA; biologics if fails. PUSTULAR: Acitretin/Methotrexate/Cyclosporine.
management: Limited plaque psoriasis managed at primary/secondary with topicals; if no response in 6-8 weeks try alternate topicals and/or systemic therapy or NB UV-B/PUVA/PUVAsol. Generalized plaque/erythrodermic/pustular preferably managed at higher centre. Erythrodermic/pustular general management at primary care: stabilize patient, treat secondary infection, maintain temperature/fluid & electrolyte balance, admit if febrile & unstable vitals, high protein diet, refer to higher center for specific management. Systemic steroids should not be given except generalized pustular psoriasis of pregnancy.
referralRedFlags: If first-line treatment options fail or are contraindicated -> refer to tertiary care center for combination/rotational therapy/novel small molecules/biologics. Erythrodermic psoriasis (>90% BSA, febrile, unstable vitals) and generalized pustular psoriasis -> admit & refer. Diagnostic doubt -> skin biopsy.
algorithmNotes: Identify variant (plaque/guttate/palmoplantar/erythrodermic/pustular); choose therapy by BSA and severity (topical <5%, phototherapy/systemic >5% or severe, biologics resistant). Psoriasis is completely treatable but has a chronic course. ABBREV: BSA=Body Surface Area; PUVA=Psoralen Ultraviolet A; PUVAsol=Psoralen Ultraviolet A Solar; NB UV-B/NbUVB=Narrow Band Ultraviolet B.

condition: Rational Use of Topical Medications (Topical Steroids)
whenToSuspect: Reference workflow for rational use of topical medications. TOPICAL CORTICOSTEROIDS (TCS): most commonly prescribed topical medication in dermatology; because of quick results it has high abuse potential; unmonitored use can cause both local and systemic adverse effects (e.g. TCS damaged face, TCS induced striae). Topical steroids are a double edged sword - use judiciously.
risk: GENERAL PRINCIPLES FOR TCS USE: Before prescribing make sure the dermatosis is steroid responsive; Rule out fungal and bacterial infections at the local site; Super potent and potent TCS usually for a maximum duration of 2 weeks; For children and over face - only low potency TCS; For larger surface area use finger tip unit (FTU) method; for smaller area less than 1 FTU may be required (advise not to apply beyond the lesion); Treatment under occlusion should be avoided (only by specialists).
scores: FINGERTIP UNIT (FTU) requirements by body area: Face & neck 2.5 FTU; Trunk front & back 14 FTU; One arm 3 FTU; One hand 1 FTU; Groin 1 FTU; One leg 6 FTU; One foot 2 FTU. One FTU = line of medication from fingertip to first skin crease.
investigations: Confirm dermatosis is steroid responsive and rule out local fungal/bacterial infection before TCS. COMMON FORMULATIONS: Cream (oil-and-water emulsion, preferred for oozy/wet lesions); Ointment (semi-solid, greasy, occlusive; better penetration over thick keratotic lesions); Gel (aqueous/alcoholic monophasic; cosmetic acceptance, hairy areas); Lotion (thicker than solution; hairy areas/larger BSA); Aerosol foam/spray; Powder (doubtful penetration/efficacy).
drugs: DOSE AND AMOUNT: single application to whole body of an adult requires 20 to 30 g of product (cream/ointment/lotion); area of one hand (palm and digits) requires 0.3 g per application; no more than 45 g/week of potent or 100 g/week of a moderately potent TCS should be applied; treatment under occlusion avoided (only by specialists). ACCEPTABLE COMBINATIONS WITH TCS (specific situations, strict supervision): TCS + Fusidic acid 2% cream/ointment (impetiginized eczematous lesions); TCS + Salicylic Acid 3-6% ointment (thick hyperkeratotic eczema/psoriasis); Topical Calcipotriene-TCS (mild-moderate psoriasis); Hydroquinone 2% + Tretinoin 0.025% + Fluocinolone Acetonide 0.1% Cream (use with great caution in melasma - high abuse potential). RATIONAL TOPICAL COMBINATIONS FOR ACNE: Clindamycin 1%+Tretinoin 0.025% gel; Adapalene 0.1%+Clindamycin phosphate 1%; Clindamycin 1%+Benzoyl peroxide 5% cream; Adapalene 0.1%+Benzoyl peroxide 2.5% gel.
management: TOPICAL ANTIBIOTIC USE IN ACNE: Benzoyl peroxide (BPO) alone or with Retinoids/Clindamycin effective for mild acne, or with topical retinoid/systemic antibiotic for moderate-severe acne; BPO prevents bacterial resistance; topical antibiotics (Clindamycin) effective but not as monotherapy (resistance risk). MOISTURIZERS: cornerstone for normal & diseased skin barrier; alone therapeutic in eczema and psoriasis; prefer bland, fragrance-free (white soft paraffin/light liquid paraffin, glycerin with water, coconut oil). SUNSCREEN: for photosensitive dermatoses (lupus erythematosus) apply liberal uniform film (2 mg/cm2) on sun-exposed sites at least 15 minutes before sun exposure; routine use not essential except intense/prolonged exposure (e.g. mountaineering).
referralRedFlags: Super potent/potent TCS only for short courses (max ~2 weeks); face and children only low potency. Occlusion only by specialists. Watch for TCS abuse signs (steroid damaged face, striae, atrophy). High abuse potential combinations (e.g. triple combination creams in melasma) - use with great caution.
algorithmNotes: Choose formulation by lesion type; quantify with FTU and weekly limits (max 45 g/week potent, 100 g/week moderately potent TCS). Topical steroids are a double edged sword - use judiciously. ABBREV: TCS=Topical corticosteroids; FTU=Fingertip unit; BPO=Benzoyl peroxide; BSA=Body Surface Area.

whenToSuspect: Scabies is an infestation by a mite Sarcoptes Scabiei var hominis; transmission by skin-to-skin contact, sexual contact and infested fomites (towels, clothes, beddings); symptoms start 3-6 weeks after primary infestation but faster (2-3 days) after re-infestation; multiple cases in schools/orphanages/cluster settings. SYMPTOMS AND SIGNS: Intense itch worse at night; other family members often affected; red itchy papules and excoriations mainly over fingers (interdigital spaces), wrists, periumbilical area, breasts, buttocks, axillary folds, waist, genitalia, extensor aspects of limbs; face, palms and soles usually spared in adults but typically involved in young children; Burrow is the most characteristic lesion (thin brown-grey lines 0.5-1 cm in web spaces and wrists) but often not observed. OTHER: itchy persistent nodules over male genitalia; secondary bacterial infection with poor hygiene; CRUSTED SCABIES (severe form in immunosuppression/neurological disorders/incapacity - thick yellow brown crusts densely packed with mites, localised to hands and feet including nails).
risk: CRUSTED SCABIES develops in those with predisposing factors: immunosuppression (disease or drugs including topical steroids), neurological disorders, physical incapacitation or mental retardation (associated inability to scratch). Secondary bacterial infection (Staphylococcal/Streptococcal) can occur with poor hygiene, especially in children.
scores: Most patients treated with topical alone. Crusted scabies requires intensified oral + topical regimen. Treat the entire skin (not lesions alone) and treat the family/contacts (not the patient alone).
investigations: Diagnosis is usually clinical. Demonstration of mite, mite eggs, or mite faeces (scybala) may be attempted from burrows (if visible) or by dermoscopy (if available), and from the thick crusts in case of crusted scabies (where mite is easily demonstrable). Scabies mite seen in KOH smear (400X).
drugs: TOPICAL (most patients): Permethrin 5% cream - apply over the whole skin surface (neck downwards) on dry and clean skin; wash off after 8-12 hours (apply late evening, keep overnight); in infants the face and scalp must also be treated; special attention to interdigital webspaces, axillae, area under fingernails and toenails, wrists, external genitalia and buttocks; re-apply if hands washed (ensure 8 hours contact); about 30 grams of cream for one application in adults and children >=5 years, 15 grams for children <5 years; repeat after 7-14 days. Alternatively 1% Gamma Benzene Hexa-Chloride (GBHC/lindane) - avoid in infants. ORAL (poor compliance or response to topicals): Oral Ivermectin 200 mcg/kg (up to 12 mg); two doses 1 week apart; taken with food. Avoid Ivermectin in infants, children <5 years or <15 kg, and in pregnancy (Permethrin safely prescribed in these). Antihistamines as per requirement. CRUSTED SCABIES: Ivermectin on days 1, 2, 8, 9 and 15 (additionally days 22, 29 in severe cases) with Permethrin 5% cream daily for 7 days then twice weekly until cure; keratolytic 3-6% Salicylic acid over crusts. NODULAR LESIONS: potent topical steroid (Clobetasol propionate) or intralesional steroid (Triamcinolone acetonide 10 mg/mL) for persistent nodules.
management: GENERAL MEASURES: All family members and close contacts must be simultaneously treated to prevent re-infestation; clothes and fabrics (towels, bed linen) used in preceding three days must be washed with hot water and dried in the sun; items may be sealed in a plastic bag for at least 3 days (for shoes and non-washable items). Treatment of secondary infection (Staphylococcal/Streptococcal) - refer to Bacterial skin infection STW.
referralRedFlags: Crusted scabies (severe, immunosuppressed) needs intensified regimen and specialist input. Secondary bacterial infection -> treat per bacterial skin infection STW. Re-infestation likely if itching persists >3-4 weeks or new lesions appear (all close contacts not simultaneously treated).
algorithmNotes: POST TREATMENT ADVICE: itching can continue for several weeks after successful treatment and repeated applications are not required; continue antihistamines for symptomatic management. If itching persists >3-4 weeks or new lesions appear, a reinfestation is likely (occurs if close contacts not simultaneously treated). Treat the entire skin, not lesions alone; treat the family/contacts, not the patient alone. ABBREV: KOH=Potassium hydroxide; GBHC=Gamma Benzene Hexa-Chloride.

whenToSuspect: URTICARIA: sudden appearance of wheals, angioedema, or both. A WHEAL: a sharply circumscribed superficial central swelling of variable size and shape, surrounded by reflex erythema; associated with itching/burning sensation, fleeting nature (resolves within 1-24 hours). Chronic urticaria implies duration more than 6 weeks. ANGIOEDEMA: sudden pronounced erythematous or skin-colored swelling of lower dermis and subcutis with frequent involvement of mucous membranes; associated pain rather than itching; resolution slower (up to 72 hours). CLASSIFICATION OF CHRONIC URTICARIA: Chronic spontaneous (spontaneous wheals/angioedema/both for >=6 weeks) vs Inducible/physical (Symptomatic dermographism, Delayed pressure urticaria, Cholinergic urticaria, Cold/Heat urticaria, Solar urticaria, Aquagenic urticaria, Contact urticaria).
risk: HISTORY: Time to onset; Frequency/duration; Diurnal variation; Associated angioedema; Associated pain/itch; Induction by physical agents or exercise; Family history; Previous allergies; Surgical implantations; Gastric/intestinal problem; Drug history; Correlation with food; Correlation with menses; Smoking; Work profile; Hobbies; Stress; Quality of life impact; Response to therapy. Avoid triggers including drugs such as NSAIDs, PCM, ACE inhibitors if history suggestive of drug induced/exacerbated urticaria/angioedema. DIFFERENTIALS: Insect/Bedbug bites; Urticarial vasculitis (painful, persist 24-48 hours, fade to leave bruising, +/- fever and arthralgia); Pre bullous phase of bullous pemphigoid; Maculopapular drug/viral rash.
scores: Chronic urticaria = duration more than 6 weeks. Remits spontaneously in 12-24 months in ~50% patients. Treatment escalated stepwise (1st line -> 4-fold dose -> add-on agents) if symptoms persist. Urticaria treatment goal is disease remission - not cure.
investigations: Generally no investigations needed to confirm diagnosis. Skin biopsy if other diagnoses suspected. C4 and C1 inhibitor quantitation to detect C1 inhibitor deficiency in suspected hereditary angioedema (angioedema without urticaria). Tests for viral/bacterial/parasitic infections guided by history. Lab tests if immunosuppressive treatment planned. Limited utility tests sometimes ordered: Thyroid function tests and antithyroid peroxidase (TPO) antibodies; Autologous serum skin test (ASST); Skin prick/specific IgE test.
drugs: PRIMARY CARE: First Line - 2nd generation non-sedating antihistamines (e.g. Cetirizine 10mg, Levocetirizine 5mg, Loratadine 10mg, Fexofenadine 180mg once daily); if symptoms persist after 2 weeks Second Line - increase dosage (up to fourfold) of 2nd generation antihistamines; if persist after 2-4 further weeks refer to higher centre. SEVERE URTICARIA with respiratory distress - maintain airway, injectable Hydrocortisone and Pheniramine (Avil) may be required; Intramuscular Adrenaline 1:1000 dilution (1 mg in 1 mL), 0.2 to 0.5 mg (0.01 mg/kg in children; maximum 0.3 mg) IM every 5 to 15 minutes if choking/respiratory distress/shock; angioedema with respiratory/laryngeal symptom requires emergency management - refer to higher center after vital stabilization; oral Prednisolone may be initiated for biphasic response. SECONDARY/TERTIARY: First line 2nd generation antihistamines; Second line up to fourfold dose; Third line add-on - Cyclosporine A (3-5 mg/kg) or Montelukast (10 mg HS), short course (max 10 days) of corticosteroids (Prednisolone 0.3-0.5 mg/kg); Tertiary third line add Omalizumab (300 mg s/c every 4 weeks) or Cyclosporine A or Montelukast. Oral/injectable corticosteroids generally not used except uncontrolled disease or associated respiratory symptoms. Long-term first generation antihistamines (Chlorphenamine, Hydroxyzine) avoided if possible due to sedation/psychomotor impairment.
management: Primary: stepwise antihistamines, refer if uncontrolled. Secondary: 2nd gen antihistamine -> fourfold dose -> add Cyclosporine A/Montelukast +/- short corticosteroid course. Tertiary: add Omalizumab/Cyclosporine A/Montelukast. Reassure - remits spontaneously in 12-24 months in ~50%; prolonged treatment with long-acting non-sedating antihistamines is not harmful.
referralRedFlags: REFER TO A HIGHER CENTRE: Urticaria difficult to control with antihistamines despite fourfold higher dosage than licensed doses of Cetirizine/Levocetirizine/Fexofenadine; Patients with polypharmacy; Unusual urticaria (long lasting lesions >24-48 hours with bruising - suspect urticarial vasculitis); Associated angioedema that is unresponsive or presents with choking/dyspnoea; Investigations not available. Angioedema with respiratory/laryngeal symptom - emergency, stabilize then refer.
algorithmNotes: Confirm wheals (fleeting <24h) +/- angioedema (slower, up to 72h); classify chronic urticaria (spontaneous vs inducible). Stepwise therapy: 2nd gen antihistamine -> fourfold dose -> add-on (Montelukast/Cyclosporine/Omalizumab). Urticaria treatment goal is disease remission - not cure. ABBREV: TPO=Thyroid peroxidase; ASST=Autologous serum skin test; PCM=Paracetamol; ACE=Angiotensin Converting Enzyme; HS=at bedtime.

whenToSuspect: VARICELLA (CHICKEN POX): Fever, malaise; generalized vesicular lesions on erythematous base (dew drop on a rose petal sign); skin lesions in different stages of evolution (erythematous macules, papules, vesicles and crusted lesions). HERPES ZOSTER: Acute, grouped, vesiculo-pustular eruption in a dermatomal distribution; dermatomal pain. HISTORY (varicella): recent contact with varicella; past history of varicella/vaccination; immunosuppression (malignancy, HIV/AIDS, transplant). HISTORY (zoster): previous varicella; previous episode of herpes zoster; immunosuppression (diabetes mellitus, malignancy, transplant, HIV).
risk: VARICELLA RED FLAGS: Hemorrhagic vesicles; Difficulty in breathing; Chest pain; Abdominal pain; Stiff neck, confused behaviour (CNS symptoms); Hemodynamic instability. ZOSTER RED FLAGS: V1 dermatomal involvement (forehead, periorbital, nose tip - risk of eye involvement, look for watering of eye, redness, photophobia); Lesions on the ear or inside ear canal (risk of facial/vestibulocochlear nerve palsy - vertigo, tinnitus, hearing loss, facial asymmetry/weakness); Multi-dermatomal involvement; Disseminated herpes zoster; Hemorrhagic/necrotic lesions. PREGNANCY AND VARICELLA: infection in 1st 20 weeks may lead to congenital varicella syndrome (treat with acyclovir); maternal perinatal varicella may lead to neonatal varicella (initiate treatment and refer to a specialist).
scores: Specific antiviral indicated for: infants, children >12 years, adults, pregnant women and immunosuppressed patients (risk of severe varicella). Maximum benefit if acyclovir initiated within 24 hours of onset of rash (varicella) / start zoster antivirals <72 hours of onset for maximum benefit.
investigations: Diagnosis usually clinical. Tzanck smear from a fresh vesicle shows multinucleate giant cells and acantholysis. Optional: VZV PCR (skin swab/vesicular fluid), skin biopsy. Varicella symptom-directed: Chest X-ray, ECG, ECHO, transaminases, renal function test, brain imaging.
drugs: VARICELLA - General: isolate from high risk contacts; daily bath with soap; antipyretics Paracetamol (avoid aspirin - Reye's syndrome in children); antihistamines. Specific: Adults/children >40kg - Oral Acyclovir 800mg, 5 times a day for 5-7 days; Children <40kg - Acyclovir 20mg/kg/dose (max 800mg) four times a day for 7 days; Alternative Valacyclovir (adults 1g TDS); Intravenous Acyclovir 10mg/kg/dose 8 hourly if systemic complications/hemorrhagic varicella/immunosuppressed/neonatal varicella (higher dose may be required). HERPES ZOSTER - Analgesics (NSAIDs); if uncontrolled add step-wise: Pregabalin 150-600mg/day (start 150mg HS, titrate), Gabapentin start 300mg/day up to 1800mg/day, Amitriptyline 10-25mg HS, Nortriptyline 10-25mg/day up to 30-75mg/day, Carbamazepine 200mg HS to start. Specific: Acyclovir 800mg five times a day x 7 days OR Valacyclovir 1gm three times a day x 7 days (start <72 hours of onset; intravenous Acyclovir if multi-segmental/disseminated zoster or systemic complications).
management: VARICELLA COMPLICATIONS: Secondary skin infections, Pneumonia, Encephalitis, Hepatitis, Pancreatitis, Myocarditis, Reye's syndrome. ZOSTER COMPLICATIONS: Secondary skin infections; Herpes zoster ophthalmicus (lesions over side/tip of nose - Hutchinson's sign); Ramsay Hunt syndrome (facial nerve palsy with vesicles in ear canal); Aseptic meningitis/encephalitis (elderly/immunosuppressed); Post-herpetic neuralgia (pain >3 months, common in elderly). PREVENTION - Varicella: active immunization (live vaccine) <13 years 1st dose at 12-15 months, 2nd dose at 4-6 years; >=13 years 2 doses weeks apart; passive immunization (varicella zoster immunoglobulin) where active immunization contraindicated (pregnant, immunosuppressed). Zoster: active immunization may be offered to patients >50 years irrespective of previous history.
referralRedFlags: VARICELLA REFER IF: Diagnosis in doubt; Systemic complications; Hemodynamic instability; Hemorrhagic varicella; Not responding to oral Acyclovir; Immunosuppressed patient; Neonatal varicella syndrome. ZOSTER REFER IF: Multi-dermatomal/disseminated Herpes Zoster; Systemic complications (facial nerve palsy, eye involvement, neurological involvement); Post-herpetic neuralgia. Initiate specific antiviral treatment at the earliest to prevent complications.
algorithmNotes: Varicella - lesions in different stages (dew drop on rose petal); zoster - dermatomal vesicular eruption with pain. Antivirals most effective early (varicella within 24h, zoster within 72h). Watch V1/ear involvement (ophthalmic zoster/Ramsay Hunt). ABBREV: VZV=Varicella Zoster Virus; PCR=Polymerase Chain Reaction; CNS=Central Nervous System; HS=at bedtime; TDS=thrice daily.

whenToSuspect: Vitiligo is an acquired skin disease characterized by depigmented (white) macules, global prevalence 1-2%. NON-SEGMENTAL VITILIGO: Generalized vitiligo (lesions in generalized distribution, usually trunk, extremities and face; no predilection; also called vitiligo vulgaris); Acrofacial vitiligo (distal extremities and/or face/genitals; less responsive to treatment); Other variants (Focal, Follicular, Mucosal, Universal >=80% body surface area). SEGMENTAL VITILIGO: Unilateral with a midline demarcation; onset in childhood; leucotrichia both within and beyond the lesion; usually stabilizes within a year after initial progression; response to medical treatment variable, most may require surgical treatment.
risk: DISEASE ACTIVITY: Progressive (new lesions or spread of existing) - Rapidly progressive: >5 new lesions in last 1 month, or >15 lesions in last 3 months; Slowly progressive: <5 new lesions in last 1 month, or <15 lesions in last 3 months; Stable: no new lesions, no spread. EXTENT: limited (<=5%) or extensive (>5%). DIFFERENTIALS: Leprosy (hypopigmented not depigmented macules, overlying sensory loss, enlarged peripheral nerves); Pityriasis alba (hypopigmented scaly lesions usually on a child's face); Nevus depigmentosus (present since birth/early childhood, single hypopigmented macule/segmental lesion).
scores: Treatment plan based on disease activity (progressive rapid/slow vs stable) and extent (limited <=5% vs extensive >5%). Counselling: not the same as leprosy, does not spread by touch, not caused by foods (milk, curd, lemon, fish), treatment is available, multifactorial (predominantly autoimmune).
investigations: Diagnosis is clinical. Monitoring of patients on systemic treatment: Height (children), weight, blood pressure and blood sugar in patients on oral corticosteroids; Complete Hemogram, Liver Function Test in patients on Azathioprine, Methotrexate.
drugs: LIMITED STABLE/SLOWLY PROGRESSIVE: Topical mid-potent/potent corticosteroids, tacrolimus, topical PUVA/PUVAsol (avoid prolonged use). EXTENSIVE STABLE/SLOWLY PROGRESSIVE: Narrow-band ultraviolet B (NbUVB), oral Psoralen + Ultraviolet A (PUVA)/PUVAsol. RAPIDLY PROGRESSIVE (limited or extensive): Oral corticosteroids (minipulse) and/or Azathioprine/Methotrexate. By site - Acrofacial/Generalized/Segmental: Primary/secondary - Face, flexures, genitals: Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment BD; Other body sites: Betamethasone valerate/Mometasone/Fluticasone/Fluocinolone cream OD (clobetasol NOT to be used); refer non-responders to higher center after 3 months. Tertiary: Topical PUVA/PUVAsol, Handheld/whole body NbUVB, Targeted phototherapy/Excimer LASER, Levamisole (slowly progressive), Oral steroid (minipulse) and/or Azathioprine/Methotrexate (rapidly progressive), Surgical management (minipunch grafting, suction blister epidermal grafting, noncultured epidermal suspension). UNIVERSAL VITILIGO: Sunscreen/photoprotection; depigmenting agent monobenzyl ether of hydroquinone 20% may be considered if patient wishes complete depigmentation.
management: Primary/secondary level: topical corticosteroids/tacrolimus by site, refer non-responders after 3 months; in pregnancy prefer only topical corticosteroids. Tertiary level: phototherapy (PUVA/NbUVB/targeted/Excimer), systemic agents for progressive disease, surgical management for stable limited/segmental vitiligo unresponsive to medical treatment, camouflage for poorly responsive lesions. Non-responders: consider combining different modalities.
referralRedFlags: Refer non-responders to higher center after 3 months. Progressive vitiligo -> higher center. Consider surgical treatment for stable limited/segmental vitiligo (unresponsive to medical treatment) at tertiary level. Rapidly progressive disease needs systemic immunosuppression with monitoring.
algorithmNotes: Classify non-segmental (generalized/acrofacial/other) vs segmental; assess activity (rapid/slow progressive vs stable) and extent (<=5% vs >5%) to choose topical vs phototherapy vs systemic vs surgical. Vitiligo can be treated; treatment depends on extent and activity of disease. ABBREV: NbUVB=Narrow-band Ultraviolet B; PUVA=Psoralen Ultraviolet A; PUVAsol=Psoralen Ultraviolet A Solar.

whenToSuspect: Approach to hyponatremia begins with assessment of volume status. Severe hyponatremia (<125 mmol/L) symptoms: Vomiting, seizures, obtundation, coma, respiratory distress, unstable gait/falls. Likely chronic (>48 hours) features: Headache, irritability, difficulty in concentration, altered mood, depression. Mild/moderate hyponatremia may have significant sequelae unlikely.
risk: HYPOVOLEMIA (Total body water down, Total body sodium down down): Urinary Na >20 = Renal losses (Diuretic excess, Mineralocorticoid deficiency, Salt losing deficiency, Bicarbonaturia with renal tubular acidosis and metabolic alkalosis, Ketonuria, Osmotic diuresis, Cerebral salt wasting syndrome); Urinary Na <20 = Extrarenal losses (Vomiting, Diarrhea, Third spacing of fluids - Burns, Pancreatitis, Trauma). EUVOLEMIA (No edema, Total body water up, Total body sodium normal): Urinary Na >20 = Glucocorticoid deficiency, Hypothyroidism, Stress, Drugs, SIADH. HYPERVOLEMIA (Total body water up up, Total body sodium up): Urinary Na >20 = Renal failure; Urinary Na <20 = Nephrotic syndrome, Cirrhosis, Cardiac failure.
scores: Is Hyponatremia severe? (<125 mmol/L). If Yes + severe symptoms (vomiting, seizures, obtundation, coma, respiratory distress, unstable gait/falls), assess Duration. Acute <48 hours vs Chronic/Unknown (>48 hours). If No: significant sequelae unlikely; likely chronic; urgent intervention unnecessary - assess ECF volume and restrict fluid intake to 500-1000 ml/24 hour. Rapid correction of sodium is NOT advisable as it may lead to central pontine myelinosis.
investigations: Assessment of volume status. Urinary sodium (UNa >20 or <20). Serum sodium concentration and monitoring (6-8 hourly monitoring of serum Na). Assess ECF volume status. Evaluate for SIADH, hypothyroidism, glucocorticoid deficiency, renal/cardiac/hepatic failure as per volume status.
drugs: HYPOVOLEMIC HYPONATREMIA: Intravenous hydration with isotonic normal saline. HYPERVOLEMIC HYPONATREMIA: Treatment of underlying disease. SEVERE HYPONATREMIA ACUTE <48 hours: Emergency correction with hypertonic 3% saline 1-2 ml/kg/hr until safe Na target to a total 4-6 mM (this modest increase is sufficient to alleviate severe symptoms); after that corrective guidelines for chronic hyponatremia are appropriate. CHRONIC/UNKNOWN (>48 hours): Correction with 3% saline @ 0.5-1 ml/kg/hour till resolution of symptoms or until safe Na target; correction not >8-10 mM within first 24 hours; Oral Tolvaptan 15 mg on day one and then 30-60 mg per day if serum Na remains below 135 or rise is less than 5 mmol per 24 hours; 6-8 hourly monitoring of serum Na to be done. FORMULAE: Na deficit = 0.6 x body weight (target Plasma Na - starting Na concentration); 1 litre of 3% hypertonic saline = 513 mM Na (1 ml/kg/hr increases serum Na by 1 mM/hr); 1 litre of isotonic saline = 154 mM Na.
management: Assess volume status first (hypovolemia/euvolemia/hypervolemia). Hypovolemic: isotonic saline. Hypervolemic: treat underlying disease, restrict fluid 500-1000 ml/24hr. Severe symptomatic acute: 3% hypertonic saline 1-2 ml/kg/hr to raise Na 4-6 mM. Chronic: slow correction 3% saline 0.5-1 ml/kg/hr, not >8-10 mM/24hr; consider Tolvaptan. Monitor serum Na 6-8 hourly. Keep a high threshold for invasive procedures.
referralRedFlags: Severe hyponatremia (<125 mmol/L) with vomiting, seizures, obtundation, coma, respiratory distress, unstable gait/falls requires urgent correction. RED FLAG: Rapid correction of sodium is not advisable as it may lead to central pontine myelinosis - correction must not exceed 8-10 mM within first 24 hours. Refer for ICU/specialist care in severe symptomatic hyponatremia.
algorithmNotes: Stepwise: assess volume status -> urinary sodium -> classify cause -> determine severity (<125 mmol/L) and chronicity (<48h vs >48h) -> correct at safe rate. Avoid overcorrection (central pontine myelinosis). ABBREV: ECF=Extracellular fluid; Na=Sodium; SIADH=Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion; UNa=Urinary sodium.

whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: Polydipsia; Polyuria/Nocturia; Polyphagia; Weight loss; Short duration of complaints; Diabetic ketoacidosis as first presentation. Characteristic of T1 diabetes: urine/blood ketones moderate-large (in >50%); Continuous requirement of insulin since diagnosis.
risk: DIAGNOSIS of diabetes: Fasting plasma glucose >=126 mg%; post-glucose >=200 mg%; HbA1c >=6.5% (all to be re-confirmed); random glucose >=200 mg% with symptoms. HYPOGLYCAEMIA: Mild/moderate glucose <70 mg% with or without symptoms; Severe hypoglycemia: coma/seizures/inability to treat oneself.
scores: TARGET (SMBG): Pre-meal 80-130 mg%; 2 hours post-meal 120-180 mg%. Glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) every three months, target <7% (should be individualized). Ketones should be checked if blood glucose is >250 mg/dl. Glucose at midnight (12.00-2.00 am) occasionally to rule out nocturnal hypoglycemia.
investigations: HbA1c, creatinine, hemoglobin, TSH, tTG (tissue transglutaminase) antibody, lipid profile. SMBG: Check before each meal and at bedtime; more frequent if A1c not controlled or frequent hypoglycemia. COMPLICATIONS & COMORBIDITIES (5 years after diagnosis, then annually): Fundus examination (Retinopathy), Foot examination (Neuropathy), Urine albumin/creatinine ratio, S-creatinine, TSH, lipid profile.
drugs: INSULIN TREATMENT: Insulin administration 0.25 to 1.0 U/kg depending on age and pubertal status. Basal and bolus regimen: Basal - glargine or detemir or NPH 40-50% of daily requirement; Bolus - regular or rapid acting 50% of daily requirement / 3 injections before each meal. Insulin doses adjusted depending upon: 1. Pre-meal and post-meal glucose level, 2. Carbohydrates in the meal, 3. Exercise pattern. HYPOGLYCAEMIA TREATMENT: If glucose <70 mg% take 3 tsf glucose powder or sugar; if severe caregiver should give inj. glucagon 1 mg s.c./i.m., OTHERWISE IMMEDIATELY take to hospital for intravenous glucose injection (1-2 ml/kg of 25% dextrose). SICK DAY: In addition to usual insulin doses, take extra regular insulin s.c. every 6 hourly (10-15% of total daily insulin dose).
management: AMBULATORY MANAGEMENT - Nutrition: calories appropriate to expected body weight, pubertal status, activity; balanced diet including all food groups; avoid simple sugars and excessive fats; meals/snacks individualized to reflect insulin schedule (usually 3 meals, 2 snacks). Regular exercise: beneficial and encouraged. Education: emphasize diabetes related education to patient and caregivers. MONITORING at every visit: Growth & pubertal development (children/adolescents), Dietary and medication compliance, BP/Weight, Insulin site and injection technique, Review SMBG record, Hypoglycemia. SICK DAY RULES/DKA: Measure glucose frequently, check urine ketones if glucose >250 mg%; Drink plenty of fluids, monitor urine output; Eat small light meals 4-5 times/day; If glucose not falling, excess vomiting, low urine output, high or rising ketone - admit the patient. DKA management as per STW on Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA).
referralRedFlags: REASONS FOR REFERRAL TO HIGHER CENTRES: Uncontrolled hyperglycemia; For education of patient & family / For insulin injection techniques/SBGM/identifying hypoglycemia s/s; Recurrent hypoglycemia; Severe diabetic ketoacidosis (altered sensorium, rapid breathing); Chronic diabetes specific complications. PREVENTION of hypoglycemia: identify mismatch of food, exercise, insulin.
algorithmNotes: T1DM is insulin-dependent from diagnosis; manage with basal-bolus insulin, structured education, SMBG and target HbA1c <7%. Screen complications from 5 years after diagnosis then annually. Keep a high threshold for invasive procedures. ABBREV: BP=Blood pressure; DKA=Diabetic ketoacidosis; SBMG=Self-monitoring of blood glucose; TSH=Thyroid-stimulating hormone; tTG=Tissue transglutaminase. REFERENCE: American Diabetes Association; Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes-2022.

whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: May be asymptomatic; Osmotic symptoms ie., polyuria, polydipsia and polyphagia; Recurrent infections; Blurring of vision; Weight loss; Non healing wounds; Paraesthesia.
risk: DIAGNOSIS: FPG >=126 mg/dl (after 8hr fasting) OR 2-h plasma glucose >=200 mg/dl during OGTT OR HbA1C >=6.5% OR Diabetes symptoms + random plasma glucose >=200 mg/dl. PREDIABETES: Impaired fasting glucose FPG 100-125 mg/dL; Impaired glucose tolerance 140-199 mg/dL; HbA1c 5.7-6.4%. CO-MORBIDITIES to assess: Hypertension, Dyslipidaemia, CAD, CKD.
scores: METABOLIC TARGETS: HbA1c <=7.0% (except elderly and those with significant comorbid conditions where higher target may be acceptable); Pre-prandial capillary plasma glucose 80-130 mg/dl; Post-prandial capillary plasma glucose <180 mg/dl; BP=140/90 (130/80 in CKD); LDL <100 mg/dl (<70 mg/dl in CAD). EXAMINATION: BMI/waist circumference, BP, Peripheral pulses, Pin-prick sensation/monofilament test/vibration/DTR, Skin/oral cavity/foot, Fundus (dilated) examination.
investigations: HbA1c; Creatinine; K+; Fasting lipid profile; Urine routine examination and spot albumin:creatinine ratio (best carried out after initial glycaemic control); LFT/ALT/AST; ECG; Others like Echo, USG abdomen as indicated. MONITORING: Blood glucose FPG and 2 hours PPG once monthly, more frequent as required including SMBG or CGM; HbA1c every 6-12 months (3 monthly if uncontrolled); Annual monitoring - ECG, urine ACR (albumin creatinine ratio), dilated fundoscopy, foot examination.
drugs: TREATMENT: Dietary modification; Avoidance of tobacco and restriction/avoidance of alcohol; Physical activity. PHARMACOTHERAPY: HbA1c <8.5% - Monotherapy Metformin; HbA1c 8.5-10% - Dual therapy Metformin + SU's/TZD/DPP-IVi/SGLT2i/AGI/GLP-1RA; HbA1c >10% - Basal Insulin + Metformin + another OAD / triple OAD combination.
management: Lifestyle plus stepwise pharmacotherapy guided by HbA1c. MONITORING: blood glucose FPG and 2h PPG monthly; HbA1c every 6-12 months (3 monthly if uncontrolled); annual ECG, urine ACR, dilated fundoscopy, foot examination. SCREENING FOR DIABETES: In apparently normal adult - obese/overweight (BMI >=27.5 or >=23 kg/m2) with risk factors (first degree relative with diabetes, h/o cardiovascular disease, BP >=140/90, dyslipidemia TG>250/HDL<40 male <50 female, physical inactivity, PCOS, acanthosis nigricans, age >30 years, previous GDM). In adult with illness presenting with osmotic symptoms, unexplained weight loss, unexplained depression/dementia, acute coronary syndrome, deep seated infections, recurrent infections, non-healing ulcers, Cushing's. IN PREGNANCY: H/O GDM/pre-existing diabetes; screen all pregnant women in 1st trimester with FPG; FPG>=126 and/or HbA1c>=6.5% considered pre-existing diabetes; FPG 92-125 considered GDM; normal 1st trimester screening - 75g OGTT at 24-28 weeks; all GDM women tested 6 weeks post-partum and once every 3 years.
referralRedFlags: REFERRALS: Endocrinology - for uncontrolled hyperglycemia; Ophthalmology - at initial evaluation and every year; Nephrology - for deranged renal function; Cardiology - for CAD/HF/arrhythmia. Keep low threshold for diagnosis. Make sure to follow up to meet targets.
algorithmNotes: Diagnose by FPG/OGTT/HbA1c/random glucose; assess comorbidities and complications; treat with lifestyle + stepwise OADs/insulin by HbA1c; monitor and refer for complications. ABBREV: ALT=Alanine transaminase; AST=Aspartate aminotransferase; BMI=Body mass index; CAD=Coronary artery disease; CGM=Continuous glucose monitor; CKD=Chronic kidney disease; DTR=Deep tendon reflex; ECG=Electrocardiogram; FPG=Fasting plasma glucose; GDM=Gestational diabetes mellitus; HDL=High-density lipoprotein; LDL=Low-density lipoprotein; LFT=Liver function test; OAD=Oral antidiabetic drug; OGTT=Oral glucose tolerance test; SMBG=Self-monitoring of blood glucose; TG=Triglyceride.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT DKA IF THERE IS HISTORY OF: May be the initial presentation in T1DM; Pain abdomen; Recurrent vomiting; Rapid/labored breathing; Altered sensorium. ASSESS: Sensorium (GCS), pulse rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, temperature; Signs of dehydration (dry tongue, sunken eyes, skin turgor, urine output).
risk: LOOK & ADDRESS FOR PRECIPITATING FACTORS: Skipping/missing insulin doses; Fever/cough/loose stools/burning micturition. ASSESS SEVERITY OF DKA: pH - Mild 7.25-7.3, Moderate 7.0-7.25, Severe <7.0; HCO3 - Mild 15-18, Moderate 10-15, Severe <10; Level of Sensorium - Mild Alert, Moderate Mild Drowsiness, Severe Stupor/Coma. Severe case: ICU Admission.
scores: DKA severity by pH, bicarbonate and sensorium (Mild/Moderate/Severe). Severe case (pH <7.0, HCO3 <10, stupor/coma) requires ICU admission. Search for another cause of obtundation if osmolality is <320 mOsm/kg H2O.
investigations: Spot capillary blood glucose (venous blood preferable in case of shock); Serum ketone/urine ketone by dipstick; VBG (for pH, bicarbonate, anion gap); Na+/K+/BUN/Creatinine/ECG.
drugs: TREATMENT: Replace fluids - 1 l of 0.9% saline over first hour followed by 250-500 ml/hour (10-20 ml/kg/hour initially for children); Administer regular insulin - 0.1 IU/kg IV then 0.1 IU/kg/hour IV infusion; Double infusion rate if less than 10% fall in blood glucose after 1 hour; When blood glucose < 250 mg/dl, add 5% dextrose @ 50 ml/hour; Supplement potassium before insulin if serum K+ < 3.3 mEq/L (or ECG changes); Replace potassium @ 10-20 mEq/hour with insulin infusion if serum K+ < 5.5 mEq/L; If pH < 7.0, add sodium bicarbonate; 50 mmol in 200 ml sterile water over 2 hour; Bicarbonate should be given only: if pH is less than 6.9 or if pH is less than 7.1 along with hypotension or if hyperkalemia is present. WHEN TO STOP INSULIN INFUSION: Patient accepting orally, blood glucose consistently < 250 mg/dl, normalization of anion gap and correction of metabolic acidosis; Administer SC dose of long/intermediate-acting & short acting insulin at least 30 mins before stopping insulin infusion; shift to basal-bolus/pre-mixed insulin regimen.
management: MONITORING: Strict input/output charting every 1 hour (report if urine output <30 ml/hour for 2 consecutive hours); One hour after starting treatment till resolution of DKA; BP and vital signs every 1 hour; Blood glucose every 1 hour; Venous pH, Na, K, HCO3 2-4 hourly; Blood ketones (if available)/Urine for ketones 12 hourly; After resolution of DKA blood glucose monitoring every 4 hours. COMMON ERRORS/PITFALLS: Initiating Insulin therapy before I/V fluid therapy; Failure to review fluid replacement therapy in elderly; Failure to identify underlying cause; Potassium may be normal despite depletion of body stores; Elevated leucocyte count does not suggest infection until >15 x 10^9/l; Monitor for cerebral edema especially in children; Body temperature cannot be used as guide to infection; Hyperamylasemia cannot be used as marker for pancreatitis; Ketosis may worsen paradoxically with successful treatment initially; do not stop I/V insulin before S/C insulin given.
referralRedFlags: Severe DKA (pH <7.0, HCO3 <10, stupor/coma) requires ICU Admission. Altered sensorium, rapid/labored breathing are red flags. Keep a low threshold for timely diagnosis and management of DKA. Monitor for cerebral edema especially in children.
algorithmNotes: Suspect DKA in T1DM with pain abdomen/vomiting/rapid breathing/altered sensorium; confirm with glucose, ketones, VBG; treat with fluids first then insulin, replace potassium, correct acidosis only in severe cases; monitor hourly. ABBREV: BUN=Blood urea nitrogen; DKA=Diabetic ketoacidosis; ECG=Electrocardiogram; GCS=Glasgow coma scale; I/V=Intravenous; ICU=Intensive care unit; SC=Subcutaneous; VBG=Venous blood gas.

whenToSuspect: WHAT ARE FRAGILITY FRACTURES: To be suspected in fractures resulting from trivial trauma or fall from a standing height or less. For example fracture neck of femur, forearm fracture (Colle's), vertebral fracture.
risk: WHAT TO ASK: Postmenopausal females; Family history of fracture; Previous history of fracture; Renal stone disease; Pancreatitis; Steroid abuse or alternative medications or clinical stigma of Cushing's; Premature ovarian failure (less than 40 years); Diabetes; Chronic diarrhoea or bloating sensation; Use of antiepileptics like phenytoin etc; Cushings with hypogonadism; Chronic systemic illnesses like rheumatoid arthritis; Smoking, chronic systemic diseases, CKD, CLD, Endocrine disorders, Thyroid disorders, Hypogonadism.
scores: DXA scan osteoporosis: T score osteoporosis >= -2.5; severe osteoporosis = fracture or T score >= -3.0. Use Z score for age less than 50 for men and premenopausal women.
investigations: BIOCHEMICAL: Fasting serum calcium, phosphate, alkaline phosphatase and albumin (if available) hemogram, myeloma-proteins in serum or urine, Fasting blood glucose, PTH (parathyroid), 25 hydroxy Vitamin D, IgA tTg, Renal function tests, bone markers beta cross LAP. BONE IMAGING: DXA scan; X-ray of fracture site; X-ray lumbar spine (Lateral), pelvis (AP), skull (lateral), both hands. Ultrasound abdomen (gall stones, renal stones and nephrocalcinosis), Ultrasound neck (enlarged parathyroid), Sestamibi scan for parathyroid enlargement.
drugs: TREATMENT: Daily oral calcium 1-1.5 gm/day; Vitamin D supplementation to maintain serum 25OHD levels of 30.0-50 ng/ml; Stop smoking alcohol; Inj Zoledronic acid 5mg I/V infusion OR Inj Denosumab 60mg S/C every 6 months OR Inj rPTH 20 microg S/C daily for maximum 2 years.
management: HOW TO TREAT: Resuscitate the patient if needed; Stabilize the fracture. Then calcium + vitamin D supplementation and anti-resorptive/anabolic therapy. WHEN AND WHERE TO REFER: Refer to orthopaedician for fracture management/surgical management; Refer to endocrinologist for evaluation and treatment of osteoporosis.
referralRedFlags: Refer to orthopaedician for fracture management/surgical management. Refer to endocrinologist for evaluation and treatment of osteoporosis. Keep a high threshold for invasive procedures. Suspect secondary causes (steroid use, Cushing's, hyperparathyroidism, malabsorption, hypogonadism).
algorithmNotes: Fragility fracture = fracture from trivial trauma/fall from standing height; evaluate for secondary osteoporosis causes; investigate biochemistry + DXA + imaging; stabilize fracture and treat osteoporosis (calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonate/denosumab/rPTH); refer ortho + endocrine. ABBREV: CKD=Chronic kidney disease; CLD=Chronic liver disease; rPTH=recombinant Parathyroid hormone; DXA=Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry.

whenToSuspect: PRIMARY HYPOTHYROIDISM Symptoms: Fatigue / Weight gain with poor appetite / Dry skin and cold intolerance / Hair loss / Constipation / Hoarseness of voice / Dyspnea / Muscle weakness and cramps / Menorrhagia (later oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea) / Infertility / Difficulty concentration and poor memory / Paraesthesia / Impaired hearing. Signs: Dry coarse skin / Cool peripheral extremities / Puffy face, hands and feet (myxoedema) / Diffuse alopecia / Goitre / Bradycardia / Peripheral Oedema / Delayed tendon reflex relaxation / Carpel tunnel syndrome / Serous cavity effusions. CONGENITAL HYPOTHYROIDISM: New born screening (usually asymptomatic), Prolonged icterus / Edema of eyelids, hands and feet / Hypotonia / Inactivity / Gestation >42 wk / Birth weight >4 kg / Poor feeding / Hypothermia / Abdominal distention / Open posterior fontanelle (>5mm). CENTRAL (SECONDARY) HYPOTHYROIDISM: Mild-moderate symptoms of hypothyroidism / Signs and symptoms of other pituitary deficits / Manifestations of concomitant hypothalamic pituitary disease (less pronounced as multiple pituitary hormone deficiencies can mask features).
risk: BILLEWICZ SCORING for diagnosis of Hypothyroidism (Score if present). Symptoms: Hearing impairment 1, Diminished sweating 1, Constipation 1, Paraesthesia 1, Hoarseness 1, Weight increase 1, Dry skin 1. Physical signs: Slow movement 1, Periorbital puffiness 1, Delayed ankle reflex 1, Coarse skin 1, Cold skin 1. Add 1 point for women younger than 55 years. Total score 12. Interpretation: Hypothyroid >=6 points; Intermediate 3-5 points; Euthyroid <=2 points.
scores: Billewicz score: Hypothyroid >=6 points, Intermediate 3-5 points, Euthyroid <=2 points. Overt hypothyroidism - TSH elevated with low FT4 or T4 levels. Subclinical hypothyroidism - TSH elevated with normal FT4 or T4 levels. Congenital screening - TSH > 30 mU/L; T4 <10th centile; Confirmatory TSH > 9 mU/L, FT4 < 0.6 ng/ml. Central - TSH levels normal or low with low FT4 or T4 levels.
investigations: PRIMARY: TSH, FT4 or Total T4, TPO antibodies (if available). CONGENITAL (tests after 72 hours): TSH, FT4 or T4, USG neck, nuclear imaging (not a must, do not delay treatment). CENTRAL (SECONDARY): FT4 or T4, TSH, Other pituitary profile, Imaging of sella.
drugs: INITIATING THERAPY - PRIMARY: Levothyroxine 1.6 to 1.8 mcg per kg per day single dose, fasting status, no calorie intake for 1 hour thereafter; titrate based on TSH levels; Elderly and CAD patients start with 12.5-25 mcg/d with 12.5-25 mcg/d incremental dose every 3-4 wk; consider treating subclinical hypothyroidism in presence of large goitre / positive TPO antibody / ASCVD / heart failure / dyslipidemia / infertility / depression / refractory anaemia / personal or family history of autoimmune disease. CONGENITAL: Levothyroxine 10 to 15 mcg per kg per day single daily dosing, given with breast milk in powdered form; titrate based on FT4 levels and TSH initially, later based on TSH levels. CENTRAL (SECONDARY): Levothyroxine 1.3 mcg per kg per day; treatment to be initiated only after treating co existing adrenal insufficiency with Hydrocortisone replacement as there is risk of precipitating adrenal crisis; titrate based on FT4 or T4 levels.
management: FOLLOW UP - PRIMARY (titrate based on TSH): Target TSH young 1-2.5 mU/L, middle-aged 1.5-3, elderly <60y >4.5, 60-70y >6.0, 70-80y >7.0 to 8.0 mU/L; once in 3 to 6 months initially, once stable dose achieved annual follow up. CONGENITAL (titrate based on FT4/T4 and TSH): Target T4 10 to 16 mcg/dl, FT4 1.4 to 2.3 ng/dl, TSH 0.5 to 2 mU/L; initial follow up at 2 and 4 weeks; every 1 to 2 months in first 6 months; every 3 to 4 months from 6 months to 3 years of age; every 6 to 12 months till growth complete. CENTRAL (titrate based on FT4/T4): Target T4 or FT4 young - upper half of normal range, elderly - mid normal range; once in 3 to 6 months initially, once stable annual follow up.
referralRedFlags: Central hypothyroidism: treat adrenal insufficiency first (Hydrocortisone) before levothyroxine to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis. Congenital hypothyroidism: do not delay treatment for imaging. Keep a high threshold for invasive procedures. Refer complex/secondary cases to endocrinology.
algorithmNotes: Classify hypothyroidism (primary/congenital/central); use clinical suspicion (Billewicz) + TSH/FT4; treat with weight-based levothyroxine; titrate and follow up by TSH (primary) or FT4 (central/congenital). ABBREV: ASCVD=Atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease; CAD=Coronary Artery Disease; TPO=Thyroid peroxidase; TSH=Thyroid-stimulating hormone; USG=Ultrasound sonography. REFERENCE: Billewicz WZ et al. Statistical methods applied to the diagnosis of hypothyroidism. Q J Med 1969.

condition: Acute Gastrointestinal Bleed in Adults - Part A
whenToSuspect: Diagnose Acute GI Bleed if there is history of: Vomiting of blood (Hematemesis); Bleeding per rectum (Hematochezia) - red stools; Black tarry stools (Melena) - black stools; Blood in nasogastric tube (NG) (Active GI bleed).
risk: ASSESS FOR HIGH RISK (classify as high risk if any of these present): Pulse rate >100/min; Systolic BP <90 mmHg; H/O Syncope; Oxygen saturation <90%; Altered sensorium; Age >60 years and/or significant co-morbid conditions. CLINICAL EVALUATION - Site of bleed: Hematemesis/blood in NG tube/melena = Upper GI bleed; Fresh blood per rectum/maroon stools = Lower/Upper GI bleed. Etiology: H/o alcohol intake/jaundice/blood transfusion, O/E jaundice/ascites/splenomegaly = Variceal bleed; H/o epigastric pain/NSAID intake/antiplatelets = Ulcer bleed; lower GI bleed with H/o fever/diarrhea = Infective causes (eg Typhoid); H/o bleeding per rectum with concomitant yellow stools = Hemorrhoids/rectal lesion. Rate of blood loss: Large volume hematemesis/fresh blood/frequent melena/postural giddiness/breathlessness/hypotension = Rapid blood loss. Precipitants: Aspirin/NSAIDs/antiplatelets/anticoagulants = Stop all precipitants. Co-morbid: Cardiovascular/renal disease/malignancy = Assess functional status.
scores: RESUSCITATION TARGETS: Pulse rate <100/min; Systolic BP >90 mmHg; Oxygen saturation >90%; Hemoglobin >7 g/dL (in case of heart disease >9 g/dL). Refer all high risk cases after initial resuscitation. All cases of acute GI Bleed must undergo endoscopy within 24 hours of initial stabilisation; patients with active ongoing bleed may require an earlier endoscopy; appropriate informed consent to be taken prior to endoscopy.
investigations: Hemoglobin, platelets, TLC, PTL, INR. Blood grouping and cross matching to arrange blood. DESIRABLE TESTS: Prothrombin time/INR, liver function tests, blood urea and creatinine, HBsAg, Anti HCV, ultrasound abdomen.
drugs: RESUSCITATE: Place atleast one IV cannula (minimum 18 G) and start crystalloids (Ringer's lactate or normal saline); Place a NG tube and perform lavage; Start supplemental oxygen at 2 L/min in high risk cases and those in shock; Stop antiplatelets and anticoagulants (if H/o recent myocardial infarction or stent placed, consult a cardiologist). BLOOD TRANSFUSION: Give packed RBC/whole blood if Hb <7 g/dL (or Hb <9 g/dL in case pre-existing heart disease). PHARMACOTHERAPY - All patients (PPIs): Inj. Pantoprazole or Esomeprazole 80 mg I.V. stat, followed by 40 mg 12 hourly; if I.V. not available, give oral Pantoprazole/Esomeprazole; stop if variceal bleed is documented. Suspected variceal bleed (Vasoconstrictors): Inj Terlipressin 2 mg I.V. stat, followed by Terlipressin 1 mg 6 hourly X 3-5 days OR Inj Somatostatin 250 microg I.V. stat, followed by 250 microg/hr infusion X 3-5 days OR Inj Octreotide 50 microg stat I.V. followed by 50 microg/hr infusion X 3-5 days (avoid Terlipressin in suspected heart disease or peripheral vascular disease; if on Terlipressin examine for signs of peripheral/cardiac ischemia regularly). Suspected variceal bleed (Antibiotics): Inj Ceftriaxone I.V. 1 g 12 hourly x 3-5 days OR Inj Cefotaxime I.V. 1 g 8 hourly X 3-5 days. Lower GI bleed with fever (Antibiotics): Inj Ceftriaxone 2g I.V. 12 hourly AND Inj Metronidazole 500 mg I.V. 8 hourly X 5 days.
management: MANAGEMENT: Continue resuscitation; Blood transfusion (packed RBC/whole blood if Hb <7 g/dL, <9 g/dL if pre-existing heart disease); Patient may need ICU care depending on overall general condition; if patient in altered sensorium and bleeding actively, secure airway. All cases of acute GI Bleed must undergo endoscopy within 24 hours of initial stabilisation. Refer to Part B of treatment workflow for endoscopic therapy and/or surgery.
referralRedFlags: High risk (pulse >100, SBP <90, syncope, SpO2 <90%, altered sensorium, age >60/comorbid) - refer all after initial resuscitation. Secure airway if altered sensorium with active bleeding. In elderly patients with GI bleeding, ensure that malignancy has been ruled out. Endoscopy within 24 hours of stabilisation.
algorithmNotes: Recognise GI bleed (hematemesis/melena/hematochezia/NG blood); assess high-risk criteria; resuscitate (IV access, crystalloids, oxygen, transfuse to targets); start PPI for all, vasoconstrictors + antibiotics for variceal; endoscopy within 24h. ABBREV: HCV=Hepatitis C virus; INR=International normalized ratio; NG=Nasogastric; NSAID=Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs; PPIs=Proton pump inhibitors; PTL=Platelet count; RBC=Red blood cell; TLC=Total leukocyte count.

condition: Acute Gastrointestinal Bleed in Adults - Part B
whenToSuspect: Part B covers interventional management after Upper GI Endoscopy (UGIE) for acute GI bleed (continuation of Part A). Findings stratified as: Portal Hypertension (PHT); Gastric or Duodenal Ulcer; Presentation with hematochezia or melena (but no hematemesis) and UGIE is normal.
risk: PORTAL HYPERTENSION (PHT) - Esophageal varices vs Fundal varices. GASTRIC OR DUODENAL ULCER - Ulcer with active spurt/slow oozing/visible vessel. LOWER GI BLEED - If active lower GI bleeding (UGIE normal). Continue vasoactive drugs for total of 5 days in patients with: active bleeding on endoscopy, patients who are Child-Pugh class C status, received >4 packed red cell transfusions.
scores: DISCHARGE CRITERIA: Hemodynamically stable; Heart rate <90/min; Patient is conscious; No bleeding for at least the past 72 hours (as indicated by no further fall in hemoglobin). Recommendations for transfusion: consider in case of active bleeding; transfuse platelets if platelet count is <50000/mm3.
investigations: LFT and viral markers; USG abdomen; Try to ascertain the etiology of portal hypertension. For ulcer: repeat endoscopy and H. pylori testing at 4-6 weeks. Relook endoscopy after 24 hours if index endoscopy showed active bleeding and endoscopic hemostasis was inadequate.
drugs: INTERVENTIONAL MANAGEMENT (Upper GI Endoscopy). PORTAL HYPERTENSION - Esophageal varices: Variceal band ligation is preferable; Sclerotherapy if band ligation not feasible/available. Fundal varices: 0.5-1 mL of Cyanoacrylate glue injection per varix. Failure to control bleeding: Fully covered large bore esophageal metal stent or TIPS. GASTRIC OR DUODENAL ULCER - Ulcer with active spurt/slow oozing/visible vessel: Inject around the ulcer 1:10000 Adrenaline diluted in saline + hemoclips/thermal device (APC/electrocoagulation); Continue PPI infusion for 72 hours; if rebleeding try another attempt to control with endotherapy; failure to control bleeding refer to higher centre where surgical treatment available. LOWER GI BLEED (UGIE normal) - If active lower GI bleeding after stabilisation and facility for colonoscopy available: prepare bowel with polyethylene glycol solution before colonoscopy, hemostasis as in UGI bleeding; if colonoscopy normal consider small bowel evaluation; if facility not available stabilise & refer. Continue I.V. antibiotics for 5 days or longer depending on nature of infection. TREATMENT AT DISCHARGE - PHT: Propranolol (20 mg) 12 hourly, increase incrementally to achieve heart rate of 60/min unless contraindicated; repeat endoscopy and assessment for EVL after 2 weeks, continue EVL every 2 to 3 weekly till varices eradicated; plan long term care for underlying liver disease. Ulcer: Pantoprazole/Omeprazole (40 mg) twice a day for 4 weeks; reconsider need for NSAIDs; repeat endoscopy and H. pylori testing at 4-6 weeks and appropriate treatment. Infective: continue oral antibiotics for total of 10-14 days.
