management: FURTHER MANAGEMENT (PHT): LFT and viral markers, USG abdomen, ascertain etiology; continue vasoactive drugs 5 days; in case of rebleeding try another attempt with endotherapy; failure - esophageal metal stent or TIPS. FURTHER MANAGEMENT (Ulcer): continue PPI infusion 72 hours; relook endoscopy after 24 hours if active bleeding and inadequate hemostasis; rebleeding - another endotherapy attempt; failure - refer to surgical centre. FURTHER MANAGEMENT (Lower GI): continue I.V. antibiotics 5 days or longer; rebleeding - refer to centre with surgery/angiography; if unidentified source on UGIE and colonoscopy refer to higher centre. RESTARTING OF ANTI PLATELETS/ANTICOAGULANTS (if indicated): after control of bleed resume low dose Aspirin on day 1; other antiplatelets/anticoagulants may be resumed by day 3-5; consultation with cardiologist should be taken.
referralRedFlags: Failure to control variceal bleeding - esophageal metal stent or TIPS, refer. Ulcer rebleeding/failure - refer to higher centre with surgical treatment. Lower GI bleed rebleeding - refer to centre with surgery/angiography; unidentified source on UGIE and colonoscopy - refer to higher centre. In elderly patients with GI bleeding, ensure that malignancy has been ruled out.
algorithmNotes: After UGIE, manage by finding: varices (band ligation/glue, vasoactive drugs, propranolol, TIPS), ulcer (adrenaline + hemoclips/thermal, PPI 72h), or normal UGIE with lower bleed (colonoscopy/refer). Discharge when stable, conscious, HR<90, no bleed 72h. ABBREV: APC=Argon plasma coagulation; EVL=Endoscopic variceal ligation; GI=Gastrointestinal; H. pylori=Helicobacter pylori; LFT=Liver function test; NSAIDs=Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs; PPI=Proton pump inhibitor; TIPS=Transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt; USG=Ultrasonography.

whenToSuspect: SIGNS & SYMPTOMS: Viral prodrome; Loss of appetite/nausea/vomiting; High coloured urine; Pain abdomen; Fever; Itching, clay coloured stools. ESSENTIAL TESTS: Serum Bilirubin > 1.5 mg/dL. Pathway 1 (Anorexia/nausea/vomiting, Viral Prodrome): LFT/SGOT/SGPT markedly raised, SAP mildly raised, USG no biliary dilatation = Parenchymal liver disease (Hepatitis). Pathway 2 (Pain, Pruritus, Clay coloured stool): LFT/SGOT/SGPT raised, SAP markedly raised, USG biliary dilatation = Biliary obstruction.
risk: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS COMMON CAUSES: 1) Jaundice (isolated raised bilirubin) - Hemolytic anaemia, Congenital hyperbilirubinemia. 2) Obstructive jaundice - Benign: Common bile duct stone, Biliary stricture; Malignant: Carcinoma gall bladder, Carcinoma pancreas, Peri-ampullary carcinoma, Cholangiocarcinoma. 3) Parenchymal liver disease - Viral hepatitis, Alcoholic hepatitis, Drug induced hepatitis (eg ATT), Autoimmune hepatitis. 4) Systemic infections (usually with fever) - Complicated malaria, Enteric fever, Dengue fever, Scrub typhus, Leptospirosis.
scores: SUPPORTIVE LAB EVIDENCE: Isolated raised bilirubin - isolated rise in bilirubin (indirect bilirubin > direct bilirubin), normal SGOT/SGPT/SAP/GGT, normal ultrasonography of liver & biliary system. Obstructive - significantly elevated SAP (>4-5 X upper limit of normal), normal/mildly elevated SGOT & SGPT, imaging shows biliary obstruction. Parenchymal - elevated SGOT & SGPT (usually >5 x upper limit of normal; <500 in alcoholic hepatitis), viral markers/history of alcohol/hepatotoxic drugs. Systemic infection - in appropriate clinical setting peripheral smear for malarial parasite or blood culture or widal test/appropriate serology.
investigations: Serum Bilirubin (direct/indirect), LFT/SGOT/SGPT, SAP (Serum Alkaline Phosphatase), GGT, USG of liver & biliary system. Viral markers. Peripheral smear for malarial parasite, blood culture, widal test/appropriate serology in systemic infections. CT scan/MRCP for further work up of obstructive jaundice.
drugs: MANAGEMENT - Hemolytic disease: Start tablet Folic acid 5 mg once a day and refer to a hematologist. Congenital hyperbilirubinemia: Reassurance & refer to higher center for confirmation; Normal diet. Obstructive: Start IV antibiotics if patient has fever and/or elevated TLC for suspected cholangitis; Start IV fluids if patient dehydrated; Refer to higher centre with facility for CT scan/MRCP for further work up; Rx ERCP/PTBD/Surgery; Normal diet. Parenchymal liver disease: Maintain hydration; Symptomatic Rx eg antiemetics; Normal diet; Treat specific infectious illness; Thiamine for alcoholic hepatitis; AVOID ALCOHOL AND ALL NON PRESCRIPTION DRUGS. Systemic infections: Treat specific systemic infection; Normal diet.
management: Manage by cause: hemolytic (folic acid + hematologist), congenital (reassure + refer), obstructive (antibiotics if cholangitis, IV fluids, refer for CT/MRCP and ERCP/PTBD/surgery), parenchymal (hydration, antiemetics, thiamine for alcoholic hepatitis, avoid alcohol/non-prescription drugs), systemic infection (treat specific infection). Identify whether parenchymal vs obstructive using LFT pattern and USG.
referralRedFlags: REFERRAL TRIGGERS: INR >1.5 or rising INR (may be an early indicator of liver failure); Altered sensorium; Bleeding; Recurrent vomiting with dehydration; Hypotension (systolic BP <90 mmHg). Refer obstructive jaundice to higher centre with CT/MRCP facility.
algorithmNotes: Confirm jaundice (bilirubin >1.5); split into parenchymal (high SGOT/SGPT, no biliary dilatation) vs obstructive (high SAP, biliary dilatation) vs isolated bilirubin (hemolytic/congenital) vs systemic infection; manage by cause; watch INR/sensorium/bleeding as referral triggers for liver failure. ABBREV: ATT=Anti tubercular drugs; ERCP=Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography; GGT=gamma-glutamyl transferase; LFT=Liver function test; MRCP=Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography; PTBD=Percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage; SAP=Serum Alkaline Phosphatase; SGOT=Serum Glutamic-Oxaloacetic Transaminase; SGPT=Serum Glutamic Pyruvic Transaminase; TLC=Total Leucocyte Count.

whenToSuspect: ACUTE LIVER FAILURE OR ACUTE ON CHRONIC LIVER FAILURE. ACUTE LIVER FAILURE (ALF): Acute liver injury, No underlying liver disease. ACUTE ON CHRONIC LIVER FAILURE (ACLF): Underlying liver disease, Acute precipitating event. DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA - ALF: Jaundice < 4 weeks; Encephalopathy; Coagulopathy [INR >=1.5]; No evidence of prior chronic liver disease such as splenomegaly, ascites etc. ACLF: Jaundice (Bilirubin >= 5mg/dL) and coagulopathy (INR >= 1.5); Ascites/Hepatic encephalopathy within 4 weeks of onset of jaundice; other organ failure; Evidence of chronic liver disease.
risk: CAUSES - ALF: Primary liver disease (Viral hepatitis, Drug induced hepatitis e.g. ATT, Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy/HELLP syndrome, Poisoning); Systemic infection with secondary liver involvement (Malaria, Leptospirosis, Typhoid, Rickettsial disease) - suspect if fever is predominant symptom, rash (Rickettsial), renal dysfunction, anemia/thrombocytopenia/subconjunctival haemorrhage. ACLF: Acute precipitating event (Acute hepatitis, sepsis, GI bleeding, alcohol and drugs); Chronic liver disease (Alcohol/hepatitis B or C/non-alcoholic fatty liver disease/autoimmune liver disease/Wilson's disease); Severity assessment of ACLF - additional organ failure indicates severe disease.
scores: ALF diagnostic criteria: jaundice <4 weeks, encephalopathy, INR >=1.5, no prior chronic liver disease. ACLF: bilirubin >=5 mg/dL + INR >=1.5 + ascites/encephalopathy within 4 weeks + chronic liver disease. Severity of ACLF: additional organ failure indicates severe disease. INR is a key marker of severity and coagulopathy.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: Hemoglobin, Leucocyte count (Total and Differential), Platelet count, Prothrombin time-INR, Blood Sugar, Liver function test, Blood Urea, Serum Creatinine, Sodium/Potassium, Ascitic fluid analysis & culture, Ultrasound abdomen. DESIRABLE: Arterial blood gas and pH, Blood NH3 levels, UGIE in ACLF. DIAGNOSTIC: Primary liver disease serology - HBsAg, IgG Anti HBC, IgM anti-HAV, IgM anti HEV and anti HCV antibodies; Systemic infection - work up for Malaria/Typhoid/Leptospira/Rickettsial infection in acute febrile illness.
drugs: PRIMARY TREATMENT/STABILIZATION: I.V. Fluids Normal saline/Ringer's lactate (add 50% dextrose if blood sugar low); O2 supplementation if required; Secure airway by tracheal intubation if grade 3-4 coma; Antibiotics/antimalarials depending on clinical suspicion after taking blood culture; Inj. Pantoprazole 40mg IV once a day for stress ulcer prophylaxis; I.V. mannitol 100ml SOS for cerebral edema/grade 3-4 coma provided there is no renal failure in ALF; IV infusion N-Acetylcysteine 150mg/kg in drug (induced ALF) over 1 hour, Loading 150 mg/kg over 1 hour, 50 mg/kg over 4 hours, Maintenance 100 mg/kg over 16 hours every day. MANAGEMENT AT HIGHER CENTRE: Admission in intensive care; Prophylactic broad spectrum antibiotics after taking blood culture; Correct hypo-/hyper-kalemia; No role of prophylactic Fresh Frozen Plasma (FFP) for coagulopathy; If hepatitis B - Tenofovir or Entecavir; Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy/HELLP - prompt delivery. ORGAN FAILURE: Hypotension - Fluid resuscitation 20ml/kg over 2 hours, maintenance fluid guided by hydration/urine output, if no response Vasopressors Noradrenaline I.V. infusion. Respiratory failure - O2 inhalation, nebulization if bronchoconstriction, may require ventilation. Acute renal failure - maintain fluid and electrolyte balance, stop diuretics No NSAIDs, in ACLF Terlipressin 1mg IV 6 hourly plus 20-40g albumin (20%) over 6-12 hours for suspected hepatorenal syndrome, may require dialysis. SEPSIS - Fluid resuscitation; I.V. antibiotics for unidentified source broad spectrum within an hour, for SBP IV Ceftriaxone 1g BD; to prevent hepatorenal syndrome IV Albumin 20-40g over 6-12 hours. ENCEPHALOPATHY - treat underlying precipitating factor, usual care for comatosed patient, secure airway if grade 3-4. FOR ACLF: Syrup Lactulose 20-30ml 6 hourly titrate dose to produce 3-4 stools/day; Rifaximin 400mg TDS.
management: MANAGEMENT: Urgent referral to a higher centre after initial stabilization of patient/if no improvement/worsening despite therapy. Primary treatment/stabilization at lower level (fluids, O2, airway, antibiotics, PPI, mannitol, NAC). Management at higher centre: ICU admission, prophylactic antibiotics, correct potassium, hepatitis B antivirals, prompt delivery in AFLP/HELLP, re-investigate to diagnose acute and chronic liver injury. Treatment at higher centre by organ failure (hypotension/respiratory/renal), sepsis, encephalopathy, and ACLF-specific (lactulose, rifaximin). If GI Bleeding refer to STW on GI bleeding.
referralRedFlags: Urgent referral to higher centre after initial stabilization or if no improvement/worsening despite therapy. Grade 3-4 coma - secure airway by tracheal intubation. INR >=1.5 with encephalopathy indicates liver failure. Additional organ failure in ACLF indicates severe disease requiring ICU. If GI bleeding occurs refer to STW on GI bleeding.
algorithmNotes: Distinguish ALF (no chronic disease) from ACLF (chronic disease + acute insult); diagnose by jaundice + encephalopathy + INR>=1.5; find cause (viral/drug/systemic/precipitant); stabilize then refer; at higher centre manage organ failures, sepsis, encephalopathy; ACLF use lactulose + rifaximin. No prophylactic FFP. ABBREV: HELLP=Haemolysis elevated liver enzymes low platelet count; IgM anti-HAV=Immunoglobulin M antibody to hepatitis a virus; HBsAg=Hepatitis B virus surface antigen; IgM anti-HBc=Immunoglobulin M antibody to Hepatitis B core antigen; IgM anti-HEV=Immunoglobulin M antibody to hepatitis E virus; ATT=Anti-Tubercular treatment; INR=International normalised ratio; UGIE=Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy.

whenToSuspect: PRESENTATION OF APPENDICITIS MAY BE AS: 1) Acute appendicitis - early/delayed; 2) Appendicular mass; 3) Recurrent appendicitis. ACUTE APPENDICITIS (early presentation within 72 hours). SYMPTOMS: 1) Pain - a) Periumbilical or epigastric colic (in nonobstructive type pain may start at RIF), b) Shifting of pain from periumbilical region; 2) Nausea/vomiting; 3) Pyrexia (usually absent in first 6 hours); 4) Loss of appetite. NB: Pain always precedes vomiting (Murphy); onset more acute and abrupt in acute obstructive appendicitis; if patient has rigor and high fever within 24hrs of onset of pain, appendicitis is most unlikely. SIGNS: 1) Pointing sign; 2) Cough test (pain at right iliac fossa on coughing); 3) Tenderness at Mc Burney's point; 4) Muscle guard at right iliac fossa (RIF); 5) Rovsing's sign (pain at RIF with sudden thrust of palpation at left flank); 6) Rebound tenderness; 7) Generalised rigidity (sign of generalized peritonitis).
risk: FACTORS FOR PREPONDERANCE/COMPLICATIONS: 1. Extremes of age; 2. Immunosuppression; 3. Diabetes Mellitus; 4. Faecolith obstruction of appendicular lumen; 5. Previous abdominal surgery. NB on atypical presentations: retrocaecal appendix signs may be masked; inflamed pelvic appendix near urinary bladder/rectum may cause cystitis/tenesmus; post ileal appendix may cause diarrhoea and marked retching; with progress of pregnancy appendicular pain may be up at right flank as caecum and appendix pushed up; females with inflammatory pelvic organ disease (salpingitis) may have dysmenorrhoea and purulent vaginal discharge.
scores: ACUTE APPENDICITIS (late presentation beyond >=72 hours): No palpable mass = Appendicectomy open/laparoscopic; Palpable mass = Ochsner-Sherren regimen (conservative). Signs of improvement: reduced pain, patient feeling better, appetite improves, tenderness diminishes - continue conservative. Signs deteriorating: assess by USG for pus collection, USG guided aspiration/open drainage. Criteria for stopping conservative treatment: rising pulse and body temperature, increasing intensity and spreading abdominal tenderness, increasing size of mass, vomiting or copious gastric aspirate.
investigations: The diagnosis of acute appendicitis is essentially clinical. Investigations include: 1. Full blood count (usually shows leucocytosis with raised polymorphs); 2. Urinalysis to exclude urinary tract infection; 3. Ultrasonography (very helpful to confirm diagnosis and identify periappendicular collection of exudate or abscess); 4. Plain X-ray abdomen (to rule out ureteric calculus or peptic perforation); 5. CT scan of abdomen (useful in special situation of uncertain diagnosis). Essential preoperative investigations including routine blood sugar, urea, creatinine, Hb%, chest X-ray and ECG in all elderly patients.
drugs: TREATMENT: 1) Treatment of a diagnosed case of acute appendicitis is appendicectomy except in special situations where surgical facility could not be provided or the patient presented late with appendicular mass; Intravenous fluid and broad-spectrum antibiotics to be started on admission. 2) Acute appendicitis should be recognized early before it reaches the stage of peritonitis or abscess formation. OCHSNER-SHERREN CONSERVATIVE TREATMENT (in presence of appendicular mass, surgery may cause bleeding, injury to caecum and ileum, faecal fistula): IV fluid; Broad spectrum antibiotics; Vitamins; No purgative; No Enema. APPENDICECTOMY MAY BE: 1) Conventional open surgery; 2) Laparoscopic appendectomy.
management: Acute appendicitis (early <72h): appendicectomy (open or laparoscopic) + IV fluids and broad-spectrum antibiotics. Late presentation (>=72h): no palpable mass - appendicectomy; palpable mass - Ochsner-Sherren conservative regimen (IV fluids, antibiotics, vitamins, no purgative/enema), monitor for improvement vs deterioration. CHECKLIST FOR UNWELL PATIENT FOLLOWING APPENDICECTOMY: examine operation wound for induration/collection/purulent discharge; consider residual abscess (pelvis, RIF, Hepatorenal pouch of Morrison); examine lungs for pneumonia/collapse; thrombophlebitis; jaundice/enlarged liver; urinary tract infection. RECURRENT APPENDICITIS: diagnosis mainly clinical, treatment is appendicectomy (open or laparoscopic).
referralRedFlags: Operation for appendicular mass if indicated should always be done at higher centre and performed by an experienced surgeon. Deterioration on conservative treatment (rising pulse/temperature, spreading tenderness, increasing mass size, vomiting) - assess by USG, aspirate/drain. Keep a high threshold for invasive procedures.
algorithmNotes: Diagnose appendicitis clinically (pain shift to RIF, McBurney tenderness, Rovsing/rebound); early - appendicectomy + IV fluids/antibiotics; appendicular mass - Ochsner-Sherren conservative then interval appendicectomy; watch for deterioration. ABBREV: RIF=Right iliac fossa; USG=Ultrasonography; ECG=Electrocardiogram.

whenToSuspect: CLINICAL PRESENTATION OF CBD STONES: Asymptomatic/detected on work up for gallbladder stones. Abdominal pain = Obstructive jaundice. Abdominal pain + Fever with chills + Shock = Acute cholangitis. Pain typically radiating to back + Increased amylase = Biliary pancreatitis. WHEN TO SUSPECT CBD STONES IN CASE OF GALL STONE DISEASE: Deranged LFT; Dilated CBD on USG; Raised TLC; Features of acute cholangitis.
risk: Suspect CBD stones in gall stone disease with deranged LFT, dilated CBD on USG, raised TLC, or features of acute cholangitis. Palpable Gall bladder with jaundice may be due to malignancy and grossly dilated CBD may be due to choledochal cyst - should be referred to higher centers. Indications for management decisions: Cholangitis, Biliary pancreatitis with CBD stones on imaging, Non-dilated CBD with CBD stones, Multiple comorbidities, Limited surgeon experience with CBD exploration.
scores: Stone size guides management: Stone > 10 mm vs Stone <= 10 mm. CBD > 2 cm dilated indicates drainage procedures. Risk situations needing endoscopic stenting then ERCP & cholecystectomy: cholangitis, biliary pancreatitis with CBD stones on imaging, non-dilated CBD with CBD stones, multiple comorbidities, limited surgeon experience.
investigations: BIOCHEMICAL INVESTIGATIONS: Haemogram (TLC), blood urea, serum creatinine, amylase/lipase; LFT - Serum bilirubin, AST/ALT, ALP, PT/INR; Other investigations depending on comorbidities. USG ABDOMEN: Gall bladder stones, IHBRD, cholangitic abscess. If CBD stones not seen on USG - MRCP/endoscopic USG (Investigation of choice): Anatomy of biliary tree, IHBRD, stone size, location and CBD diameter; Roadmap for intervention.
drugs: MANAGEMENT OPTIONS (preferably at tertiary care center). GALL BLADDER STONES WITH CBD STONES: Stone > 10 mm - Lap/open cholecystectomy + CBD exploration; Stone <= 10 mm - ERCP (includes sphincterotomy plus clearance of stones); In case of cholangitis/biliary pancreatitis with CBD stones on imaging/non-dilated CBD with CBD stones/multiple comorbidities/limited surgeon experience - Endoscopic stenting followed by ERCP & cholecystectomy (if failure, Lap/open CBD exploration). POST CHOLECYSTECTOMY CBD STONES: ERCP; if ERCP fails - Lap/open CBD exploration. OPTIONS FOR CBD CLOSURE AFTER CBD EXPLORATION: Primary closure; Primary closure with endobiliary stent (prevents bile leak, needs endoscopy for stent removal within 6 weeks); T Tube placement (indications: purulent cholangitis, excessive CBD manipulation, incomplete clearance; removal after T-tube cholangiogram); Drainage procedures (indications: multiple stones/hepatolithiasis, CBD > 2 cm dilated, ampullary stricture; surgical options Choledochoduodenostomy, Hepaticojejunostomy, Transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty).
management: Work up suspected CBD stones with biochemistry + USG, then MRCP/EUS if not seen. Management preferably at tertiary care center. Gall bladder + CBD stones: by stone size (>10mm cholecystectomy + CBD exploration; <=10mm ERCP); high-risk situations - endoscopic stenting then ERCP & cholecystectomy. Post cholecystectomy CBD stones - ERCP, then CBD exploration if fails. Choose CBD closure (primary/with stent/T-tube/drainage) per findings.
referralRedFlags: Palpable Gall bladder with jaundice may be due to malignancy and grossly dilated CBD may be due to choledochal cyst - should be referred to higher centers. Management preferably at tertiary care center. Acute cholangitis with shock is an emergency. Keep a high threshold for invasive procedures.
algorithmNotes: Suspect CBD stone in gall stone disease with deranged LFT/dilated CBD/raised TLC/cholangitis; confirm USG then MRCP/EUS; manage by stone size and risk (cholecystectomy + CBD exploration vs ERCP vs stenting); choose CBD closure method. ABBREV: ALP=Alkaline phosphatase; ALT=Alanine Transaminase; AST=Aspartate transferase; CBD=Common Bile Duct; ERCP=Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography; IHBRD=Intrahepatic Biliary Radical Dilatation; INR=International Normalised Ratio; LFT=Liver Function Test; MRCP=Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography; PT=Prothrombin Time.

whenToSuspect: RED FLAG SIGNS - SYSTEMIC: Sick look, drowsy, abnormal breathing, abnormal pulse, fever; LOCAL: Claudication/rest pain, gangrene, osteomyelitis, acute charcot's foot - Refer to higher centre. EVALUATION: A. General condition (Vitals; Glycaemic control - FBS/PPBS regularly, HbA1c once in 6 months). B. Local examination - check both feet for: 1. Ulcer; 2. Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD); 3. Loss of Protective Sensations (LOPS); 4. Deformities. PAD: history of intermittent claudication, absent pulsations in posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis artery, ankle brachial index < 0.9. LOPS: history of tingling, numbness, feels like walking on mattress; check foot sensation using 10gm monofilament. Deformities: hammer toes, claw toes, hallux valgus, pes cavus, rocker bottom foot.
risk: PREDISPOSING FACTORS - General conditions: Older age, Uncontrolled hyperglycemia, Duration of diabetes mellitus, Peripheral artery disease, Visual impairment, Chronic kidney disease. Local conditions: Loss of peripheral sensations, Structural foot deformity, Limited joint mobility, Improperly fitting footwear, Callus, History of ulcer/amputation. RISK ASSESSMENT & FREQUENCY OF FOLLOW UP: Low (Callus alone, No LOPS, No PAD) - once a year; Medium (Deformity with LOPS or PAD) - once in 6 months; High (Previous amputation or ulceration or any two of Deformity/LOPS/PAD) - once in 3 months.
scores: INFECTION SEVERITY: Non infected wound - No antibiotics. Mild infection (at least two of: swelling/induration, pain/tenderness, warmth/redness 0.5-2cm, purulent discharge) - oral antibiotics for 1-2 weeks (target only aerobic gram-positive cocci). Moderate infection (redness, deep tissues affected - abscess/osteomyelitis/fasciitis/septic arthritis; no systemic signs) - Refer to higher centre. Severe infection (local findings + systemic findings of SIRS at least two of: Temperature >38C or <36C, Heart rate >90/min, Respiratory rate >20/min or PaCO2 <32 mm Hg, WBC >12000 or <4000 or immature bands >10%) - Refer to higher centre.
investigations: Vitals; Glycaemic control - FBS/PPBS regularly, HbA1c once in 6 months. Ulcer: check for osteomyelitis with bedside probe-to-bone test or an X-ray. PAD: ankle brachial index (<0.9 abnormal). LOPS: foot sensation using 10gm monofilament. SIRS parameters for severe infection (temperature, heart rate, respiratory rate/PaCO2, WBC).
drugs: ULCER: If osteomyelitis not suspected - do moist dressings, achieve good glycaemic control, off-load the foot; if osteomyelitis suspected - refer to higher centre. WOUND CARE - DO: Moist dressings, change dressings daily for dirty wounds and on alternate days for clean wounds. DON'T USE: Hydrogen peroxide, EUSOL, povidone iodine, chlorhexidine etc; Hyperbaric oxygen, antimicrobial dressings and stem cell therapy has insufficient evidence to be recommended. ANTIBIOTICS: Non infected - no antibiotics; Mild infection - oral antibiotics for 1-2 weeks (target only aerobic gram-positive cocci); Moderate/Severe - refer. OTHER FOOT CONDITIONS: Corns/callosity - scaling in OPD, footwear modification; Web space fungal infection - topical antifungals, maintain local hygiene; In-growing toe nails - regular nail trimming; Charcot's foot - refer to higher centre.
management: Evaluate general condition + local exam (ulcer/PAD/LOPS/deformities). Manage ulcer (moist dressings, glycaemic control, off-loading; refer if osteomyelitis/necrotic tissue/cellulitis needing fasciotomy/wet gangrene needing amputation/negative pressure wound therapy). PAD - refer for evaluation and revascularisation. LOPS - if sensations absent and asymptomatic, educate patient, advise protective footwear, regular follow up per risk category. Deformities - refer. Risk-based follow up (low yearly, medium 6 monthly, high 3 monthly). PATIENT EDUCATION - DO: daily self inspection of foot, wear comfortable proper fitting footwear, cut toe nails straight, keep blood sugars controlled, regular foot check up; DON'T: walk barefoot even at home, remove calluses/corns at home, smoking (delays healing).
referralRedFlags: ALWAYS KEEP A LOW THRESHOLD FOR REFERRAL TO HIGHER CENTRE. RED FLAG SIGNS - Systemic: sick look, drowsy, abnormal breathing, abnormal pulse, fever; Local: claudication/rest pain, gangrene, osteomyelitis, acute charcot's foot. Refer for: surgical debridement of wounds with necrotic tissue, cellulitis needing fasciotomy, wet gangrene needing amputation, negative pressure wound therapy, moderate/severe infection, PAD for revascularisation, deformities, charcot's foot.
algorithmNotes: Screen both feet for ulcer/PAD/LOPS/deformity; stratify infection (none/mild/moderate/severe) and refer moderate+; wound care with moist dressings (avoid antiseptics); risk-based follow up; emphasize patient education and offloading. ABBREV: EUSOL=Edinburgh university solution of lime; FBS=Fasting blood sugar; LOPS=Diabetic peripheral neuropathy with loss of protective sensation; PAD=Peripheral arterial disease; PPBS=Post prandial blood sugar; SIRS=Systemic inflammatory response syndrome.

whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: 1) Pain - A) Biliary colic (slowly progressive, constant pain in right upper quadrant or mid-epigastrium, crescendo-decrescendo pattern); B) Acute cholecystitis (prolonged pain more than biliary colic >24 hrs associated with fever); 2) Nausea or vomiting; 3) Dyspepsia; 4) Flatulence; 5) Food intolerance; 6) Jaundice (GB stone impacted at the neck or Hartmann's pouch that compresses CBD); 7) Acute cholangitis (pain, fever, jaundice). PRESENTATION: Symptomatic, Asymptomatic, or Complicated. COMPLICATED: 1. Acute cholecystitis; 2. Empyema; 3. Mucocoele; 4. Acalculous cholecystitis (critically ill patients - unexplained fever, lack of right upper quadrant tenderness, leukocytosis); 5. Gangrene/perforation; 6. Biliary obstruction; 7. Acute pancreatitis.
risk: ABSOLUTE INDICATIONS for cholecystectomy in asymptomatic GSD: Suspected malignancy, GB polyp > 1cm, Porcelain GB, Chronic hemolytic anemia, Transplant patient especially cardiac transplant, Calculi > 3cm, Multiple small stones, Paediatric gall stones. RELATIVE INDICATIONS: Calculi > 2cm, Calculi < 3mm with patent cystic duct, Diabetes mellitus, Non functioning GB.
scores: Stratify presentation as Asymptomatic / Symptomatic / Complicated. Acute cholecystitis stable: Early LC within 24-72 hours (if expertise available) OR Delayed LC after 6 weeks by experienced lap surgeon. Non resolving: Cholecystostomy followed by laparoscopic cholecystectomy by experienced surgeon after 6 weeks. Mild/moderate pancreatitis: Lap cholecystectomy within same hospital admission. Severe pancreatitis: Lap cholecystectomy after 4-6 weeks of resolution of pancreatitis.
investigations: Haemogram, RFT, electrolytes, CXR, RBS, ECG (to distinguish from cardiac pain); LFT - Serum bilirubin, SGOT/PT, Alkaline Phosphatase; Amylase, lipase; USG abdomen (investigation of choice, sensitivity 95%) - status of gall bladder and characteristic distal acoustic shadow, status of liver/CBD/intra hepatic biliary radicle dilatation (IHBRD), other intra abdominal pathology like renal stones, ovarian pathology. MRCP (indications: jaundice, high ALP, dilated CBD on USG, suspected CBD stones or Mirizzi's syndrome). EVALUATION OF COMORBIDITIES: DM (fasting & post prandial blood sugar, HbA1c, sugar charting), Cardiac (ECHO), COPD (PFT), Coagulation profile (PT/INR), Thyroid function test.
drugs: MANAGEMENT - Asymptomatic: Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy +/- (only for absolute/relative indications). Symptomatic: Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy. Complicated (if possible managed in tertiary centre after initial resuscitation): Acute calculous cholecystitis - Conservative then LC (early 24-72h if expertise OR delayed after 6 weeks); Gangrene/perforation - Emergency lap/open cholecystectomy; Acute pancreatitis - Conservative management (mild/moderate LC within same admission; severe LC after 4-6 weeks of resolution); Acalculous cholecystitis - Conservative management (unstable with non resolving symptoms - Cholecystostomy; stable - conservative only, LC if symptoms persist after resolution of acute phase); Concomitant GSD + CBD stones - Refer to CBD stones STW. MANAGEMENT OF ACUTE CHOLECYSTITIS (CONSERVATIVE): At PHC level - initial resuscitation, IV antibiotics (3rd generation cephalosporin, metrogyl +/- aminoglycosides), analgesics, bowel rest, USG abdomen if available and refer; At district hospital level - IV hydration, antibiotics, analgesics, bowel rest, USG abdomen, surgical consultation; Tertiary level - early (if presents within 72 hrs)/interval laparoscopic cholecystectomy depending on expertise in laparoscopy.
management: PHC: resuscitation, IV antibiotics (3rd gen cephalosporin, metronidazole +/- aminoglycosides), analgesics, bowel rest, USG if available, refer. District: IV hydration, antibiotics, analgesics, bowel rest, USG, surgical consultation. Tertiary: early (within 72h)/interval laparoscopic cholecystectomy depending on expertise. POST LAP CHOLECYSTECTOMY COMPLICATIONS to watch: patient not looking well/non ambulatory/not tolerating orally, pain out of proportion/not explained/not responding to analgesics, tachycardia, fall in BP, abdominal distention, bile/blood in drain. FOLLOW UP: suture removal after 1 week, HPE report; continue antibiotics if mucocele/empyema/diabetic. CONVERT EARLY IN CASE OF DOUBT IN LAP CHOLECYSTECTOMY. REFER PATIENT EARLY IN CASE OF ANY DOUBT IN POST OP.
referralRedFlags: Complicated GSD should be managed in tertiary centre after initial resuscitation. Concomitant GSD + CBD stones - refer to CBD stones STW. Post-op red flags: patient not looking well, pain out of proportion, tachycardia/fall in BP, bile/blood in drain - refer early. Convert early in case of doubt in lap cholecystectomy. Keep a high threshold for invasive procedures.
algorithmNotes: Classify GSD presentation (asymptomatic/symptomatic/complicated); USG is investigation of choice; manage symptomatic with lap cholecystectomy; complicated by type (cholecystitis, gangrene, pancreatitis, acalculous, CBD stones); conservative antibiotics + timed cholecystectomy; refer complicated to tertiary. ABBREV: CBD=Common bile ducts; GSD=Gall stone disease; HPE=Histopathological examination; LC=Laparoscopic cholecystectomy; MRCP=Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography.

whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: 1) Swelling on anterior abdominal wall; 2) Cough impulse; 3) Reduces on lying down (especially during early phase). Severe local pain or generalized colicky pain is generally indicative of complications: Obstruction, Incarceration, Strangulation (surgical emergency). History of surgery at same site: No = Primary Ventral hernia, Epigastric hernia, Umbilical hernia, Paraumbilical hernia; Yes = Incisional Hernia.
risk: RED FLAG SIGNS NEEDING REFERRAL TO HIGHER CENTRE: 1) Large hernias (>8-10cm) requiring component separation; 2) Parastomal hernias; 3) Comorbidities; 4) Loss of domain; 5) Non availability of mesh for hernias >2cm in size - to be checked. POST OP COMPLICATIONS: Seroma, Infection (including mesh infection), Skin necrosis, Recurrence. CLINICAL EVALUATION: severe local pain or generalized colicky pain or fever generally sign of complications; colicky abdominal pain and irreducible hernia = intestinal obstruction (immediate surgery for relief of obstruction); local redness and severe pain with fever = strangulation (immediate surgery, hernia repair deferred). Rule out respiratory disease (raised intra-abdominal pressure can worsen respiratory condition).
scores: Defect size guides management. No prior surgery: Investigate if doubtful diagnosis; Defect size > 2 cm = Mesh hernioplasty; Defect size < 2 cm = Primary anatomical closure. Prior surgery (Incisional Hernia): USG/CT scan to determine accurate number and size of defects, then Mesh hernioplasty. Surgical emergency if obstruction/incarceration/strangulation.
investigations: Investigate if doubtful diagnosis (primary hernias). For incisional hernia: USG/CT scan to determine accurate number and size of defects. Rule out other diseases or complications on history, particularly related to respiratory system. Assess features of swelling: reducibility of hernia, size and number of defects.
drugs: CHOICE OF REPAIR by defect size: < 2cm - Anatomical repair, IPOM (Open Intraperitoneal onlay mesh); 2-4 cm - IPOM, open sublay repair, onlay repair; 4-8 cm - IPOM plus, open sublay repair, onlay repair; More than 8 cm - Component separation required (anterior or posterior component separation depending on expertise; Botox can be used as an adjunct in case of loss of domain); Subxiphoid hernias - mesh overlap extends below diaphragm in case of IPOM or extraperitoneal repairs; Suprapubic hernias - mesh should extend behind pubic bones in extraperitoneal repair, IPOM after dividing peritoneum so lower end of mesh in retropubic space; Parastomal hernia - if stoma can be closed perform delayed repair, in case of permanent stoma a Sugarbaker technique generally advisable. Mesh placement: sublay manner preferred. Drains recommended.
management: In general ventral hernias should be repaired as untreated hernias are at risk of life threatening complications. Exceptions: untreated ascites especially with portal hypertension, severe comorbidities precluding safe surgery, large hernias where repair may cause more morbidity such as bowel injury. Small midline primary hernias less than 2cm diameter may be closed primarily (anatomical repair); larger hernias and all incisional hernias should undergo mesh reinforcement. Laparoscopic hernia repair suitable for defect size <6 cms and absence of skin complications.
referralRedFlags: Refer to higher centre if: defect size > 6 cms (might need component separation); uncommon site eg subxiphoid, suprapubic, large lateral hernias; loss of domain; large hernias (>8-10cm) requiring component separation; parastomal hernias; significant comorbidities; non availability of mesh for hernias >2cm. SURGICAL EMERGENCY: obstruction, incarceration, strangulation (immediate surgery). Keep a high threshold for invasive procedures.
algorithmNotes: Diagnose ventral/incisional hernia by reducible anterior abdominal wall swelling with cough impulse; emergency if obstructed/strangulated; elective repair by defect size (<2cm primary closure, >2cm mesh; >8cm component separation); mesh sublay preferred with drains; refer complex/large hernias. ABBREV: IPOM=Intraperitoneal onlay mesh.

whenToSuspect: GENERAL INTRODUCTION: Hemolytic anemia, where RBCs sickle under hypoxia or stress; sickling and inflammation lead to vaso-occlusive crisis (VOC) and organ damage; Autosomal recessive - mutations in the beta-globin gene; ~88% of sickle homozygous cases in Asia are Indians. CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS OF SCD: Common presentations - Pain, anemia, icterus, increased risk of infection; Acute morbidity/events - Splenic sequestration, fatigue, acute chest syndrome, priapism; Long term complications - End organ damage, hepatopathy, chronic kidney disease, hypersplenism, avascular necrosis of femur, osteomyelitis, pulmonary hypertension, cholelithiasis, functional disability, retinopathy, foot ulcers (refer to a higher center for adequate management). MANIFESTATIONS OF VOC: Experienced as pain or swelling; each VOC can lead to long lasting problems and end-organ damage; typical sites - hands and feet, limbs, abdominal viscera, ribs, sternum; crisis usually precipitated by fever, strenuous exercise, dehydration, drenching in rain, surgery, infection.
risk: SUBTYPES: Carriers/heterozygous (HbAS) - have only one disease allele, usually asymptomatic; Disease/sufferer/homozygous (HbSS) - have both defective alleles, usually symptomatic; Other symptomatic genetic variants - HbS-beta thalassemia, HbSD-Punjab disease, HbSE disease etc. Mendelian transmission: HbAS x HbAS = 25% normal, 50% sickle cell carrier, 25% sickle cell disease. VOC precipitated by fever, strenuous exercise, dehydration, drenching in rain, surgery, infection.
scores: SCREENING - Antenatal Mothers or pre-pregnancy planning: CBC all women in first trimester; in endemic pockets/high risk population solubility test/POC tests for sickle cell, or HPLC and electrophoresis if available; if mother is sickle cell carrier/disease then testing of father is mandatory (ideally by HPLC; if not available refer to higher center); if father tests positive, counselling and pre-natal testing should be performed to prevent risk of birth of affected newborn. Newborn: POC tests to initiate penicillin prophylaxis in baby and enrolling vaccination program; HPLC and electrophoresis if available or at later date. Population screening/patient of any age: in endemic pockets/high risk population solubility test/POC tests for sickle cell.
investigations: CBC, solubility test/POC tests for sickle cell, HPLC and electrophoresis (confirmatory). HOW TO PRESCRIBE HYDROXYUREA - Baseline Investigations: Complete physical examination, CBC, Liver function test, Renal function, Pregnancy test for relevant population; CBC monitoring 1-3 months when starting the medicine or if dose change. Evaluate for anaemia - iron supplements cautiously (low dose, not more than 3 months), evaluate other nutritional causes (Vit B12, folic acid deficiency) and infectious causes (worm infestations).
drugs: PROPHYLAXIS FOR ALL SCD PATIENTS - New born HbSS till 5 years of age: Penicillin prophylaxis 65mg BD less than 12 months, 125 mg BD till 2 years, then 250mg BD till 5 years (lifelong if post splenectomy). To prevent megaloblastic crises: Folic acid - less than 1 year of age 2.5 mg daily, 1 year of age 5 mg daily. Common recommended vaccinations: Pneumococcal Vaccine, H-influenza vaccine, Typhoid Vaccine, Influenza vaccine, COVID 19 vaccine. PAIN MANAGEMENT: paracetamol, diclofenac or tramadol; for severe pain refer to higher centre. HYDROXYUREA (HU) - Indications: above 2 years of age, all children more than 9 months of age may be offered; Dosing: Infants and Children 10-15 mg/kg/day, Adolescents 15mg/kg/day, dose escalation by 5 mg/kg every 2-3 months only in definite indications; Toxicity: common dose dependent toxicity (anaemia, nausea, diarrhoea, gastritis), nail/skin hyperpigmentation, long term toxicity (mucositis or leg ulcers). Only curative therapy is hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (recommended in small subset not responding to HU or newer disease modifying agents).
management: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF MANAGEMENT: Carriers are usually asymptomatic and need no treatment; goal is to improve quality of life and life expectancy; episodes of fever dealt with early and aggressively; early aggressive pain management (pain may indicate microvascular organ damage), use paracetamol/diclofenac/tramadol, refer for severe pain; malaria present with same frequency as endemic prevalence; evaluate for anaemia (cautious iron supplements, treat nutritional/infectious causes); prophylaxis for infections (penicillin, immunizations, folic acid); disease modifying agents (hydroxyurea) and blood transfusions have specific indications; acute morbidity events require management and regular monitoring; only curative therapy is hematopoietic stem cell transplantation. EDUCATION AND GENETIC COUNSELING: medical disease counselling (clinical presentation, importance of early diagnosis by newborn screening, avoid infections/dehydration/over-exercise/extreme temperatures, penicillin prophylaxis, regular follow up); genetic counselling (carrier state, risk of affected child, pedigree chart, inheritance pattern, risk of recurrence); preconception care counselling for at-risk couples; pre and post test support to family; cascade screening of extended family.
referralRedFlags: RED FLAG FOR HOSPITALIZATION OR REFERRAL TO HIGHER CENTRE: Acute illness requiring immediate medical care including emergencies; Persistent Temperature >38C; Pain inadequately relieved by home measures; Significant respiratory symptoms (cough, shortness of breath, chest pain) or hypoxia; Abdominal pain, distention, acute enlargement of spleen; Any neurological signs or symptoms; Significant increase in pallor, fatigue, lethargy; Significant vomiting and diarrhea. Long term complications - refer to higher center for adequate management. EARLY AND AGGRESSIVE MANAGEMENT OF PAIN AND INFECTIONS WILL HELP IMPROVE LONG TERM OUTCOME.
algorithmNotes: SCD is autosomal recessive hemolytic anaemia with vaso-occlusive crises; screen antenatal/newborn/population; carriers need no treatment, HbSS need penicillin prophylaxis + folic acid + vaccinations + early aggressive pain/infection management; hydroxyurea for definite indications; stem cell transplant only cure; refer red-flag presentations. ABBREV: HbAS=carrier; HbSS=homozygous; HU=Hydroxyurea; SCD=Sickle Cell Disease; VOC=Vaso-occlusive crisis; HPLC=High Performance Liquid Chromatography; POC=Point of care.

whenToSuspect: WHAT IS INFERTILITY: Infertility is a disease of the male or female reproductive system defined by the failure to achieve a pregnancy after 12 months or more of regular unprotected sexual intercourse in women < 35 years of age or within 6 months in women > 35 years of age; affects up to 15% of couples. WHEN TO SUSPECT INFERTILITY: Any couple who by definition has infertility; Those with high risk of infertility - Women >35 years (expedited evaluation), Women >40 years (immediate evaluation), Oligomenorrhoea or amenorrhoea, Known or suspected uterine/tubal/peritoneal disease, History of tubal/ovarian surgery, History of chemotherapy/radiotherapy, Any female with endometriosis, Known or suspected male infertility.
risk: HISTORY OF THE COUPLE: Duration of infertility/contraceptive use, fertility in previous/current relationships, previous fertility investigations/treatment. MENSTRUAL HISTORY: menarche, cycle length & duration of flow, dysmenorrhea, amenorrhea episodes, HMB/IMB. OBSTETRIC HISTORY: number of previous pregnancies (MTP/miscarriage/ectopic), time to initiate previous pregnancies. PAST HISTORY: chronic illness like chronic hypertension, diabetes mellitus, arthritis, tuberculosis. DRUG HISTORY: agents causing increased Prolactin/Chemotherapy/Radiotherapy/anabolic steroids/anti-androgens. SURGICAL HISTORY: previous abdominal/pelvic/gynecological surgeries. SEXUAL HISTORY: coital frequency/timing/knowledge of fertile period, dyspareunia, postcoital bleeding. PERSONAL HISTORY: smoking, alcohol, substance abuse, vigorous exercise/eating disorder. CAUTION: Always keep in mind age of the female as it is the most important prognostic factor.
scores: PHYSICAL EXAMINATION OF FEMALE - General: Height, Weight, BMI, Waist circumference, Blood pressure, Fat & Hair distribution, Acne, Acanthosis nigricans, Thyroid examination, Breast examination. Abdominal: abdominal mass & tenderness, type and site of scars. Pelvis: state of hymen/clitoris/labia, vaginal infection/septum/endometriotic deposits, cervical polyps, accessibility of cervix for insemination, uterine size/position/mobility/tenderness, adnexal fornices tenderness, cervical smear. For male physical examination refer to STW for management of male infertility (ICD-10-N46.9), STW Volume 1, 2019.
investigations: ESSENTIAL INVESTIGATIONS - Baseline ultrasound (Transvaginal ultrasound) to assess: Uterus (endometrial thickness/pattern, space occupying lesions like fibroid, adenomyosis, polyp or mullerian anomalies); Adnexa (hydrosalpinx or para-ovarian cyst); Ovary (antral follicle count Day 2-5 of menstrual cycle, volume, position, characterization of cyst); POD (presence of free fluid). Test for tubal patency: Hysterosalpingography (HSG). OPTIONAL INVESTIGATIONS: Endometrial aspiration for AFB/PCR (rule out tuberculosis), Serum AMH, Viral markers (HIV, HBsAg, HCV), VDRL, Rubella IgM/IgG, Day 2/3 FSH LH, Serum TSH Prolactin.
drugs: MANAGEMENT DEPENDING UPON THE FACTOR OF INFERTILITY (Level 1 ART coded blue, Level 2 ART coded yellow). TUBAL STATUS: Bilateral tubes blocked - confirmatory test Laparoscopy with chromo-pertubation (if confirmed bilateral blocked - IVF/ICSI); Tubes patent - OS +/- IUI x 3-6 cycles (if failed IVF/ICSI); Hydrosalpinx present - prior counselling/consent/discussion regarding future fertility treatment plan and cost, Salpingectomy/Clipping then OS +/- IUI (if other tube patent) or IVF (if other tube not patent/bilateral salpingectomy done). OVULATORY STATUS: Documented ovulation (Day 2-5 AFC normal >=5, Serum AMH >=1.2 ng/ml) - OS +/- IUI x 3-6 cycles (if failed IVF/ICSI); Anovulation - Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (Gonadotropins with/without IUI/IVF), Normo-gonadotropic normogonadism (OVI + IUI x 3-6 cycles, if failed IVF/ICSI), Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism or poor ovarian reserve (AFC<5, AMH<1.2) - IVF/ICSI individualized with self or donor oocyte. UTERINE FACTOR: Cavity pathology - Hysteroscopic management (Polypectomy, Septum resection, Adhesiolysis); Myometrium pathology - Laparoscopic/open surgery (Myomectomy if distorting the cavity, Adeno-myomectomy if required); then OVI/OS +/- IUI or IVF/ICSI individualized treatment; Surgical correction failed - IVF followed by surrogacy.
management: Evaluate both partners; female evaluation by history, examination, baseline TVS and tubal patency (HSG). Manage by factor: tubal (OS/IUI if patent, IVF/ICSI if blocked or failed, salpingectomy for hydrosalpinx), ovulatory (OS/IUI for documented ovulation, gonadotropins/OVI for anovulation by gonadotropin status, IVF/ICSI for poor ovarian reserve), uterine (hysteroscopic/laparoscopic correction then OVI/OS/IUI/IVF; surrogacy if surgical correction failed). Level 1 ART (OS, IUI, OVI) vs Level 2 ART (IVF, ICSI, donor oocyte, surrogacy). Follow evidence based individualized treatment.
referralRedFlags: Women >35 years need expedited evaluation; women >40 years need immediate evaluation. Age of the female is the most important prognostic factor. Refer for ART (IVF/ICSI) when first-line OS/IUI fails or for tubal block/poor ovarian reserve/failed surgical correction. Rule out genital tuberculosis (endometrial aspiration AFB/PCR). Follow evidence based individualized treatment.
algorithmNotes: Define infertility (12 months <35y, 6 months >35y); evaluate couple; female workup with TVS + HSG; manage by tubal/ovulatory/uterine factor escalating from OS/IUI to IVF/ICSI; age is key prognostic factor. ABBREV: AFB=Acid fast bacilli; AFC=Antral follicle count; AMH=Anti-mullerian hormone; ART=Assisted reproductive technology; FSH=Follicular stimulating hormone; HMB=Heavy menstrual bleeding; ICSI=Intracytoplasmic sperm injection; IMB=Inter menstrual bleeding; IUI=Intrauterine insemination; IVF=In vitro fertilization; LH=Luteinizing hormone; OS=Ovarian stimulation; OVI=Ovulation induction; POD=Pouch of Douglas; TSH=Thyroid stimulating hormone; VDRL=Venereal disease research laboratory test.

whenToSuspect: INDICATIONS FOR TRANSPORT IN NEONATES - Referral to higher centre: Any newborn who is assessed by the Health Care Provider as sick and needs referral. NBCC/NBSU TO SNCU: Birth weight <1800 grams and/or gestational age <34 weeks; Neonates with apnea or gasping, respiratory distress with retractions or grunt or not maintaining SpO2 with oxygen, persistent hypothermia or hyperthermia, severe jaundice requiring intensive phototherapy, vomiting or abdominal distention, central cyanosis, need of positive pressure ventilation >60 seconds at birth, non-passage of stool or urine for more than 24 hours after birth, shock (cold periphery with CFT > 3 seconds and weak/fast pulse), refusal to feed/less movement/abnormal movements, significant bleeding. SNCU TO NICU: Birth weight <1000 grams and/or gestational age < 28 weeks; Neonates with respiratory distress requiring mechanical ventilation, unresponsive shock, jaundice requiring exchange transfusion (if facility not available), refractory seizures, need for surgical intervention, birth asphyxia qualifying for therapeutic hypothermia, multiorgan failure, refractory hypoglycemia, acute kidney injury needing dialysis.
risk: Levels of care escalation: NBCC/NBSU to SNCU vs SNCU to NICU based on birth weight, gestational age and clinical instability. Sick neonates requiring higher level care/services not available locally need transport.
scores: Decision to transport based on whether required services/bed are available at referral facility and the level of care needed (SNCU vs NICU). If shock present - start treatment before transport.
investigations: Enclose: (1) Complete summary, (2) All investigations, (3) Mother's blood sample. Check temperature and blood glucose prior to transport. MONITORING DURING TRANSPORT - Parameters to be monitored: Temperature, Heart rate, Respiratory rate, Air entry, SpO2, GI Aspirates, Position of tubes (ET, OG, Catheter, ICD, IV cannula), Ventilator/CPAP settings; Frequency of monitoring every 30 minutes depending on sickness of the baby.
drugs: PRE-TRANSPORT STABILIZATION: If shock present start treatment before transport; All doses of antibiotics and drugs should be timed prior to transport; Ensure clear airway, appropriate respiratory support and secure IV access. MANAGEMENT DURING TRANSPORT: Maintain temperature and warmth (incubator/clothing/Kangaroo Mother Care); Position, clear the secretion and assess for need of intubation; Assist with appropriate respiratory support (Oxygen, CPAP, Neonatal ventilation) - stop the vehicle if needed for urgent care e.g. intubation; Manage shock by titrating the fluids and inotropes; Appropriate quantity, frequency and modality of feeding during transport (preferably breastfeeding or expressed breastmilk).
management: PREPAREDNESS AND PRE-TRANSPORT STABILIZATION: Identify and communicate with referral facility; check availability of services and bed (e.g. Ventilator); explain condition, need for transport, expected plan and prognosis to family; discuss possible expenses; take informed consent; share contact numbers of referring and receiving facility; enclose complete summary/investigations/mother's blood sample; identify transport team with skilled persons; ensure logistics and vehicle organised; time all drug doses; check temperature and blood glucose; ensure airway, respiratory support, IV access. TRANSFER (HANDING OVER): transport team assists transfer to SNCU/NICU; baby stabilized by both teams; receiving doctor has one-to-one discussion with handing over team; all documents handed over; family introduced to new team in person.
referralRedFlags: AVOID INVASIVE PROCEDURES DURING TRANSPORT. Any newborn assessed as sick needs referral. Start shock treatment before transport. Stop the vehicle if urgent care needed (e.g. intubation). Communicate any change in condition to parents and receiving doctor.
algorithmNotes: Identify sick neonate needing referral; choose level (SNCU/NICU) by weight/gestation/instability; stabilize before transport (airway, breathing, circulation, temperature, glucose, drugs timed); monitor every 30 min during transport; structured handover with documents and mother's sample. ABBREV: CFT=Capillary filling time; ET=Endo tracheal; ICD=Intercostal drain; NBCC=Newborn care corner; NBSU=Newborn stabilization unit; NICU=Neonatal Intensive care unit; OG=Orogastric; SNCU=Special Newborn care unit; SpO2=Pulse Oxygen saturation.

condition: Neonatal Emergency Triage Assessment and Management
whenToSuspect: SICK OR AT-RISK NEONATE PRESENTING TO HEALTH FACILITY: Place under radiant warmer; Attach temperature probe and pulse oximeter; Assess for emergency signs using TABCD - Temperature, Airway and Breathing, Circulation (CFT, pulse, BP), Coma/Convulsions, Dehydration. EMERGENCY SIGNS: Apnea or gasping; Severe respiratory distress (severe retractions, grunt, RR>70); Central cyanosis/oxygen saturation <91%; Shock (cold peripheries, mottled/grey skin, CFT>3 sec, weak & fast pulse, low BP); HR > 200/min; Coma or convulsions; Severe dehydration (in cases of diarrhoea); Severe Hypothermia (<32C). PRIORITY SIGNS: Weight <1800 g or >3.8 kg; Respiratory distress (RR>60, no retractions); Severe jaundice (onset <24h/palm or sole staining/duration >2 weeks); Severe pallor; Bleeding; Major malformation (tracheo-esophageal fistula, meningomyelocele, gastroschisis, anorectal malformation); Abdominal distension; Irritable/Restless; Refusal to feed; Moderate (32-35.9C) or mild (36-36.4C) hypothermia. NON-URGENT SIGNS: Jaundice, Transitional stools, Minor birth trauma, Minor malformations, Superficial infections, Breastfeeding difficulty, Regurgitation.
risk: Triage into Emergency signs (initiate emergency treatment and stabilize), Priority signs (assess and act rapidly), and Non-urgent signs (assess and counsel). A neonate may have more than one condition - follow specific STWs. Elicit perinatal risk factors for sepsis and evaluate if sepsis workup is needed.
scores: TABCD assessment (Temperature, Airway/Breathing, Circulation, Coma/Convulsions, Dehydration). Identification and prompt treatment of emergency and priority signs is the key to prevent mortality. Hypothermia grading: Mild 36-36.4C, Moderate 32-35.9C, Severe <32C. Hypoglycemia: BG <45 mg/dL asymptomatic vs <20 mg/dL or symptomatic.
investigations: Check SpO2; Check blood glucose (glucometer); draw CBC and blood culture. Investigations as per clinical findings. Urgent ECG (look for p waves) if HR>200/min. Evaluate sepsis workup if perinatal risk factors present (refer to STW on Sepsis in neonates).
drugs: EMERGENCY (initiate emergency treatment and stabilize): Resuscitation as per NRP; Maintain TABC; Check SpO2 and start oxygen if <91%; Start CPAP if respiratory distress; Start IV fluids as per weight and postnatal age; Check blood glucose, draw CBC and blood culture, give first dose of antibiotics. SPECIFIC MANAGEMENT - Shock: provide warmth, IV NS 10mL/kg bolus over 30-60 min, may repeat bolus if volume deficit, consider inotropes. HR>200/min: urgent ECG, if SVT consider ice-pack and IV adenosine, correct hyperthermia. Severe dehydration: provide warmth, IV 30 mL/kg of RL or NS in 1 hour followed by 70 mL/kg in next 5 hours (WHO plan C), if IV not possible give ORS at 20 mL/kg/h for 6 hours. Hypothermia: Mild - warm environment/skin-to-skin/breastfeeding; Moderate - servo-controlled warmer, skin-to-skin till arranged; Severe - as moderate plus IV fluids and inj. vitamin K. Hypoglycemia: BG <45 mg/dL asymptomatic - supervised breastfeeding or EBM; <20 mg/dL or symptomatic - 2mL/kg 10% dextrose IV followed by infusion @ 6mg/kg/min. Jaundice: serious jaundice - intensive phototherapy, consider IV fluids if dehydration, prepare for exchange blood transfusion. Seizures: maintain TABC, check blood glucose (if <45 give 2mL/kg 10% dextrose IV then infusion @ 6mg/kg/min), if not controlled 2mL/kg 10% calcium gluconate IV diluted 1:1 over 10 min under cardiac monitoring, if not controlled Inj. Phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV over 15 mins (consider another bolus 10mg/kg over 10 min). Surgical: cover skin defects with warm saline sterile gauze, maintain hydration, consult surgeon. Breastfeeding difficulty: observe positioning and attachment, counsel mother.
management: Place under radiant warmer, attach probes, assess TABCD. Emergency signs - initiate NRP resuscitation, oxygen/CPAP, IV fluids, blood glucose, antibiotics. Priority signs - maintain TABC, check SpO2/glucose, start IV fluids or gavage feeds, evaluate sepsis, investigations per findings. Non-urgent signs - assess and counsel, explain danger signs, counsel breastfeeding. Follow specific STWs; a neonate may have more than one condition. Specific management workflows for shock, tachycardia, dehydration, hypothermia, hypoglycemia, jaundice, seizures, surgical, breastfeeding difficulty.
referralRedFlags: IDENTIFICATION AND PROMPT TREATMENT OF EMERGENCY AND PRIORITY SIGNS IS THE KEY TO PREVENT MORTALITY. Emergency signs (apnea/gasping, severe respiratory distress, central cyanosis, shock, HR>200, coma/convulsions, severe dehydration, severe hypothermia) require immediate stabilization. Refer per specific STWs (Sepsis, Neonatal Transport, Feeds & Fluids).
algorithmNotes: Triage every sick neonate with TABCD into emergency/priority/non-urgent; stabilize emergency signs immediately (NRP, oxygen, fluids, glucose, antibiotics); act rapidly on priority signs; counsel non-urgent; apply condition-specific management. ABBREV: CFT=Capillary filling time; CPAP=Continuous positive airway pressure; ECG=Electrocardiogram; EBM=Expressed breastmilk; NRP=Neonatal resuscitation protocol; NS=Normal saline; RL=Ringer lactate; SpO2=Pulse oxygen saturation; SVT=Supraventricular tachycardia; STW=Standard treatment workflow; TABC=Temperature airway breathing circulation.

whenToSuspect: Sudden alteration in motor, behavior or autonomic activity, with or without alteration of consciousness. NEONATES AT RISK FOR SEIZURES: Birth asphyxia, Sepsis, Meningitis, Preterm, Small for gestational age, Metabolic or electrolyte abnormalities, Major bleeding. IDENTIFICATION OF SEIZURES - Motor manifestations: Rhythmic jerks of limb(s) or facial part(s); Tonic contraction of limb(s); Stereotypical movements - Limbs (Pedalling, rowing, swimming, cycling, stepping), Oral (Pouting of lips, mouthing, repeated sucking), Eyes (Vacant stare, transient eye deviation, nystagmoid movements, repeated blinking). Behavioural manifestations: Sudden change in consciousness or cry characteristic. Autonomic manifestations: Fluctuations in heart rate, sudden change in BP, sudden appearance of unexplained apneic episodes.
risk: HISTORY - Antenatal: First trimester viral illness, PIH, diabetes, PROM/chorioamnionitis, STDs, drugs or substance abuse, decreased fetal movements. Intrapartum: Fetal distress, difficult delivery, cord complications, mode of delivery, instrumentation. Postnatal: Resuscitation, other organ system involvement, feeding history, seizure details (onset, duration, description - review videos). Family: Consanguinity, early neonatal deaths, mental retardation, epilepsy.
scores: Goal of treatment is total or near total elimination of seizures. With higher doses and addition of more anti-epileptic drugs, the neonate requires intensive respiratory and cardiac monitoring. If recurrent seizures, isolated twitches may be just monitored. Blood glucose threshold BG < 45 mg/dL; ionized Calcium <1.0 mmol/L.
investigations: EXAMINATION - Vital signs: Temp, BP, HR, RR, CFT, SpO2; General: pallor, icterus, rash, skin lesions; Head to toe: Head circumference, bulging fontanelle, needle marks on scalp, dysmorphism, malformations, petechie, ecchymoses; Systemic exam: level of alertness, cranial nerve and motor exam, all systems, Fundus examination. INVESTIGATIONS in all neonates: Blood glucose, Serum electrolytes, hemogram, ionized calcium, blood urea/creatinine, liver function tests, blood gas analysis, cranial ultrasound. Specific circumstances: Suspected sepsis - cerebrospinal fluid examination; Suspected TORCH infections - paired mother and baby serology (toxoplasma, CMV, rubella), body fluids for PCR (urine for CMV), CSF for toxoplasma/CMV/herpes; Suspected intracranial bleed - Ultrasound or CT or MRI head, Platelet count and Coagulogram; Electroencephalography.
drugs: ACUTE MANAGEMENT: Ensure TABC, IV access, check blood glucose by Glucometer. If BG < 45 mg/dL: 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg bolus, followed by infusion @ 6 mg/kg/min (follow STW on Neonatal Hypoglycemia). If BG normal: 2 mL/kg 10% calcium gluconate IV, diluted 1:1 with D5, D10 or DW over 10 min under cardiac monitoring (if available measure ionized Calcium; if iCa <1.0 mmol/L and seizures persist repeat Calcium dose). If seizures persist: Inj. Phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg IV over 15 mins (if persist after 15 min consider another bolus of 10mg/kg phenobarbitone over 10 min, assess seizure control after 15 min). If persist: Phenytoin or Fosphenytoin 20 mg/kg PE infuse over 20 min under cardiac monitoring (assess after 30 min). If persist: Give Lorazepam 0.05 mg/kg IV over 2-5 mins (can repeat dose once). If persist: Midazolam 0.1 mg/kg IV bolus followed by continuous infusion (1 microg/kg/min increasing by 0.5 to 1 microg/kg/min every 2 min until favorable response or max 18 microg/kg/min); Levetiracetam 40 mg/kg IV bolus followed by 40-60 mg/kg/day IV or oral in 2-3 divided doses.
management: DURATION OF ANTICONVULSANTS: Maintenance therapy not needed for single brief seizure that needs only one loading dose of phenobarbitone; if more than one loading dose or more than one drug needed - start maintenance dose 24h after loading dose (prefer oral route if no contraindication); after a seizure-free period of 72h stop all other anticonvulsants one by one except phenobarbitone; after one week or at discharge (whichever earlier) stop phenobarbitone if neurological examination and EEG normal (if abnormal discharge on maintenance therapy); review at monthly intervals and taper anticonvulsants if neurological examination and EEG become normal; if anticonvulsants required beyond 3 months consult a neurologist and switch to other drugs.
referralRedFlags: Consider referral (follow STW on Neonatal Transport) if seizures persist despite escalating anticonvulsants. NEONATES WITH SEIZURES REQUIRE LONG TERM NEURODEVELOPMENTAL FOLLOW-UP AND HEARING ASSESSMENT. Higher doses/multiple anti-epileptics require intensive respiratory and cardiac monitoring.
algorithmNotes: Recognize subtle/motor/autonomic neonatal seizures; correct glucose and calcium first; escalate phenobarbitone -> phenytoin/fosphenytoin -> lorazepam -> midazolam/levetiracetam with cardiac monitoring; investigate cause; taper anticonvulsants by EEG/exam; long-term neurodevelopmental follow-up. ABBREV: BG=Blood glucose; CFT=Capillary filling time; CSF=Cerebrospinal fluid; DW=Distilled water for injection; EEG=Electroencephalography; iCa=Ionised calcium; PIH=Pregnancy induced hypertension; SGA=Small for gestational age; STD=Sexually transmitted diseases; TABC=Temperature airway breathing circulation.

whenToSuspect: Presence of any one: Tachypnea (RR >60 bpm), OR lower chest retractions, nasal flaring, grunting OR cyanosis. ACTIONS: Rapid assessment of TABC (temperature, airway, breathing, circulation) and stabilize the baby; Admit the baby in SNCU/NICU; Nurse in a radiant warmer/incubator and monitor with continuous pulse oximetry; Quantify the severity of RD using Silverman Anderson Score (SAS); Closely monitor RR, SAS, SpO2, and CFT; Most neonates with RD can be fed enterally (by breastfeeding if RR<70 bpm and not on respiratory support, or orogastric tube); those with severe distress or contraindication to enteral feeding should be given IV fluids.
risk: SILVERMAN ANDERSON SCORE (SAS) graded 0-2 for: Upper chest, Lower chest, Xiphoid retractions, Nares dilatation, Expiratory grunt. Grade 0 (synchronized, no retractions, none, none, none); Grade 1 (lag on inspiration, just visible, just visible, minimal, heard with stethoscope); Grade 2 (see-saw, marked, marked, marked, audible). GOALS: alleviate work of breathing by appropriate respiratory support; maintain oxygen saturations from 91% to 95%; identify and treat the underlying cause.
scores: Severity by SAS. SpO2 < 91%: oxygen by nasal prongs 0.5-1.0 Lpm (max 2 Lpm). Gestation >=32 weeks: CPAP if SAS 4 or more, OR no improvement with NP oxygen. Gestation <32 weeks: CPAP if SpO2 <91% OR SAS 1-3. Severe RD (SAS of 5 or more, FiO2 more than 60-70%), unresponsive to CPAP, having shock or repeated episodes of apnea may require mechanical ventilation and referral (see STW on Transport).
investigations: Monitor RR, SAS, SpO2, CFT continuously. DO NOT perform unnecessary investigations (CBC, CRP, routine ABG). DO NOT do routine chest X-ray in all neonates with RD - perform chest X-ray if RD is persisting beyond 6 hours of age, there is worsening, or a diagnostic dilemma.
drugs: RESPIRATORY SUPPORT: Respiratory distress or low SpO2 (<91%) - Start oxygen by nasal cannula @ 0.5-1 LPM. If SpO2 <91%: increase flow up to 2 LPM, consider using oxygen hood if no retractions/grunt; if no improvement consider CPAP. If SpO2 91-95%: monitor clinically and SpO2; if clinical deterioration or SpO2 <91% escalate; if SpO2 >95% wean oxygen flow. Consider early CPAP if gestation <32 weeks AND SpO2 <91% OR SAS 3 or more. ASSESS AND TREAT THE UNDERLYING CAUSE: Respiratory Distress Syndrome (RDS) - consider surfactant replacement therapy as per indication; Pneumonia-Sepsis - treat with antibiotics as per unit's protocol (refer to sepsis STW).
management: Stabilize (TABC), admit to SNCU/NICU, nurse in warmer with pulse oximetry, grade with SAS. Provide oxygen titrated to SpO2 91-95% (nasal prongs then hood then CPAP). Early CPAP for preterm <32 weeks. Mechanical ventilation and referral for severe/unresponsive RD. Feed enterally if RR<70 and not on respiratory support; IV fluids if severe distress. Treat underlying cause (surfactant for RDS, antibiotics for pneumonia/sepsis). WHAT NOT TO DO: do not let SpO2 exceed 95% (retinopathy of prematurity risk); do not give unnecessary IV fluids/antibiotics/blood products/drugs; do not perform unnecessary investigations; do not do routine chest X-ray.
referralRedFlags: PREVENT HYPOXIA AND HYPEROXIA. Severe RD (SAS 5+, FiO2 >60-70%), unresponsive to CPAP, shock or repeated apnea may require mechanical ventilation and referral (see STW on Transport). DO NOT let SpO2 exceed 95% (high oxygen saturation is a risk factor for retinopathy of prematurity).
algorithmNotes: Diagnose neonatal RD by tachypnea/retractions/flaring/grunting/cyanosis; grade with Silverman Anderson Score; provide stepwise oxygen (prongs->hood->CPAP) targeting SpO2 91-95%; early CPAP for preterm; ventilate/refer severe cases; treat cause (surfactant/antibiotics); avoid hyperoxia and over-investigation. ABBREV: BW=Birth weight; CPAP=Continuous positive airway pressure; CFT=Capillary filling time; GA=Gestational age; IV=Intravenous; RD=Respiratory distress; RR=Respiratory rate; SAS=Silverman Anderson score.

whenToSuspect: Temperature measurement for neonates is mandatory in the given settings to diagnose hypothermia: Delivery room (in the first hour after delivery); Prior to and during transport; At the time of admission; Continuous monitoring for all babies nursed in radiant warmer/incubator; At-risk neonates staying with mother e.g. LBW, preterms - every 4 hourly. TEMPERATURE RANGES: >37.5 C Hyperthermia; 36.5 to 37.5 C Normal; 36.0-36.4 C Mild hypothermia; 32-35.9 C Moderate Hypothermia; <32.0 C Severe Hypothermia. STANDARD TECHNIQUE FOR MEASUREMENT: Use a standard digital thermometer; Place the tip in the neonate's axilla keeping it parallel to the trunk; Read once the beep sound is heard.
risk: Is it <36.5 C? If YES: check for possible cause of hypothermia; assess for risk factors & clinical features of sepsis (refer to sepsis STW); check room temperature. Classify hypothermia: Mild 36-36.4 C, Moderate 32-35.9 C, Severe <32 C. Differentiate hyperthermia from sepsis: if both trunk & extremities are hot, environmental cause likely; if trunk is hot & extremities cold, consider sepsis.
scores: Hypothermia grading guides treatment intensity: Mild (36-36.4 C), Moderate (32-35.9 C), Severe (<32 C). HYPOTHERMIA IN NEWBORNS INCREASES MORTALITY. Hyperthermia >37.5 C needs cooling and evaluation for sepsis.
investigations: Temperature measurement with standard digital thermometer in axilla. Check blood sugar in moderate hypothermia (if <45 mg/dL follow STW on Hypoglycemia). Assess for risk factors and clinical features of sepsis if hypothermic (refer to sepsis STW).
drugs: MILD HYPOTHERMIA (36-36.4 C): Ensure room temperature 25-28 C; Provide skin-to-skin (STS) contact; Continue breastfeeding; If sick nurse under radiant warmer; RECHECK TEMPERATURE IN 1 HOUR (if normal wrap properly; if still <36.5 C treat as moderate hypothermia). MODERATE HYPOTHERMIA (32-35.9 C): Nurse under radiant warmer in servo mode with temperature probe attached; Continue skin-to-skin contact till warmer available ensuring mother-neonate dyad covered with pre-warmed linen; Start O2 if SpO2 <91%; Check blood sugar (if <45 mg/dL follow STW on Hypoglycemia); Recheck temperature every 15 minutes till it normalizes; Continue feeding if stable and abdominal examination normal. SEVERE HYPOTHERMIA (<32 C): Manage as per moderate hypothermia; Make nil per oral; Start IV fluids (refer to STW on Feeds and fluids); Give Inj. Vitamin K; Refer to higher centre if develops shock or respiratory failure (refer to STW on Neonatal Transport).
management: PREVENTION OF HYPOTHERMIA - MAINTENANCE OF WARM CHAIN. Delivery Room: radiant warmer must in Neonatal Care Corner, area air draught free, room thermometer, maintain DR temperature >25 C, switch on radiant warmer 20-30 minutes before delivery (manual mode, heater output 100%), pre-warm sterile towels, early skin-to-skin contact for stable neonates for 1 hour or till first breastfeeding, dry newborn immediately, remove wet linen, weigh/check temperature after breastfeeding. Postnatal Wards: cover neonate adequately, rooming-in 24x7, avoid air draughts, start Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC) early for eligible neonate, promote exclusive breastfeeding, delay bath till after discharge, remove wet clothes early, educate mother on identification of hypothermia using touch method. Warm chain during transport: without external heat source (fully wrapped neonate with cap in adult's arms in closed vehicle, skin-to-skin, upright position covered snugly); with external heat source (thermal mattress or transport incubator, indigenous insulated boxes in resource-limited settings; no neonate placed naked in trolley/bed without external heat source).
referralRedFlags: HYPOTHERMIA IN NEWBORNS INCREASES MORTALITY. PREVENT HYPOTHERMIA - MAINTAIN WARM CHAIN. Severe hypothermia (<32 C): refer to higher centre if develops shock or respiratory failure (refer to STW on Neonatal Transport). Assess for sepsis in any hypothermic neonate. Hyperthermia: if persists after cooling, needs further evaluation.
algorithmNotes: Measure axillary temperature in all mandated settings; classify hypothermia (mild/moderate/severe); rewarm appropriately (skin-to-skin/radiant warmer servo mode/IV fluids + vitamin K for severe) and recheck; prevent by maintaining warm chain from delivery room through transport; differentiate hyperthermia from sepsis. ABBREV: DR=Delivery room; KMC=Kangaroo Mother Care; LBW=Low birth weight; STS=Skin-to-skin.

whenToSuspect: WHOM TO SCREEN FOR HYPOGLYCEMIA: Preterm infants (<37 weeks gestational age); Low birth weight infants (<2500 g); Small for gestation age (SGA) - birth weight <10th percentile; Large for gestation age (LGA) - birth weight >90th percentile; Infant of diabetic mother (IDM); Sick infants (eg sepsis, asphyxia, respiratory distress, shock, polycythemia, seizure); Post exchange blood transfusion; Infants on intravenous fluids and parenteral nutrition. Do not monitor blood glucose routinely in term healthy AGA infants. SYMPTOMS AND SIGNS (when BG <45 mg/dL): Stupor, lethargy, limpness; Jitteriness, tremors, convulsions; Episodes of cyanosis, apnea or tachypnea; Weak and high-pitched cry; Difficulty in feeding.
risk: SCHEDULE OF BLOOD GLUCOSE (BG) MONITORING (PREFEED): At-risk infants - at 2, 6, 12, 24, 48, 72 hours of life; Infants on IV fluids/parenteral nutrition - every 6-8 hours. Always search for an underlying cause - polycythemia, sepsis, meningitis, hypothermia, IUGR. Classify: asymptomatic vs symptomatic or BG < 20 mg/dL.
scores: BLOOD GLUCOSE <45 mg/dL is the threshold. Asymptomatic vs Symptomatic or BG < 20 mg/dL determines management pathway. Refractory hypoglycemia: high (>12 mg/kg/min for 24 hours) or persistent (>7 days) GIR requirement. SYMPTOMATIC AS WELL AS ASYMPTOMATIC HYPOGLYCEMIA CAN LEAD TO PERMANENT BRAIN DAMAGE.
investigations: HOW TO MONITOR BLOOD GLUCOSE: Use Glucose reagent strips along with a glucometer; Low value (<45 mg/dL) - send a blood sample to the lab for confirmation; do not delay treatment. PRACTICAL POINTS: Send blood in fluoride or oxalate vial for laboratory glucose estimation. Always search for an underlying cause (polycythemia, sepsis, meningitis, hypothermia, IUGR). Do not give antibiotics unless sepsis is suspected (refer to STW on sepsis).
drugs: ASYMPTOMATIC: Immediate supervised feeding; Breastfeeding or a measured volume of expressed breast milk (formula milk if EBM not available) by paladai or gavage; RE-CHECK BG AFTER 1 HOUR - if BG >=45 mg/dL continue feeds and BG monitoring every 6 hourly for 24 hours; Start IV glucose infusion if BG <45 despite one attempt of feeding OR baby becomes symptomatic. SYMPTOMATIC OR BG < 20 mg/dL: IV bolus 2 mL/kg 10% dextrose; Start IV infusion of dextrose at a glucose infusion rate (GIR) of 6 mg/kg/min; Re-check BG every 30 mins till 2 values >=45 mg/dL & then every 6 hrs. If BG < 45 mg/dL: Increase GIR @ 2 mg/kg/min till max GIR 12 mg/kg/min; Refractory hypoglycemia (High >12 mg/kg/min for 24 hours or persistent >7 days GIR requirement) - consider drugs and refer to higher centre. If BG >=45 mg/dL: Euglycemic for 24 hours on IV fluids, wean @ 2 mg/kg/min every 6 hourly, increase oral feeds, monitor BG every 6 hourly, stop IV fluids when euglycemic on GIR 4 mg/kg/min. DRUGS FOR REFRACTORY HYPOGLYCEMIA: Hydrocortisone 5 mg/kg/day IV in two divided doses; Diazoxide 10-25 mg/kg/day PO in three divided doses; Glucagon 300 microg/kg SC or IM; Octreotide 2-10 microg/kg/day SC. PRACTICAL POINTS: Avoid >12.5-15% dextrose infusion through a peripheral vein; use a syringe/infusion pump to deliver glucose; avoid frequent dextrose boluses.
management: Screen at-risk infants per schedule (not routine in term healthy AGA). At BG <45 mg/dL check for symptoms. Asymptomatic - supervised feeding, recheck in 1 hour, IV glucose if not improving/symptomatic. Symptomatic or BG <20 - IV bolus 10% dextrose then infusion at GIR 6 mg/kg/min, titrate GIR up to 12, recheck BG every 30 min till stable. Wean when euglycemic 24 hours. Refractory hypoglycemia - drugs (hydrocortisone/diazoxide/glucagon/octreotide) and refer. PREVENTION: support mother for early initiation and regular breastfeeding, maintain normothermia, do not feed dextrose as substitute for breast milk.
referralRedFlags: SYMPTOMATIC AS WELL AS ASYMPTOMATIC HYPOGLYCEMIA CAN LEAD TO PERMANENT BRAIN DAMAGE. Refractory hypoglycemia (high >12 mg/kg/min for 24 hours or persistent >7 days GIR requirement) - consider drugs and refer to higher centre. Always search for an underlying cause (polycythemia, sepsis, meningitis, hypothermia, IUGR). Do not delay treatment for lab confirmation.
algorithmNotes: Screen at-risk neonates (not routine term AGA); treat BG <45 mg/dL - asymptomatic with feeding then recheck, symptomatic or <20 with IV dextrose bolus + GIR 6 escalating to 12; refractory needs drugs and referral; wean gradually; always seek underlying cause. ABBREV: AGA=Appropriate for Gestational Age; EBM=Expressed breast milk; GIR=Glucose infusion rate; IM=Intramuscular; IUGR=Intra uterine growth retardation; IV=Intravenous; LGA=Large for gestation age; PO=Per oral; SC=Subcutaneous; SGA=Small for gestation age.

whenToSuspect: PART A - Nutritional plan for infants NOT on enteral feeds at admission. PART B - Nutritional plan for infants on partial or full enteral feeds at admission. Preferred mode of feeding: <32 weeks Oro-Gastric tube; 32-34 weeks Spoon/Paladai; >=35 weeks Breast feeds. Choice of milk in order of preference: Expressed breast milk (EBM) >> pasteurized donor human milk >> formula milk.
risk: PART A - if any of the following present, keep NPO and reassess: Gestation <35 wks & REDF in antenatal USG (NPO for 24h, reassess, if abdomen soft no distension start feeds @ 10-20 ml/kg/d, if tolerates increase @ half of weight/gestation specific increments); Gestation <35 wks and birth weight <3rd centile or AEDF, need for extensive resuscitation, HIE, 5 minute Apgar <7, has RED signs (keep NPO for 6h, reassess, start feeds as per gestation/weight protocol); Suspected GI malformation or suspected NEC (keep NPO, take pediatric surgery consultation). PART B RED SIGNS: Significant respiratory distress (Silverman/Downe score>6), Ongoing hypoxia or moderate to severe apneas, Ongoing seizures, Severe hypothermia, Shock needing inotropes, Feed intolerance (abdominal distension with vomiting, bilious or bloody gastric aspirates).
scores: PART A feed initiation by gestation/weight: GA <26 weeks/BW <750g - NPO 24h, trophic feeds @ 10 ml/kg/d for 48h, increase @ 10-15 ml/kg/d; GA 26-27 weeks/BW 750-999g - NPO 24h, start feeds @ 20 ml/kg/d, increase @ 15-20 ml/kg/d; GA 28-30 weeks/BW 1000-1250g - start feeds @ 30 ml/kg/d, increase @ 30 ml/kg/d; GA >30 weeks/BW >1250g - all fluid as feeds from D1. Frequency: q 2h if PMA <32 weeks/weight <1500g and q 3h if >=32 weeks/weight >=1500g.
investigations: Monitor blood glucose. Monitor growth by regular measurement of weight and head circumference; plot growth parameters on intergrowth 21st postnatal charts for preterm neonates. Daily increments based on daily weight change, urine output, serum sodium and co-morbidities such as PDA or sepsis.
drugs: IV FLUIDS (TABLE 1 - maintenance volume mL/kg/d): BW <1000g or Gestation <28 weeks - Day 1 80-100, advance strictly as per clinical and lab hydration status; BW 1000-1250g/28-30 weeks - Day1 80, Day2 100, Day3 120, Day4 140, Day5 150, Day6-7 150-160; BW >1250g/>30 weeks - Day1 60, Day2 80, Day3 100, Day4 120, Day5 140, Day6 150, Day7 150-160. Type of IV fluids: Day 1 start with D10 titrate dextrose concentration as per blood glucose, then N/5 in D10 with KCl. Remaining fluid requirement after accounting for feed volume given as IV fluids, and if feasible as PN in neonates born at less than 28 weeks or 1000g. IV fluids can be stopped once infant tolerating feeds @ 120 mL/kg/d if blood glucose maintained. SUPPLEMENTS (TABLE 2 - start when infant on 100ml/kg/day of enteral feeds, start Iron at 2 weeks of age): Weight <1800 gram or Gestation <35 weeks - if on EBM or donor milk HMF + Iron + Vitamin D3; if on breastfeeds Iron + Calcium + Phosphorus + Multivitamins + Vitamin D3; if on preterm formula Iron and Vitamin D3. Weight >=1800 gram and Gestation >=35 weeks - Vitamin D3 and Iron (only for gestation <37 weeks). Doses: Iron 2mg/kg/day; Vit-D3 400 IU to 800 IU/day; Calcium 120mg/kg/day; Phosphorus 60mg/kg/day. Duration: Iron and Vit-D3 till 1 year; Calcium and Phosphorus till term PMA; Multivitamins till 6 months.
management: PART A (not on feeds): keep NPO and reassess per risk; initiate and advance feeds by gestation/weight; give remaining fluid as IV/PN.
