PART B (on feeds): if RED signs present keep NPO and start IV fluids (consider PN if gestation <28 weeks or weight <1000g, pediatric surgery consultation if GI obstruction/peritonitis), reassess every 12-24h for readiness of feed (soft abdomen, no distension, absence of red signs), restart at 50% of volume tolerated before making NPO; if no red signs continue and advance feeds. Monitor growth; if not gaining weight adequately on exclusive enteral feeds after 2 weeks, increase feed volume gradually up to 200-250 mL/kg/d as per tolerance.
referralRedFlags: EARLY AND AGGRESSIVE ENTERAL FEEDING BY BREASTMILK DECREASES MORTALITY AND MORBIDITY. Conditions needing admission/referral to tertiary care: GA <26 weeks/BW <750g, GA 26-27 weeks. Suspected GI malformation or NEC - take pediatric surgery consultation. RED signs (Part B) require NPO and IV fluids. Suspected GI obstruction or peritonitis - pediatric surgery consultation.
algorithmNotes: Decide feeding plan by whether infant is on feeds (Part A vs B) and presence of red signs; initiate/advance feeds by gestation/weight; supplement remaining fluid with IV/PN; add iron/vitamin D/calcium/phosphorus supplements per Table 2; prefer EBM; monitor growth. ABBREV: AEDF=Absent end diastolic flow; HIE=Hypoxic ischemic encephalopathy; HMF=Human milk fortifiers; NEC=Necrotizing enterocolitis; PDA=Patent ductus arteriosus; PMA=Post menstrual age; PN=Parenteral nutrition; REDF=Reversed end diastolic flow.

whenToSuspect: Approach to neonatal jaundice in infants >=35 weeks. VISUAL ASSESSMENT: Examine the baby in bright natural/white fluorescent light; Make sure the baby is naked and no yellow/off white background; Examine blanched skin; Assess severity of jaundice. SERIOUS JAUNDICE: Visible jaundice in first 24 hrs OR Yellow palms and soles anytime OR Signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy (ABE) like poor suck/feeding, lethargy, hypotonia OR Abnormal posturing such as arching, retrocollis, opisthotonus, convulsion, fever, high pitched cry.
risk: LOOK FOR THESE RISK FACTORS: Gestation < 38 weeks; Previous sibling requiring treatment for jaundice; Blood group incompatibility (ABO/Rh); High prevalence of G6PD deficiency; Exclusively breast fed baby with weight loss >3% per day or >10% cumulative; Total serum bilirubin (TSB)/Transcutaneous bilirubin (TcB) value in the high/high-intermediate risk zone. KRAMER ZONES (approx serum bilirubin): Zone 1 Face and neck 4-6 mg/dL; Zone 2 Chest and upper abdomen 8-10 mg/dL; Zone 3 Lower abdomen and thighs 12-14 mg/dL; Zone 4 Legs and arms/forearms 15-18 mg/dL; Zone 5 Palms and soles >15 to 20 mg/dL.
scores: Clinical examination every 12 hrs during the initial 3 to 5 days of life; use TcB if available. Does the infant have serious jaundice? If YES - start intensive phototherapy. Does the infant require TSB measurement? (Jaundice in first 24 hrs; beyond 24 hrs more than 12-14 mg/dL on visual assessment/TcB or near PT cut-off; unsure about visual assessment). Stop phototherapy when TSB falls below 13-14 mg/dL or 2 mg/dL below cut-off.
investigations: Total serum bilirubin (TSB), Transcutaneous bilirubin (TcB). INVESTIGATION (when phototherapy/EBT indicated): Blood type and DCT (if mother is O or Rh -ve); G6PD status; Peripheral smear and reticulocyte count. Frequency of repeat TSB measurement depends on cause, severity, age and gestation: Hemolytic jaundice 6 to 8 hourly during initial 24 to 48 hrs; Non-hemolytic jaundice 12-24 hourly. Do not rely on visual assessment/TcB while the baby is under phototherapy.
drugs: MANAGEMENT: If serious jaundice - Start Intensive Phototherapy (document serum bilirubin simultaneously; prepare for exchange blood transfusion (EBT) if signs of ABE present); as per TSB determine if baby requires phototherapy/EBT if TSB at/more than cut-off. If not serious - determine if TSB measurement required, continue visual assessment/TcB every 12 to 24 hrs till discharge. ENSURING OPTIMAL PHOTOTHERAPY: Keep the baby naked (only small nappy to cover genitalia and eye covers); Place the baby close to the lights; Phototherapy can be interrupted for feeding & clinical procedures; Encourage frequent breastfeeding; Monitor temperature regularly; Maintain equipment as per manufacturer's instructions. ENSURING OPTIMAL EXCHANGE BLOOD TRANSFUSION (EBT): Immediate EBT recommended if infant shows signs of ABE or if TSB above recommended age and risk specific cut off; Exchange volume = Twice the estimated blood volume of 80-100 mL/kg. DON'TS: Sunlight should not be used for treatment of hyperbilirubinemia; Do not give phenobarbitone for treatment of hyperbilirubinemia; Do not stop breastfeeding.
management: Visual assessment + Kramer zones + risk factors; serious jaundice - intensive phototherapy + consider EBT; determine TSB need and treat to age/risk-specific cut-offs. Stop phototherapy when TSB below cut-off. DO'S: encourage frequent breastfeeding, avoid exposure to naphthalene balls, complete evaluation of newborn for risk factors and underlying causes, do pre-discharge risk assessment. DISCHARGE ADVICE: reinforce breastfeeding at discharge; if discharged before 72 hrs follow up at 48 to 72 hrs after discharge.
referralRedFlags: HYPERBILIRUBINEMIA IS A PREVENTABLE CAUSE OF BRAIN DAMAGE. SERIOUS JAUNDICE (visible in first 24 hrs, yellow palms/soles, signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy, abnormal posturing) requires intensive phototherapy and preparation for exchange blood transfusion. Immediate EBT if signs of ABE or TSB above cut-off. Do not use sunlight or phenobarbitone; do not stop breastfeeding.
algorithmNotes: Assess jaundice visually (Kramer zones) + risk factors; identify serious jaundice (first 24h, palms/soles, ABE signs); start intensive phototherapy and measure TSB; treat to age/risk-specific cut-offs; EBT for ABE or high TSB; avoid sunlight/phenobarbitone; follow up early after discharge. ABBREV: ABE=Acute bilirubin encephalopathy; DCT=Direct coombs test; EBT=Exchange blood transfusion; G6PD=Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase; TcB=Transcutaneous bilirubin; TSB=Total serum bilirubin.

whenToSuspect: IMMEDIATE MANAGEMENT OF AN ASPHYXIATED NEONATE: Need for Positive pressure ventilation (PPV) at birth OR Referred with history of delayed cry at birth. Two pathways: PPV for <60 seconds OR delayed cry and resuscitation details not available; PPV for 60 seconds or more OR needed intubation or medications OR Cord pH <7.1 (if available - at risk of hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy HIE). ASSESS 15 minutes after birth OR at admission: 1. Lethargy/coma or irritability? 2. Absent spontaneous movements? 3. Absent Suck and Moro's reflex? 4. Abnormal movements (limb jerks, cycling, swimming, chewing, sucking movements which are persistent and monotonous)? 5. Respiratory distress? 6. Features of shock?
risk: At risk of HIE if PPV for 60 seconds or more, needed intubation or medications, or cord pH <7.1. ASSESS FOR MODERATE TO SEVERE HIE: Lethargic or comatose with ANY ONE of the following: 1. Seizures; 2. Decreased muscle tone; 3. Absent/weak Suck or Moro's reflex. Classify as No HIE or Mild HIE vs Moderate or severe HIE.
scores: THERAPEUTIC HYPOTHERMIA CRITERIA - fulfills all: >=36 weeks gestational age; >=1800g birth weight; <6 hrs old; Admission temperature 36-37.4 C. AND all of: pH <7 or base excess >=16 on cord or arterial blood gas done within 1 h of life AND Apgar score <5 at 10 minutes or at least 10 min of PPV AND History of acute perinatal event (such as placental abruption, uterine rupture, cord prolapse). If criteria met = Moderate to Severe HIE eligible for cooling.
investigations: Check vitals and blood glucose: Temperature, heart rate, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation (SpO2), capillary refill time; Check blood glucose (if any value abnormal follow appropriate STW for Neonatal Triage). Cord/arterial blood gas (pH, base excess) within 1 h of life. FREQUENT MULTI-SYSTEM MONITORING IS A MUST. Continuous rectal temperature monitoring during therapeutic hypothermia.
drugs: STABILIZATION AND SUPPORTIVE TREATMENT: Secure airway; If SpO2 <91% start O2 by prongs/hood; If respiratory distress persists follow STW for respiratory distress (consider CPAP if chest retractions or persistent hypoxia); Start IV 10% dextrose at 60 mL/kg/day; Maintain normal temperature, avoid hyperthermia; Target blood glucose of 50-125 mg/dL; If infant has seizures follow STW for seizure. THERAPEUTIC HYPOTHERMIA (if eligible and written protocol + facility for therapeutic hypothermia and level-3 intensive care available): Initiate whole body cooling using a servo-controlled mattress/phase-change material (PCM) based device/ice or gel packs (if using gel/ice packs/PCM ensure presence of nurse in 1:1 ratio); Continuous rectal temperature monitoring required from initiation until 8 hrs after rewarming; Target rectal temperature 33-34 C; Induction: aim to attain target temperature in first 30 minutes; Maintenance: continue target temperature for 72 hrs after initiation; Rewarming: increase rectal temperature to 36.5 C over 6-12 hrs at a rate <= 0.5 C per hour.
management: For PPV <60s with no neuro signs: shift to mother's side, initiate breastfeeding, skin-to-skin contact, observe every 15 minutes for 1 hour. At risk of HIE: check vitals/glucose, stabilize and support (airway, oxygen, IV 10% dextrose 60 mL/kg/day, normothermia, target glucose 50-125 mg/dL, treat seizures). Assess for moderate to severe HIE. No HIE/Mild HIE: initiate feeds once vitals stable, switch to breastfeeding early, continue monitoring 24 hrs. Moderate or severe HIE: needs further assessment, may need therapeutic hypothermia (cooling per protocol if eligible and facility available). If cooling criteria met but no facility - refer to higher centre. Provide supportive treatment if not eligible for cooling.
referralRedFlags: FREQUENT MULTI-SYSTEM MONITORING IS A MUST. Moderate or severe HIE needs further assessment and may need therapeutic hypothermia. REFER TO HIGHER CENTRE if therapeutic hypothermia criteria met but no written protocol/facility for cooling and level-3 intensive care. Therapeutic hypothermia must be initiated within 6 hrs of birth.
algorithmNotes: Identify asphyxiated neonate (PPV at birth/delayed cry); stabilize (airway, oxygen, dextrose, normothermia, treat seizures); assess HIE severity; eligible moderate-severe HIE within 6h gets therapeutic hypothermia (target rectal 33-34 C for 72h then slow rewarming) at centres with protocol/level-3 care, else refer. ABBREV: BE=Base excess; CBC=Complete blood count; CRP=C reactive protein; CSF=Cerebrospinal fluid; HIE=Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy; NICU=Neonatal intensive care unit; PPV=Positive pressure ventilation; SNCU=Special newborn care unit.

whenToSuspect: Assess every neonate born in or brought to a health facility for presence of sepsis, at admission and during hospital stay, by looking for red and yellow flag signs and risk factors. RED FLAG SIGNS: Shock; Hardening of skin so that it cannot be pinched off the underlying tissue or bone (look at cheeks and thighs); Respiratory distress needing intubation or Silverman's score >6; Bleeding from multiple sites; Respiratory distress onset more than 6 hrs after birth; If age of baby is less than 7 days and mother has foul smelling discharge or chorioamnionitis. YELLOW FLAG SIGNS: Seizures; Refusal to feed; HR>160 persisting for one hour despite normal temperature; Respiratory distress; Floppiness; Lethargy; Feed intolerance; New or increased apneic episodes; Fever or hypothermia not due to environmental temperature.
risk: MATERNAL RISK FACTORS (if age of baby is less than 7 days and mother has): Dai handling or unclean vaginal examination; Rupture of membranes >=18 hrs; pPROM; Urinary tract infection; Diarrhea; Fever. HIGH PROBABILITY OF SEPSIS (start treatment and investigate): Any RED flag sign is present OR Two YELLOW signs/maternal risk factors are present OR One YELLOW sign or maternal risk factor is present AND baby's gestation at birth is <=32 weeks. AT-RISK/SUSPECT SEPSIS (observe): One YELLOW sign or maternal risk factor is present AND baby's gestation at birth is >32 weeks.
scores: Stratify as High probability of sepsis (treat) vs At-risk/suspect sepsis (observe) based on red/yellow flags, maternal risk factors and gestation (<=32 vs >32 weeks). Main utility of both CRP and procalcitonin is to rule-out sepsis; a positive test may be due to non-infective conditions, interpret carefully giving weightage to clinical course. CRP <12 mg/L with disappeared signs allows stopping antibiotics.
investigations: Obtain blood sample for culture and sensitivity. Appropriate laboratory investigations as indicated clinically (Chest X-ray, CBC, platelet count, RBS, serum electrolytes, renal functions). Perform lumbar puncture (LP) for CSF analysis when baby is hemodynamically stable (or if blood culture positive). If blood culture was not done, a negative CRP or Procalcitonin at 24-48 hrs after starting antibiotics can help in early stopping of antibiotics.
drugs: HIGH PROBABILITY OF SEPSIS: Admit in the NICU/SNCU; Obtain blood sample for culture and sensitivity; Start empirical antibiotics as per local/unit policy pending reports; Provide supportive care and do appropriate laboratory investigations; Perform lumbar puncture (LP) for CSF analysis when baby is hemodynamically stable. AT-RISK/SUSPECT SEPSIS: Keep baby under close observation for 48-72 hrs; Start antibiotics if another yellow/red sign appears during observation; Obtain sample for blood culture and sensitivity before starting antibiotics; Perform LP for CSF analysis if starting antibiotics or if blood culture is positive. DURATION OF ANTIBIOTICS: Pneumonia 5-7 DAYS; Sepsis with CRP >12 mg/L AND sterile blood culture AND normal CSF analysis 5-7 DAYS; Blood culture positive 10-14 DAYS; CSF suggestive of meningitis 21 DAYS.
management: REVIEW AT 48 HRS: Signs of sepsis disappeared and CRP <12 mg/L - stop antibiotics, keep under observation till blood culture reported as sterile after 48 hrs of incubation. Signs of sepsis improving but still present - continue antibiotics, duration based on blood culture and LP report. Signs of sepsis worsened or a red sign appeared after starting treatment - upgrade antibiotics as per antibiotic local/unit policy, duration based on blood culture and LP report. If antibiotics continued, review again at 5 days: if baby now well from last 48 hrs, blood culture sterile and CSF normal - stop antibiotics. REMEMBER: Do not start antibiotics without indication (clinical features in neonates are non-specific; look for alternative reasons for sickness, do careful serial observations); Believe a negative blood culture report and stop antibiotics if baby has recovered.
referralRedFlags: PREVENT SEPSIS BY ENSURING HAND HYGIENE, ASEPSIS DURING PROCEDURES AND DILIGENT HOUSEKEEPING. RED FLAG SIGNS (shock, sclerema, respiratory distress needing intubation/Silverman >6, bleeding from multiple sites, respiratory distress onset >6 hrs after birth, maternal foul smelling discharge/chorioamnionitis if <7 days) indicate high probability of sepsis - admit to NICU/SNCU and treat. Upgrade antibiotics if signs worsen.
algorithmNotes: Screen every neonate for red/yellow flags + maternal risk factors; classify high probability (treat with culture + empirical antibiotics + LP) vs at-risk (observe 48-72h); review at 48h to stop/continue/upgrade; antibiotic duration by diagnosis (pneumonia/sepsis 5-7d, culture-positive 10-14d, meningitis 21d); avoid unnecessary antibiotics. ABBREV: CBC=Complete blood count; CRP=C-reactive protein; CSF=Cerebrospinal fluid; LP=Lumbar puncture; NICU=Neonatal intensive care unit; pPROM=Preterm premature rupture of membranes; RBS=Random blood sugar; SNCU=Special newborn care unit.

whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS (ABCD): A - Asymmetry of breast or nipple areola or axilla; B - Breast lump, bulge, blood vessels prominent; C - Colour change of skin or nipple areola; D - Deformed breast/nipple areola (nipple retraction), dimpling of skin, Discharge from nipple, Direct spread-skin (satellite nodule, ulcer, skin oedema), chest wall, Distant spread - headache, jaundice, dyspnoea, bone pains, ascites. SIGNS: A Breast changes (asymmetry in shape/size, breast lump, nipple retraction/ulcer, skin changes - puckering/dimpling/thickening/ulcer/redness/edema/satellite nodules, fixity to underlying muscles or chest wall); B Lymph node (lymph node(s) in axilla or supra-clavicular fossa); C Systemic changes (enlarged liver, ascites, bony tenderness, dyspnoea, pleural effusion). Evaluation and management by multidisciplinary team (MDT) of oncology experts.
risk: STAGING: Early breast cancer T1, T2, N0, N1, M0; Advanced breast cancer T3, T4, any N / Any T, N2, N3. Locally Advanced Breast Cancer (no metastasis) - intent curative; Metastatic Breast Cancer (yes metastasis) - intent palliative care. DO NOT: ignore any lump or changes in breast & nipple areola complex; perform excision biopsy for diagnosis; perform FNAC or core needle biopsy before imaging.
scores: TRIPLE ASSESSMENT (CBE, USG breast and axilla, mammography and core biopsy). STAGING - T1, T2 N0 N1: Upto Stage 2A no metastatic work up; Stage 2B upwards metastatic work up. Adjuvant chemotherapy: consider for all patients with pT > 1 cm or node positive disease based on ER/PR/HER2/Ki-67.
investigations: WORK UP - TRIPLE ASSESSMENT: Clinical breast examination. IMAGING: Bilateral mammogram (for women >30 years); Ultrasound breast and axilla; MRI breast in selected cases; Chest radiograph; Ultrasound whole abdomen; Bone scan; CECT chest and abdomen; PET-CT (optional). PATHOLOGY: Core needle biopsy (preferred) for type, grade, ER, PR, HER2/neu, Ki-67; FISH test if HER-2/neu on IHC-2+/equivocal.
drugs: ADJUVANT THERAPY (AT) - Chemotherapy: consider for all patients with pT > 1 cm or node positive disease based on ER/PR/HER2/Ki-67. Radiotherapy: after breast conservation surgery; after mastectomy with node-positive disease or pathological T3/T4. Targeted therapy: all HER-2/neu positive (3+) or FISH HER-2 amplified patients should receive trastuzumab for 12 months (shorter schedules 9 weeks to 6 months acceptable in some). Hormone therapy: all ER and/or PR positive cases; premenopausal women tamoxifen, postmenopausal women tamoxifen or aromatase inhibitors; minimum 5 years (up to 10 years if high risk like node positive); zoledronic acid or bisphosphonates can be added. METASTATIC BREAST CANCER - Chemotherapy: Anthracyclines, taxanes, platinum, capecitabine, cyclophosphamide, methotrexate; sequential single agents preferred over combinations. Hormonal therapy: tamoxifen, aromatase inhibitors, fulvestrant, megesterol acetate, CDK 4/6 inhibitors, everolimus; ovarian suppression in premenopausal MBC (surgical bilateral oophorectomy or radiotherapeutic ovarian radiation or medical GnRH analogues). HER2 targeted: trastuzumab, lapatinib, pertuzumab, trastuzumab-emtansine. Bone targeted: all patients with bone metastases should receive a bone modifying agent (e.g. zoledronic acid) 4-12 weekly.
management: MULTIDISCIPLINARY CARE. EARLY BREAST CANCER (T1, T2, N0, N1, M0): surgery followed by adjuvant therapy, metastatic work-up usually not indicated. For breast - surgery or primary systemic therapy then surgery, discuss adjuvant therapy in MDT. For axilla (clinico-radiological): Node -ve - SLNB/axillary sampling (if -ve No ALND, if +ve ALND); Node +ve - ALND. ADVANCED BREAST CANCER (T3, T4, any N / Any T, N2, N3): metastatic work up; Locally Advanced (no metastasis) intent curative - neoadjuvant systemic therapy, discuss extent of surgery (MRM or Breast conservation surgery), adjuvant systemic treatment + RT surgery; Metastatic (yes metastasis) intent palliative - hormone therapy/chemotherapy/targeted therapy as indicated. Role of surgery in metastatic: palliation of local tumour symptoms; ICD for malignant pleural effusion + chemical pleurodesis. Role of radiotherapy: pain relief in bone metastasis, brain metastasis, hemostatic RT for bleeding ulcer. Pain control and palliative care.
referralRedFlags: ENHANCE AWARENESS AND EARLY DETECTION OF BREAST CANCER BY SCREENING AS PER NATIONAL PROGRAMME. Evaluation and management by multidisciplinary team (MDT) of oncology experts. DO NOT ignore any lump or changes in breast & nipple areola complex; DO NOT perform excision biopsy for diagnosis; DO NOT perform FNAC or core needle biopsy before imaging. Systemic changes (enlarged liver, ascites, bony tenderness, dyspnoea, pleural effusion) indicate metastatic disease.
algorithmNotes: Suspect breast cancer by ABCD changes; triple assessment (CBE + imaging + core biopsy); stage early vs advanced; early - surgery + adjuvant therapy (chemo/RT/targeted/hormone by receptor status); advanced - neoadjuvant then surgery (curative) or palliative systemic therapy (metastatic); MDT care throughout. ABBREV: ALND=Axillary lymph node dissection; CECT=Contrast-enhanced computed tomography; ER/PR=Estrogen receptor/Progesterone receptor; FISH=Fluorescence in situ hybridization; HER2=Human epidermal growth factor receptor 2; IHC=Immunohistochemistry; MBC=Metastatic breast cancer; PET-CT=Positron emission tomography-computed tomography scan; RT=Radiotherapy; SLNB=Sentinel lymph node biopsy.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT - PRESENTATION: Cough, Chest pain; Hemoptysis, Hoarseness; Breathlessness, Non resolving pneumonia; Mass lesion, Symptoms persist even after treating pneumonia. Clinical examination: Palpable lymph nodes, Chest wall tenderness, Skeletal tenderness, Pleural effusion. All lung shadows are not tuberculosis! Obtain diagnostic investigations before starting empirical ATT. Evaluation and management by multidisciplinary team (MDT) of oncology experts.
risk: IMPORTANT ASSESSMENT PARAMETERS: Pulmonary function; Age and comorbidities; Performance status. Two main types: SMALL CELL LUNG CARCINOMA vs NON SMALL CELL LUNG CARCINOMA (NSCLC). NSCLC staging: Early localized T1-3 N0-2; Locoregionally advanced T4, N3; Metastatic disease any T, any N, M1.
scores: Pulmonary reserve: Effort tolerance, Walk test, Pulmonary function tests (PFT). SMALL CELL: Non metastatic disease T1-4, N0-3, M0 vs Metastatic disease any T, any N, M1. NSCLC: Non metastatic T1-4, N0-3 vs Metastatic any T, any N, M1. Driver mutation testing for non-squamous/squamous to guide targeted therapy (EGFR/ALK/ROS-1).
investigations: INITIAL EVALUATION: CXR, Sputum cytology, Pulmonary reserve (effort tolerance, walk test, PFT). LIMITED DISEASE: CECT thorax and upper abdomen, obtain tissue for diagnosis percutaneously by image guidance or by bronchoscopy. ADVANCED DISEASE: Pleural fluid cytology, Pleural biopsy (image guidance if available), Cervical lymph node aspiration cytology/biopsy. PATHOLOGY ASSESSMENT: Biopsy/cell block/smear; Histopathology (adenocarcinoma, squamous carcinoma, poorly differentiated carcinoma, small cell carcinoma); Immunohistochemistry (TTF 1, p40, synaptophysin/chromogranin); Preserve tissue for molecular analysis - Molecular tests for adenocarcinoma EGFR, ALK, ROS-1. Metastatic workup PET-CT scan and MRI brain.
drugs: SMALL CELL LUNG CARCINOMA - Non metastatic: metastatic work up PET CT & MRI brain, consider surgery for T1-2 N0, Concurrent CT + RT. Metastatic: prophylactic cranial irradiation, symptomatic & supportive care, palliative chemotherapy carboplatin + etoposide. NSCLC available treatment options - Chemotherapy doublet: Carboplatin or cisplatin with pemetrexed or paclitaxel or gemcitabine or etoposide; EGFR mutation positive: gefitinib, afatinib, osimertinib, erlotinib, dacomitinib; Immune checkpoint inhibitors: nivolumab, atezolizumab, pembrolizumab, ipilimumab. NSCLC by mutation: EGFR +ve - Oral TKI; ALK +ve/ROS-1 - Oral TKI; EGFR/ALK -ve/ROS-1 - Platinum doublet chemotherapy or chemoimmunotherapy. PALLIATIVE CARE: Radiotherapy; Pain management (Opioids - morphine, tramadol, oxycodone; Paracetamol, NSAIDs); Cough suppressants; Treatment of COPD; Treatment of anemia, anorexia, electrolyte abnormalities.
management: MANAGEMENT OF NSCLC (metastatic workup PET-CT scan and MRI brain): Early localized T1-3, N0-2 - EBUS/EUS +/- mediastinoscopy (<N2 - lung resection lobectomy with SMLND; N2/N3 - NACT then resection; if operated without NACT Stage II and III A T>4cm - adjuvant RT for positive cut margins & pN2, adjuvant chemotherapy, consider Osimertinib if EGFR mutation+); unfit or patient refuses surgery - SBRT/RFA T1-2 N0-2 or Concurrent CT+RT. Locoregionally advanced T4, N3 - Concurrent CT+RT. Metastatic any T any N M1 - Brain/symptomatic bone metastases (palliative radiation, bisphosphonates for bone) or other visceral metastases (histology - non-squamous/squamous, driver mutation test, mutation +ve oral TKI, mutation -ve platinum doublet chemotherapy or chemoimmunotherapy). Refer to oncology centre.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. All lung shadows are not tuberculosis! Obtain diagnostic investigations before starting empirical ATT. Refer to oncology centre for metastatic disease. Evaluation and management by multidisciplinary team (MDT) of oncology experts.
algorithmNotes: Suspect lung cancer (cough/hemoptysis/non-resolving pneumonia/mass); confirm tissue before empirical ATT; classify small cell vs NSCLC; stage and test driver mutations (EGFR/ALK/ROS-1); treat NSCLC by stage (surgery/CTRT/targeted/immunotherapy), small cell with CT+RT or palliative chemo; MDT and palliative care. ABBREV: ALK=Anaplastic lymphoma kinase; ATT=Anti tubercular therapy; CECT=Contrast-enhanced computed tomography; EBUS=Endobronchial ultrasound; EGFR=Epidermal growth factor receptor; NACT=Neoadjuvant chemotherapy; NSCLC=Non-small cell lung cancer; PFT=Pulmonary function test; RFA=Radiofrequency ablation; ROS=Ros proto-oncogene 1; SBRT=Stereotactic body radiotherapy; SMLND=Systematic lymph node dissection; TKI=Tyrosine kinase inhibitors.

whenToSuspect: WHEN TO SUSPECT: Non healing ulcer/sore in the mouth especially in a tobacco chewer or smoker; Neck mass; Difficulty in opening mouth; Difficulty in protrusion of tongue; Pain referred to ear; Oral premalignant disorders (OPMD): leukoplakia/erythroplakia/sub mucous fibrosis, lichen planus. RISK FACTORS: Tobacco, Alcohol, Areca nut, Sharp tooth, Ill-fitting dentures. Screening can detect OPMD and invasive cancer early and improve outcome. Treatment of oral cancer is ideally delivered by a multidisciplinary team (MDT).
risk: RISK FACTORS: Tobacco, Alcohol, Areca nut, Sharp tooth, Ill-fitting dentures. STAGING groups: T1 T2 N0 cancer; T3 T4A N0 N1 N2; T4B N3 (tongue and buccal cancers with skull base/internal carotid artery involvement - palliation). Oral premalignant disorders (OPMD): leukoplakia, erythroplakia, sub mucous fibrosis, lichen planus.
scores: INDICATIONS FOR ADJUVANT RT: Close margin, positive node(s), or presence of any two of following - LVI, PNI, high grade. INDICATIONS FOR ADJUVANT CT-RT: Metastatic nodes with extracapsular extension, involved margins. Define clinical and radiological staging, goals of treatment.
investigations: EVALUATION: Clinical examination, +/- examination under anaesthesia (EUA), assess pain, nutritional status & oro-dental hygiene; USG neck/CT scan head & neck; Evaluate upper aerodigestive tract for second primary; Biopsy from primary site, FNAC from neck node; CBC, LFT, RFT, blood sugar, chest X-ray, ECG; Tobacco cessation for patient and care givers; Pure tone audiometry (PTA); Speech and swallowing assessment; Define clinical and radiological staging, goals of treatment.
drugs: T1 T2 N0 CANCER (curative intent): Initial surgery preferred (wide excision with 1 cm margins & supra-omohyoid neck dissection Level I-III with reconstruction) OR Radical radiation therapy. T3 T4A N0 N1 N2 (curative intent): Initial surgery (wide excision with 1 cm margins + comprehensive neck dissection and reconstruction) OR Chemoradiation OR Neo-adjuvant CT followed by surgery. T4B N3 (palliation): Palliative chemotherapy, RT, Immunotherapy, Best supportive care. The drug of choice for concurrent chemotherapy is CISPLATIN. Adjuvant radiation: minimum post-operative radiation dose is 60 Gy/6 weeks/30# or equivalent to primary and nodal areas using conventional treatment planning, 3DCRT or IMRT. Radical radiation: 66-70 Gy delivered using conventional planning/3DCRT/IMRT through telecobalt machine or LINAC at 1.8 to 2 Gy per fraction over 7-8 weeks with adequate margins all around the lesion and including level I, II and III nodes.
management: Treatment by stage with curative intent (surgery preferred for T1-T2 N0, surgery or chemoradiation or neoadjuvant CT for T3-T4A) or palliation (T4B N3). Concurrent chemotherapy drug of choice is cisplatin. Adjuvant RT/CT-RT by margin/node/risk factor indications. FOLLOW UP: 3 monthly for first 3 years, 6 monthly for years 4 & 5, annually thereafter with clinical examination at every visit, evaluation of symptoms, endoscopy of upper aerodigestive tract annually (to identify recurrences and second primary cancers). Treatment of common side effects - xerostomia, speech and swallowing issues, nutrition and physical rehabilitation, dental care by MDT. EMPHASIZE TOBACCO CESSATION: set a quit date, remove tobacco/cigarettes from home/car/work; manage withdrawal symptoms; counsellor help; can combine nicotine replacement with or +/- bupropion.
referralRedFlags: PREVENT ORAL CANCER BY TOBACCO CONTROL. Screening can detect OPMD and invasive cancer early and improve outcome. T4B N3 (tongue and buccal cancers with skull base/internal carotid artery involvement) - aim of treatment is palliation. Treatment of oral cancer is ideally delivered by a multidisciplinary team (MDT). Evaluate upper aerodigestive tract for second primary.
algorithmNotes: Suspect oral cancer in tobacco users with non-healing ulcer/neck mass/trismus; biopsy + imaging + staging; treat by stage (surgery preferred early, surgery/chemoradiation advanced, palliation for T4B N3); cisplatin for concurrent chemo; adjuvant RT/CT-RT by risk; long-term follow up + tobacco cessation. ABBREV: CBC=Complete blood count; CT=Chemotherapy; EUA=Examination under anaesthesia; FNAC=Fine needle aspiration cytology; IMRT=Intensity-modulated radiation therapy; LFT=Liver function tests; LVI=Lymphovascular invasion; MDT=Multidisciplinary team; OPMD=Oral premalignant disorders; PNI=Perineural invasion; PTA=Pure tone audiometry; RFT=Renal function tests; RT=Radiotherapy; USG=Ultrasound sonography test.

whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: Decrease in vision, progressive change in power of glasses, glare, uniocular polyopia, white pupillary reflex. SIGNS: VA <= 6/9, not improving with pinhole or glasses, along with lens opacity. PRELIMINARY EXAMINATION: Torch, distant direct ophthalmoscopy. SYSTEMIC ASSESSMENT: Detailed medical history including history of allergy and review of records if available and assessment of general health status.
risk: CATARACT WITH OCULAR COMORBIDITY: explain implications of associated corneal opacity/glaucoma/uveitis/retinal disease/optic nerve disease/amblyopia/squint/uncontrolled systemic disease; prioritize care according to severity; refer to specialist. CATARACT WITH SYSTEMIC COMORBIDITY - Medicine specialist referral essential: Ischaemic heart disease (with request for monitored anaesthetic care and decision on withholding anticoagulant/fibrinolytics), Systemic malignancy; Medicine specialist referral desirable: Hypertension, Diabetes mellitus, Chronic renal disease, Collagen vascular diseases, Thyroid disease. CATARACT ABSENT: look for other causes of vision impairment (corneal pathology, glaucoma, retinal disease, optic nerve disease, amblyopia) and refer.
scores: INDICATIONS FOR SURGERY: 1. Clinically significant cataract causing visual loss enough to warrant surgery (BCVA in affected eye < 6/12 or patient feeling visually handicapped even with BCVA >= 6/12; advanced cataracts with severe visual loss BCVA <6/60 or worse should be operated on priority); 2. Clinically significant cataract for other visually disturbing symptoms (glare, loss of contrast, polyopia); 3. Significant cataract hampering visualization of fundus for examination or treatment of retinal disorders; 4. Cataract with narrow angle glaucoma where cataract surgery is required to improve control of IOP. FITNESS FOR SURGERY: General health stable; BP <= 150/90mm Hg; Blood sugar (mg/dl) FBS < 150, PPBS < 200 / RBS < 200.
investigations: OPHTHALMIC EXAMINATIONS - Essential: Vision and refraction, intraocular pressure (IOP), slit lamp examination, pupillary reflexes, pressure over lacrimal sac area, fundus examination (if not possible due to dense cataract then ultrasound B-scan); Desirable: Slit lamp-specular reflection, ultrasound-B scan (if suspected vitreous haemorrhage or retinal detachment). ESSENTIAL INVESTIGATIONS: Blood pressure, Blood sugar (FBS, PPBS/RBS), Ophthalmic biometry (Axial length and keratometry for IOL power calculation). DESIRABLE: Lacrimal sac syringing, ECG. OPTIONAL: Xylocaine sensitivity test dose if h/o allergy, Specular microscopy, Serology testing, others based on existing ocular & systemic disease.
drugs: PRE-OPERATIVE PREPARATION: Topical broad spectrum antibiotics, QID for 1-3 days advisable. Surgery to be performed in sterile OT following strict aseptic procedures and universal precautions. SURGICAL PREPARATION: Periocular cleaning with 10% povidone iodine followed by instillation of 5% povidone iodine in conjunctival sac, rinse after 3 minutes, sterile surgical eye drape. SURGICAL OPTIONS: 1. Small Incision Cataract Surgery (SICS) with PMMA IOL; 2. Phacoemulsification (Phaco) with Indian foldable IOL; 3. Phaco with imported or premium foldable IOL; 4. ECCE (large incision) with PMMA IOL. POST OP CARE: Topical broad spectrum antibiotics QID for 1-2 weeks or longer if required; Topical steroids 4-6 times per day for 2 Weeks then taper over 2-4 weeks; Follow up 1 day, 1-2 weeks (optional) & 2-4 weeks after cataract surgery; Prescription of glasses at 2-4 weeks after cataract surgery; Refer to higher centre in case of adverse event.
management: PHC/PRIMARY LEVEL: detailed examination, refraction for BCVA, preliminary diagnosis, referral to ophthalmologist if BCVA/vision with pinhole <= 6/12, postoperative follow up, timely referral for drop in vision or fresh complications (PCO/CME/corneal decompensation/raised IOP/uveitis/displaced IOL/delayed endophthalmitis/scleritis/wound dehiscence). SECONDARY LEVEL: cataract surgery, diagnose/manage/refer comorbidities (Glaucoma, Diabetic Retinopathy, Corneal opacity), postoperative follow up, manage PCO(VAO)/complications. TERTIARY LEVEL: cataract surgery, diagnose and manage comorbidities, postoperative follow up, manage complications.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. INDICATIONS FOR URGENT REFERRAL: White cataract, shallow AC, sluggish pupil, sudden vision loss with cataract, bilateral advanced cataract. Medicine specialist referral essential for ischaemic heart disease and systemic malignancy. Refer to higher centre in case of adverse event. Negative indicator: vision worse than pre-op or unexplained lack of improvement or serious complications (endophthalmitis/irreversible corneal decompensation/dropped nucleus/IOL dislocation/resurgery).
algorithmNotes: Diagnose cataract by reduced VA not improving with pinhole + lens opacity; operate when visually significant (BCVA <6/12 or symptomatic, advanced <6/60 on priority); ensure fitness; SICS/Phaco/ECCE with IOL under aseptic technique; structured post-op antibiotics/steroids and follow up; refer urgent red flags. ABBREV: BCVA=Best corrected visual acuity; CME=Cystoid macular edema; ECCE=Extra capsular cataract extraction; FBS=Fasting blood sugar; IOL=Intraocular lens; IOP=Intraocular pressure; PCO=Posterior capsular opacification; PMMA=Polymethyl methacrylate; PPBS=Post prandial blood sugar; RBS=Random blood sugar; SICS=Small incision cataract surgery; VAO=Visual axis opacification.

whenToSuspect: KEY POINTS: Diabetic retinopathy can be asymptomatic in early as well as advanced stages; Every newly diagnosed diabetic should be screened for retinopathy at the point of detection of diabetes and thereafter annually or more frequently as required by the retinopathy grade. PRELIMINARY SCREENING - History: duration of diabetes, compliance with treatment and blood sugar monitoring, any visual complaints, any other systemic illness. Examination: Vision, refraction, ophthalmic examination including pupillary reflexes, IOP, dilated fundus examination with a direct/indirect ophthalmoscope.
risk: TABLE 1 CLASSIFICATION OF DIABETIC RETINOPATHY: No Apparent retinopathy (no abnormalities); Mild non proliferative DR (micro aneurysms only); Moderate non proliferative DR (more than just micro aneurysms but less than severe NPDR); Severe non-proliferative DR (any of: intra-retinal haemorrhages >=20 in each quadrant, definite venous beading in 2 quadrants, intra retinal micro vascular abnormalities in 1 quadrant, and no signs of proliferative retinopathy); Proliferative DR (severe non proliferative DR and 1 or more of: neovascularization, vitreous/pre retinal haemorrhage). TABLE 2 CLASSIFICATION OF DIABETIC MACULAR EDEMA: DME Absent; DME Present; Mild DME; Moderate DME; Severe DME (retinal thickening or hard exudates involving the centre of the macula). For non ophthalmologist, any DR should be referred to retina specialist.
scores: INDICATIONS FOR URGENT REFERRAL: Vision loss, Hard exudates, Haemorrhages, Non-dilating pupil, Blurred disc margins, No view of fundus, Absent Foveal Reflex. INDICATION FOR SURGERY: Sudden vision loss, Clinically recognizable macular edema, Rubeosis iridis, Proliferative DR. FITNESS FOR SURGERY: General health stable; BP <= 150/90mm Hg; Blood sugar (mg/dl) FBS < 140, PPBS < 180 / RBS < 200.
investigations: DEFINITIVE DIAGNOSIS - Essential: Slit lamp bio microscopy (retinal exam), ultrasound-B scan (when fundus not visible); Desirable: Indirect ophthalmoscopy, Fundus photography; Optional: OCT, FFA, OCTA if indicated.
drugs: INTERVENTION: Pre-op topical broad spectrum antibiotics, QID for 1-3 days. SURGICAL PREPARATION: Periocular cleaning with 10% povidone iodine followed by instillation of 5% povidone iodine in conjunctival sac, rinse after 3 minutes, wipe, aseptic precautions, sterile surgical eye drape. TERTIARY LEVEL treatment: Intravitreal injections/laser photocoagulation/vitreoretinal surgery.
management: PHC/PRIMARY LEVEL: detailed history & examination, refraction for BCVA, preliminary diagnosis, referral to ophthalmologist (as per Table 1 and 2), counselling regarding metabolic control, preventive advice/counselling and regular follow up. SECONDARY LEVEL: refraction for BCVA, detailed work up including indirect ophthalmoscopy, diagnose/classify/advice (as per Table 1 and 2), point to point guided referral, ensure follow up and compliance, counselling regarding metabolic control and systemic comorbidities (hypertension, nephropathy). TERTIARY LEVEL: diagnose/classify/advice, intravitreal injections/laser photocoagulation/vitreoretinal surgery, ensure postoperative follow up and compliance including collaboration with district hospital ophthalmologists, counselling regarding metabolic control.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. For non ophthalmologist, any DR should be referred to retina specialist. INDICATIONS FOR URGENT REFERRAL: Vision loss, Hard exudates, Haemorrhages, Non-dilating pupil, Blurred disc margins, No view of fundus, Absent Foveal Reflex. Every newly diagnosed diabetic should be screened for retinopathy at detection and annually.
algorithmNotes: Screen all diabetics for retinopathy at diagnosis then annually; grade DR (none/mild/moderate/severe NPDR/proliferative) and DME by dilated fundus exam; refer any DR to retina specialist; treat advanced disease with intravitreal injections/laser/vitreoretinal surgery; emphasize metabolic control. ABBREV: BCVA=Best corrected visual acuity; DME=Diabetic macular edema; FFA=Fundus fluorescein angiography; IOP=Intra ocular pressure; OCT=Optical coherence tomography; OCTA=Optical coherence tomography angiography.

whenToSuspect: KEY POINTS: Glaucoma can be asymptomatic; Can lead to irreversible vision loss if not treated in time; Everybody >= 40 years age to be screened; Everybody with a family history of Glaucoma to be screened. SCREENING - History taking: Unilateral intermittent headache, blurring of vision, eye pain, coloured haloes; Previously diagnosed/glaucoma suspect; Treatment history (medical/surgical/laser and compliance). SIGNS: Abnormalities of optic nerve head (Cup to disc ratio > 0.7; asymmetry > 0.2); IOP >20mmHg; Evidence of ocular co-morbidities that could lead to secondary glaucoma; Torch light examination (shadow test - shallow anterior chamber and Iris changes: Iris atrophy and sphincter pupil atrophy); Visual field defects; Evidence of previous surgery or laser; Evidence of intermittent angle closure glaucoma (if normal but associated with other features think of normal-tension glaucoma).
risk: HISTORY: Highest baseline IOP before any treatment; Systemic Hypertension, Cardiovascular diseases, Transient ischemic attacks, DM; Systemic and ocular medications used; Any OTC medication especially steroid for allergy; Any ocular trauma. CLASSIFICATION by tonometry + gonioscopy: IOP 14-18 mmHg - Open angles POAG Suspect / Narrow angles PACS/PAC; IOP >20 mmHg - Narrow angles + PAS PACG / Open Angles POAG.
scores: SUGGESTED MANAGEMENT PROTOCOL BASED ON IOP: <25 mmHg - First line drug Prostaglandins/beta-Blocker (look for 15% IOP), review 2 weeks/switch to another drug if nonresponder, review every 4 mths if target IOP achieved; 25-30 mmHg - First line drug Prostaglandins/beta Blocker, review 10 days + another drug if required, review every 3 mths if target IOP achieved; >30 mmHg - Prostaglandins + beta-Blocker + Tab Diamox 250mg TDS only x3 days, review 10 days add brimonidine if required, review every 3 mths if target IOP achieved. FITNESS FOR SURGERY: General health stable; BP <= 150/90mm Hg; Blood sugar (mg/dl) FBS < 140, PPBS < 180 / RBS < 200.
investigations: EXAMINATION: Vision, Refraction, Ophthalmic examination including pupillary reflexes, IOP, Fundus examination, Anterior chamber depth with direct ophthalmoscope/slit lamp biomicroscope with 90D. DEFINITIVE DIAGNOSIS - Essential: Slit lamp bio microscopy, AC Depth, gonioscopy, Pupillary reflex, Estimation of IOP (3 measurements), Visual field assessment; Desirable: Diurnal variation, Central Corneal thickness; Optional: UBM, OCT, HRT, GCC for RNFL thickness. Tonometry (repeat twice) + Gonioscopy; Perimetry + baseline IOP (preferably morning and evening) to determine target IOP.
drugs: MANAGEMENT BY IOP (see above): First line Prostaglandins/beta-Blocker, escalate by IOP level with addition of brimonidine, and oral Tab Diamox (acetazolamide) 250mg TDS only x3 days for IOP >30 mmHg. RED FLAG: initiate initial therapy for acute attack of angle closure glaucoma - oral diamox, iv mannitol and pilocarpine 2 percent tds if confirmed narrow angle before yag PI. INTERVENTION: Consult flowchart, pre-op topical broad spectrum antibiotics QID for 1-3 days. AIM OF GLAUCOMA MANAGEMENT: Achieve target IOP with minimal fluctuation (refer NPCB Guidelines); Iridotomy in all primary angle closure patients; Trabeculectomy or referral to higher center if target IOP not achievable. SPECIAL INSTRUCTION FOR GLAUCOMA MEDICATION: Punctal Occlusion; Not to squeeze eyes after instillation; 1 drop in conjunctival sac.
management: PHC: Evaluate for open angle (deep AC), narrow angle (shallow AC) with torchlight, detailed history/examination, refraction for BCVA, preliminary diagnosis, referral to ophthalmologist as soon as possible if IOP > 21/shallow anterior chamber/cup-disc ratio > 0.7, counsel on medication spacing/phasing and side effects, counsel that surgery is not a cure but lowers IOP, follow up is mandatory. DISTRICT HOSPITAL: refraction for BCVA, detailed work up (slit lamp, AC depth, IOP, optic nerve head), gonioscopy/fields, diagnose/classify, point to point guided referral, surgical intervention (Yag PI and Trabeculectomy), counselling. TERTIARY CARE: detailed work up, optional investigations (UBM/OCT/HRT/GCC for RNFL), surgical intervention (YAG PI, Trabeculectomy, tube shunts), ensure postoperative follow up.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. RED FLAG SIGNS FOR URGENT REFERRAL: Acute angle closure attack; IOP > 30; Loss of pupillary reflex with visual impairment; Single eyed patient with glaucoma. INDICATIONS FOR SURGERY: IOP above target despite maximal tolerated medical therapy; Inability to review regularly; Unable to afford medications; Progression of the disease on maximal tolerated medical therapy; Non compliance.
algorithmNotes: Screen everyone >=40 or with family history; suspect glaucoma by raised IOP/cupping/field defects; classify by IOP and angle (open vs narrow); manage by IOP protocol (prostaglandin/beta-blocker escalating, acetazolamide for IOP>30); iridotomy for angle closure; trabeculectomy/refer if target IOP not achieved; treat acute angle closure urgently. ABBREV: AC=Anterior chamber; GCC=Ganglion cell complex; HRT=Heidelberg retina tomograph; OCT=Optical coherence tomography; NPCB=National Programme for Control of Blindness; PI=Peripheral iridectomy; POAG=Primary open angle glaucoma; RNFL=Retinal nerve fiber layer; UBM=High-frequency ultrasound biomicroscopy.

whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: Fever with pain, swelling and inability to move the joint; Malaise and/or listlessness, irritability, poor feeding, immobility and joint dysfunction. SIGNS: Swelling, erythema, tenderness, painful limitation of joint motion, painful limp; Patients may or may not appear acutely ill or toxic (especially malnourished/immunocompromised patients). ASSESSMENT: Detailed medical history to look for primary focus of infection including history of antecedent mild trauma. WHEN TO SUSPECT SEPTIC ARTHRITIS: Cloudy or purulent synovial fluid; Synovial fluid white blood cell count >100,000 cells/mL (neutrophils > 90%) and Gram's staining; Synovial fluid glucose level 50 mg/dL less than serum glucose levels.
risk: Look for primary focus of infection and history of antecedent mild trauma. Differentiate from NOT SEPTIC causes (definite alternative diagnosis): Inflammatory arthritis, Crystal arthritis, Haemarthrosis, Trauma, Bursitis or cellulitis, Transient synovitis. Malnourished/immunocompromised patients may not appear acutely ill.
scores: Synovial fluid suggestive of septic arthritis: cloudy/purulent, WBC >100,000 cells/mL (neutrophils >90%), Gram's staining positive, synovial fluid glucose 50 mg/dL less than serum glucose. Monitor CRP, ESR levels every 2 days during hospital stay. Conversion to oral antibiotics based on clinical improvement, fall in inflammatory markers, and resolution of systemic symptoms.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: Hemogram with ESR; C-reactive protein (CRP); Blood culture; Joint fluid aspirate for examination (Gram stain, aerobic and anaerobic culture and sensitivity C&S testing). DESIRABLE: Plain radiographs of affected joint, Ultrasound of affected joint, Doppler to rule out DVT, Renal and liver function, ELISA serology for HIV/HBsAg/HCV, Blood sugar (FBS, PPBS/RBS), Synovial fluid for fungal and mycobacterial staining with C&S for suspected cases, Synovial fluid for polarising microscopy, Synovial fluid examination for synovial sugar and protein levels. OPTIONAL: Serum Procalcitonin, CE-MRI (contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging).
drugs: PRIMARY CARE (clinical impression of septic arthritis) - refer immediately to higher centre after: 1. Adequate analgesia; 2. Immobilisation of the part; 3. First dose IV broad spectrum antibiotic (only if access to higher centre will take more than 2-3 hours). HIGHER CENTRE: same as above plus joint aspiration for Gram stain + C&S, radiograph and ultrasound of affected joint, IV antibiotic to be continued (change according to C&S report), surgical treatment, IV antibiotic to be continued for two weeks followed by oral antibiotics for four weeks; conversion to oral antibiotics based on clinical improvement, fall in inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR monitored every 2 days), and resolution of systemic symptoms; consult with microbiologist; manage complications like DVT, osteomyelitis, pathological fracture. SURGICAL TREATMENT: Surgical decompression - Arthrotomy + thorough saline lavage +/- suction drainage; if surgery contraindicated - serial aspiration and lavage preferably under ultrasound guidance.
management: Clinical impression of septic arthritis - primary care gives analgesia + immobilisation + first dose IV antibiotic (if transfer >2-3h) then refer; higher centre aspirates, images, continues/adjusts IV antibiotics, surgical treatment. No definite alternative diagnosis - refer after analgesia/immobilisation, higher centre aspirates and investigates joint fluid; if confirmed treat as per septic arthritis protocol. Definite alternative diagnosis (NOT SEPTIC - inflammatory/crystal arthritis, haemarthrosis, trauma, bursitis/cellulitis, transient synovitis) - treat as appropriate. After surgical decompression, if clinical improvement continue antibiotic; if inadequate response (incorrect organism - stop and re-culture, modify per microbiology, look for other foci/sepsis, further imaging for osteomyelitis); if no improvement re-evaluate, repeat arthrotomy/debridement/culture, consider atypical infection.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. Septic arthritis is an emergency - refer immediately to higher centre after analgesia, immobilisation and first dose IV antibiotic (if access >2-3 hours). Manage complications like DVT, osteomyelitis, pathological fracture. Inadequate response/no resolution - re-culture, modify antibiotics, repeat surgery, consider atypical infection.
algorithmNotes: Suspect septic arthritis with hot painful swollen joint + fever; aspirate joint fluid (cloudy, WBC>100000, low glucose) and culture before antibiotics; refer urgently with analgesia/immobilisation/first IV antibiotic; higher centre does surgical decompression (arthrotomy + lavage) + IV antibiotics 2 weeks then oral 4 weeks; monitor CRP/ESR. ABBREV: C&S=Culture and sensitivity; CE-MRI=Contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging; CRP=C-reactive protein; DVT=Deep vein thrombosis; ESR=Erythrocyte sedimentation rate.

condition: Supracondylar Fracture of Humerus in Children
whenToSuspect: CLINICAL PRESENTATION: Pain, swelling and loss of movement at elbow; Most commonly in children aged 5-7 years; Fall on outstretched arm; Fracture is just above the epicondyles of humerus (upper limb). EXAMINATION: Swelling, Deformity, Ecchymosis, Limited active and passive elbow motion. DISTAL NEUROVASCULAR EXAMINATION (always compare with normal limb) - Vascular examination: assess radial pulse, assess vascular perfusion (Well perfused = warm and pink; Poorly perfused = cold and pale); Neurological examination: Median nerve, Radial nerve, Ulnar nerve (detailed examination if suspected). If vascular injury is suspected it should be treated as an emergency.
risk: GARTLAND CLASSIFICATION OF SUPRACONDYLAR FRACTURES: TYPE I - Undisplaced fracture; TYPE II - Displaced fracture with variable angulation, posterior cortical continuity of humerus preserved; TYPE III - Completely displaced fractures with cortical discontinuity, soft tissue gap variable. Vascular status: Well perfused (warm and pink) vs Poorly perfused (cold and pale).
scores: Management by Gartland type and neurovascular status. Type III with neurovascular deficit present needs open reduction; nerve injury without vascular injury - reduce and observe for recovery 3 weeks. Emergent closed reduction of displaced pediatric supracondylar fractures must be performed in patients with decreased perfusion of the hand.
investigations: ESSENTIAL: Radiographs - AP and lateral x-ray elbow. The comparative x-ray of contralateral elbow should be done if suspicion is strong and x-ray of injured elbow appears normal. DESIRABLE: Immediate arterial doppler/CT angiography (in case of suspected vascular injury).
drugs: MANAGEMENT OF OPEN FRACTURES - Primary Care: 1. Inj Tetanus toxoid; 2. Pain management; 3. Liberal saline wash; 4. Removal of visible dirt and debris; 5. Sterile dressing and splintage; 6. First dose antibiotic after test dose (Broad spectrum antibiotics); 7. Refer to higher center. Secondary/Tertiary Care: all of above plus reassess patient. MANAGEMENT OF CLOSED FRACTURES - Primary Care: 1. Pain management; 2. Splintage using triangular sling/lateral elbow sling/well padded moulded cramer wire splint in the position of deformity; 3. If vascular injury suspected treat as emergency; 4. Refer to higher centre. SECONDARY/TERTIARY CARE (X-ray available): TYPE I - Immobilise: above elbow well padded cast with 90 degree elbow flexion for three weeks (follow-up X-ray at 1 week to assess for displacement). TYPE II - one attempt of closed reduction under anaesthesia with radiological control; a) Closed reduction possible: above elbow plaster cast in flexion (10 degrees short of radial pulse) and pronation of forearm, observe for distal neuro-vascular deficits and swelling for at least 24 hours, follow-up Xray at 5 days for any displacement; b) Closed reduction failed/impossible: Open reduction and pinning. TYPE III - flexion type/medial column collapse; a) Neurovascular deficit absent: Closed reduction and percutaneous pinning (CRPP); b) Neurovascular deficit present: Open Reduction and pinning; c) Nerve injury without Vascular Injury: reduce fracture and observe for recovery of nerve injury for 3 weeks; in case of suspected/proved distal neurovascular injury refer to tertiary centre.
management: PRIMARY CARE: open fractures - tetanus toxoid, pain management, saline wash, debris removal, sterile dressing/splintage, first dose antibiotic, refer; closed fractures - pain management, splintage in position of deformity, treat vascular injury as emergency, refer. SECONDARY/TERTIARY CARE (X-ray available): manage by Gartland type (Type I immobilise in cast, Type II closed reduction +/- pinning, Type III CRPP or open reduction by neurovascular status). Emergent closed reduction must be performed in patients with decreased perfusion of the hand.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. If vascular injury is suspected it should be treated as an emergency. RED FLAG SIGNS FOR REFERRAL (signs of Compartment syndrome/Volkmann's ischaemia, may require urgent fasciotomy along with management of vascular injury and fracture fixation): Pain on passive stretching, Pallor, Paresthesia, Pulselessness, Paralysis. Emergent closed reduction in patients with decreased perfusion of the hand; refer to tertiary centre for distal neurovascular injury.
algorithmNotes: Suspect supracondylar fracture in 5-7 yr old after fall on outstretched arm with elbow pain/swelling; always check distal neurovascular status (vascular injury = emergency); classify by Gartland type; treat Type I in cast, Type II closed reduction, Type III CRPP/open reduction by neurovascular status; watch for compartment syndrome (5 Ps). ABBREV: AP=Anteroposterior; CRPP=Closed reduction and percutaneous pinning.

whenToSuspect: DEFINITION: Acute painful swelling of the scrotum or its contents, accompanied by local signs and general symptoms. DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF ACUTE SCROTUM (pathology - frequency - age): Extravaginal torsion of testis (Uncommon, perinatal period); Intravaginal torsion of testis (Common, anytime peak at 13-16 yrs); Testicular appendage torsion (Very Common, anytime peak at 11 yrs); Epididymitis/Epididymo-orchitis (Rare, 0-6 months); Mumps orchitis (Uncommon, only after puberty); Idiopathic scrotal edema (Uncommon, 0-5 yrs); Fat necrosis of scrotum (Rare, 5-15 yrs); Henoch Schonlein Purpura (Rare, 4-10 yrs); Testicular Trauma (Uncommon, anytime common in 5-15 yrs).
risk: PAINFUL SCROTAL SWELLING DECISION TREE by onset: SUDDEN onset - Focal tenderness, blue dot sign: YES = Testicular appendage torsion, NO = Testicular torsion. INSIDIOUS onset - involving perineal/inguinal region: YES = Idiopathic scrotal edema, NO = Epididymitis. DIFFERENTIATING FEATURES - Torsion testis: sudden onset pain in testis/lower abdomen/groin, nausea and vomiting, very painful local palpation, red and edematous bluish hemiscrotum (infarction), transverse lie of testis, absent cremasteric reflex. Torsion of testicular appendage: sudden onset pain less severe, bluish black spot (blue-dot) at upper pole through skin, palpation less painful. Epididymitis: inflammatory, epididymis affected before puberty (0-6 months), epididymo-orchitis common after puberty, urinary tract abnormalities/urethral instrumentation, usually E. coli.
scores: TESTICULAR TORSION IS MOST IMPORTANT CONDITION TO RULE OUT: Unequivocal cases - no investigations, immediate scrotal exploration; Equivocal cases - Doppler study of scrotum, Radionuclide testicular scan. Golden window of 4-8 hours for salvage of torted testis. Transverse lie, absent cremasteric reflex and bluish discoloration suggest torsion (infarction).
investigations: TESTICULAR TORSION: unequivocal cases - no investigations, immediate scrotal exploration; equivocal cases - Doppler study of scrotum, Radionuclide testicular scan. TORSION OF TESTICULAR APPENDAGE: Mandatory USG scrotum and Doppler scrotum, Desirable Urine analysis. EPIDIDYMO-ORCHITIS: Mandatory Urine analysis, Desirable Ultrasonography of scrotum. TESTICULAR TRAUMA: Mandatory USG scrotum.
drugs: TREATMENT - TESTICULAR TORSION: Immediate scrotal exploration in golden window of 4-8 hours if investigative facilities not available; Clinical exploration if bell clapper deformity seen; Contralateral orchiopexy if bell clapper anomaly on affected side; Orchidectomy preferable in older children if other testis is normal; Refer if no surgical facility available; Testicular prosthesis at a later date. TORSION OF TESTICULAR APPENDAGE: Restricted activity, Warm compression, Anti inflammatory drugs; if not differentiable from torsion testis - Exploration and excision of necrotic appendage. IDIOPATHIC SCROTAL EDEMA: Anti-histaminics, Topical corticosteroids. HENOCH-SCHONLEIN PURPURA: Supportive treatment, Rarely systemic corticosteroids. TESTICULAR INJURY: Mostly supportive; Surgery if large hematoma/tunica albuginea rupture on USG.
management: Use the painful scrotal swelling decision tree (sudden vs insidious onset) to differentiate. Rule out testicular torsion first - unequivocal cases need immediate scrotal exploration (no delay for investigations) within 4-8 hour golden window; equivocal cases get Doppler/radionuclide scan. Manage by cause: torsion (exploration + orchiopexy/orchidectomy + contralateral orchiopexy), appendage torsion (conservative or excision), idiopathic scrotal edema (antihistamines, topical steroids), HSP (supportive), testicular injury (supportive or surgery for large hematoma/tunica rupture). Refer if no surgical facility.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. Testicular torsion is the most important condition to rule out - immediate scrotal exploration in golden window of 4-8 hours; refer if no surgical facility available. Transverse lie, absent cremasteric reflex, bluish discoloration suggest torsion with infarction. Do not delay exploration for investigations in unequivocal cases.
algorithmNotes: Differentiate acute scrotum by onset (sudden = torsion/appendage torsion, insidious = idiopathic edema/epididymitis); rule out testicular torsion urgently (transverse lie, absent cremasteric reflex, blue infarction); explore within 4-8h golden window; manage other causes conservatively or surgically; blue dot sign = appendage torsion. ABBREV: USG=Ultrasonography; HSP=Henoch Schonlein Purpura.

whenToSuspect: WHAT IS CONSTIPATION: Decreased frequency of bowel motions (<3 per week); Passage of hard or large stools; Painful bowel motions with difficulty in pushing out. CONSTIPATION IN <1 YEAR OLDS: Not passing stools with abdominal distension; Associated vomiting; Absent or ectopic anal opening; Changes in infant formula, weaning, insufficient fluid intake. CONSTIPATION IN CHILD OLDER THAN 1 YEAR: Starts after a few weeks of life; Bottle fed or change of diet; Fissures, timing of potty/toilet training; Generally weight and height within normal limits; History of poor fibre diet and/or insufficient fluid intake.
risk: RED FLAG SIGNS: Constipation reported from birth or first few weeks of life; Failure to pass meconium/delay (more than 48 hours after birth in term baby); All abnormal location or calibre of anal opening; Ribbon stools (more likely in <1 year olds); Previously unknown/undiagnosed weakness in legs, locomotor delay, signs of hypothyroidism; Abdominal distension with vomiting. BRISTOL STOOL FORM SCALE: Type 1 (Separate hard lumps - Severe constipation); Type 2 (Lumpy and sausage like - Mild constipation); Type 3 (sausage shape with cracks - Normal); Type 4 (smooth soft sausage - Normal); Type 5 (Soft blobs - Lacking Fibre); Type 6 (Mushy - Mild diarrhea); Type 7 (Liquid - Severe diarrhea).
scores: Differentiate IDIOPATHIC CONSTIPATION (perineal normal, abdomen soft/fat/distension explained by age, normal spine, normal gait/tone/strength, reflexes present) vs PATHOLOGICAL DISEASE (abnormal anal appearance/position/patency, gross distension, abnormal spine - asymmetry/sacral agenesis/discoloured skin/naevi/sinus/hairy patch/lipoma/central pit, lower limb deformity like talipes/abnormal neuromuscular signs, abnormal reflexes). Red flags suggest organic cause (Hirschsprung's, hypothyroidism, spinal/neuromuscular).
investigations: Abdominal and rectal examination; Serum T3, T4, TSH; X-ray erect abdomen; X-ray spine AP and Lateral; Contrast enema; Anorectal manometry; Rectal biopsy. INDICATIONS FOR RECTAL BIOPSY: Persistent constipation, Contrast enema showing transitional zone, Absent ano-rectal reflex on manometry, Positive acetylcholinesterase fibers in rectal biopsy, Biopsy showing absent ganglion cells.
drugs: MEDICAL MANAGEMENT: Disimpaction of stools - manual or with retention enemas; Laxatives - Sodium picosulfate, Bisacodyl, Polyethylene glycol, Lactulose, Senna, Docusate sodium; Dietary modifications - proper weaning, no dilution of milk, reduce milk and increase roughage. PATHWAY: Constipation -> X-ray erect abdomen -> Fecaloma -> Stool softeners, Enema -> Not responding to stool softeners or enema -> Refer to higher center for contrast enema -> Contrast enema for persistent constipation -> Rectal biopsy. SURGICAL (if absent ganglion cells): Colostomy -> Definitive pullthrough surgery (Duhamel's, Scott Boley or Swensons pull through) OR single stage pullthrough in neonates and infants after adequate decompression.
management: MANAGEMENT: Proper toilet training; Adequate liquids and fibre in diet; Biofeedback; Laxatives; Suppositories; Evacuant enema; Surgical intervention. Disimpact then maintain with laxatives + dietary modification. If not responding to stool softeners/enema, refer to higher center for contrast enema; persistent constipation needs rectal biopsy. Hirschsprung's disease (absent ganglion cells) - colostomy then definitive pullthrough surgery or single stage pullthrough after decompression.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. RED FLAG SIGNS (suggest organic/pathological cause): constipation from birth/first weeks, failure to pass meconium/delay >48 hours, abnormal anal opening, ribbon stools, leg weakness/locomotor delay/hypothyroidism signs, abdominal distension with vomiting. Refer to higher center for contrast enema if not responding to stool softeners or enema. Indications for rectal biopsy to diagnose Hirschsprung's disease.
algorithmNotes: Diagnose constipation by frequency/hard stools (Bristol scale); screen for red flags suggesting organic cause; differentiate idiopathic vs pathological by exam; treat idiopathic with disimpaction + laxatives + diet + toilet training; investigate persistent cases (contrast enema, manometry, rectal biopsy) for Hirschsprung's; surgery (pullthrough) if aganglionosis. ABBREV: AP=Anteroposterior; TSH=Thyroid stimulating hormone.

whenToSuspect: WHAT IS IT: Condition wherein the processus vaginalis fails to obliterate after descent of the respective testis resulting in protrusion of bowel, omentum or other intra-abdominal contents into the inguinal canal or beyond. Occurs in 1-5% of new-borns, 10% of preterm new-borns. PRESENTATION: SWELLING - located in groin, labia, scrotum or inguino-scrotal, intermittent, reducible or irreducible, more prominent upon straining or crying; ABDOMINAL DISTENTION, OBSTIPATION OR VOMITING (bilious or non-bilious) when hernia is obstructed or incarcerated; CONSTITUTIONAL SYMPTOMS when hernia is incarcerated and bowel perforated. EXAMINATION: Swelling inguinal or inguinoscrotal (inguinolabial), reducible (with/without gurgling sound) or irreducible, cough impulse; Silk glove sign (palpable silky thickening of cord); Contralateral inguinal hernia (up to 20% may have synchronous).
risk: AGGRAVATING FACTORS: In males with bilateral inguinal hernia (especially if associated with umbilical hernia), lower urinary tract outflow obstruction must be ruled out; connective tissue disorders. CONTENT OF SWELLING: Usually only bowel and omentum, ovary (and/or fallopian tube) in females and testis in boys with associated cryptorchidism; torsion of gonad to be ruled out. DANGER SIGNS: Irreducibility of swelling in isolation or associated with - Irritable, inconsolable child; Distention of abdomen and obstipation; Bilious vomiting; Unilateral swollen erythematous labia (may suggest torsion of ovary); Peritonitis.
scores: INGUINAL HERNIA DECISION TREE: Uncomplicated - Lap repair of hernia along with Herniotomy. Complicated - Irreducible with No signs of peritonitis (Reduction + sedation; if successful Herniotomy after 24-48 hours; if not successful Urgent surgery) OR Irreducible with features of peritonitis (Stabilize followed by urgent surgery). Surgery approach by surgeon's comfort: Herniotomy by inguinal approach OR Laparotomy by lower transverse incision, reduction of contents & deep ring closure/tightening.
investigations: INVESTIGATION - PRE-ANAESTHESIA ASSESSMENT - ESSENTIAL: Hemogram, serum electrolytes, other blood investigations depending upon general condition of patient and co-morbidities as per anaesthetist. DESIRABLE: Ultrasonography & Karyotype (in all female inguinal hernias) to rule out complete androgen insensitivity syndrome.
drugs: TREATMENT OF CHOICE: Inguinal herniotomy or laparoscopic repair under general anaesthesia. Complicated hernias may need additional manoeuvres: simple reduction or laproscopic reduction for irreducible hernias, bowel repair/resection-anastomosis for vascular comprise of bowel. In female hernia, the sac should be opened and inspected for presence of fallopian tube which must be preserved. Recommended that surgery is carried out by a paediatric surgeon and that anaesthetist is experienced in paediatric and neonatal anaesthesia. Children with complicated hernia without peritonitis: should attempt reduction without sedation; with peritonitis: Insert NG and initiate reduction and refer to higher facility immediately.
management: MANDATORY FACILITIES IN THE CENTER: Term neonate or pre-term neonate (less than 60 weeks post-conception age) - dedicated Surgical NICU managed by pediatric surgeon or NICU managed by neonatologist; Older kids - round-the-clock paediatrician or paediatric surgeon for post-operative monitoring; The primary/community/district health centre should make the diagnosis, explain the danger signs to the parents and refer the patient to a higher centre with defined infrastructure. TIMING AND PLACE OF SURGERY: As early as possible but not a dire emergency; danger signs should be explained to the parents at the time of making the diagnosis; in inborn neonates diagnosed with inguinal hernia, surgery should preferably be performed prior to discharge. FOLLOW-UP: first follow-up after discharge should be with the operating surgeon; subsequent follow-up may be with the primary health centre close to residence subject to approval by operative surgeon.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. DANGER SIGNS: irreducibility (in isolation or with irritable inconsolable child, abdominal distention and obstipation, bilious vomiting, unilateral swollen erythematous labia suggesting ovarian torsion, peritonitis). Children with complicated hernia without peritonitis - attempt reduction without sedation; with peritonitis - insert NG, initiate reduction and refer to higher facility immediately. Surgery by paediatric surgeon with experienced anaesthetist.
algorithmNotes: Inguinal hernia from patent processus vaginalis; present as reducible groin/scrotal swelling with cough impulse; uncomplicated - elective herniotomy/lap repair; complicated (irreducible) - reduce +/- sedation then herniotomy 24-48h, urgent surgery if reduction fails or peritonitis; rule out ovarian torsion in girls, karyotype female hernias. ABBREV: NG=Nasogastric; NICU=Neonatal intensive care unit.

whenToSuspect: DEFINITION: Presence of pus or microorganism in the pleural fluid, observed on smear examination or on culture. Criteria for diagnosis in the absence of microorganism: (a) Pleural fluid pH< 7.0; (b) Lactic dehydrogenase (LDH) > 1000 IU/L; (c) Glucose <40 mg/dL; or (d) Lactate > 5 mol/L or 45 mg/dL. CLINICAL FEATURES - Symptoms: Fever, Chest pain, Cough, Respiratory distress, Abdominal pain and vomiting; Signs: Auscultation - decreased breath sounds & crackles, Dullness to percussion. Any child with diagnosed pneumonia with no improvement and spiking fever after 48 hours of antibiotic treatment may signal empyema.
risk: CAUSE OF EMPYEMA: Bacterial pneumonia (develop in 50-70% cases of complicated pneumonia); Tubercular empyema; Chest wall trauma; Lung Abscess/Aspiration; Common organisms - S pneumoniae, S aureus, and group A streptococci. PHASES OF EMPYEMA (treatment depends on stage): Exudative phase (1-3 days); Fibrino-purulent phase (4-14 days); Organising phase (>14 days).
scores: Management by phase/stage: Stage 1 (exudative) - ICD/Aspiration/Antibiotics; Stage 2 or 3 complex effusion - CECT & VATS, if thick peel thoracotomy. FIBRINOLYTICS IN STAGE II indications: within 2 weeks duration, preferably no ICD placed, imaging shows echogenic collection with septation, fluid analysis shows frank pus/exudative effusion. Contraindications: bleeding diathesis, suspected TB, hypersensitivity to fibrinolytic, complicated pneumonia/lung abscess, air leak on insertion of ICD.
investigations: INVESTIGATIONS - Blood: Blood counts, Blood Culture, ASO Titre, CRP; Sputum: Sputum AFB, Gram Staining and Culture; Radiological: Chest X-ray PA (Obliteration of Costophrenic angle or Meniscus sign - early signs of pleural effusion), USG Chest (amount of fluid & echogenicity, loculation and pleural thickening, guide for thoracocentesis or ICD placement), CT Chest (role in complicated cases and to detect underlying lung problems, HRCT ideal before surgery); Pleural fluid Analysis: Microbiology (Gram stain, Culture aerobic & anaerobic), Cytology (type of cells), Biochemistry (LDH, PH, Glucose).
drugs: TREATMENT: Child not sick (small effusion) - IV fluids, Oxygen, IV Antibiotics, Analgesics; Child looks sick (larger effusions & respiratory compromise) - IV Antibiotics and Intercostal Chest tube Drainage. Antibiotics: 3rd Gen Cephalosporins/Extended Penicillins like Co-Amoxiclav for 1-4 weeks; change based on cultures, continue until afebrile/ICD removed. ICD INSERTION: Adequate sized tubes under USG guidance preferably in mid axillary line through safe triangle under IV sedation; if USG not available needle aspiration, confirmation of pus and tube insertion; maximum fluid to be drained 10ml/kg in small children, 1.5 litres in older children; tube removed at complete clinical resolution or changed when blocked. FIBRINOLYTICS - Urokinase: twice daily for maximum three days (6 instillations), Age <1 year 10000 IU diluted in 10 mL NS, Age >1 year 40000 IU diluted in 40 mL NS, instilled through ICD and kept blocked for 30 minutes.
management: ALGORITHM: Suspected empyema - X ray Chest suggests significant effusion - Chest USG for quantifying effusion: No effusion - treat as pneumonia; Effusion present - Paediatric surgery consultation for ICD insertion or refer to higher center. Stage 1 phase - ICD/Aspiration/Antibiotics; Stage 2 or 3 complex effusion - CECT & VATS, if thick peel thoracotomy. ROLE OF SURGERY: Thoracoscopy if facilities available, if not thoracotomy; Indications - failure of ICD/antibiotics, persisting sepsis beyond 7 days of antibiotics, pleural collection despite chest tube drainage or complex/delayed empyema with loculation. THORACOSCOPY preferred in early empyema; THORACOTOMY/decortication for stage 3 and delayed cases (thick peel, thick pyogenic material, inability to develop pleural window, complex/chronic empyema, underlying diseased lung). MONITORING: resolution of fever/tachypnoea, daily USG & X-ray; ICD removed when drain output <10mL/kg/day and good lung expansion; discharged with antibiotic cover 1-2 weeks.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. Effusion present - Paediatric surgery consultation for ICD insertion or refer to higher center. FAILURE/INDICATION FOR SURGERY: persistence of collection on x-ray/ultrasound after 3 days, clinical/radiological worsening during therapy, failure of ICD/antibiotics, persisting sepsis beyond 7 days. Any child with pneumonia not improving and spiking fever after 48 hours of antibiotics may signal empyema.
algorithmNotes: Suspect empyema in pneumonia with persistent spiking fever; confirm with pleural fluid (pus/pH<7/LDH>1000/glucose<40); stage (exudative/fibrinopurulent/organising); treat by stage - antibiotics + ICD for early, fibrinolytics (urokinase) for stage II, VATS/thoracotomy + decortication for stage 3/thick peel; refer to paediatric surgery. ABBREV: ASO=Antistreptolysin O; CECT=Contrast-enhanced computed tomography; HRCT=High resolution computed tomography; ICD=Intercostal drain; LDH=Lactic dehydrogenase; NS=Normal saline; VATS=Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery.

whenToSuspect: WHAT IS CRYPTORCHIDISM: Absence of one or both testis in the scrotum. Cryptorchidism can be: True undescended testis (arrested along normal line of descent); Ectopic Testis (arrested outside line of normal descent). WHAT TO ASK: Testis are absent in scrotum since birth or present initially and later disappeared; Any history of torsion - redness/pain or bulge in the inguinal region/lower abdomen. WHAT TO SEE: Testis palpable anywhere along normal line of descent (superficial inguinal ring, inguinal canal, deep inguinal ring); Testis palpable outside the normal line of descent (pubic tubercle, perineum, thigh, opposite scrotum, penis); Testis not palpable (impalpable undescended testis); Associated anomalies (hernia, hydrocele, hypospadias, ambiguous genitalia, poorly developed ipsilateral scrotum, contralateral testicular hypertrophy); Rule out retractile testis (which does not require surgery - if testis manoeuvrable into scrotum and stays there by itself, needs regular follow up).
risk: RED FLAGS REQUIRING SPECIAL MANAGEMENT: Possibility of Disorders of Sexual Differentiation (DSD) to be considered if - Bilateral undescended testis with hypospadias, Unilateral undescended testis with severe hypospadias; Undescended testis with torsion (red painful lump in the undescended testis); Undescended testis with large inguinal hernia. Classify as Palpable, Impalpable testis, or Red Flag Signs (DSD/torsion/syndromic).
scores: Guiding Principle: Diagnosis made at birth and reconfirmed at 3 and 6 months. Further management if descent has not occurred. Surgery (orchiopexy) between 6 months-1 year (mostly at 1 year). Timing escalation by red flags: undescended testis with torsion needs immediate exploration; undescended testis with large inguinal hernia needs early surgery before waiting for 6 months due to risk of obstructed hernia.
investigations: ESSENTIAL INVESTIGATIONS: No investigation is essential for diagnosis or localisation of testis; routine blood and urine investigations required for anaesthetic fitness. OPTIONAL: Hormonal test (HCG stimulation test for bilateral undescended testis); MRI scan in cases suspected to be DSD; Diagnostic laparoscopy in impalpable UDT (can be combined with therapeutic procedure). SPECIAL SITUATIONS: DSD - hormonal assay, USG, genitogram, karyotyping; Syndromic child - genetic assessment, karyotyping, hormonal assays; Undescended testis with torsion - USG Doppler.
drugs: MANAGEMENT - Surgery (orchiopexy) between 6 months-1 year (mostly at 1 year). Palpable testis - open orchiopexy under general anaesthesia (may be done as day care procedure). Impalpable - Diagnostic laparoscopy: Absent testis (no intervention); Atrophic testis (orchidectomy); if vas and vessels going into the deep inguinal ring (inguinal exploration); intra-abdominal tests (single or two stage orchidopexy); Inguinal exploration if access to laparoscopy is not available. SPECIAL SITUATIONS: DSD - needs complete evaluation and treatment planning based on genotype, phenotype and psychological counselling; Undescended testis with torsion - needs immediate exploration and orchiopexy/orchidectomy; Undescended testis with large inguinal hernia - needs early surgery before waiting for 6 months due to the risk of obstructed hernia.
management: MANAGEMENT FLOWCHART: Undescended testis - Palpable (open orchiopexy at 1 year by Paediatric Surgeon); Impalpable testis (palpate under GA; if still impalpable laparoscopic/open orchiopexy); Red Flag Signs - DSD/torsion/syndromic (radiological, genetic and hormonal investigations, then proceed with orchiopexy/orchidectomy and other procedures accordingly). PHC/DISTRICT HOSPITAL: diagnose in newborn and reconfirm at 3 and 6 months; if uncomplicated counsel regarding timing of surgery and red flags, basic lab investigations for anaesthesia fitness, refer to centre with paediatric surgeon and anaesthesia facilities for surgery between 6m-1yr; assess for special situations (refer immediately if present); after surgery follow-up at 1 month, 3 month, 1 year and annually till puberty. TERTIARY CARE HOSPITAL: diagnose or confirm early, carry out open orchiopexy for palpable testis and laparoscopic exploration for impalpable testis under general anaesthesia at appropriate age, identify red flag situations, follow-up immediate and first week.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. RED FLAGS REQUIRING SPECIAL MANAGEMENT: DSD (bilateral UDT with hypospadias, unilateral UDT with severe hypospadias), undescended testis with torsion (immediate exploration), undescended testis with large inguinal hernia (early surgery before 6 months due to obstructed hernia risk). Refer to centre with paediatric surgeon and paediatric anaesthesia facilities. Rule out retractile testis (does not require surgery).
algorithmNotes: Diagnose cryptorchidism at birth, reconfirm at 3 and 6 months; rule out retractile testis; classify palpable vs impalpable vs red flags (DSD/torsion/syndromic); orchiopexy at 6m-1yr (open for palpable, laparoscopic exploration for impalpable); special situations need genetic/hormonal workup; follow up till puberty for position and fertility. ABBREV: UDT=Undescended testes; DSD=Disorders of sexual differentiation; FU=Follow up; GA=General anaesthesia.
