whenToSuspect: Acute aortic syndrome (AAS) is a term used to describe a constellation of life-threatening aortic diseases that have similar presentation but appear to have distinct demographic, clinical, pathological and survival characteristics. THREE TYPES: Aortic Dissection (AD) - commonest and most lethal (tear in the aortic intima and blood enters into the media separating the layers, formation of entrance tear); Intramural Hematoma (IMH) - hematoma within the medial layer of the aortic wall without intimal injury (rupture of vasa vasorum); Penetrating Aortic Ulcer (PAU) - ulcer-like lesion in the intima eroding through the media of the aortic wall and forming localized hematoma (ulceration of atherosclerotic plaque). CLINICAL PRESENTATION: Abrupt onset of severe chest and/or back pain is the most typical feature; pain is sharp, ripping, tearing, knife-like; anterior chest pain more common in Type A, back/abdominal pain more common with Type B; pain may migrate following the dissection path; other manifestations - shock, syncope, neurological deficit including stroke and paraplegia, acute congestive heart failure, myocardial ischemia, lower extremity ischemia, renal and visceral ischemia, sudden death.
risk: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: Blood enters through the intimal tear and separates intima from media/adventitia creating a false lumen; dissection can proceed antegrade or retrograde. Two factors primarily responsible: higher wall stress due to hypertension or dilatation of aorta; inherently weak/degenerated aortic media. PREDISPOSING FACTORS: commonest is hypertension; genetically mediated aortopathies (Marfan's syndrome, Vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, Bicuspid aortic valve, Familial aortic dissection); trauma and iatrogenic injury. CLASSIFICATION - Chronological: Acute (within 14 days), Subacute (15-90 days), Chronic (>90 days). Anatomical (Stanford, most common): Type A (dissection involves intrapericardial aorta regardless of site of intimal tear, also Proximal dissection); Type B (does not involve intrapericardial aorta, also Distal dissection).
scores: Stanford classification (Type A vs Type B) determines management: Type A is a surgical emergency; Type B mostly medical unless complicated. TYPE B DISSECTION REQUIRING INTERVENTION (complications): Unremitting pain, Persistent uncontrolled hypertension, Hemodynamic instability, Rupture/Impending rupture/Leak, Rapid Aortic Expansion, Malperfusion, Retrograde Ascending aortic dissection, Aneurysmal dilatation (>5.5 cm). Targets: Systolic BP 100-120 mm Hg; Mean arterial pressure 70 mm Hg in significant aortic regurgitation; Heart Rate 60-70 beats/min; adequate pain control.
investigations: IMAGING AND DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM: Combination of ECG, Chest X-ray, Echocardiography and ECG gated CT angiography. D-dimers in stable patient with low suspicion (normal levels virtually rule out dissection, but IMH and PAU may still be present; in dissection D-dimers very high). DIAGNOSTIC METHODS AND OUTPUT: Chest X-ray (widened mediastinum, pleural collection); CT angiography (confirmation of diagnosis, anatomical extent, localization of entry/re-entry tears, aortic rupture, state of arch vessels/coronary/visceral/renal/ilio-femoral arteries, state of lungs and kidneys); Transthoracic Echocardiography (aortic valve, pericardial effusion, tamponade, myocardial function, mitral/tricuspid valve); ECG (myocardial ischemia); Vascular Ultrasound (state of axillary, carotid and femoral arteries). MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION VS ACUTE TYPE A DISSECTION: if dissection diagnosed with myocardial ischemia it should be managed on the line of dissection and thrombolytic therapy should NOT be initiated.
drugs: INITIAL MEDICAL THERAPY: directed at pain control and limiting propagation by reducing aortic wall stress (velocity of ventricular contraction dP/dt, heart rate, blood pressure). Beta blockers control these 3 parameters; reasonable targets heart rate nearly 60/minute and systolic BP 100-120 mm Hg; in severe aortic regurgitation maintain mean arterial pressure 70-80 mm Hg and target heart rate near 80/minute. ANTIHYPERTENSIVE DRUGS: BETA-BLOCKERS (one to be used) - Labetalol (Loading 20 mg IV in 2 minutes, Maintenance 1-2 mg/minute); Esmolol (Loading 250-500 microg/kg in one minute, Maintenance 50-100 microg/kg/min max 300 microg/kg/min); Metoprolol (Loading 5mg IV over 2min up to 3 doses, Maintenance 3-5 mg every hour). CALCIUM CHANNEL BLOCKERS (in addition to beta blocker) - Diltiazem (Loading 0.25 mg/kg over 2-5 min, Maintenance 5mg/h IV infusion); Nicardipine (2.5-15 mg/hour IV infusion). VASODILATOR (in addition to beta blocker) - Nitroprusside (0.3-0.5 microg/kg/min, max 10 microg/kg/min). PAIN RELIEF: IV Morphine 2-10 mg or Fentanyl 10-50 mcg. FLOW CHART: start beta blocker; if targets not achieved and both SBP and HR high add Calcium Channel blocker; if HR controlled but SBP high add Vasodilator (Nitroprusside).
management: TREATMENT OF ACUTE DISSECTION: Acute Type A dissection is a surgical emergency; Acute Type B mostly medical unless complicated; irrespective of surgical/endovascular intervention, medical therapy to control blood pressure and pain is essential. Dissection patients need management at specialized centers - after initiation of medical therapy refer to specialized centre as soon as possible. SURGERY FOR ACUTE TYPE A: life-saving; Type A dissection itself is an indication for surgery (exceptions: extremely old/moribund with comorbidities, deeply comatose, advanced mesenteric ischemia/gangrene/sepsis); primary objective replacing the ascending aorta and excision of primary tear; other objectives correction of aortic regurgitation, treatment of coronary ostial dissection, restoration of distal true lumen and correction of malperfusion. MANAGEMENT OF TYPE B: gold standard optimal medical therapy (control of BP and pain); surgery/endovascular repair for complicated/high risk; Thoracic Endo-vascular Aortic Repair (TEVAR) aims at covering primary intimal tear, expansion of true lumen, thrombosis/remodeling of false lumen, correction of malperfusion. MANAGEMENT OF IMH AND PAU: diagnosis is radiological and management similar to acute aortic dissection.
referralRedFlags: HIGH INDEX OF SUSPICION AT THE EARLIEST IS LIFE SAVING. If Acute Aortic Syndrome is suspected kindly refer the patient to higher center. Dissection patients need management at specialized centers - after initiation of medical therapy refer as soon as possible. Type A dissection is a surgical emergency. If dissection diagnosed with myocardial ischemia, manage on line of dissection and do NOT initiate thrombolytic therapy. Type B requiring intervention: unremitting pain, uncontrolled hypertension, hemodynamic instability, rupture/leak, rapid expansion, malperfusion, retrograde ascending dissection, aneurysmal dilatation >5.5cm.
algorithmNotes: Suspect AAS with abrupt severe tearing chest/back pain; confirm with CT angiography (+ECG/echo/CXR); classify Stanford Type A (surgical emergency) vs Type B (medical unless complicated); start beta-blocker to lower HR/BP (add CCB/vasodilator), pain control; Type A surgery, Type B medical/TEVAR; refer to specialized centre. ABBREV: AAS=Acute Aortic Syndrome; AD=Aortic Dissection; CT=Computed Tomography; FL=False Lumen; IMH=Intramural Hematoma; JVP=Jugular Venous Pressure; PAU=Penetrating Aortic Ulcer; TL=True Lumen; TEVAR=Thoracic Endo-vascular Aortic Repair.

whenToSuspect: WHAT IS ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA: A sudden decrease in limb perfusion that threatens limb viability and requires urgent evaluation and management. SYMPTOMS: Pain at rest, paresthesia, muscle weakness/paralysis of the affected limb. SIGNS: Absence of pulses distal to the occlusion, cool and pale or mottled skin, reduced sensation and decreased strength. REMEMBER THE FIVE P's (P5): Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, and Paralysis (the last two may or may not be present depending upon extent and duration of vascular occlusion; in advanced cases there may be frank gangrene).
risk: COMMON CAUSES OF LIMB ISCHEMIA - NON-TRAUMATIC: Embolic (embolism from the heart or atherosclerotic aorta; embolus may arise from heart in case of atrial fibrillation, acute myocardial infarction, mitral stenosis, left ventricular dysfunction, left atrial myxoma, prosthetic heart valves or endocarditis); Thrombosis (acute thrombosis of a limb artery at the site of a pre-existing atherosclerotic plaque). TRAUMATIC: Blunt injury (traction injury or contusion); Sharp or penetrating injury. EMBOLISM VS THROMBOSIS: history of cardiac disorder predicts acute embolism; history of previous claudication suggests acute thrombosis in pre-existing atherosclerotic lesion; Doppler differentiates hypoechoic thrombus in normal vessel (embolism) vs heterogeneous echogenic plaque with calcification (thrombosis).
scores: STAGES OF ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA (Rutherford): Stage I (limb viable, not immediately threatened - no sensory loss, no muscle weakness, arterial and venous Doppler audible); Stage IIa (marginally threatened, salvageable if promptly treated - minimal/no sensory loss in toes, no muscle weakness, arterial and venous Doppler audible); Stage IIb (immediately threatened, salvageable with immediate revascularization - sensory loss more than toes with rest pain, mild/moderate muscle weakness, arterial Doppler usually inaudible, venous audible); Stage III (irreversibly damaged, major tissue loss or permanent nerve damage inevitable - profound anaesthetic sensory loss, profound paralysis/rigor, arterial and venous Doppler inaudible).
investigations: HISTORY: Duration of symptoms, H/o intermittent claudication, H/o cardiac disorder (palpitations, chest pain, shortness of breath, loss of consciousness), H/o Diabetes/Hypertension/Smoking/Drugs (OCPs, HRT), long standing fever, H/o trauma/surgical or endovascular interventions. PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: Blood Pressure, all peripheral pulses, condition of limb (temperature, colour, hair loss, nail bed capillary filling, sensation and motor power, gangrene/pre-gangrene), cardiac murmurs, wound inspection, bone/nerve injury in traumatic ischemia. DUPLEX ULTRASOUND/DOPPLER for arteries and veins. Radiological imaging (CT angiography, MR angiography, catheter angiography) for thrombosis.
drugs: MANAGEMENT OF NON-TRAUMATIC ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA: Injection Heparin 100 units/Kg Intravenous. EMBOLISM: Stage I, IIa, IIb - Surgical Embolectomy/Catheter directed Thrombolysis (send retrieved thrombus for microscopic/microbiological/histopathological examination), then investigate cause of embolism (cardiac/aortic) and manage; Stage III - Amputation. THROMBOSIS: Stage I, IIa, IIb - Thrombolysis/Heparinization, then radiological imaging (CT/MR/catheter angiography), then Surgical/Endovascular Revascularization; Stage III - Amputation. MANAGEMENT OF TRAUMATIC ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA - Sharp/penetrating injury: control bleeding by pressure bandage, resuscitation, vascular repair (Stage I/IIa/IIb), amputation (Stage III), manage concomitant bone/nerve injury simultaneously, supportive treatment/pain relief/antibiotic prophylaxis/tetanus prophylaxis. Blunt injury: vascular repair (Stage I/IIa/IIb), amputation (Stage III), manage bone/nerve injury, supportive treatment. CONTRAINDICATIONS TO THROMBOLYSIS: established cerebrovascular event (excluding TIA within previous 2 months), active bleeding diathesis, recent gastrointestinal bleeding (within previous 10 days), neurosurgery (intracranial/spinal) within previous 3 months, intracranial trauma within previous 3 months.
management: Evaluate (history, examination, Doppler) and stage (Rutherford I-III). Heparinize non-traumatic ALI. Differentiate embolism (surgical embolectomy/catheter thrombolysis + investigate cardiac/aortic cause) vs thrombosis (thrombolysis/heparinization + imaging + surgical/endovascular revascularization); Stage III - amputation. Traumatic ALI - control bleeding, resuscitate, vascular repair (or amputation Stage III), manage bone/nerve injury, supportive care. POST REVASCULARIZATION: Compartment syndrome (can occur in Stage II due to revascularization edema - severe pain/hypoesthesia/weakness, myoglobinuria and elevation of creatine kinase; four-compartment fasciotomy when even an iota of doubt); Reperfusion injury (hyperkalemia, acidosis, myoglobinuria, cardiopulmonary depression; require monitoring, proper hydration).
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. Acute limb ischemia threatens limb viability and requires urgent evaluation and management. Stage IIb (immediately threatened) needs immediate revascularization; Stage III (irreversibly damaged) needs amputation. Compartment syndrome - four-compartment fasciotomy when there is even an iota of doubt. Reperfusion injury requires monitoring and hydration.
algorithmNotes: Recognize ALI by 5 Ps (pain, pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, paralysis); distinguish embolism vs thrombosis (history + Doppler); stage by Rutherford; heparinize; embolism - embolectomy/thrombolysis, thrombosis - thrombolysis + revascularization; Stage III - amputation; watch for compartment syndrome (fasciotomy) and reperfusion injury. ABBREV: CT=Computed Tomography; HRT=Hormone Replacement Therapy; OCPs=Oral Contraceptive Pills; TIA=Transient Ischaemic Attack.

whenToSuspect: MECHANISM AND SCENARIO: Body acceleration and deceleration (Road traffic injury); Body compression (Crush injuries, fall from height); Penetrating injuries (Missile injuries, stabs). PRESENTATION: Pain over the chest, Shortness of breath, Cyanosis, Swelling of chest wall, Facial/limb puffiness, Unconsciousness, Associated blood loss/pallor. ABCDE primary survey: Airway maintenance with cervical spine control, Breathing and ventilation, Circulation with haemorrhage control, Disability (brief neurological assessment), Exposure/Environmental control (prevent hypothermia). ALSO LOOK FOR: Neurological deficits, Tenderness over chest, first rib fracture, Subcutaneous emphysema, Altered/Muffled Heart Sounds.
risk: IMMEDIATE LIFE THREATENING CHEST INJURIES (LETHAL SIX): Change in voice/airway obstruction; Tension Pneumothorax (absent breath sounds, hypotension, hyper-resonance); Massive Hemothorax (absent breath sounds, hypotension, dull note); Open pneumothorax (chest wall defect); Tracheobronchial injury (massive air leak on ICD/emphysema); Cardiac tamponade (muffled heart sounds, hypotension, FAST pericardial fluid). POTENTIALLY LIFE THREATENING CHEST INJURIES (HIDDEN SEVEN): Oesophageal injury, Flail Chest, Pulmonary Contusion, Ruptured Thoracic Aorta, Cardiac Contusion, Simple Pneumothorax, Ruptured Diaphragm.
scores: Primary survey (ABCDE) aims at recognition and management of immediate life-threatening injuries based on clinical examination. Triage into immediate life-threatening (lethal six) requiring instant intervention vs potentially life-threatening (hidden seven) found on secondary survey. Respiratory distress, tachypnoea, low SpO2 trigger emergent evaluation.
investigations: ADJUNCTS: Monitors (Vitals) SpO2/ABG/Hb%, NGT, Urinary Catheter, CXR, X-ray Pelvis, FAST, DPL; CT Scan, MRI Special Studies. ADJUNCT TO PRIMARY SURVEY: Obtain a portable AP chest x-ray to assess intra-thoracic injuries, endotracheal tube placement (if intubated), post chest tube insertion. Ruptured thoracic aorta - suspected on Chest x-ray, confirmed on CT angiography chest. Cardiac contusion - ECG, Echocardiograph and troponin levels. Pulmonary contusion - diagnosed on Chest x-ray. Oesophageal injury - food particles in ICD drainage or leak of dye in pleural cavity.
drugs: TREATMENT OF LETHAL SIX: Airway obstruction - removal of foreign body if any, definitive airway (endotracheal intubation or surgical airway). Tension Pneumothorax - immediate decompression with wide bore needle in 4th/5th intercostal space in anterior axillary line followed by chest tube insertion. Massive Hemothorax - fluid resuscitation to correct haemorrhagic shock, insert chest tube. Open pneumothorax - cover the defect with sterile dressing and secure from 3 sides followed by chest tube (size >=28Fr) insertion and secure dressing on all 4 sides. Tracheobronchial injury - ICD + Surgery. Cardiac tamponade - surgical drainage or pericardiocentesis. HIDDEN SEVEN: Oesophageal injury - Surgery. Flail Chest - pain control, oxygenation (consider endotracheal intubation), chest tube insertion if pneumothorax/hemothorax, consider transfer. Pulmonary Contusion - adequate analgesia, humidified oxygenation, consider endotracheal intubation, transfer. Ruptured Thoracic Aorta - stenting/open surgery. Cardiac Contusion - supportive treatment, transfer. Simple Pneumothorax - chest tube insertion (>28Fr) in 4th/5th intercostal space just anterior to midaxillary line. Ruptured Diaphragm - Surgery.
management: INITIAL ASSESSMENT & MANAGEMENT OF A TRAUMA PATIENT: Primary Survey ABCDE -> immediate life threatening injuries -> resuscitation -> re-evaluation -> detailed secondary survey -> potentially life threatening injuries -> re-evaluation -> definitive care. Manage lethal six immediately (airway, tension pneumothorax decompression, hemothorax chest tube, open pneumothorax 3-sided dressing + chest tube, tracheobronchial injury ICD+surgery, cardiac tamponade pericardiocentesis/drainage). Identify and manage hidden seven on secondary survey, consider transfer to closest appropriate facility for flail chest/pulmonary contusion/cardiac contusion.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. Immediate life-threatening chest injuries (lethal six) require instant intervention. Respiratory distress, tachypnoea, low SpO2 are red flags. Consider transfer to closest appropriate facility for flail chest, pulmonary contusion, cardiac contusion, oesophageal injury. First rib fracture, subcutaneous emphysema and muffled heart sounds warrant careful evaluation.
algorithmNotes: Manage chest trauma by ATLS primary survey (ABCDE); identify and treat lethal six immediately (tension pneumothorax, massive hemothorax, open pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, airway obstruction, tracheobronchial injury); detect hidden seven on secondary survey; portable CXR and FAST as adjuncts; transfer for definitive care. ABBREV: ABG=Arterial Blood Gas; CT=Computed Tomography; CXR=Chest Radiography; DPL=Diagnostic Peritoneal Lavage; ECG=Electrocardiogram; FAST=Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma; ICD=Intercostal Drainage Tube; MRI=Magnetic Resonance Imaging; NGT=Nasogastric Tube.

whenToSuspect: CLLI is a manifestation of objectively proven Peripheral Arterial Disease (PAD) defined by ankle brachial index of <0.90, due to chronic (>2 week) inadequate tissue perfusion. PRESENTATION: Claudication or pain at rest, paresthesia, with or without tissue loss, impaired healing or infection (ulcer or gangrene); Absent/weak pulse depending on level of occlusion. CLINICAL STAGING: a) Asymptomatic; b) Mild claudication (No life-style limitation); c) Moderate or severe claudication (Life style limiting); d) Chronic severe (or critical) limb ischemia (Compromised blood flow causing limb pain at rest +/- ulcers or gangrene).
risk: COMMON CAUSES OF CLLI: Atherosclerosis (Elderly, smokers with diabetes mellitus or chronic renal insufficiency); Vasculitis (Buerger's disease, Takayasu arteritis); Aortic coarctation; Delayed presentation of an Acute Ischemic Insult (Trauma, Thromboembolism, Dissection). DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS OF CLLI (differentiating test): Spinal stenosis/root compression (ABI, Doppler); Arthritis (ABI, Doppler, X-Ray); Venous Claudication (ABI, Doppler); Compartment Syndrome (ABI, Doppler, compartment pressure). Risk factors: Diabetes, hypertension, smoking, ischemic heart disease, family history, dyslipidaemia.
scores: ANKLE BRACHIAL INDEX (ABI): ratio of Blood Pressure in ankle and arm (resting ABI is initial diagnostic test). Interpretation: 0.90 to 1.40 is normal; <0.90 abnormal indicates presence of PAD; 0.41 to 0.90 indicates mild to moderate PAD; <0.40 indicates severe PAD; >1.40 indicates abnormal (calcified arteries). REVASCULARISATION PREREQUISITES: good distal vessels (run-off), able to walk before critical limb ischemia, life expectancy of >1 year, satisfactory general condition.
investigations: EXAMINATION: Blood Pressure (including ankle-brachial index, toe-brachial index in diabetes/elderly/renal insufficiency), all peripheral pulses, condition of limb (temperature, colour, hair loss, atrophy, nail bed capillary filling, sensation and motor power, gangrene/pre-gangrene), wound inspection, comorbidity evaluation (CVS, Renal, Diabetes mellitus, CNS, neuropathy), evaluation for possible venous conduits. Ankle Brachial Index (initial test); Duplex Ultrasound/Doppler to confirm obstruction and localization; CT Angiography (CTA)/MRA/DSA for anatomical details (if conservative treatment fails and intervention planned, or for critical limb ischemia).
drugs: MEDICAL MANAGEMENT: Antiplatelets - Aspirin 75-100mg orally/Clopidogrel 75mg orally OD; Analgesic - Paracetamol + Opioid. CIRCULATORY MODULATORS: Cilostazol 100mg orally (contraindicated in CHF, unstable Angina, Recent MI, Tachyarrythmias); Naftidrofuryl 200mg orally TDS; Pentoxifylline 400mg orally TDS (contraindicated in recent cerebral/retinal haemorrhage, intolerance of methyl xanthines). RISK FACTOR MANAGEMENT: Lifestyle modification (graded exercise), Control of HTN (BP <140/90), Control of Diabetes Mellitus (HbA1c <7.0), Low fat diet/exercise, Atherosclerosis - start statins/antiplatelets. EXERCISE REHABILITATION FOR CLAUDICATION: graded and supervised walking three times a week, beginning with 30mins and increasing to 1 hour per session, at an intensity that induces claudication within 3-5 mins. LOCAL WOUND MANAGEMENT: prevention/treatment of infection, leg dependency, off-loading, non-adherent dressing, abscess drainage, debridement, digital amputation. AMPUTATION: non-salvageable limbs, fixed contractures, severe infected/necrosis, failed revascularisation with persistent tissue loss.
management: Diagnostic evaluation: history/examination -> suspected CLLI -> Ankle Brachial Index (>0.9 ruled out; <0.9 Duplex Ultrasound/Doppler + risk factor assessment + comorbid evaluation). MANAGEMENT OF INTERMITTENT CLAUDICATION: Mild claudication no lifestyle limitation - antiplatelet, exercise training, risk factor modification, annual follow up; Moderate/severe claudication lifestyle limitation - antiplatelet, exercise training, Cilostazol/Naftidrofuryl/Pentoxifylline, risk factor modification, follow-up at 1,3,6 months and annually; if no improvement/worsening - CTA/MRA/DSA then revascularization (Endovascular/Surgery). MANAGEMENT OF CRITICAL LOWER LIMB ISCHEMIA: CTA/MRA/DSA for anatomy, evaluation of general condition/comorbidities, antiplatelet therapy, limb evaluation, pain relief +/- antibiotics, Cilostazol/Naftidrofuryl/Pentoxifylline, risk factor management; if candidate for revascularization (discrete disease, good distal vessels, satisfactory condition) - revascularization + antiplatelet + risk management + follow-up; if not candidate (diffuse disease poor distal vessels, poor general condition) - stable pain/lesion medical treatment, not-tolerable pain/spreading infection amputation + prosthesis/rehabilitation.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. Critical Lower Limb Ischemia (rest pain, ulcers, gangrene) requires urgent evaluation and revascularization assessment. Amputation for non-salvageable limbs, fixed contractures, severe infection/necrosis, failed revascularization with persistent tissue loss. Revascularization prerequisites: good distal run-off, ability to walk, life expectancy >1 year, satisfactory condition.
algorithmNotes: Diagnose CLLI/PAD by ABI <0.90; stage (asymptomatic/mild/moderate-severe claudication/critical limb ischemia); manage claudication with antiplatelet + exercise + cilostazol + risk factors, escalate to revascularization if failing; critical limb ischemia needs imaging + revascularization or amputation; aggressive risk factor and wound management. ABBREV: ABI=Ankle Brachial Index; CTA=Computed Tomography Angiography; DSA=Digital Subtraction Angiography; MRA=Magnetic Resonance Angiography; PAD=Peripheral Arterial Disease.

condition: Surgical Management of Coronary Artery Disease
whenToSuspect: For the medical management of Acute Coronary Syndrome and Chronic Stable angina please refer to respective STWs. HEART TEAM APPROACH: Decision to operate is a joint decision of the heart team consisting of a Cardiac Surgeon, Interventional Cardiologist, attending Cardiologist or primary physician and the patient. Consider definitive survival benefits of CABG before making a final decision based on: clinical presentation, morphology and extent of coronary artery lesions, condition of left ventricle (ejection fraction, associated mitral regurgitation, left ventricular aneurysm), associated comorbidities.
risk: SURGICAL REVASCULARIZATION IN ACUTE CORONARY SYNDROME (CABG): CABG IN NSTE-ACS (preferred over PCI in left main disease, multivessel CAD and diabetics; timing after medical stabilization and/or IABP support followed by early CABG); CABG IN STEMI ACUTE (preferred over PCI in left main, multivessel CAD, diabetics; mechanical complications emergency surgery; coronary anatomy unsuitable for PCI/failed PCI; timing preferably wait for one week unless hemodynamic instability or mechanical complications); CABG IN EVOLVED STEMI DELAYED (choice depends on coronary artery anatomy, LV function, myocardial viability). TIMING OF CABG: Chronic stable angina - Elective; NSTE-ACS - early CABG (<48 hrs) even in higher-risk patients; STEMI - in absence of persistent pain or haemodynamic instability surgery after 7 days.
scores: DEFINITIVE SURVIVAL BENEFIT OF CABG (in following subsets): Left main stenosis greater than 50%; Three vessel disease with diabetes/LV dysfunction; Two vessel disease with critical proximal LAD disease; Two vessel disease without proximal LAD (with severe ischemic burden); Single vessel disease with critical proximal/ostial LAD disease. REVASCULARIZATION IN CAD WITH HEART FAILURE: CABG preferred over PCI in congestive heart failure; prior myocardial viability assessment mandatory; surgical ventricular restoration for LV aneurysm and concomitant CABG with/without mitral valve repair.
investigations: PREOPERATIVE ASSESSMENT - Pre operative requisites: ECG, Echocardiography, +/- Stress testing, Catheter coronary angiography, +/- Viability assessment, Evaluation for conduits. EVALUATION FOR CO-MORBIDITIES: Medical conditions (diabetes, hypertension, obesity), Carotid Doppler if age >60 yrs or h/o TIA/stroke, Neurological evaluation, USG for renal arteries/abdominal aorta/ilio femoral arteries in clinically relevant cases, Pulmonary function tests, RFT/LFT/lipid profile/TSH, Screening for viral and bacterial infections.
drugs: PRE-OPERATIVE MEDICATION: Beta-blockers should not be discontinued (to avoid acute ischemia); Statins continued till day of surgery or initiated if not previously started; Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors and similar drugs might be discontinued 1-2 days prior to surgery; Before elective surgery discontinue Aspirin 48 hours and other anti-platelet drugs at least 5 days before surgery; add LMW heparin in high thrombogenic conditions (atrial fibrillation, recent coronary/renal stenting, prosthetic heart valve in-situ, limb ischaemia, left ventricular clot). LONG TERM POST-OP (GO WITH THE GUIDELINES GOAL): Antiplatelets - Single-antiplatelet Aspirin 75 to 325mg/day indefinitely; Dual Antiplatelets - Aspirin plus Clopidogrel/Ticagrelor (one year for OPCAB, 6 months for on-pump CABG operated during ACS), thereafter single antiplatelet indefinitely; Beta Blockers indefinitely for all; ACE inhibitors/ARB/ARNI indefinitely for post-MI/CHF/diabetes/hypertension/CKD/LV dysfunction/PVD; Lipid reduction (statins target LDL <70 mg/dl); Diabetes (HbA1c <7%); Hypertension (BP <140/85 most, <130/85 CHF/renal failure, <130/80 diabetes); Smoking complete cessation; Physical Activity 30 min 5 times/week; Weight (BMI 18.5-24.9).
management: CORONARY ARTERY BYPASS GRAFTING: On-pump CABG (with Cardiopulmonary bypass) vs Off-pump CABG (beating heart surgery without CPB). CONDUITS USED: LIMA to LAD is the standard of care with proven survival benefit (if LIMA unsuitable use RIMA); additional conduits - Saphenous vein, RIMA, Radial artery. Heart team decides CABG vs PCI based on lesion morphology/extent, LV function, comorbidities and survival benefit. CABG timing by presentation (elective for stable angina, early <48h for NSTE-ACS, after 7 days for STEMI unless unstable). POSTOPERATIVE COMPLICATIONS to monitor: Bleeding, Peri-operative MI, Rhythm disorders, Stroke, Acute kidney injury, Post-pericardiotomy syndrome, Pericardial collection, Pneumothorax, Sternal wound infection, Conduit harvest site infection, Urinary tract infection, Pulmonary infection, Septicaemia.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. Decision to operate is a heart team decision (Cardiac Surgeon, Interventional Cardiologist, Cardiologist/primary physician, patient). Consider definitive survival benefits of CABG before final decision. STEMI mechanical complications need emergency surgery. CABG preferred over PCI in left main disease, multivessel CAD, diabetics, and CAD with heart failure. Monitor for postoperative complications.
algorithmNotes: Decide CABG via heart team for survival-benefit subsets (left main >50%, 3-vessel with diabetes/LV dysfunction, proximal LAD disease); time by presentation (elective/early NSTE-ACS/delayed STEMI); LIMA-to-LAD standard conduit; manage pre-op meds (continue beta-blocker/statin, stop aspirin 48h); long-term guideline-directed secondary prevention; monitor postoperative complications. ABBREV: ACS=Acute Coronary Syndrome; ARB=Angiotensin Receptor Blockers; ARNI=Angiotensin Receptor Neprilysin Inhibitor; CABG=Coronary Artery Bypass Graft; CAD=Coronary Artery Disease; CHF=Congestive Heart Failure; CPB=Cardiopulmonary Bypass; IABP=Intra-aortic balloon pump; LAD=Left Anterior Descending Artery; LIMA=Left Internal Mammary Artery; OPCAB=Off-Pump Coronary Artery Bypass Surgery; PCI=Percutaneous coronary intervention; RIMA=Right Internal Mammary Artery; STEMI=ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction.

condition: Image Guided Drainage of Intra Abdominal Abscess
whenToSuspect: Signs and symptoms: Pain; Fever; Weight loss; Anorexia; Local tenderness; Organomegaly. When to suspect: Patient having unexplained fever especially with chills and rigors; Local pain - Right hypochondrium (liver abscess), left hypochondrium (splenic abscess), pelvis (post operative status); Post operative patient developing fever and increased leucocyte count. An abscess forms like a round to irregular collection within the liver parenchyma or other abdominal organs/peritoneal cavity. A: Liver abscess; B: Subdiaphragmatic abscess; C: Peri renal abscess; D: Peripancreatic abscess; E: Splenic abscess; F: Paracolic abscess; G: Right iliac fossa/periappendiceal collection.
risk: Treatment of an abscess depends on its: Location; Size; Degree of clinical symptoms (patient with septicaemia {tachycardia, hypotension} should be treated aggressively whereas a stable patient can be discharged on oral medical treatment). LIVER ABSCESS: Right upper quadrant pain; May present with pleuritic right sided chest pain; Tender hepatomegaly on examination. SPLENIC ABSCESS: Left upper quadrant pain; Tender splenomegaly on examination. PERIPANCREATIC ABSCESS: Upper abdominal pain; Patient usually has underlying acute/acute exacerbation of chronic pancreatitis. PELVIC ABSCESS: Deep seated pelvic abscess; Common in post operative patients after bowel/gynaecological surgeries; Tender lower abdomen/signs of peritonitis on examination.
scores: RED FLAG SIGNS (Liver abscess): Left lobe abscess; Segment VIII abscess - can rupture into pleura; Superficially located abscess; Abscess volume >100 ml. RED FLAG SIGN (Peripancreatic): May deteriorate rapidly if splenic vein thrombosis occurs. Consider image guided drainage if the patient has the following despite medical treatment: Persistent leucocytosis; Signs of septicaemia (tachycardia, hypotension); Impending signs of abscess rupture on imaging. Abscess volume >100 ml, superficially located liver abscess at risk of rupture into pericardium/peritoneum.
investigations: HEMATOLOGICAL - Essential: CBC, CRP, ESR. Desirable: LFTs, HIV serology, HBs Ag. IMAGING - Essential: USG Abdomen. Desirable: Contrast enhanced CT study of the abdomen. Ultrasound image showing a liver abscess as a well-defined round area appearing less bright than the surrounding liver. CT scan (coronal/axial) showing intrasplenic abscess, peripancreatic collection, or multiple collections in post cholecystectomy patient.
drugs: Medical management: Send the aspirate for microbiological analysis but don't defer treatment for the result of the same. Drug dosages: Inj Metronidazole 500mg IV 6-8 hourly plus Inj Ceftriaxone 2gm IV OD for 10-14 days (for liver and splenic abscess). For pancreatic and pelvic/lower abdominal abscesses: Meropenem 1-2gm IV 8 hourly plus Levofloxacin 500-750mg IV daily and Ofloxacin 200mg for 5-7 days; Cefoperazone 1000-2000mg plus Sulbactam 500-1000mg (as a combination) IV BD for 5-7 days.
management: Surgical management: To be done in cases of ruptured/impending rupture into the pericardium, peritoneal cavity and pleural cavity. Options include: Laparotomy; Chest tube placement/Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS). IMAGE GUIDED DRAINAGE: Involves Ultrasound/CT guided placement of catheter in the abscess cavity. INDICATIONS: Patient developing tachycardia and hypotension; Persistent leucocytosis; Impending signs of rupture of abscess into adjacent cavity (pleural/peritoneal/pericardial). CONTRAINDICATIONS: Uncorrectable coagulopathy; Vital structures in the approach path (large vessel, bowel). Days of required hospitalisation: 2-5 days. Expected outcome: Relief in pain and tenderness within 6-8 hrs; Resolution of fever within 24 hours. Associated adverse events: Vasovagal syncope (sweating, hypotension, bradycardia, loss of consciousness). How to prevent vasovagal syncope: Reassure patient; Place a large bore (18G/20G) IV cannula in the arm before the procedure; Keep normal saline infusion bag ready; Atropine IV to be available for use. How to treat vasovagal syncope: Raise the legs to prevent peripheral venous pooling; Inject Atropine 0.6mg IV if heart rate is <60/min (usually responds, otherwise same dose can be repeated after 5 min). When to take out the catheter: When the output is <10 ml/24 hours.
referralRedFlags: COMPLICATIONS: Injury to vessels in vicinity of the abscess; Injury to the pleura in case of liver and splenic abscesses. POST PROCEDURE WARNING SIGNS SIGNIFYING COMPLICATIONS: Frank blood in the drainage tube (haemorrhage); Drop in BP with tachycardia (haemorrhage); Patient developing breathlessness/desaturation (pneumothorax). Immediate step if warning signs (red flag) occur: Clamp the drainage catheter (Do not take it out), fast fluid infusion to be started. Steps once complication recognised: Appropriate imaging to look for source of bleeding (CT angiography followed by DSA if necessary); CT thorax if pleural breach is suspected. Refer to nearest centre having facility of ICU, CT Scan, IR services.
algorithmNotes: ALGORITHM: Patient with abdominal pain, high grade fever, malaise (suspected intra abdominal abscess) -> Laboratory and imaging investigations (USG and CT) to confirm diagnosis -> Start medical treatment -> Symptoms resolve after 3-5 days? YES: Follow up imaging ultrasound at 1 month. NO improvement/Patient develops signs such as increasing abdominal pain, tachycardia, hypotension -> Percutaneous image guided drainage of abscess -> Continued medical treatment. AFTER CARE IN UNCOMPLICATED CASES: Continue standard medical treatment; Investigation - Ultrasound on day 1 and day 3 post drainage. PATIENT SUITABLE FOR DISCHARGE: Afebrile patient with resolved leucocytosis. FOLLOW UP: Repeat ultrasound at 1 month to look for residual abscess. ABBREV: CBC=Complete Blood Count; CRP=C-reactive protein; CT=Computed Tomography; ESR=Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate; HBs Ag=Hepatitis B surface Antigen; HIV=Human Immunodeficiency Virus; LFT=Liver Function Test; USG=Ultrasonography.

whenToSuspect: Haemoptysis refers to the expectoration of blood, originating from the lower respiratory tract. LTH (Life Threatening Haemoptysis) - Any haemoptysis that: (1) is >100 ml in 24 hr; (2) causes respiratory failure (SpO2 <60%) necessitating intubation and mechanical ventilation; or (3) causes haemodynamic instability. LTH is a respiratory emergency, mortality 50-100%. Patient dies because of asphyxiation and not exsanguination. Optimal diagnosis and treatment - mortality <20%. Rule out other source of bleeding: Upper Respiratory tract -> alkaline, bright red, foamy blood, breathing difficulty, sensation of warmth in the thorax; GIT -> hematinized blood, acidic pH, food particles, abdominal pain, nausea (pseudohaemoptysis).
risk: ETIOLOGY AND SOURCE OF HAEMOPTYSIS: Pulmonary parenchymal source (65-79%): Tubercular Pneumonia, Lung abscess, Mycetoma ("fungus ball"). Tracheobronchial source (31-57%): Bronchiectasis, Neoplasm. Primary vascular source (1-5%): Arteriovenous malformation, Pulmonary embolism, Pulmonary artery aneurysm/rupture. Cardiac source (1-5%): Mitral Stenosis, Pulmonary Edema, CHD with MAPCAS. Miscellaneous and rare causes: Systemic coagulopathy or thrombolytic agents.
scores: RED FLAG SIGNS: PR >120/min; Systolic BP <100 mm Hg; Tachypnoea; SpO2 <90%; Deterioration of sensorium. Vitals stable (green): PR 60-100/min; Systolic BP >100 mm Hg; SpO2 >95%; Conscious and oriented. Consider Intubation in: Ineffective cough; Worsening hypoxemia; Presence of red flag sign.
investigations: Lab Investigations: BG, CM; CBC; PT-INR; viral markers, KFT, CHEST X-RAY. CTA AORTA +/- CTPA. Bronchoscopy may be used for: Lateralization of bleeding (active haemoptysis within 24-48 hrs); Clot extraction; Balloon tamponade. Imaging examples: Fibrocalcific lesion; L side-Bronchiectasis; Hypertrophied Bronchial artery in MDCT, MIP and VRT.
drugs: Bad lung down (dependent position-decubitus); Correct coagulopathy; Reverse anticoagulation; Nebulized Tranexamic acid 1gm f/b 0.5 gm Q8H. Resuscitation: Airway, Breathing, Circulation.
management: CHART 2 (LTH): LTH -> Resuscitation (Airway, Breathing, Circulation) -> Refer to nearest centre having facility of ICU, CT Scan, IR services -> Vitals Stable? YES -> CTA AORTA +/- CTPA. NO -> DSA + embolization of BAs/NBSAs/PA/MAPCAS. CTA findings and management: Normal/No identifiable source -> Medical management -> Fail -> DSA + Embolisation of Bronchial arteries. Hypertrophied BAs (90%), NBSAs (5%) -> BAE by PVA, Embospheres, Glue (Coil and gelfoam for aneurysm/prox occlusion). PA aneurysm/PAVM -> PA/PAVM embolization (Peripheral PA aneurysm -> DSA/CT guided glue embolization; PAVM -> Coil/Plug Embo of feeders, No use of PVA). CHD with MAPCAS -> In consultation with appropriate specialists, plan for MAPCAS embolization (Proximal vessel embolization by gelfoam/coil followed by definitive surgery for CHD). Lung CA -> Embolization of supplying artery (PA/BA) as bridge. Fail -> Surgery.
referralRedFlags: CHART 3 (NON LTH): CHEST XRAY -> Infiltrate (Antibiotics/sputum C/S -> Resolution/Non resolution -> Repeat CXR at 8 weeks -> Normal/Abnormal); Mass lesion; Other parenchymal disease -> HRCT/CTA/CECT; Suspicion of cardiac disease -> ECG + ECHO -> Specific management and treatment. Recurrence/High risk of CA lung -> HRCT/CTA/CECT. SURGERY FOR SEVERE/REFRACTORY HAEMOPTYSIS: Cavitating lesion with fungal ball; In unsuitable anatomy/not amenable for angioembolization/unsuccessful embolization; >600 ml/24 hours; Surgeries: Lobectomy, Pneumonectomy, Cavernostomy; Large cavity in a patient with very poor pulmonary functions and massive bleeding -> Resection and a cavernostomy with cauterization of the bleeding point and packing of the cavity.
algorithmNotes: ALGORITHM: Patient with suspected haemoptysis -> History & clinical examination -> Confirmed hemoptysis (vs Pseudohaemoptysis -> Appropriate supportive care and management) -> LTH (-> Chart 2) or Non-LTH (-> Chart 3). ABBREV: BAE=Bronchial Artery Embolization; BAs=Bronchial Arteries; NBSAs=Non-Bronchial Systemic Arteries; PA=Pulmonary Artery; PAVM=Pulmonary Arteriovenous Malformation; PVA=Polyvinyl Alcohol; MAPCAS=Major Aortopulmonary Collateral Arteries; CHD=Congenital Heart Disease; DSA=Digital Subtraction Angiography; CTA=CT Angiography; CTPA=CT Pulmonary Angiography; LTH=Life Threatening Haemoptysis; HRCT=High Resolution CT.

condition: Image Guided Therapies for Primary Liver Tumors
whenToSuspect: Primary liver tumors majorly consist of Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (IHCC) and combined hepatocellular cholangiocarcinoma (cHCC-CC); HCC being the most common. There are no specific signs or symptoms for primary liver tumors. Radiological and lab investigations are the main tool for diagnosis. In patients with underlying cirrhosis new onset of ascites, jaundice and/or variceal haemorrhage should prompt investigations to look for HCC. Patients with pre-existing cirrhosis are at high risk of developing HCC and should be under surveillance for development. Serum Alpha fetoprotein: elevated AFP value should prompt imaging work up.
risk: Risk Factors: Hepatitis B (33%); Alcoholic liver disease (30%); Hepatitis C (21%); NASH; Other causes of cirrhosis. DIAGNOSTIC WORK-UP: LI-RADS classification system should be used for diagnosis. Multiphasic CT/MRI or contrast enhanced ultrasound is needed for diagnosis. In patients at risk, if a lesion of size >2cm with arterial phase hyperenhancement and washout on subsequent phase is diagnostic of HCC. Biopsy is needed if: Equivocal imaging findings; Non-cirrhotic liver.
scores: Performance status (ECOG): PS 0, 1 suitable for IR therapies, PS 2 may consider TARE. Liver function tests: Serum bilirubin >3 mg/dl & AST >five times the upper limit of normal contraindication for TACE. Child-Pugh class: IR therapies should be considered for Child-Pugh class <B8. Treatment response should be assessed using mRECIST criteria and reported as complete response (CR), partial response (PR), Stable disease (SD) and progressive disease (PD).
investigations: Essential - Lab investigations: Liver function tests, Kidney function tests, CBC, PT/INR, Alpha feto-protein (AFP). Imaging: Recent contrast enhanced multiphasic CT/MRI. Desirable: PIVKA II. Optional: FDG PET CECT. Image guidance for ablation: USG (contrast optional), CT (contrast optional), Both CT & USG. Image guidance for TACE/TARE: DSA (Cone beam CT optional). T99m Tc MAA (Macro aggregated albumin) used for pre-procedure assessment as it has diameter and distribution similar to Y90 microspheres; Tc99 (2-5 mCi) microspheres used in preprocedure assessment - in case of rhenium-188 isotope.
drugs: TACE (Transarterial chemoembolization): Intra-arterial infusion of cytotoxic agent followed by embolisation of the tumor-feeding blood vessels. cTACE: Emulsion of Lipiodol & chemotherapeutic agent is injected into the arteries supplying the tumor. DEB TACE: Chemotherapy loaded microspheres are selectively injected into the arteries supplying the tumor. TARE (Transarterial radioembolisation): infusion of radioactive substances or microspheres into the arteries supplying the tumor; mostly contains yttrium-90 (Y90). After care: Pain management - NSAIDs and if required intravenous Narcotics; Post embolization syndrome - Paracetamol and/or antiemetics. Ablative therapies: Chemical ablation (Ethanol, Acetic acid); Thermal ablation (Radiofrequency Ablation, Microwave ablation, Cryoablation); Non chemical-non thermal ablation (Irreversible electroporation).
management: PATIENT MANAGEMENT: Multiphasic contrast-enhanced CT/MRI for tumor characterization, staging and presence of extrahepatic disease. IR therapies based on size and number of lesions: <3 lesions of <3 cm - consider ablation; Lesion >3cm & <5cm may consider combination of TACE & Ablation; Lesion >5 & <8 cm consider TACE; Lesion >8 cm consider TARE. Portal vein status: Portal vein tumoral thrombus - consider TARE/SBRT; TACE may be considered for segmental/sub-segmental branch tumoral thrombus. Location of the tumor: Tumors at critical location like Perivascular/pericholedochal/exophytic/subdiaphragmatic lesion - Consider TACE / Combination of TACE & ablation/MWA in experienced centres. THERMAL ABLATION INDICATIONS: Very early (single lesion <2 cm) and early HCC single lesion or upto three lesions each less than 3cm; Bridging therapy for liver transplantation; Residual and recurrent HCC; Combination therapy with TACE; Repeat ablation for focal residual lesion <3 cm along periphery or within ablation zone. TACE INDICATIONS: Multinodular or single nodule HCC of size >5 cm with preserved portal flow, preserved liver function and PS=0 (INASL-BCLC/BCLC-2022 stage B); In small HCC where ablation is not possible. TARE INDICATIONS: Palliation for unresectable HCC with or without PVTT; Bridge to transplantation; Neoadjuvant therapy for resection; Definitive ablative radiotherapy for smaller lesions. PROCEDURE DETAILS OF BAE: Pre op CTA/Flush DTA and B/L SCA Aortogram; Locate Hypertrophied BAs; Selective hand-injection angiogram; Advance coaxial microcatheter; Embolization by pulsatile injection; Locate other BAs and NBSAs and embolise all pathological arteries.
referralRedFlags: CONTRAINDICATIONS (Thermal ablation): Ascites; Sepsis and uncorrectable coagulopathy; Intrahepatic biliary dilatation; Intravascular invasion or extrahepatic metastatic disease; Arrhythmias (for IRE); Poor PS (>2); Severely deranged liver function (CTP class C). TACE CONTRAINDICATIONS: Decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B >=8, including jaundice with Serum Bilirubin >3.0 mg/dl, hepatic encephalopathy, refractory ascites and hepatorenal syndrome); Portal vein tumoral thrombus; Extensive tumor involving both liver lobes; Untreatable arteriovenous fistula; Renal insufficiency (creatinine >=2 mg/dL or creatinine clearance <30 mL/min). TARE CONTRAINDICATIONS: Lung shunting >20% or radiation doses to lungs >30 Gy in single treatment or cumulative dose of 50 Gy; Severe liver dysfunction (Child-Pugh C), total bilirubin >3mg/dl; Significant immediate life threatening extrahepatic disease; Patients with ECOG PS >2. POST PROCEDURE COMPLICATIONS: Immediate Post procedure - Bleed/hemoperitoneum; Post embolization syndrome (Fever may persist 2-3 days, pain 5-7 days); Visceral/diaphragmatic/lung/GB injury. Post TACE/TARE Liver failure: >10 times elevation of baseline AST/ALT; >3 times elevation of baseline serum Bilirubin; Post procedure hepatic encephalopathy; INR elevation >2.5 of baseline. Radioembolization-induced liver disease (REILD); Radiation Pneumonitis (1-6 months).
algorithmNotes: FOLLOW-UP (Common for all IR therapies): Lab investigations (LFT, KFT, CBC) repeated after 1-2 weeks; USG abdomen if prolonged pain/fever/abdominal distension; Response evaluation and follow up consists of clinical, biochemical and imaging at 1 month. To assess treatment response as per mRECIST criteria at 1 month for Ablation/TACE and 6 or 12 weeks (12 weeks preferable) for TARE. OUTCOME MEASURES: Treatment response assessed using mRECIST criteria - CR, PR, SD, PD. If complete response achieved, periodic follow-up at 3, 6, 9, 12 months and 6-12 months thereafter. Partial response at 1 month: plan repeat session of same or different modality. Progressive disease at one month: change treatment plan based on advanced HCC as per INASL-BCLC/BCLC-2022 classification. ABBREV: AFP=Alpha Fetoprotein; CBC=Complete Blood Count; CT=Computed Tomography; DSA=Digital subtraction angiography; ECOG=Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group; HCC=Hepatocellular Carcinoma; IHCC=Intrahepatic Cholangiocarcinoma; IRE=Irreversible Electroporation; KFT=Kidney Function Test; LFT=Liver Function Test; LI-RADS=Liver Imaging Reporting & Data System; MRI=Magnetic Resonance Imaging; MWA=Microwave Ablation; PIVKA II=Protein Induced by Vitamin K Absence-II; PS=Performance Status; PVT=Portal Vein Tumoral Thrombus; mRECIST=modified Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors; SBRT=Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy; TACE=Transarterial Chemoembolization; TARE=Transarterial Radioembolization; USG=Ultrasonography.

condition: Image Guided Management of Obstructive Jaundice
whenToSuspect: Clinical presentation: Jaundice; Pruritus; Dark coloured urine & Pale stool. COMMON ETIOLOGIES: Non obstructive - Hepatitis related (viral hepatitis A,B,C,E,NASH, alcohol, auto-immune cirrhosis). Obstructive - Mechanical obstruction. Benign: stone, sludge, stricture, worm, primary sclerosing cholangitis, bilio-enteric anastomotic stricture (HJ stricture). Malignant: Carcinoma GB, hepatocellular carcinoma, cholangiocarcinoma, hepatic metastasis, pancreatic head carcinoma, extrinsic compression by lymph node/mass, pseudotumor.
risk: KEY TO DIAGNOSIS: In presence of jaundice - High AST/ALT + relatively normal SAP/GGT suggests hepatitis; Elevated SAP & GGT + relatively normal AST/ALT suggests obstructive etiology; USG abdomen would mostly differentiate between obstructive and non-obstructive causes. Do not suspect obstructive jaundice if: AST/ALT elevation >1000 IU; ALP/GGT normal/mildly elevated (s/o hepatitis). If non obstructive jaundice: refer to district hospital/tertiary care to be managed by physician (medicine/gastroenterologist/hepatologist). Common causes of biliary obstruction: GB mass/cholangiocarcinoma causing biliary radicle dilatation; GB/CBD stone causing biliary dilatation; Periampullary mass causing biliary dilatation.
scores: RED FLAGS: Cholangitis - Pain in right hypochondrium, Fever, Chills, Tachycardia & tachypnoea. Patients should be administered IV fluids & antibiotics - Cefoperazone + Sulbactam in a ratio of 1:1 administered IV 20-40 mg/kg/day in equal doses over duration of 6-12 hrs.
investigations: HEMATOLOGICAL - Essential: LFT, CBC, PT/INR. Desirable: KFT, Screen for Hepatitis B/C markers. Optional: Hepatitis A/E markers. IMAGING - Essential: USG Abdomen. Desirable: MRCP, CECT Abdomen. BASIC HEMATOLOGICAL AND USG FINDINGS: LFT - Serum bilirubin Elevated, AST/ALT Normal to elevated, ALP/GGT Markedly elevated (ALP>GGT). CBC - Hb Normal to low, TLC Normal to elevated, PT/INR Normal to elevated. USG ABDOMEN - Gall bladder stone/mass, Dilatation of Common bile duct/intrahepatic biliary radicles.
drugs: PTBD IN CHOLANGITIS: IV antibiotics and fluids to be started before the procedure - Cefoperazone+Sulbactam in 1:1 ratio, 20-40 mg/kg/day in equal doses after duration of 6-12 hrs; Piperacillin 4 gm + Tazobactam 0.5 gms every 8 hrs. After care: Appropriate antibiotics - Ofloxacin/Cefixime 200 mg 12 hrly for 3-5 days. Elective cases: I/V vitamin K injection (5-10 mg) - 3 to 5 days. Emergent cases: infuse fresh frozen plasma (FFP) - 10ml/kg body weight prior to the procedure.
management: PHC: Patient with clinical features and/or red flag signs. CHC/DISTRICT HOSPITAL: Clinical examination; hematological investigations - LFT, CBC, PT/INR and Imaging - USG abdomen; If cholangitis is suspected - Fluid resuscitation and IV antibiotics and refer to tertiary level care. TERTIARY CARE: Clinical examination, repeat hematological investigations if >2 weeks; Imaging - MRCP to confirm diagnosis & look for level of obstruction, CECT abdomen to decide for definitive vs palliative care; Suspected cholangitis - Fluid resuscitation & I/V antibiotics; Biliary drainage (PTBD/ERCP) to make patient fit for surgery/palliative care; PTBD preferred for high CBD/hilar obstruction, ERCP preferred in low CBD obstruction. MANAGEMENT: Choledocholithiasis/other benign cause needing bile drainage - ERCP, PTBD (If ERCP not possible). Biliary tract malignancy - CECT Abdomen to identify malignancy and extent. DEFINITIVE TREATMENT: PTBD followed by surgery. PALLIATIVE TREATMENT: PTBD followed by Biliary stenting, Chemotherapy/Radiotherapy. PTBD INDICATIONS: Decrease bilirubin to commence appropriate therapy (surgical/palliative); Cholangitis (draining infected bile); Intense pruritus. Days of hospitalisation: 1-3 days (non-infected/no cholangitis cases); 7-14 days (Cholangitis, can be prolonged in severely septic patients). PROCEDURAL DETAILS: External drainage for infected cases; Internal drainage using internal external drainage catheter for non infected cases.
referralRedFlags: PTBD CONTRAINDICATIONS: Deranged coagulation (correct before procedure); Ascites (to be dried before therapy). COMPLICATION: Development of cholangitis post PTBD; Haemorrhagic output should prompt an immediate evaluation to rule out vascular injury. FOLLOW UP: Follow-up with IR in case of non-reducing or rise in bilirubin/sign of cholangitis/stent block; Patients to follow with respective physician (surgeon/medical or radiation oncologist) after successful biliary drainage and normalization of bilirubin. Patients with obstructive jaundice having no/minimal IHBRD with distended GB may be considered for percutaneous cholecystostomy in emergent situations.
algorithmNotes: OUTCOME MEASURES: Normal bile drainage through the catheter. EXPECTED OUTCOMES: Reduction in bilirubin to make patient fit for required therapy. Once the bilirubin starts reducing, the patient can be: taken up for surgery or chemo/radiotherapy or refer back to regional cancer centre. ABBREV: ALP=Alkaline Transferase; ALT=Alanine Aminotransferase; AST=Aspartate Aminotransferase; CBC=Complete Blood Count (Hemogram); CBD=Common Bile Duct; CECT=Contrast Enhanced Computed Tomography; ERCP=Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography; GB=Gall Bladder; GGT=Gamma Glutamyl Transferase; IHBRD=Intrahepatic Biliary Radicle Dilatation; IR=Interventional Radiology; KFT=Kidney Function Test; LFT=Liver Function Test; MRCP=Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography; NASH=Non Alcoholic Steatohepatitis; PT/INR=Prothrombin Time/International Normalized Ratio; PTBD=Percutaneous Transhepatic Biliary Drainage; SAP=Serum amyloid P; USG=Ultrasonography.

whenToSuspect: What is stroke? An episode of neurological dysfunction caused by focal cerebral, spinal or retinal infarction or haemorrhage. SYMPTOMS: Numbness or weakness especially on one side of the body; Loss of consciousness or altered consciousness; Decreased vision in one or both eyes; Language difficulties either in speaking or understanding; Difficulty in walking, loss of balance or coordination; Confusion or loss of memory; Swallowing difficulties; Paralysis of any part of the body, including face; Sudden severe headache with no known cause; Neck pain; Nausea and vomiting. WARNING SIGN (BEFAST): Balance - Loss of balance or coordination; Eyes - Sudden blurred or double vision; Face - Deviation at the angle of the mouth; Arm - Arm drift; Speech - Slurred speech or inability to speak or understand; Time - Act fast. WARNING SIGN (FAST): F - sudden numbness or weakness of FACE; A - sudden numbness or weakness of ARM or LEG; S - difficulty in SPEECH and understanding; T - TIME to rush to the hospital.
risk: TYPES OF STROKE: Ischemic stroke - Focal cerebral, spinal or retinal infarction. Intracerebral haemorrhage - Focal collection of blood within the brain parenchyma or ventricular system not caused by trauma. Subarachnoid haemorrhage - Bleeding into the subarachnoid space. Cerebral venous thrombosis of cerebral venous structure. Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA) - Transient episode of neurological dysfunction caused by focal cerebral, spinal cord or retinal ischemia without acute infarction.
scores: STROKE PATIENT FLOW: NIHSS Score Calculation -> NCCT + CTA -> No hemorrhage (<4.5 Hours: No LVO -> IVT, LVO -> IVT+MT; 4.5-24 Hours: LVO -> MT, No LVO/Clinico-radiological mismatch -> MT) or Hemorrhage -> CTA -> Aneurysm/AVM -> Surgery/Endovascular Treatment accordingly. DAWN/DIFFUSE CRITERIA (BEYOND 6 HOURS). Acute Ischemic Stroke (Large Vessel Occlusion LVO, NIHSS 6-25, No Large Infarct, CBF/CBV>1.8). Assess the 4Ps of stroke as quickly as possible: Time Vs Information.
investigations: INVESTIGATIONS - Essential: CT scan head, ECG, Blood Sugar. Desirable: CTA, Echocardiogram, Lipids, Renal Parameter. Optional: MRI/MRA, Holter Monitoring. CT SCAN findings: NCCT ASPECTS-7, Dense MCA Sign, Gangliocapsular Bleed-Likely hypertensive bleed, Intraparenchymal Bleed associated with vascular malformation, Right M1 MCA Occlusion, CT Perfusion CBV-CBF Mismatch, Mechanical Thrombectomy with removal of clot, Diffuse SAH-Ruptured Intracranial Aneurysm. IMAGING - What all we want to know: Parenchyma-bleed vs Ischemia, area involved; Pipes-Occlusion?, location (Large vessel occlusion vs Distal branch occlusion); Perfusion-collaterals? (Multiphase CT angiogram 3rd phase, predicts the outcome); Penumbra-Tissue at risk (salvageable tissue).
drugs: ISCHEMIC (Stroke onset time <4.5 hrs): IV tPA (0-4.5 Hrs) or endovascular treatment according to eligibility and availability (.9mg/kg/wt); 10% as bolus, remaining 90% as I/V infusion over an hour; BP maintained below 180/110 mm/Hg. HAEMORRHAGIC: Dysphagia assessment, Blood pressure/Blood sugar monitoring and IV fluids; Prevention of pneumonia; Prophylaxis for deep venous thrombosis etc, monitor and record ECG.
management: PRELIMINARY MANAGEMENT: Assess and manage ABCs; Initiate cardiac monitoring; Maintain O2 saturation >94%; Establish IV access; Determine blood glucose and treat accordingly; Determine time of symptom onset or last known normal, obtain family contact information; Triage and RAPID TRANSFER of patient to nearest district hospital with CT; Scan facility or Stroke center with facility for thrombolysis; Referral hospital to be notified to handle the referred patient with stroke. MANAGEMENT (Stroke onset time >4.5 hrs): Rapid Assessment, CODE Stroke, Blood pressure and Blood Sugar monitoring, NIHSS, Intravenous lines; Endovascular treatment with Mechanical thrombectomy using stent retriever, aspiration, or hybrid techniques (4.5 hrs to 24hrs) according to eligibility. ACUTE ISCHEMIC STROKE: Large Vessel Occlusion (LVO), NIHSS 6-25, No Large Infarct, CBF/CBV>1.8 -> Immediately shift to DSA Cathlab from CT room for Mechanical thrombectomy, IVT if indicated should be started simultaneously -> Groin Puncture Access -> 8Fr Balloon Guide catheter, Cannulation of targeted ICA -> Aspiration catheter/Stent Retriever/Both -> Successful recanalization or Unsuccessful (Repeat attempt, maximum 3 attempts) -> Post-procedure CT Head -> Shift to Neuro ICU for monitoring. EARLY ACUTE ISCHEMIC STROKE CARE: Out of hospital -> Recognition (by bystander) -> Assess for stroke (FAST, CPSS, LAPSS) -> First medical contact -> In emergency department -> NIHSS -> Immediate diagnostics (evaluating for IV alteplase) -> Evaluate for IV Alteplase and Mechanical Thrombectomy simultaneously -> Administer.
referralRedFlags: TIME IS BRAIN, SAVE NEURONS SAVE A LIFE. Triage and RAPID TRANSFER of patient to nearest district hospital with CT scan facility or Stroke center with facility for thrombolysis. Referral hospital to be notified to handle the referred patient with stroke. For advanced medical management refer to STW Stroke.
algorithmNotes: ABBREV: AVM=Arterio-Venous Malformation; CBF=Cerebral Blood Flow; CBV=Cerebral Blood Volume; CPSS=Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale; CT=Computed Tomography; CTA=CT Angiography; DSA=Digital Subtraction Angiography; ECG=Electrocardiogram; ICA=Internal Carotid Artery; ICU=Intensive Care Unit; IVT=Intravenous Thrombolysis; LAPSS=Los Angeles Prehospital Stroke Screen; MCA=Middle Cerebral Artery; MRA=MR Angiography; MRI=Magnetic Resonance Imaging; MT=Mechanical Thrombectomy; NCCT=Non-Contrast Computerized Tomography; NIHSS=National Institute Of Health Stroke Scale.

condition: Image Guided Management of Vaginal Bleeding
whenToSuspect: Uterus preserving treatment for two important causes of vaginal bleeding in women of reproductive age group. HEAVY MENSTRUAL BLEEDING (HMB): Losing 80ml or more in each period, having periods that last longer than 7 days, or both. POST PARTUM HAEMORRHAGE (PPH): 500ml after vaginal delivery or 1000ml after Cesarean section. SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS: Look for anaemia; Primary PPH is within the first 24 hour of delivery and secondary PPH is more than 24 hour after delivery; Prophylactic IR on patients with an increased risk of massive bleeding at delivery; Hypotension to haemorrhagic shock and multi-organ failure depending on the quantum of bleeding; Check for uterine contractility, retained placenta; Abnormal placenta on imaging.
risk: GREEN FLAG SIGN: Symptomatic intramural fibroids can be treated by UAE with preservation of the uterus. Two important causes: uterine fibroids (HMB) and postpartum haemorrhage. Other obstetric conditions like post-abortive haemorrhage secondary to uterine artery pseudoaneurysm, complications of molar pregnancy, uterine arteriovenous malformation (AVM) can also be treated similarly. Other gynaecological conditions like adenomyosis also can be managed similarly by UAE.
scores: RED FLAG SIGN: PR >120/min; Systolic BP <100 mm Hg; Tachypnoea >20 breaths per minute; SpO2 <95%; Deterioration of sensorium. Refer to uterine fibroids and polyps ICD-10-D25 & N84. Refer to Postpartum hemorrhage ICD-72. Timely referral to a higher centre must be considered where facilities for ICU, surgical and IR are available.
investigations: HEMATOLOGICAL - Essential: Hb, PT, INR, APTT and Platelet count. Optional: Thrombo-elastogram (TEG) or Rotational Thromboelastometry (ROTEM). IMAGING - Essential: USG. Optional: MRI.
drugs: FIBROID Medical: NSAIDS, Tranexamic acid, combined oral contraceptive pills, progestogens. PPH Medical: Intensive Care Support; Uterotonic drugs - Oxytocin infusion: 20 IU in 500 ml RL/NS @ 40-60 drops/minute; Transfusion of blood products; Inotropes, ventilation and other organ support. Pain management: NSAIDS and if required intravenous narcotics (Morphine sulfate 30 mg SC/IM/IV), hypogastric nerve block.
management: FIBROID MANAGEMENT: Medical (NSAIDS, Tranexamic acid, combined OCPs, progestogens); Interventional Radiology - Uterine Artery Embolisation; Surgical - Myomectomy or Hysterectomy. FIBROID IR MANAGEMENT Indications: Fibroids with heavy menstrual cycles, pain, pressure, and urinary symptoms. Contraindication: Suspected infection. Approximate days of required hospitalisation: 1-3 days. PPH MANAGEMENT: Medical (Intensive Care Support); Interventional Radiology - Uterine Artery Embolisation; Surgical - Bilateral internal iliac artery ligation or Hysterectomy. PPH IR MANAGEMENT Indications: Uterine atony despite medical treatment; Vaginal or cervical tear after failed surgical repair; Persistent hemorrhage after arterial ligation or hysterectomy; Placenta accreta - including prophylactic treatment. Contraindication: Nil; but risk of acute kidney injury to be considered. Days of required hospitalisation: 2 to 7 days. PROCEDURAL DETAILS: Under conscious sedation or anaesthesia; Arterial access (femoral/radial); Selective internal iliac arterial angiograms and cannulation of hypertrophied (uterine) arteries; Embolisaton with appropriate agent - PVA particles (PPH: PVA particles, gel foam, histoacryl etc.); Check angiogram. For patients with placenta accreta: Prophylactic balloon catheter placement of internal iliac arteries before delivery/caesarean section. ALGORITHM: Vaginal Bleeding -> HMB (Assess anemia, Look for fibroids -> USG -> NSAIDS, Tranexamic acid, combined OCPs -> No relief -> UAE -> No relief -> Myomectomy/Hysterectomy) or PPH (Clinical assessment -> Haemodynamically unstable -> USG -> Look for retained placenta, AVF -> ICU care, blood products, Oxytocin infusion -> UAE -> Continued Bleed -> Hysterectomy).
referralRedFlags: FIBROID Expected outcomes: At 12 months, menorrhagia control in 90%-92% of patients and improvement in bulk symptoms in 88%-96%. Associated adverse events/complications: Fibroid expulsion 5%; Ovarian failure with amenorrhoea 7.5% (majority in women >45 years); Uterine sepsis requiring hysterectomy 0.1%. PPH Expected outcomes: successful haemostasis >95%. Associated adverse events/complications: ovarian failure, uterine sepsis, uterine infarctions (rare; less than 2%). After care (PPH): Medical - ICU care till bleeding arrests and organ failures are reversed; Investigation - USG; Criteria and timing for safe discharge - 3 days after the procedure if uneventful. Timely referral to a higher centre must be considered where facilities for ICU, surgical and IR are available. KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES.
algorithmNotes: CONCLUSION: Uterine artery embolization is a minimally invasive image guided procedure which has an important role in management of select cases of obstetric and gynecological conditions; It is a uterus preserving procedure; It has evolving role in case of uterine malignancies. Follow up: Fibroid - after 3 months (clinical, Hb, USG); PPH - after two weeks (Clinical, Hb, USG). Other image guided minimally invasive treatment for fibroid include HIFU and ablation. ABBREV: APTT=Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time; AVF=Arteriovenous Fistula (uterine); CECT=Contrast Enhanced Computed Tomography; Hb=Haemoglobin; HIFU=High Frequency Focussed Ultrasound; HMB=Heavy Menstrual Bleeding; ICU=Intensive Care Unit; INR=International Normalized Ratio; IR=Interventional Radiology; MRI=Magnetic Resonance Imaging; NSAIDs=Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory Drugs; OCPs=Oral Contraceptive Pills; PPH=Postpartum Haemorrhage; PT=Prothrombin Time; PVA=Poly Vinyl Alcohol; UAE=Uterine Artery Embolization; USG=Ultrasonography; VB=Vaginal Bleeding.

condition: Image Guided Management of Varicose Veins (Chronic Venous Insufficiency)
whenToSuspect: SYMPTOMS: Heaviness/tiredness/aching of the legs; Itching in legs; Nocturnal cramps in calf; Swelling around ankle; Symptoms worsened after prolonged standing; Skin discoloration near the ankles; Abnormal dilated veins in leg; Bleeding from varicosities in leg; Non healing leg ulcer. SIGNS (CEAP-CLASSIFICATION): C1 - Telangiectasias or reticular veins; C2 - Varicose veins; C3 - Edema; C4a - Pigmentation or eczema; C4b - Lipodermatosclerosis or atrophie blanche; C5 - Healed venous ulcer; C6 - Active venous ulcer. CEAP C1: TELANGIECTASIAS (Intradermal spider veins, web-like, <1mm), RETICULAR VEINS (Subdermal reticular veins, <3mm). CEAP C2: Great Saphenous vein with pronounced chronic venous insufficiency in medial ankle area; Circumflex vein (Lateral branch of greater saphenous vein); Small saphenous vein varicosis.
risk: CONTRAINDICATION: Absolute contraindication - Reflux in deep veins. Relative contraindications - Severe peripheral artery disease, Severe hypercoagulability syndromes, Advanced liver disease, Serious systemic disease. MANAGEMENT ALGORITHM by grade: C1-C2 GRADE (USG color doppler: Competent SFJ, Mild reflux grade I -> Medical Management/Conservative; Grade II/III/IV reflux, Saphenous incompetence -> Endovenous thermal/Non-thermal ablation). C3-C6 GRADE (USG color doppler: Grade II/III/IV reflux, Saphenous/Tributary incompetence -> Endovenous thermal/non-thermal ablation plus management of perforator, Sclerotherapy, phlebectomy whenever needed).
scores: RED FLAGS: Bleeding from superficial veins; Venous ulcer; Recurrent history of superficial vein thrombophlebitis/Cellulitis. USG COLOR DOPPLER criteria: Incompetent SFJ - retrograde reflux venous flow lasting more than 0.5 sec; Abnormal GSV - >=4 mm on standing; Incompetent perforator - >=3.5 mm/reflux on release of distal compression.
investigations: ESSENTIAL - Imaging: USG color doppler. Hematological useful for prior to ablation: CBC, PT/INR, APTT, HBs Ag, HIV, KFT, HCV. USG COLOR DOPPLER: Rule out DVT/Deep vein reflux; Doppler evaluation in standing; Define and grade reflux at SFJ/SPJ; Identify segmental intrinsic reflux in GSV/SSV; Locate incompetent/pathological perforators; Diameter of GSV; Evaluate truncal and superficial vein varicosities and dilated tributaries. If there is proximal obstruction in common iliac vein, CTV or MRV is needed.
drugs: MEDICAL/CONSERVATIVE: Mostly adjuvant to endovenous thermal ablation; Life style modification; Graded compression stockings; Limb elevation, Limb massage, calf pumping exercise; Micronized purified flavonoid fraction (MPFF); Wound care - Unna boots, hydrocolloid dressing, silver coated dressing.
management: PHC: Signs and symptoms of varicose veins -> Refer to higher centre. CHC/DISTRICT HOSPITAL: Clinical examination, USG Color Doppler, Exclude DVT/Deep vein reflux. TERTIARY CARE: Clinical examination, USG color doppler, Endovenous thermal ablation, Adjuvant medical management. IMAGE GUIDED THERAPY: Primary modality of treating varicose veins; Percutaneous endovenous Thermal Ablation Therapy (laser/RFA); Percutaneous endovenous Non-thermal Ablation Therapy (MOCA/Glue/catheter guided sclerotherapy); Percutaneous ablation of Perforators (Laser/RFA); Percutaneous Guided Sclerotherapy. SURGICAL: Limited role - Subfascial Endoscopic Perforator Surgery, Phlebectomy. VARICOSE VEINS IR MANAGEMENT Procedural details: Performed in IR suite; Under conscious sedation or spinal anaesthesia or local anaesthesia; Sterile precautions; Percutaneous GSV access; Perivenular anaesthesia; Thermal ablation about 2 cm from SFJ junction; Management of pathological perforator, SSV, accessory GSV, tributaries and superficial vein as and when required. RECOMMENDATION: For GSV reflux - endovenous thermal ablation recommended in preference to surgery and foam sclerotherapy; For SSV reflux - endovenous thermal ablation, access gained no lower than midcalf; Foam sclerotherapy considered as primary treatment in recurrent varicose veins; When performing endovenous thermal ablation for saphenous reflux trunk, adding concomitant phlebectomy should be considered.
referralRedFlags: MANAGEMENT OF RED FLAG SIGN: Bleeding from superficial varicose veins - Leg elevation higher than the heart; Application of pressure over the bleeding site followed by a pressure dressing; Referred to tertiary centre for endovenous ablation treatment. VENOUS ULCER: Wound care/Dressing; Compression Therapy; Leg elevation; Referred to tertiary centre for endovenous ablation treatment. Symptomatic patient with Red flag sign (Bleeding, Venous ulcer) -> Tertiary care.
algorithmNotes: SAVE LIMBS SAVE LIFE. ALGORITHM FOR DIAGNOSIS, REFERRAL AND MANAGEMENT: PHC (Signs and symptoms) -> Refer to higher centre -> CHC/District hospital (Clinical examination, USG Color Doppler, Exclude DVT) -> In appropriate imaging setting -> Tertiary care (Endovenous thermal ablation, Adjuvant medical management). ABBREV: APTT=Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time; CTV=Computed Tomography Venography; DVT=Deep Vein Thrombosis; GSV=Great Saphenous Vein; Hb=Haemoglobin; HCV=Hepatitis C Virus; INR=International Normalized Ratio; IR=Interventional Radiology; KFT=Kidney Function Test; MOCA=Mechanical Occlusion Chemically Assisted Ablation; MRV=Magnetic Resonance Venography; PT=Prothrombin Time; RFA=Radio-frequency Ablation; SPJ=Sapheno-popliteal junction; SFJ=Sapheno-Femoral Junction; SSV=Short Saphenous Vein; USG=Ultrasonography.

whenToSuspect: DEMOGRAPHICS: Brain tumors are seen with equal frequency in males and females; Astrocytoma is the commonest brain tumor in children followed by embryonal tumor; Meningioma is the commonest tumor in adults followed by glioma. CLINICAL PRESENTATION - SYMPTOMS: Recent onset headache, with or without vomiting; Gradual vision loss (unilateral/bilateral); Seizures; Cranial nerve deficits (e.g. hearing loss); Focal deficit like monoparesis, hemiparesis; Increasing head-size (children with open fontanalle). HIGH INDEX OF SUSPICION FOR BRAIN TUMOR WHEN PATIENT HAS: Adult onset seizures; Progressive symptoms; Focal neurological deficits; Severe headache; Recurrent vomitings; Blurring of vision; Drowsy or altered sensorium; Recurrent seizures; Pupillary asymmetry - Anisocoria; Abnormal posturing; Respiratory distress, abnormal breathing patterns; Bradycardia and hypertension.
risk: Brain tumors require high index of suspicion. Adults: meningioma commonest followed by glioma. Children: astrocytoma commonest followed by embryonal tumor. Progressive symptoms and focal neurological deficits warrant evaluation.
scores: IF RED FLAG SIGNS PRESENT: Give Inj Dexa 4mg IV; Loading dose of antiepileptic to prevent seizure; 200ml (to adult patients or as per weight to children) 20% mannitol IV over 30 minutes (only if systolic BP >90 mmHg) and refer to higher centre immediately after stabilisation of ABC.
investigations: AT PHC LEVEL: Metabolic workup (including Serum sodium, Blood sugar). Refer to higher centre for CT Scan. AT CENTRE WITH MRI SCAN FACILITY & NEUROSURGERY: MRI Scan - Negative (Medical management) or Positive (Refer to higher centre for further workup - Medical College/Cancer Hospital with neurosurgery and MRI Scan facility). MRI image of Meningioma; MRI image of Glioblastoma.
drugs: IF RED FLAG SIGNS PRESENT: Inj Dexa 4mg IV; Loading dose of antiepileptic to prevent seizure; 20% mannitol 200ml IV over 30 minutes (only if systolic BP >90 mmHg). AT PHC LEVEL: Phenytoinization (if seizures are present); Airway protection (if patient has altered sensorium).
management: AT PHC LEVEL: Seizures/Headache, vomiting/Focal deficit (monoparesis, hemiparesis), Vision loss, Cranial nerve deficit -> Metabolic workup (including Serum sodium, Blood sugar) -> Phenytoinization (if seizures are present), Airway protection (if patient has altered sensorium) -> Refer to higher centre for CT Scan. AT CENTRE WITH MRI SCAN FACILITY & NEUROSURGERY: MRI Scan -> Negative (Medical management) or Positive (Refer to higher centre for further workup). MEDICAL COLLEGE/CANCER HOSPITAL WITH NEUROSURGERY AND MRI SCAN FACILITY - GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF MANAGEMENT: No surgery needed -> Follow-up; Surgery -> Possible here or Not possible here -> Higher centre; Radiosurgery -> Available or Not Available -> Higher centre.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. Refer to higher centre for CT Scan (from PHC). If MRI Positive, refer to higher centre for further workup (Medical College/Cancer Hospital with neurosurgery and MRI Scan facility). If surgery/radiosurgery not possible here, refer to Higher centre. If red flag signs present, refer to higher centre immediately after stabilisation of ABC.
algorithmNotes: Treatment pathway: PHC (metabolic workup, stabilise, refer for CT) -> Centre with MRI & Neurosurgery (MRI Scan - negative=medical management, positive=refer higher) -> Medical College/Cancer Hospital (surgery/radiosurgery/follow-up as per tumor). ABBREV: ABC=Airway Breathing Circulation; CT=Computed Tomography; Dexa=Dexamethasone; MRI=Magnetic Resonance Imaging; PHC=Primary Health Centre.

whenToSuspect: CLINICAL PRESENTATION - SYMPTOMS: Headache/vomiting; Seizures; Brief or persistent loss of consciousness (LOC); Local signs of scalp hematoma/laceration; Confusion/amnesia/inability to remember events related to trauma; Blood/blood stained/clear watery fluid leak from nose/ear. NEUROLOGICAL ASSESSMENT: Note pupils (size/shape/reaction/symmetry); Look for symmetry of motor response; Anisocoria (unequal pupils); Eliciting motor response to painful stimuli; Irregular respiration (Labored breathing, Accessory muscles of respiration active).
risk: GLASGOW COMA SCORE (GCS): Eye opening (E): Spontaneous 4, To speech 3, To pain 2, None 1. Verbal response (V): Oriented 5, Confused conversation 4, Inappropriate words 3, Incomprehensible sounds 2, None 1. Best motor response (M): Obeys commands 6, Localizes pain 5, Flexion withdrawal to pain 4, Abnormal flexion (decorticate) 3, Extension (decerebrate) 2, None (flaccid) 1. SCORING & INTERPRETATION: GCS Score = (E[4] + V[5] + M[6]); Score range 3 to 15; Mild = GCS 13-15, Moderate = GCS 9-12, Severe = GCS 3-8.
scores: RED FLAG SIGNS: Unconscious; Irregular respiration; Pupil size asymmetric. IF RED FLAG SIGNS PRESENT: Give 200ml (to adult patients or as per weight to children) 20% mannitol IV over 30 minutes (only if systolic BP >90 mmHg) and refer to higher centre immediately after stabilisation of ABC. Severity by GCS: Mild = GCS 13-15, Moderate = GCS 9-12, Severe = GCS 3-8.
investigations: INVESTIGATIONS - Essential: Chest X ray; Ultrasound abdomen and chest to rule out abdominal free fluid/hemothorax; NCCT Head with Cervical Spine as soon as possible in all head injury patients. Definite indication for CT SCAN if any present: GCS <15, Transient loss of consciousness, Suspected skull fracture, Post traumatic seizure, Focal neurological deficit, Persistent vomiting, Amnesia. Desirable: X rays long bone and pelvis (if indicated). Optional: Blood alcohol levels; Plain MRI brain for prognostication (not in acute cases); Biomarkers like serum S100b.
drugs: MEDICAL MANAGEMENT - Treatment of high intracranial pressure: Sedation, analgesia and mild to moderate hyperventilation (PaCO2 30-35 mmHg); Osmotic therapy - mannitol 0.25-1.00 g/kg IV bolus or 3ml/kg 3% hypertonic saline 8 hourly. Antiepileptic drugs: Phenytoin (20mg/kg over 30 min IV, followed by 5mg/kg in 2-3 divided doses); Levetiracetam (500 mg BD, continue for 1-3 months); Prophylaxis - if seizure free for 6 months; Therapeutic - if seizure free for 2 years after receiving drug therapy, consider tapering/discontinue antiepileptic; Consult neurosurgeon/neurologist before stopping. Steroids NOT useful in Head Injury.
management: PREHOSPITAL CARE: Golden hour of head injury management - ABC as in trauma guidelines; Look for associated injuries to pelvis/long bones/chest/abdomen; Any external bleeding controlled with local pressure; All suspected head injury cases shift to higher centre (where CT SCAN and neurosurgeon are available); Transfer only when ABC are stable. MILD HEAD INJURY (GCS 13-15): History, General/Neurological examination, Skull X-Ray, Cervical spine X-Ray, Blood alcohol levels; CT HEAD ideally in all except completely asymptomatic pts; ADMIT (Amnesia, H/o LOC, Deteriorating consciousness, Moderate-severe headache, Alcohol/drug intoxication, Skull fracture, Associated injury, Abnormal CT SCAN) or DISCHARGE (Does not meet criterion for admission, Discuss need to return if problem persists). MODERATE HEAD INJURY (GCS 9-12): Initial Assessment, CT SCAN IN ALL CASES; Admit even if CT is normal; Frequent neurological examinations, Follow up CT SCAN if deteriorates/before discharge; If patient Improves -> Discharge when stable; If patient deteriorates -> Repeat CT SCAN, Manage as per severe HI. SEVERE HEAD INJURY (GCS 3-8): Intubate, Sedate, Resuscitate; CT SCAN (Not available -> Refer to higher centre if <2 hrs); Diffuse lesion -> ICU (Monitor ICP, Elevate Head end, Sedate, Maintain PaO2 100 mmHg, Maintain PaCO2 30-32 mmHg); Surgical Lesion -> decompressive craniectomy; ICP still high -> Treat ICP. SURGICAL MANAGEMENT IN HEAD INJURY IF: Extradural hematoma >30cc in volume; Subdural hematoma >10mm thick with midline shift >5 mm and evidence of deterioration; Most common operation done is decompressive craniotomy with hematoma evacuation and/or removal of large bone flap (15x12cm) - to be done by neurosurgeon only.
referralRedFlags: KEEP A HIGH THRESHOLD FOR INVASIVE PROCEDURES. COMPLICATIONS TO WATCH FOR: Deterioration of GCS score; New onset or worsening focal neurological deficit; Persistent headache, vomiting, or restlessness; Bradycardia, hypertension; Abnormal initial CT SCAN (repeat at 24 hours or earlier if indicated).
