Dysmorphic features (Down syndrome are known to be associated with Atrioventricular septal defect AVSD); 11. Abnormal peripheral pulses especially feeble lower limb pulses.
risk: PHYSIOLOGY: Left to right shunt lesions lead to passage of oxygenated blood from left side of heart to right side and into the lungs; As a result there is increased flow to the lungs and over circulation of blood within the lungs and left side of the heart; Majority of symptoms of shunt lesions are due to this over circulation. COMMON LEFT TO RIGHT SHUNT LESIONS: Pre-tricuspid shunts - Atrial septal defect (ASD): Usually asymptomatic, Presents commonly as incidentally detected murmur. Post-tricuspid shunts - Ventricular septal defect (VSD), Patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), Aorto-pulmonary window (APW). Large post-tricuspid shunts present early (usually by 1.5-2 months of age) with signs of cardiac failure like feeding and breathing difficulty along with failure to thrive.
scores: Dysmorphic features: Down syndrome are known to be associated with Atrioventricular septal defect (AVSD). Bounding (high volume) pulse in PDA and APW; Abnormal peripheral pulses especially feeble lower limb pulses. Large shunts may not have loud murmurs. Failure to thrive (weight less than 3rd centile for age, drop in weight by more than 2 major centile lines).
investigations: INVESTIGATIONS - Essential: X-ray Chest, Echo; ECG (To watch for unexpected abnormal axis, rate, rhythm and QRS complex); CBC, Electrolytes (Depending on clinical conditions and specific clinical circumstances). Imaging findings: Cardiomegaly & increased vascular markings in shunt lesion; Harrison sulcus; Precordial bulge (left side); 12-lead ECG showing left axis deviation in a patient with AV septal defect. Shunt lesions are confirmed by echocardiography.
drugs: Drugs: Furosemide 1-2 mg/kg/dose twice or thrice daily (reduce or temporarily stop during diarrhea or vomiting); Oral suspension contains 10 mg/ml, so can be given as 0.1 ml/kg/dose twice or thrice daily; Add Spironolactone if Furosemide is administered more frequently than once daily; Digoxin 5 microgram/kg/dose twice daily, Oral preparation contains 50 microgram/ml, so can be given as 0.1 ml/kg/dose twice daily. Vitamin D3, calcium and iron supplementation to be continued as per IAP recommendations and clinical requirement.
management: Timely referral to higher centre with pediatric cardiac facility: Shunt lesions are confirmed by echocardiography; Large post tricuspid shunts require early referral. GENERAL ADVICE: Educating parents about importance of maintaining hygiene to prevent infections; Promoting breastfeeding if tolerated (If breastfeeding is difficult then teach gavage/spoon feeding, preferably with expressed breast milk); Use top milk in case of reduced breastmilk output (Average volume intake should be approximately 120 mL/kg/day); Include energy dense weaning foods in those beyond 6 months of age; Continue vaccination as per Indian Academy of Pediatrics (IAP) schedule; Vitamin D3, calcium and iron supplementation as per IAP recommendations and clinical requirement.
referralRedFlags: TIMELY CORRECTION OF SHUNT LESION ENABLES NEAR NORMAL QUALITY OF LIFE. Timely referral to higher centre with pediatric cardiac facility. Large post tricuspid shunts require early referral. Majority of the lesions are easily correctable if detected on time. Red flag presentations: Failure to thrive, repeated chest infections/life threatening infection, signs of cardiac failure.
algorithmNotes: Suspect from failure to thrive, feeding difficulty, recurrent chest infections and signs of over-circulation; confirm by echocardiography; medical management (Furosemide +/- Spironolactone, Digoxin) with timely referral for definitive correction. ABBREV: ASD=Atrial Septal Defect; VSD=Ventricular Septal Defect; PDA=Patent Ductus Arteriosus; APW=Aorto-Pulmonary Window; AVSD=Atrioventricular Septal Defect; IAP=Indian Academy of Pediatrics; ECG=Electrocardiogram; CBC=Complete Blood Count.

whenToSuspect: SUSPECTING TACHYARRHYTHMIA SYMPTOMS: Palpitations/chest discomfort; Parents may report increased precordial activity or observe neck pulsations; Unexplained lethargy; Syncope/presyncope (Relatively rare in children but potentially serious). SIGNS: Tachycardia out of proportion to clinical condition; Irregular heart rate; Unexplained heart failure. KEY QUESTIONS: Is there hemodynamic instability? Can the heart rate be explained by clinical condition (Fever etc.)? Is the arrhythmia incessant or episodic? Is there an underlying structural heart disease? Is this a re-entrant arrhythmia or does it involve an automatic focus?
risk: REGULAR TACHYCARDIA differentiation: Sinus tachycardia suggested by - Heart rates <220 - age, Subtle variations in rates, Associated fever/systemic illness other conditions, Bronchodilators/Adrenaline nebulization, Normal p prior to every QRS. Tachyarrhythmia suggested by - Fixed rates often >220 - age, Tachycardia not explained by clinical condition, Abnormal ECG (p waves not clearly seen or different from sinus rhythm or dissociated), Adenosine administration with ECG record is often diagnostic.
scores: HEMODYNAMIC STABILITY: Stable - Common, Stable/minimally distressed, Good perfusion. Unstable - Uncommon, Distressed, In shock, Poor perfusion; pulse not felt (Connect to Defibrillator paddles). Adenosine response: Sudden termination -> Re-entrant supraventricular tachycardia; No effect -> Sinus tachycardia, Junctional ectopic tachycardia, EAT; Slow and unmask -> Atrial Flutter, ectopic atrial tachycardia (EAT).
investigations: Obtain: 1. 12 lead ECG; Limb leads alone if child does not cooperate (If ECG machine is unavailable, a video recording of the monitor must be obtained); 2. Reliable I/V access; Proximal sites preferred. Obtaining ECG during arrhythmia is of great value as it enables precise diagnosis and treatment. All efforts must be made to document the tachyarrhythmia and its response to treatment.
drugs: Adenosine 100-200 mcg/kg, fast push, Max 0.3mg/kg (Undiluted, followed immediately by 5-10 ml saline bolus, fast push; Central line, ideally Brachial line enough in small children). Adenosine administration: Proximal access; Connect three-way to I/V port; Adenosine 100-200 mcg/Kg rapid I/V push followed immediately by 5-10 ml saline bolus; Always record Electrocardiogram (ECG) during administration; Always record ECG after treating the arrhythmia also. Defibrillation: 0.5-2 J/Kg synchronized for suspected SVT/VT; 2-4 J/Kg for VF (should not be synchronized).
management: MANAGEMENT by Hemodynamic Stability: STABLE (Common, Stable/minimally distressed, Good perfusion) -> Obtain 12 lead ECG and I/V access -> Adenosine. UNSTABLE (Uncommon, Distressed, In shock, Poor perfusion, pulse not felt) -> Connect to Defibrillator paddles -> Narrow QRS tachycardia / Regular Wide QRS tachycardia -> Synchronized Cardioversion 1J/Kg; Irregular Wide QRS tachycardia -> Defibrillation 2 J/Kg. ADENOSINE response algorithm: Sudden termination -> Re-entrant supraventricular tachycardia; No effect -> Sinus tachycardia, Junctional ectopic tachycardia, EAT; Slow and unmask -> Atrial Flutter, ectopic atrial tachycardia (EAT). Defibrillation: 0.5-2 J/Kg synchronized for suspected SVT/VT; 2-4 J/Kg for VF (should not be synchronized).
referralRedFlags: ALWAYS TRY IDENTIFY AND DOCUMENT THE ARRHYTHMIA PRIOR TO TREATMENT. Unstable (Distressed, In shock, Poor perfusion, pulse not felt) - Connect to Defibrillator paddles, Synchronized Cardioversion/Defibrillation. Obtaining ECG during arrhythmia is of great value. All efforts must be made to document the tachyarrhythmia and its response to treatment.
algorithmNotes: Assess hemodynamic stability first; stable -> obtain ECG/IV access and give adenosine (response helps diagnose); unstable -> synchronized cardioversion (1 J/Kg) or defibrillation (2 J/Kg for irregular wide QRS). Distinguish sinus tachycardia from tachyarrhythmia. ABBREV: EAT=Ectopic Atrial Tachycardia; SVT=Supraventricular Tachycardia; VT=Ventricular Tachycardia; VF=Ventricular Fibrillation; ECG=Electrocardiogram.
